revision.text:
{{short description|Public university in South Australia}} {{Use dmy dates|date=September 2020}} {{Use Australian English|date=July 2011}} {{about|the existing university in Adelaide|the proposed merged university|Adelaide University}} {{Infobox university | name = The University of Adelaide | latin_name = Universitas Adelaidensis{{Cite book |title=Record of the Jubilee Celebrations of the University of Sydney |date=1903 |publisher=William Brooks and Co. |isbn=9781112213304 |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]] |language=en-AU }}{{Cite book |title=Records of The Tercentenary Festival of Dublin University |date=1894 |publisher=[[Hodges Figgis|Hodges, Figgis & Co.]] |isbn=9781355361602 |publication-place=[[Dublin]], [[United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland|Ireland]] |language=en-IE }}{{Cite book |title=Actes du Jubilé de 1909 |date=1910 |publisher=Georg Keck & Cie |isbn=9781360078335 |publication-place=[[Geneva]], [[Switzerland]] }} | other_name = [[Adelaide University]]{{#tag:ref|Although ''Adelaide University'' has been used as a colloquial name since at least 1872, it is expected to supersede the current name following its merger with the [[University of South Australia]] by 2026.|group=Note}} | former_name = Union College
(1872–1874) | image_name = University-of-Adelaide-Crest.svg | image_alt = This is the coat of arms of the university. | caption = [[Coat of arms]]{{#tag:ref|The coat of arms, which is printed on degree parchments, has been ''de facto'' retired for new students starting in 2025. University of Adelaide alumni and continuing students who started their programs on or before 2024 will continue to have the option to graduate with [[Adelaide University]] degrees bearing the coat of arms. This excludes [[University of South Australia]] students and alumni, who will have access to their existing trademark instead.|group=Note}} | image_upright = .7 | logo = The University of Adelaide Logo.svg | logo_alt = This is the logo of the university. | logo_size = 225px | motto = {{langx|la|Sub Cruce Lumen}} | motto_lang = lat | mottoeng = "The light (of learning) under the (Southern) Cross" | established = {{start date and age|df=yes|1874|11|06}} | endowment = {{AUD}}393.4 [[1,000,000|million]] (2023) | budget = {{AUD}}1.09 [[1,000,000,000|billion]] (2023) | chancellor = [[Catherine Branson]] | vice_chancellor = [[Peter Høj]] | academic_staff = 1,700 (2023) | administrative_staff = 1,978 (2023) | total_staff = 3,678 (2023) | students = 30,279 (2023) | undergrad = 19,493 [[Bachelor's degree|bachelor]] (2023) | postgrad = 7,962 coursework (2023)
2,362 research (2023) | other = 772 (2023) | doctoral = | address = [[North Terrace, Adelaide|North Terrace]] | city = [[Adelaide]] | state = [[South Australia]] | postalcode = 5001 | country = [[Australia]] | website = {{URL|https://www.adelaide.edu.au/|adelaide.edu.au}} | footnotes = | coordinates = | campus = [[Urban area|Urban]] and [[Rural area|regional]] with multiple sites | colours = Black White | sports_nickname = [[Adelaide University Sport|The Blacks]] | mascot = Gus the Black Lion | sporting_affiliations = {{hlist |[[UniSport]] |[[Australian Institute of Sport#Elite Athlete Education Network|EAEN]] |[[University Basketball League Australia|UBL]] }} | academic_affiliations = {{hlist||[[Group of Eight (Australian universities)|Group of Eight]]|[[Association of Pacific Rim Universities|APRU]]|[[Australian Space Agency]]|[[South Australian Health and Medical Research Institute|SAHMRI]]|[[University of South Australia|UniSA]]|[[Open Universities Australia|OUA]]|[[Universities Australia]]}} | type = [[Public university|Public]] [[research university]] | accreditation = [[Tertiary Education Quality and Standards Agency|TEQSA]] }} The '''University of Adelaide''' is a [[public university|public]] [[research university]] based in [[Adelaide]], South Australia. Established in 1874, it is the third-oldest university in Australia. Its main campus in the [[Adelaide city centre]] includes many [[Sandstone universities|sandstone]] buildings of historical and architectural significance, such as [[Bonython Hall]]. Its [[royal charter]] awarded by [[Queen Victoria]] in 1881 allowed it to become the [[University of London|second university]] in the [[English-speaking world]] to confer degrees to women. It plans to merge with the neighbouring [[University of South Australia]] to form [[Adelaide University]]. The university was founded at the former [[South Australian Society of Arts|Royal South Australian Society of Arts]] by the Union College and studies were initially conducted at its [[State Library of South Australia|Institute Building]]. The society was also the original birthplace of the [[University of South Australia#South Australian Institute of Technology|South Australian Institute of Technology]] as the School of Mines and Industries. The institute later became the [[University of South Australia]] during the [[Dawkins Revolution]] following a merger with an [[University of South Australia#South Australian College of Advanced Education|advanced college]] dating back to the [[South Australian School of Art|School of Art]], also founded at the society. The two universities, which then accounted for approximately three-quarters of the state's public university population, agreed to merge in mid-2023. The future combined institution will be rebranded as [[Adelaide University]], previously a colloquial name for the university, with the merged state expected to become operational by 2026. The university has four campuses, three in [[South Australia]]: its main [[North Terrace, Adelaide|North Terrace]] campus in central Adelaide, the [[Peter Waite (philanthropist)|Waite campus]] in [[Urrbrae, South Australia|Urrbrae]], a regional campus in [[Roseworthy College|Roseworthy]] and a study centre in [[Melbourne]], [[Victoria (state)|Victoria]]. Its academic activities are organised into three faculties, which are subdivided into numerous teaching schools. It also has several research subdivisions. In 2023, the university had a total revenue of {{AUD|1.13 billion}}, with {{AUD|334.15 million}} from research grants and funding. It is a member of the [[Group of Eight (Australian universities)|Group of Eight]], an association of research-intensive universities in Australia, and the [[Association of Pacific Rim Universities]]. Notable alumni of the university include the [[Julia Gillard|first female prime minister]] of Australia, two [[List of presidents of Singapore|presidents of Singapore]], the [[Andy Thomas|first astronaut]] born in Australia and the [[Mark Oliphant|first demonstrator of nuclear fusion]]. It is also associated with five [[List of Nobel laureates|Nobel laureates]], constituting [[List of Australian Nobel laureates|one-third]] of Australia's total [[List of Australian Nobel laureates|Nobel laureates]], 117 [[List of Rhodes Scholars|Rhodes scholars]] and 168 [[Fulbright Program|Fulbright scholars]]. It has had a significant impact on the public life of South Australia, having educated many of the state's earliest businesspeople, lawyers, medical professionals and politicians. It has also been associated with the development of [[penicillin]], [[space exploration]], [[sunscreen]], the [[Tank|military tank]], [[Wi-Fi]], [[polymer banknotes]] and [[X-ray crystallography]], and the study of [[viticulture]] and [[oenology]]. ==History== [[File:The Founders Of The Adelaide University by Samuel Calvert - Illustrated Australian News (1875).jpg|thumb|The founders of the University of Adelaide (1875 engraving)|alt=An illustration of the founders of the University of Adelaide from an 1875 engraving.]] === Foundation === The history of the university dates back to the Union College established in 1872 to provide education to aspiring [[Protestantism|Protestant]] ministers who were previously required to travel to the [[United Kingdom]].{{Cite web |last1=Sumerling |first1=Patricia |last2=McDougall |first2=Katrina |date=August 2006 |title=The City of Adelaide: A Thematic History |url=https://data.sa.gov.au/data/dataset/adelaide-city-council-key-historical-dates/resource/45a3f1bf-7c69-4842-a3f0-b564fd6685b5 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191216102814/https://data.sa.gov.au/data/dataset/adelaide-city-council-key-historical-dates/resource/45a3f1bf-7c69-4842-a3f0-b564fd6685b5 |archive-date=16 December 2019 |access-date=28 August 2024 |website=McDougall & Vines |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite book |last=Hodder |first=Edwin |date=July 2013 |title=The History of South Australia Volume II. |url=https://gutenberg.net.au/ebooks13/1303951h.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240428190038/https://gutenberg.net.au/ebooks13/1303951h.html |archive-date=28 April 2024 |access-date=25 August 2024 |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]] |isbn=9781334291111}} It provided education in the natural sciences, mathematics, English literature and theological studies of the Greek Testament.{{Cite web |date=9 December 1886 |title=Disestablishing Union College |url=https://digital.library.adelaide.edu.au/dspace/bitstream/2440/15033/56/page148.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707044149/https://digital.library.adelaide.edu.au/dspace/bitstream/2440/15033/56/page148.pdf |archive-date=7 July 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=11 July 2024 |publisher=[[South Australian Register|The South Australian Register]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The college approached [[Scottish Australians|Scottish-born]] [[Pastoralism|pastoralist]] [[Walter Hughes (pastoralist)|Walter Watson Hughes]] with the proposal for a then-called Adelaide University with a request for endowment towards its creation.{{Cite news |date=4 November 1872 |title=The Proposed Adelaide University |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article28689891 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240711210047/https://trove.nla.gov.au/newspaper/article/28689891 |archive-date=11 July 2024 |access-date=11 July 2024 |work=[[The Advertiser (Adelaide)|The South Australian Advertiser]] |location=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]] |language=en-AU}} Following an agreement, the Adelaide University Association was established by the Union College on 23 September 1872 to manage the creation of the university.{{Cite news |date=31 July 1926 |title=UNIVERSITY JUBILEE. |url=https://trove.nla.gov.au/newspaper/article/54864446 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240710205718/https://trove.nla.gov.au/newspaper/article/54864446 |archive-date=10 July 2024 |access-date=10 July 2024 |work=[[South Australian Register|The Register]] |location=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]] |language=en-AU}} The University of Adelaide, which is named after its founding city namesake to [[Adelaide of Saxe-Meiningen|Queen Adelaide]], was formally established on 6 November 1874 following the passage of ''The Adelaide University Act of 1874'' through the [[Parliament of South Australia|South Australian parliament]].{{Cite web |last=McGuire |first=Michael |date=3 May 2013 |title=How well do you really know our Queen Adelaide? |url=https://www.adelaidenow.com.au/news/south-australia/how-well-do-you-really-know-our-queen-adelaide/news-story/b249bd054376d472496f232a7f3d75ed |url-status=live |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The parliament also provided a 2 hectare (5 acre) land grant for a campus.{{Cite web |title=North Terrace Campus |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7322 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707201936/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7322 |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=17 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Its [[royal charter]], which was granted by [[Queen Victoria]] in 1881, allowed the university to confer degrees to women.{{Cite web |date=1981 |title=1981-1983 Calendar: Volume I |url=https://digital.library.adelaide.edu.au/dspace/bitstream/2440/104006/1/1981%20-%201983%20Calendar%20-%20Volume%201%20-%20General%20Information.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240616103036/https://digital.library.adelaide.edu.au/dspace/bitstream/2440/104006/1/1981%20-%201983%20Calendar%20-%20Volume%201%20-%20General%20Information.pdf |archive-date=16 June 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=26 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |publisher=Griffin Press |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Its early benefactors, many of whom [[Scottish Australians|Scottish immigrants]], made large donations to develop the university that are now worth tens of millions adjusted for inflation.{{Cite book |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/30399 |title=The University of Adelaide - 150 Years of Making History |publisher=The University of Adelaide |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]] |language=en-AU |access-date=19 August 2024 [[Queen Victoria]] allowed women to study at the university]] It was founded with the backing of its first benefactor Walter Hughes and [[Thomas Elder]], also a Scottish-born pastoralist and another founder of the university, who each donated £20,000 towards the association.{{Cite web |last1=Dutton |first1=Connie |last2=Sanchez |first2=Genevieve |last3=Williams |first3=Ian |last4=Cook |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Citation |last=Van Dissel |first=Dirk |title=Sir Walter Watson Hughes (1803–1887) |date=1972 |work=[[Australian Dictionary of Biography]] |volume=4 |url=https://adb.anu.edu.au/biography/hughes-sir-walter-watson-3813 |access-date=10 July 2024 £65,000 in his will following his death in 1897 of which £20,000 were allocated to set up the [[Elder Conservatorium of Music]]. Other donors include Scottish philosopher [[William Mitchell (philosopher)|William Mitchell]] who also taught literature and psychology, established many teaching schools and served as vice-chancellor and chancellor of the institution. The University Building, now the chancellery, was later renamed to the Mitchell Building in his honour.{{Cite web |title=Mitchell Building, The University of Adelaide |url=https://www.experienceadelaide.com.au/heritage-places/mitchell-building-the-university-of-adelaide-north-terrace-adelaide/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240825232805/https://www.experienceadelaide.com.au/heritage-places/mitchell-building-the-university-of-adelaide-north-terrace-adelaide/ |archive-date=25 August 2024 |access-date=25 August 2024 |website=Experience Adelaide |publisher=[[City of Adelaide|The Corporation of the City of Adelaide]] (Adelaide Economic Development Agency) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} According to its founding Act, the university was intended as a secular institution to "promote sound learning in the [[History of South Australia#Self-governing colony (1856)|Province of South Australia]]" to be "open to all classes and denominations of [[Queen Victoria|Her Majesty's]] subjects". It commenced its first class, a [[Instruction in Latin|Latin]] lecture towards the [[Bachelor of Arts]], in March 1876 following its inauguration at the [[Adelaide Town Hall]].{{Cite book and served on the University Council for 19 years, had long desired for education to be accessible to all students in Adelaide.{{Cite book |last=Gosse |first=Fayette |archive-date=22 July 2024 |url-status=live}} In 1913, Robert wrote at the age of 89 that "''tho' in its vigorous and lusty youth''," the university was poorly endowed and constructed on little land. His donations included a combined £9000 towards books for the [[University of Adelaide#Barr Smith Library|then-struggling]] university library and £500 towards radiation research by [[Lawrence Bragg|Lawrence]] and [[William Henry Bragg]] who later won the 1915 [[Nobel Prize in Physics]].{{Cite book |last=Burn |first=Margy |url=https://digital.library.adelaide.edu.au/dspace/handle/2440/15901 energy]]|alt=This is a photograph of the interior of Bonython Hall, a venue for graduations and other events, during a public forum on nuclear energy.]] [[Bonython Hall]], the great hall of the university, was built in 1936 following a donation of over £50,000 from the owner of [[The Advertiser (Adelaide)|''The Advertiser'']] newspaper, [[John Langdon Bonython]], who was inspired following his visit to the [[Great Hall of the University of Sydney]].{{Cite web |last=Elton |first=Jude |title=Bonython Hall |url=https://sahistoryhub.history.sa.gov.au/places/bonython-hall |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240611085054/https://sahistoryhub.history.sa.gov.au/places/bonython-hall |archive-date=11 June 2024 |access-date=10 July 2024 |website=SA History Hub |publisher=[[Government of South Australia]] ([[History Trust of South Australia]]) |language=en-AU}} The hall, which is used during graduation ceremonies among other events, was designed by architect [[Louis Laybourne Smith|Louis Laybourne-Smith]] based on medieval [[great hall]]s in a [[Gothic Revival architecture]] style inspired by the [[Ancient university|ancient universities]] in Europe.{{Cite web |title=Bonython Hall - University of Adelaide |url=https://simonfieldhouse.com/adelaide-architecture/bonython-hall-university-of-adelide/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240710215504/https://simonfieldhouse.com/adelaide-architecture/bonython-hall-university-of-adelide/ |archive-date=10 July 2024 |access-date=10 July 2024 |website=[[Simon Fieldhouse]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}} [[File:Bonython Hall, Adelaide by Paul Weston (cropped).jpg|alt=This is a photograph of Bonython Hall, which was inspired by the Great Hall of the University of Sydney and of the ancient universities in Europe.|thumb|[[Bonython Hall]], inspired by the [[Great Hall of the University of Sydney]] and [[Ancient university|ancient universities]]]] The institution was the third of its kind on the Australian continent after the Universities of [[University of Sydney|Sydney]] and [[University of Melbourne|Melbourne]], which then educated solely men.{{Cite web |date=3 July 2024 |title=South Australia - Government, Society, Constitution |url=https://www.britannica.com/place/South-Australia/Government-and-society |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240223150840/https://www.britannica.com/place/South-Australia/Government-and-society |archive-date=23 February 2024 |access-date=3 July 2024 |website=[[Encyclopædia Britannica]] |publisher=[[Encyclopædia Britannica, Inc.]] |language=en |publication-place=[[Chicago]], [[Illinois]]}}{{Cite web |last=Radford |first=Neil |date=2016 |title=University of Sydney |url=https://dictionaryofsydney.org/entry/university_of_sydney |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240303184130/https://dictionaryofsydney.org/entry/university_of_sydney |archive-date=3 March 2024 |access-date=10 July 2024 |website=The Dictionary of Sydney |publisher=[[State Library of New South Wales]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}}{{Cite web |title=The University of Melbourne. (1853-) |url=https://trove.nla.gov.au/people/593909 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240615031757/https://trove.nla.gov.au/people/593909 |archive-date=15 June 2024 |access-date=10 July 2024 |website=[[Trove]] |publisher=[[National Library of Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} The university, which allowed women to study alongside men since its commencement including eligibility for all academic prizes and honours, became the second university in the [[English-speaking world]] following the [[University of London]] in 1878 to formally admit women on equal terms as men in 1881.{{Cite web |title=University of Adelaide second in the world and Australia's first to admit women students on equal terms in 1881 |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/adelaide-university-second-in-world-first-in-australia-admits-women-in-1881 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240303223758/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/adelaide-university-second-in-world-first-in-australia-admits-women-in-1881 |archive-date=3 March 2024 |access-date=10 July 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} This was following a [[royal charter]] granted by [[Queen Victoria]] that year, which allowed for women to be conferred degrees. This has contributed to its long history of achieving [[University of Adelaide#Women's education|notable milestones and firsts]] for women's rights in higher education. In 1991, it formally opened two additional campuses in Greater Adelaide outside of the city centre. These included the [[Urrbrae, South Australia|Waite]] and [[Roseworthy College|Roseworthy]] campuses, though the university operated at the Waite site since at least 1924 as the Waite Agricultural Research Institute.{{Cite journal |date=1 August 1934 |title=The Waite Agricultural Research Institute |url=https://www.nature.com/articles/134258b0 |journal=[[Nature (journal)|Nature]] former [[Roseworthy College|Roseworthy Agricultural College]] which, although affiliated with the university since 1905, was an independent institution prior to their merger.{{Cite web |last=Gibson |archive-date=17 July 2021 |access-date=23 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Additionally, the university International |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} === Neighbouring amalgamations === {{See also|Dawkins Revolution}} [[File:Jubilee Exhibition Building 1885.jpg|alt=This is a photograph of the now-demolished Jubilee Exhibition Building in 1885.|thumb|The now-demolished [[Jubilee Exhibition Building]] in 1885]] The [[South Australian School of Art|SA School of Art]] was founded in 1856 by the former [[South Australian Society of Arts|Royal South Australian Society of Arts]], predating the university which was also established there.{{Cite web |last=Aland |first=Jenny |date=May 2023 |title=FRIENDS SASA 170-YEAR HISTORY BOOK |url=https://thefriendsofsasa.org.au/history-project |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240314131316/https://thefriendsofsasa.org.au/history-project |archive-date=14 March 2024 |access-date=19 August 2024 |website=Friends of the South Australian School of Art |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Our Proud Antecedent History |url=https://unisa.edu.au/connect/alumni-network/antecedent-history/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240202095428/https://unisa.edu.au/connect/alumni-network/antecedent-history/ |archive-date=2 February 2024 |access-date=9 May 2024 |website=[[University of South Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The independent art school, which went through many name changes, resided for most of its history at the [[Jubilee Exhibition Building]] which was later transferred to the university in 1929. It remained on the campus until 1962 when the building was demolished to make way for several university buildings.{{Cite web |title=Jubilee building, for 1887 global exhibition attended by twice Adelaide's population, demolished in 1962 |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/jubilee-building--home-to-1887-international-exhibition-attended-by-twice-adelaide-s-population--demolished-in-1962 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240710234803/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/jubilee-building--home-to-1887-international-exhibition-attended-by-twice-adelaide-s-population--demolished-in-1962 |archive-date=10 July 2024 |access-date=10 July 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Jubilee Exhibition Building (1887-1962) |url=https://heritageforthepeople.au/jubilee-exhibition-building-1887-1962/ |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231201034953/https://heritageforthepeople.au/jubilee-exhibition-building-1887-1962/ |archive-date=1 December 2023 |access-date=19 August 2024 |website=Heritage For The People |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The Jubilee Exhibition Building was also the birthplace of the [[University of South Australia#South Australian Institute of Technology|South Australian Institute of Technology]] which was established in 1889 as the SA School of Mines and Industries.{{Cite web |title=School of Mines and Industries starts formal technical education in South Australia from 1889 |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/school-of-mines-and-industries-brings-formal-technical-education-to-south-australia-in-1889--later-the-institute-of-technology |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240711184514/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/school-of-mines-and-industries-brings-formal-technical-education-to-south-australia-in-1889--later-the-institute-of-technology |archive-date=11 July 2024 |access-date=11 July 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |date=3 October 2023 |title=A legacy that helped shape SA |url=https://unisa.edu.au/connect/alumni-network/alumni-news/alumni-connect/2023/issue7/business/a-legacy-that-helped-shape-sa/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240711183134/https://www.unisa.edu.au/connect/alumni-network/alumni-news/alumni-connect/2023/issue7/business/a-legacy-that-helped-shape-sa/ |archive-date=11 July 2024 |access-date=11 July 2024 |website=[[University of South Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It moved to the neighbouring Brookman Building in 1903, named after the [[Scottish Australians|Scottish-born]] businessman [[George Brookman]] who contributed £15,000 towards its construction.{{Cite web |date=24 February 1902 |title=SCHOOL OF MINES. |url=https://trove.nla.gov.au/newspaper/article/4928117 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221112222958/https://trove.nla.gov.au/newspaper/article/4928117 |archive-date=12 November 2022 |access-date=20 August 2024 |website=[[The Advertiser (Adelaide)|The Advertiser]] |page=5 |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |last=Elton |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240229225703/https://sahistoryhub.history.sa.gov.au/places/brookman-building |archive-date=29 February 2024 |access-date=11 July 2024 |website=SA History Hub |publisher=[[Government of South Australia]] ([[History Trust of South Australia]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The institution acted ''de facto'' as part of the university while remaining legally distinct. The relationship was expanded in 1903 with the two institutions formally agreeing to combine teaching, laboratories and examinations across fields of engineering and sciences.{{Cite web |title=South Australian School of Mines and Industries |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25616 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240301034615/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25616 |archive-date=1 March 2024 |access-date=3 July 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=SA School of Mines and Industries |access-date=3 July 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Despite the university later establishing its own faculty of engineering in 1937, the reciprocal relationship remained intertwined to the University Council and studies completed at the institute were recognised as equivalent studies eligible for credit towards university courses.{{Citation |last=Edgar |first=Suzanne |title=Reginald Theodore Kleeman (1901–1979) |date=2000 |work=[[Australian Dictionary of Biography]] |volume=15 |url=https://adb.anu.edu.au/biography/kleeman-reginald-theodore-10757 |access-date=11 July 2024 [[Australian Capital Territory]] |publisher=[[Australian National University]] (which owns "National Centre of Biography") |language=en-AU}}{{Cite web |title=Faculty of Engineering |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7401 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230323201258/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7401 |archive-date=23 March 2023 |access-date=30 June 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The institution expanded into the site of the former Adelaide Technical High School in 1963, to the regional city of [[Whyalla]] in 1962 and to the Adelaide suburb of [[Mawson Lakes, South Australia|Mawson Lakes]] as ''The Levels'' in 1972. In 1965, it was designated an [[College of Advanced Education|advanced college]] which initiated an expansion in the variety of courses available.{{Cite web |title=Milestones |url=http://www.unisa.edu.au/about/intro/milestones.asp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120218134712/http://www.unisa.edu.au/about/intro/milestones.asp |archive-date=18 February 2012 |access-date=14 October 2024 |website=[[University of South Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} [[File:Adelaide University 1926 map.jpg|alt=This map shows the university and the locations of its neighbouring institutions in 1926.|thumb|220x220px|State of the university and its surrounding institutions in 1926]] The Adelaide Teachers College, which changed names and shifted locations multiple times throughout its existence, was established in 1876. Despite not being located at the university campus until 1900, students from the institution attended university lectures since at least 1878. In 1921, it renamed to the Adelaide Teachers College, in line with other interstate teachers colleges. Despite offers from the university to take control of the college, which was heavily integrated into the university, the Education Department retained administrative authority throughout its early history.{{Cite web |title=Adelaide Teachers College |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/records/university-archives/related-institutions/adelaide-teachers-college |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240514071951/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/records/university-archives/related-institutions/adelaide-teachers-college |archive-date=14 May 2024 |access-date=18 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The Hartley Building, named after former vice-chancellor [[John Anderson Hartley]], was built as its permanent home in 1927.{{Cite web |title=Hartley Building (originally Adelaide Teachers College), The University of Adelaide |url=https://www.experienceadelaide.com.au/heritage-places/hartley-building-originally-adelaide-teachers-college-the-university-of-adelaide-kintore-avenue-adelaide/ |url-status=live buildings such as the Scott Theatre, Madley and Schulz buildings and eventually renamed to the Adelaide College of the Arts and Education.{{Cite web |last=Hernen |first=Danielle |date=2020 |archive-date=9 July 2024 |access-date=13 July 2024 |website=[[National Centre for Vocational Education Research]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It was originally created by the [[Menzies government (1949–1966)|Menzies government]] following [[World War II]] on the advice of a committee led by physicist [[Leslie H. Martin]], during a period of high population growth and corresponding demand for secondary and tertiary education.{{Cite web |date=24 March 1965 |title=SPEECH BY RT. HON. SIR ROBERT MENZIES, K.T., C.H., Q.C., M.P., ON TERTIARY EDUCATION IN AUSTRALIA |url=https://pmtranscripts.pmc.gov.au/sites/default/files/original/00001081_0.pdf |url-status=live 2024 |website=[[Deakin University]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Melbourne]], [[Victoria (state)|Victoria]]}} This sector ceased to exist when, between 1989 and 1992, the [[Hawke–Keating government|Hawke-Keating government]] implemented the [[Dawkins Revolution|sweeping reforms]] of Education Minister [[John Dawkins]] that dismantled the binary system.{{Cite web |date=18 December 2023 |title=2022 Section 1 Commencing students |url=https://www.education.gov.au/higher-education-statistics/resources/2022-section-1-commencing-students |url-status=live Territory]]}} The states, eager for increased education funding, merged the colleges either with existing universities or with each other to form new universities.{{Cite web |date=2004 |title=Australian Higher Education Institutions as at 4 November 2004 |url=http://www.avcc.edu.au/documents/universities/AustralianHEMerges-Amalgamations.pdf |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061110170536/http://www.avcc.edu.au/documents/universities/AustralianHEMerges-Amalgamations.pdf |archive-date=10 November 2006 |archive-format=PDF Capital Territory]]}} Following its expansion and increasing autonomy from the university, the South Australian Institute of Technology was given the option to merge with either TAFE South Australia or the South Australian College of Advanced Education. It chose to merge with the latter advanced college resulting in the establishment of the [[University of South Australia]], which continues to remain neighbours with the university. === Ongoing merger with the University of South Australia === {{seealso|Adelaide University|University of South Australia}} [[File:Brookman Building on North Terrace, Adelaide, South Australia (cropped).jpg|thumb|The University of South Australia's Brookman Building located next to [[Bonython Hall]]|alt=This is a photograph of the Brookman Building of the University of South Australia through which both universities have had an intertwined history.]] The [[University of South Australia]] is the state's third public university, a continuation of the former [[University of South Australia#South Australian Institute of Technology|South Australian Institute of Technology]] that merged with most of the remaining SACAE, and maintained their presence next to the University of Adelaide, in the suburbs of [[Mawson Lakes, South Australia|Mawson Lakes]] and [[Magill, South Australia|Magill]] and in the regional city of [[Whyalla]].{{Cite book |last=Mackinnon |first=Alison |title=A New Kid on the Block: the University of South Australia in the Unified National System |date=July 2016 |publisher=[[Melbourne University Publishing]] ([[University of Melbourne|The University of Melbourne]]) |isbn=9780522870572 |location=[[Melbourne]], [[Victoria (state)|Victoria]] |language=en-AU }} Its expansion over the next few decades, including to sites on the west end of [[North Terrace, Adelaide|North Terrace]], and broadening fields of studies contributed to its status as the state's largest university by student population. It also became the second-largest university nationally by number of online students, either in the state or from other parts of the country, and expanded to [[Mount Gambier]] in 2005.{{Cite web |last1=Watkinson |first1=Julie |last2=Ellis |first2=Bronwyn Joan |last3=Sawyer |first3=Janet |date=October 2010 |title=Collaborative development of a university presence in regional South Australia |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228743416 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240820191757/https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228743416_Collaborative_development_of_a_university_presence_in_regional_South_Australia |archive-date=20 August 2024 |access-date=20 August 2024 |website=[[ResearchGate]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Berlin]], [[Germany]]}} In June 2018, the University of Adelaide and [[University of South Australia]] began discussions regarding the possibility of a merger. The proposition was dubbed a "super uni" by then South Australian premier, [[Steven Marshall]], and [[Simon Birmingham]],{{cite news |last=Griffiths |first=Luke |date=19 June 2018 |title=South Australian universities in merger talks |url=https://www.theaustralian.com.au/higher-education/south-australian-universities-in-merger-talks/news-story/cdaaba662218904f4e856ee48e32ca27?nk=b12ecadd9292107e802d5324e80aa7c9-1529400560 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220121134116/https://www.theaustralian.com.au/higher-education/south-australian-universities-in-merger-talks/news-story/cdaaba662218904f4e856ee48e32ca27?nk=b12ecadd9292107e802d5324e80aa7c9-1529400560 |archive-date=21 January 2022 |access-date=19 June 2018 |work=[[The Australian]] |publisher=[[News Corp]]oration (which owns "[[News Corp Australia]]") |location=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]] |language=en-AU}}{{Cite news |date=19 June 2018 |title=University of Adelaide and UniSA to explore potential merger to create new institution |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2018-06-19/sa-universities-to-discuss-potential-merger/9885996 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180619072551/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2018-06-19/sa-universities-to-discuss-potential-merger/9885996 |archive-date=19 June 2018 |access-date=19 June 2018 |work=[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]] |publisher=[[Australian Broadcasting Corporation]] |location=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]] |language=en-AU}}{{Cite web |date=19 June 2018 |title=Adelaide Uni and UniSA in merger talks |url=https://www.adelaidenow.com.au/news/south-australia/adelaide-university-and-unisa-to-officially-explore-merging-their-institutions/news-story/f0103cfe4e3e08a91c4fafd7246c9a47 |access-date=21 August 2024 |website=[[The Advertiser (Adelaide)|The Advertiser]] |publisher=[[News Corp]]oration (which owns "[[News Corp Australia]]") |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} but the merger was called off in October 2018 by the University of South Australia, which was less keen.{{Cite news |date=23 October 2018 |title=South Australian universities decide against merger |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2018-10-23/unisa-and-adelaide-university-decide-against-merger/10418520 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181025152513/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2018-10-23/unisa-and-adelaide-university-decide-against-merger/10418520 |archive-date=25 October 2018 |access-date=24 October 2018 |work=[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]] |location=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]] |language=en-AU}} Vice-chancellor [[David Lloyd (chemist)|David Lloyd]], in an email to University of South Australia staff, claimed that the amalgamation lacked a compelling case. This statement was contradicted by the University of Adelaide's chancellor who said that the merger continues to be in the state's best interests and a spokesperson for the university added that it was still open to future talks.{{Cite news |last=Siebert |first=Bension |date=31 October 2020 |title=Labor promises commission on merging South Australia's three top universities |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2020-10-31/sa-labor-election-promise-university-merger-commission/12834964 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240125210848/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2020-10-31/sa-labor-election-promise-university-merger-commission/12834964 |archive-date=25 January 2024 |access-date=9 July 2024 |work=[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]] |publisher=[[Australian Broadcasting Corporation]] |location=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]] |language=en-AU}} Following the release of several internal [[Freedom of Information Act 1982|FOI]] documents retrieved by [[ABC News (Australia)|''ABC News'']], it was later revealed that the merger talks failed due to disagreements on the post-merger institution's leadership structure. The name ''Adelaide University of South Australia'' was agreed upon by both universities and [[Chris Schacht]], who previously served on the University of Adelaide Council, alleged that the merger talks failed due to disagreement on which vice-chancellor would replace the other following their amalgamation.{{Cite news |last=Siebert |first=Bension |date=20 January 2021 |title=University of Adelaide texts, emails reveal backstory to merger breakdown |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2021-01-21/documents-shed-light-on-university-of-adelaide-sa-failed-merger/12960276 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240205131614/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2021-01-21/documents-shed-light-on-university-of-adelaide-sa-failed-merger/12960276 |archive-date=5 February 2024 |access-date=7 May 2024 |work=[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]] |publisher=[[Australian Broadcasting Corporation]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}} In early 2022, the topic of a merger was raised again by the new state government led by premier [[Peter Malinauskas]], which proposed setting up an independent commission to investigate the possibility of a merger between the state's three public universities should they decline.{{Cite web |last=Kelsall |first=Thomas |date=27 June 2023 |title='The government has them in a headlock': Uni merger decision imminent |url=https://www.indaily.com.au/news/2023/06/27/the-government-has-them-in-a-headlock-uni-merger-decision-imminent |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230630070912/https://www.indaily.com.au/news/2023/06/27/the-government-has-them-in-a-headlock-uni-merger-decision-imminent |archive-date=30 June 2023 |access-date=21 August 2024 |website=InDaily |publisher=[[Solstice Media]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} He had made an election promise to take a heavy-handed approach towards the merger to reduce students departing to higher-ranking institutions on the east coast and to improve the state's ability to attract international students and researchers. At the time, staff's opinions were evenly divided on the idea of the commission.{{cite web |last=Richards |first=Stephanie |date=3 June 2022 |title=Uni staff divided over latest merger push |url=https://indaily.com.au/news/2022/06/03/uni-staff-divided-over-latest-merger-push/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220605121721/https://indaily.com.au/news/2022/06/03/uni-staff-divided-over-latest-merger-push/ |archive-date=5 June 2022 |access-date=5 June 2022 |website=InDaily |publisher=[[Solstice Media]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Following the appointment of merger advocate [[Peter Høj]] as University of Adelaide vice-chancellor, both universities announced that a merger would once again be considered.{{Cite web |last=Chapman |first=Jemma |date=3 June 2022 |title=Uni staff divided over latest merger push |url=https://www.indaily.com.au/news/2022/06/03/uni-staff-divided-over-latest-merger-push |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230629074105/https://indaily.com.au/news/2022/06/03/uni-staff-divided-over-latest-merger-push/ |archive-date=29 June 2023 |access-date=9 July 2024 |website=InDaily |publisher=[[Solstice Media]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The universities began a feasibility study into a potential merger at the end of the year.{{Cite news |date=7 December 2022 |title=University of Adelaide and UniSA revive merger talks, with single uni pitched for 2026 |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2022-12-07/universities-of-adelaide-and-south-australia-move-to-merge/101746396 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240505153915/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2022-12-07/universities-of-adelaide-and-south-australia-move-to-merge/101746396 |archive-date=5 May 2024 |access-date=4 July 2023 |work=[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]] |location=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]] |language=en-AU}} The invitation to merger negotiations was rejected by [[Flinders University]], the state's third public university.{{Cite web |last=Richards |first=Stephanie |date=8 December 2022 |title=Flinders Uni 'rejected invitation' to merge with Adelaide Uni/UniSA |url=https://www.indaily.com.au/news/2022/12/08/flinders-uni-rejected-invitation-to-merge-with-adelaide-uni-unisa |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240401013212/https://www.indaily.com.au/news/2022/12/08/flinders-uni-rejected-invitation-to-merge-with-adelaide-uni-unisa |archive-date=1 April 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=InDaily |publisher=[[Solstice Media]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} [[File:Growing Universities.jpg|thumb|The University of Adelaide (right) is set to merge with the [[University of South Australia]] (left) by 1 January 2026|alt=This is a photograph of two neighbouring buildings, each belonging to the University of South Australia and the University of Adelaide respectively.]] The agreement for the merger was reached on 1 July 2023 by the two universities, which then accounted for approximately two-thirds of the state's public university population, in consultation with the [[Government of South Australia|South Australian Government]].{{Cite news |last1=Richards |first1=Stephanie |last2=Boscaini |first2=Joshua |last3=Kagi |first3=Jacob |date=1 July 2023 |title=University of South Australia and University of Adelaide reach agreement to merge |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2023-07-02/sa-adelaide-university-merger-deal/102547600 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240308064913/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2023-07-02/sa-adelaide-university-merger-deal/102547600 |archive-date=8 March 2024 |access-date=26 March 2024 |work=[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]] |publisher=[[Australian Broadcasting Corporation]] |location=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]] |language=en-AU}}{{Cite news |last1=Richards |first1=Stephanie |last2=Lim |first2=Josephine |last3=Clarke |first3=Bernadette |date=16 August 2023 |title=University merger delay could come at $250 million cost, but impact on jobs unclear |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2023-08-16/adelaide-university-merger-delay-could-cost-millions/102730508 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240220223808/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2023-08-16/adelaide-university-merger-delay-could-cost-millions/102730508 |archive-date=20 February 2024 |access-date=26 March 2024 |work=[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]] |publisher=[[Australian Broadcasting Corporation]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}}{{Cite web |date=June 2024 |title=2023 Pocket Statistics |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/planning/ua/media/1614/pocket-stats-2024_web.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240701145118/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/planning/ua/media/1614/pocket-stats-2024_web.pdf |archive-date=1 July 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=19 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |date=30 June 2024 |title=2023 Annual Review |url=https://unisa.edu.au/siteassets/about-unisa/docs/unisa-annual-review-2023.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240901125742/https://unisa.edu.au/siteassets/about-unisa/docs/unisa-annual-review-2023.pdf |archive-date=1 September 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=19 September 2024 |website=[[University of South Australia]] |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |date=30 June 2023 |title=2023 Annual Report |url=https://www.flinders.edu.au/content/dam/documents/about/annual-reports/2023-annual-report.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240919145642/https://www.flinders.edu.au/content/dam/documents/about/annual-reports/2023-annual-report.pdf |archive-date=19 September 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=19 September 2024 |website=[[Flinders University]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The rationale for the amalgamation was a larger institutional scale may be needed in order to increase the universities' ranking positions, ability to secure future research income and a net positive impact on the state economy.{{Cite news |last=McClaren |first=Rory |date=18 August 2023 |title=The unis might be in unison, but is the SA merger deal a unicorn? |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2023-08-19/debate-over-south-australia-adelaide-university-merger/102747032 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240410154632/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2023-08-19/debate-over-south-australia-adelaide-university-merger/102747032 |archive-date=10 April 2024 |access-date=7 May 2024 |work=[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]] |publisher=[[Australian Broadcasting Corporation]] |location=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]] |language=en-AU}}{{Cite web |date=17 October 2023 |title=Report of the Joint Committee on the Establishment of Adelaide University |url=https://adelaideuni.edu.au/siteassets/docs/report-of-the-joint-committee-on-the-establishment-of-adelaide-university.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240426070938/https://adelaideuni.edu.au/siteassets/docs/report-of-the-joint-committee-on-the-establishment-of-adelaide-university.pdf |archive-date=26 April 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=7 May 2024 |publisher=[[Parliament of South Australia]] |language=en-AU }} The two universities argued that by combining their expertise, resources and finances into a single institution, they can be more financially viable, with stronger teaching and research outcomes. Support for the merger among existing staff were mixed, with a [[National Tertiary Education Union]] SA survey showing that only a quarter were in favour of the amalgamation.{{Cite web |last=Kelsall |first=Thomas |date=16 June 2023 |title=Uni staff fears over merger impact on education and research |url=https://www.indaily.com.au/news/2023/06/16/uni-staff-fears-over-merger-impact-on-education-and-research |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240811235248/https://www.indaily.com.au/news/2023/06/16/uni-staff-fears-over-merger-impact-on-education-and-research |archive-date=11 August 2024 |access-date=21 August 2024 |website=InDaily |publisher=[[Solstice Media]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} [[Warren Bebbington]], who previously served as vice-chancellor at the University of Adelaide, described the proposed institution as a "lumbering dinosaur" in reference to its timing during an ongoing federal review of the higher education sector. Vice-chancellor [[Colin Stirling]] described plans to provide the new institution with {{AUD|300 million}} in research funding and scholarships as "unfair" to students who choose to study at Flinders University. The combined figure was later revised to {{AUD|464.5 million}} to include land purchases, with an additional {{AUD|40 million}} research fund set up for Flinders University. [[File:Murray House, UniSA.JPG|alt=This is a photograph of the Murray House on the Magill site, one of two University of South Australia campuses where land will be sold for housing and commercial development.|thumb|The Magill campus is [[University of Adelaide#Land re-development|one of two]] UniSA campuses where land will be sold for development]] In November 2023, legislation passed [[Parliament of South Australia|state parliament]] enabling the creation of the new university to be named ''Adelaide University'', previously a colloquial name used by the University of Adelaide.{{cite web |date=16 November 2023 |title=University merger legislation clears parliament |url=https://www.indaily.com.au/news/2023/11/16/university-merger-legislation-clears-parliament |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240326085055/https://www.indaily.com.au/news/2023/11/16/university-merger-legislation-clears-parliament |archive-date=26 March 2024 |access-date=7 May 2024 |website=InDaily |publisher=[[Solstice Media]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Search |url=https://trove.nla.gov.au/search?keyword=%22Adelaide+University%22 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240714011736/https://trove.nla.gov.au/search?keyword=%22Adelaide+University%22 |archive-date=14 July 2024 |access-date=14 July 2024 |website=[[Trove]] |publisher=[[National Library of Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} An application for self-accreditation authority was submitted to the [[Tertiary Education Quality and Standards Agency]] (TEQSA) on 15 January 2024, which was needed for the institution to offer courses that issue qualifications.{{Cite web |title=TEQSA application submitted |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/staff/news/news/list/2024/02/02/teqsa-application-submitted |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240507020825/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/staff/news/news/list/2024/02/02/teqsa-application-submitted |archive-date=7 May 2024 |access-date=7 May 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |date=19 September 2022 |title=Application Guide for Self-Accrediting Authority |url=https://www.teqsa.gov.au/sites/default/files/saa-application-guide-2022-v0-6.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230320212437/https://www.teqsa.gov.au/sites/default/files/saa-application-guide-2022-v0-6.pdf |archive-date=20 March 2023 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=7 May 2024 |website=[[Tertiary Education Quality and Standards Agency]] |publisher=[[Australian Government]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} Following approval on 22 May 2024, students starting studies at the pre-merger institutions from 2025 onwards will be issued degree certificates from Adelaide University.{{Cite web |date=22 May 2024 |title=Adelaide University |url=https://www.teqsa.gov.au/provider/adelaide-university |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240625164250/https://www.teqsa.gov.au/provider/adelaide-university |archive-date=25 June 2024 |access-date=25 June 2024 |website=[[Tertiary Education Quality and Standards Agency]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Melbourne]], [[Victoria (state)|Victoria]]}}{{Cite web |last= |first= |title=FAQs |url=https://adelaideuni.edu.au/ |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240506194851/https://adelaideuni.edu.au/#students |archive-date=6 May 2024 |access-date=7 May 2024 |website=[[Adelaide University]] |language=en-AU}} Students enrolled on or prior to 2024 will also be able to opt in adding antecedent institutions' names and logos on their parchments. The combined institution is expected to become operational by January 2026, with an additional transitional period extending to 2034.{{Cite news |last1=Richards |first1=Stephanie |last2=Boscaini |first2=Joshua |last3=Kagi |first3=Jacob |date=1 July 2023 |title=University merger deal reached to create one of Australia's largest education institutions |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2023-07-02/sa-adelaide-university-merger-deal/102547600 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240308064913/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2023-07-02/sa-adelaide-university-merger-deal/102547600 |archive-date=8 March 2024 |access-date=4 July 2023 |work=[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]] |publisher=[[Australian Broadcasting Corporation]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}}{{Cite web |date=15 April 2024 |title=Our Strategic Ambition and Direction 2024-2034 |url=https://adelaideuni.edu.au/siteassets/docs/au-strategic-ambition-and-direction-2024-34.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240508183254/https://adelaideuni.edu.au/siteassets/docs/au-strategic-ambition-and-direction-2024-34.pdf |archive-date=8 May 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=8 May 2024 |website=[[Adelaide University]] |language=en-AU }} It is projected to have 70,000 students at launch, with one-in-four students being international students, and contribute approximately {{AUD|4.7 billion}} to the Australian economy annually.{{Cite web |date=14 October 2024 |title=Turning to the future: Adelaide University will bring about much change |url=https://www.indaily.com.au/news/sponsored-content/2024/10/15/turning-to-the-future-adelaide-university-will-bring-about-much-change |access-date=17 October 2024 |website=InDaily |publisher=[[Solstice Media]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The amalgamation has been [[University of Adelaide#University merger|subject to mixed reactions]]. ==Campuses and buildings== ===Adelaide=== [[File:The Mitchell Building on the University of Adelaide campus on North Terrace. - 24599815649.jpg|thumb|220x220px|The Mitchell Building, formerly the University Building, is the oldest building on campus|alt=This is a photograph of the Mitchell Building, formerly the University Building, which is the oldest building on campus.]] The primary campus of the university is located on [[North Terrace, Adelaide|North Terrace]] in the [[Adelaide city centre]], one of four terraces bounding the inner city's [[central business district]].{{Cite web |date=30 May 2017 |title=Capital City Policy Review (Design Quality) Development Plan Amendment |url=https://plan.sa.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0009/856818/Capital_City_Policy_Review_Design_Quality_DPA_-_approval.PDF |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240411152047/https://plan.sa.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0009/856818/Capital_City_Policy_Review_Design_Quality_DPA_-_approval.PDF |archive-date=11 April 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[City of Adelaide|The Corporation of the City of Adelaide]] via PlanSA |publisher=[[Government of South Australia]] (Department of Housing and Urban Development) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It is co-located on its west with the historical Royal South Australian Society of Arts which included the [[Art Gallery of South Australia]], the [[South Australian Museum]] and the [[State Library of South Australia]].{{Cite web |last1=Baker |first1=David |last2=Dutkiewicz |first2=Adam |date=9 September 2020 |title=Royal South Australian Society of Arts: A Brief History |url=https://rsasarts.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2020/09/RSASA-A-Brief-History.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240321134011/https://rsasarts.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2020/09/RSASA-A-Brief-History.pdf |archive-date=21 March 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=3 July 2024 |website=Royal South Australian Society of Arts |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Built in the [[Gothic Revival architecture]] style in 1882, the Mitchell Building is the oldest building on the campus.{{Cite web |last=Elton |first=Jude |title=Mitchell Building |url=https://sahistoryhub.history.sa.gov.au/places/mitchell-building/ |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240717045620/https://sahistoryhub.history.sa.gov.au/places/mitchell-building/ |archive-date=17 July 2024 |access-date=19 September 2024 |website=SA History Hub |publisher=[[Government of South Australia]] ([[History Trust of South Australia]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It was called the University Building until 1961 when it was renamed after [[William Mitchell (philosopher)|William Mitchell]].{{Cite web |title=Mitchell Building |url=https://sahistoryhub.history.sa.gov.au/places/mitchell-building |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240520084816/https://sahistoryhub.history.sa.gov.au/places/mitchell-building |archive-date=20 May 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=SA History Hub |publisher=[[Government of South Australia]] ([[History Trust of South Australia]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The [[Barr Smith Library]] is the main library on the site and is notable for its large reading room. On the western border, the campus is planned to merge with the neighbouring ''City East'' campus of the [[University of South Australia]] and the ''City West'' campus on the west end of the terrace to form the combined ''Adelaide City'' campus following the merger.{{Cite web |title=City East Campus |url=https://unisa.edu.au/siteassets/campus-facilities/maps/city-east-campus-map.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240507095448/https://unisa.edu.au/siteassets/campus-facilities/maps/city-east-campus-map.pdf |archive-date=7 May 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[University of South Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=City West Campus |url=https://unisa.edu.au/siteassets/campus-facilities/maps/city-west-campus-and-access-map.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240507095452/https://unisa.edu.au/siteassets/campus-facilities/maps/city-west-campus-and-access-map.pdf |archive-date=7 May 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[University of South Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Adelaide City Campus |url=https://adelaideuni.edu.au/why-adelaide/campuses/adelaide-city-campus/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240715103947/https://adelaideuni.edu.au/why-adelaide/campuses/adelaide-city-campus/ |archive-date=15 July 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[Adelaide University]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} [[File:Elder Conservatorium.jpg|alt=This is a photograph of the Elder Hall, a venue for concerts and events with a statue of early benefactor Thomas Elder in front.|thumb|Elder Hall, named after early donor [[Thomas Elder]], integrates freestone from [[Mount Gambier]]]] [[Bonython Hall]], the [[great hall]] used during graduation ceremonies, is a prominent building facing the terrace. The hall takes inspiration from the [[Great Hall of the University of Sydney]] and is also constructed in the [[Gothic Revival architecture]] style to resemble the medieval halls used by the [[Ancient university|ancient universities]] in Europe. In between it and the Mitchell Building, which both face the terrace, is the [[Elder Conservatorium of Music|Elder Hall]] which is its oldest great hall.{{Cite web |title=Elder Hall |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7320 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240815210034/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7320 |archive-date=15 August 2024 |access-date=20 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It is a large concert hall that is used by the [[Elder Conservatorium of Music]] among others and, along with Bonython Hall, both feature large [[Organ (music)|organs]].[[File:Adelaide (AU), Barr Smith Library -- 2019 -- 0677.jpg|alt=This is a photograph of the Barr Smith Library.|thumb|The [[Barr Smith Library]] on the main Adelaide campus]] The university also has other venues including the Scott Theatre, Little Theatre and the College Green. The Scott Theatre is the largest [[Lecture hall|lecture theatre]] on site and is often hired out for performances of various kinds such as the [[Adelaide Fringe]] events.{{Cite web |title=Scott Theatre |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7369 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240722141224/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7369 |archive-date=22 July 2024 |access-date=6 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Scott Theatre mixes Adelaide University lectures with music, dance and theatre performances |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/scott-theatre-mixes-adelaide-university-lectures-with-music--dance-and-theatre-performances |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240305100958/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/scott-theatre-mixes-adelaide-university-lectures-with-music--dance-and-theatre-performances |archive-date=5 March 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{cite web |title=Scott Theatre at The University of Adelaide |url=https://adelaidefringe.com.au/fringetix/venues/space-8b5be012-2280-4c61-9ee6-263ce6c75dec |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310232221/https://adelaidefringe.com.au/fringetix/venues/space-8b5be012-2280-4c61-9ee6-263ce6c75dec |archive-date=10 March 2023 |access-date=9 January 2023 |website=[[Adelaide Fringe]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It features two revolving stages and a seating capacity of 635 people. The Little Theatre is located in the [[Cloister]]s and is primarily used for dramatic performances by the [[University of Adelaide Theatre Guild|Theatre Guild]].{{cite web |title=Little Theatre |url=https://www.onlyadelaide.com.au/little-theatre |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240323154821/https://www.onlyadelaide.com.au/little-theatre |archive-date=23 March 2024 |access-date=9 January 2023 |website=OnlyAdelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=The 1970s Little Theatre at Adelaide University and a Parks theatre in Angle Park share style via Ralph Middenway |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/little-theatre-at-adelaide-university-and-a-theatre-at-parks-centre--in-northwest-angle-park-share-similar-style-via-ralph-middenway |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240223044504/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/little-theatre-at-adelaide-university-and-a-theatre-at-parks-centre--in-northwest-angle-park-share-similar-style-via-ralph-middenway |archive-date=23 February 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Theatre Guild |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/theatreguild/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707105357/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/theatreguild/ |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=20 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The College Green stretches from the [[Cloister]]s across the lawns down to Victoria Drive, next to the [[River Torrens]].{{cite web |last=Von Einem |first=Johnny |date=22 October 2020 |title=The College Green is turning the University of Adelaide's Cloisters into 'Adelaide's biggest beer garden' |url=http://citymag.indaily.com.au/habits/plate-and-cup/the-college-green-is-turning-the-university-of-adelaides-cloisters-into-adelaides-biggest-beer-garden/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205135724/https://citymag.indaily.com.au/habits/plate-and-cup/the-college-green-is-turning-the-university-of-adelaides-cloisters-into-adelaides-biggest-beer-garden/ |archive-date=5 December 2020 |access-date=26 December 2020 |website=CityMag |publisher=[[Solstice Media]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It hosts various social events throughout the year including parties, live bands, DJs and [[Outdoor cinema|open-air cinema]] among others.{{cite web |date=23 October 2020 |title=UniBar, Cloisters & Lawns at Adelaide Uni to host the most epic party series all summer long |url=https://glamadelaide.com.au/unibar-cloisters-lawns-at-adelaide-uni-to-host-the-most-epic-party-series-all-summer-long/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205032911/https://glamadelaide.com.au/unibar-cloisters-lawns-at-adelaide-uni-to-host-the-most-epic-party-series-all-summer-long/ |archive-date=5 December 2020 |access-date=26 December 2020 |website=Glam Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It was created in response to the impact of [[social distancing]] restrictions owing to the [[COVID-19 pandemic in Australia]], which hit many [[live music venue]]s.{{cite web |title=College Green |url=https://www.broadsheet.com.au/adelaide/event/college-green-2020 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201226103409/https://www.broadsheet.com.au/adelaide/event/college-green-2020 |archive-date=26 December 2020 |access-date=26 December 2020 |website=Broadsheet |publisher=Broadsheet Media |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}} The Napier and Ligertwood Buildings were built following the demolition of the [[Jubilee Exhibition Building]] in 1962.{{Cite web |last=Elton |first=Jude |date=14 July 2024 |title=Exhibition Building and Grounds |url=https://sahistoryhub.history.sa.gov.au/places/exhibition-building-and-grounds,%20/places/exhibition-building-and-grounds |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240814180124/https://sahistoryhub.history.sa.gov.au/places/exhibition-building-and-grounds |archive-date=14 August 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=SA History Hub |publisher=[[Government of South Australia]] ([[History Trust of South Australia]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |last= |first= |date=31 August 2017 |title=Remembering the Jubilee Exhibition Building |url=https://www.adelaideheritage.net.au/remembering-the-jubilee-exhibition-building/ |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240717045617/https://www.adelaideheritage.net.au/remembering-the-jubilee-exhibition-building/ |archive-date=17 July 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=Adelaide City Heritage |publisher=National Trust of South Australia |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} They are named after [[Mellis Napier]] and [[G. C. Ligertwood|George Ligertwood]] who were both former chancellors.{{Citation |last=Howell |first=Peter Anthony |title=Sir Thomas John Mellis Napier (1882–1976) |date=2000 |work=Australian Dictionary of Biography |volume=15 |url=https://adb.anu.edu.au/biography/napier-sir-thomas-john-mellis-11220 |access-date=17 July 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240708122732/https://adb.anu.edu.au/biography/napier-sir-thomas-john-mellis-11220 |archive-date=8 July 2024 |url-status=live |place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]] |publisher=[[Australian National University]] (which owns "National Centre of Biography") |language=en-AU}}{{Citation |last=Zelling |first=Howard |title=Sir George Coutts Ligertwood (1888–1967) |date=2000 |work=Australian Dictionary of Biography |volume=15 |url=https://adb.anu.edu.au/biography/ligertwood-sir-george-coutts-10829 |access-date=20 September 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240726232038/https://adb.anu.edu.au/biography/ligertwood-sir-george-coutts-10829 |archive-date=26 July 2024 |url-status=live |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]] |publisher=[[Australian National University]] (which owns "National Centre of Biography") |language=en-AU}} The Mawson Building (originally Mawson Laboratories){{cite web | title=Mawson Laboratories at Adelaide North Terrace | website=StudentVIP | url=https://studentvip.com.au/adelaide-uni/north-terrace/maps/121210 | access-date=12 July 2025}} was named after geologist and Antarctic explorer Sir [[Douglas Mawson]], who taught at the university from 1905 (professor from 1921) until his retirement in 1952. The building was completed in 1953.{{cite web | title=Earth Sciences History | website=School of Physics, Chemistry and Earth Sciences | date=21 February 1948 | url=https://set.adelaide.edu.au/physics-chemistry-earth-sciences/history/earth-sciences#proposal | access-date=12 July 2025}} The [[#Tate Museum|Tate Museum]], which houses the Mawson Institute for Antarctic Research (founded 1959),{{cite web | title=60-year anniversary of Douglas Mawson Antarctic Collection | website=Faculty of Sciences, Engineering and Technology | date=29 April 2021 | url=https://set.adelaide.edu.au/news/list/2021/04/29/60-year-anniversary-of-douglas-mawson-antarctic-collection | access-date=22 August 2025}} and Mawson Geo Centre are located in this building.{{cite web | title=Home | website=Mawson Geo Centre | date=28 March 2022 | url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/mawson-geo/ | access-date=12 July 2025}} Some other notable buildings include the Ingkarni Wardli Building, Darling Building, [[John Anderson Hartley|Hartley]] Building, and the [[Helen Mayo]] North and South Buildings.{{Cite web |date=December 2023 |title=Self-guided tour |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/publications/ua/media/264/tour.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230815023228/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/publications/ua/media/264/tour.pdf |archive-date=15 August 2023 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=3 July 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The {{AUD|100 million}} Braggs Building, named after two Nobel laureates associated with the university, was built in 2013 and features a large number of cross-disciplinary scientific research facilities.{{Cite web |last=Davis |first=Lauren |date=26 August 2013 |title=University of Adelaide 'braggs' about transdisciplinary facility |url=https://www.labonline.com.au/content/lab-design-fit-out-services/article/university-of-adelaide-lsquo-braggs-rsquo-about-transdisciplinary-facility-355231955 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240323152040/https://www.hamessharley.com.au/project/the-braggs-building |archive-date=23 March 2024 |access-date=14 July 2024 |website=Hames Sharley |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Melbourne]], [[Victoria (state)|Victoria]]}} The Adelaide University Footbridge was constructed in 1937 following a decade of delays during the [[Great Depression]].{{Cite web |date= |title=University Footbridge |url=https://adelaidecityexplorer.com.au/items/show/247 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231027202452/https://adelaidecityexplorer.com.au/items/show/247 |archive-date=27 October 2023 |access-date=27 October 2023 |website=Adelaide City Explorer |publisher=National Trust of South Australia |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |date=1996 |title=UNIVERSITY FOOTBRIDGE |url=https://d31atr86jnqrq2.cloudfront.net/heritage-places/heritage-place-information-sheet-university-footbridge.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210809120346/https://d31atr86jnqrq2.cloudfront.net/heritage-places/heritage-place-information-sheet-university-footbridge.pdf |archive-date=9 August 2021 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=17 September 2024 |website=Heritage of the City of Adelaide: An Illustrated Guide 1996 |publisher=[[City of Adelaide|The Corporation of the City of Adelaide]]
