for [[love lock]]s.{{Cite news |date=16 January 1937 |title=UNIVERSITY BRIDGE OVER TORRENS |url=https://trove.nla.gov.au/newspaper/article/74334377 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240917145149/https://trove.nla.gov.au/newspaper/article/74334377 |archive-date=17 September 2024 |access-date=17 September 2024 |work=[[The Advertiser (Adelaide)|The Advertiser]] |page=18 |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=River Torrens Running Loops |url=https://d31atr86jnqrq2.cloudfront.net/docs/map-running-loops.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220504015826/https://d31atr86jnqrq2.cloudfront.net/docs/map-running-loops.pdf |archive-date=4 May 2022 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=17 September 2024 |website=[[Adelaide Park Lands]] |publisher=[[City of Adelaide|The Corporation of the City of Adelaide]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} [[File:UofAdelaide-Cloisters-Aug08.jpg|alt=This is a photograph of the Cloisters that form part of the Adelaide University Union redevelopment that connect the Union buildings with the surrounding buildings.|thumb|The [[Cloister]]s are a [[war memorial]] to University of Adelaide members who served and died during [[World War I]]]] The [[Adelaide University Union redevelopment]], also known as Union Buildings or Union Building Group, was completed in stages between 1967 and 1975.{{Cite web |title=Building Details |url=https://architectsdatabase.unisa.edu.au/build_full.asp?B_ID=396 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240423094319/https://architectsdatabase.unisa.edu.au/build_full.asp?B_ID=396 |archive-date=23 April 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=Architects of South Australia |publisher=[[University of South Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It created some of the most significant buildings in the complex.{{Cite web |title=Union Building Group, The University of Adelaide (including the Lady Symon Building, the George Murray Building, the Cloisters, the Western Annexe and the multi-level Union House) |url=http://www.experienceadelaide.com.au/heritage-places/union-building-group-the-university-of-adelaide-including-the-lady-symon-building-the-george-murray-building-the-cloisters-the-western-annexe-and-the-multi-level-union-house-victoria-drive-adelaide/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240423094318/http://www.experienceadelaide.com.au/heritage-places/union-building-group-the-university-of-adelaide-including-the-lady-symon-building-the-george-murray-building-the-cloisters-the-western-annexe-and-the-multi-level-union-house-victoria-drive-adelaide/ |archive-date=23 April 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=Experience Adelaide |publisher=[[City of Adelaide|The Corporation of the City of Adelaide]] (Adelaide Economic Development Agency) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |last=Laurence |first=Sarah |date=7 February 2002 |title=ASSESSMENT OF HERITAGE VALUE |url=https://data.environment.sa.gov.au/Content/Publications/17619_Research.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240409200135/https://data.environment.sa.gov.au/Content/Publications/17619_Research.pdf |archive-date=9 April 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=Enviro Data SA |publisher=[[Government of South Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The redevelopment was designed by lead architect [[Robert Dickson (architect)|Robert Dickson]] and includes a heritage-listed group of buildings including the Union House, the Lady Symon Building named after the wife of [[Josiah Symon]], the [[George John Robert Murray|George Murray]] Building, the Cloisters and the Western Annexe.{{Cite web |title=Architect Details |url=https://architectsdatabase.unisa.edu.au/arch_full.asp?Arch_ID=40 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240423094318/https://architectsdatabase.unisa.edu.au/arch_full.asp?Arch_ID=40 |archive-date=23 April 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=Architects of South Australia |publisher=[[University of South Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{cite web |author= |date=25 July 2002 |title=Assessment of heritage value: Union Building Group, Adelaide University |url=https://d31atr86jnqrq2.cloudfront.net/heritage-places/heritage-place-information-sheet-union-building-group.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121212633/https://d31atr86jnqrq2.cloudfront.net/heritage-places/heritage-place-information-sheet-union-building-group.pdf |archive-date=21 January 2021 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=18 January 2021 |website=[[City of Adelaide|The Corporation of the City of Adelaide]] |series=File 17619 |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]] }} The earlier [[Georgian architecture|Georgian-style]] buildings were designed by the architects [[Woods, Bagot, Jory and Laybourne-Smith]] who also designed Bonython Hall, the Mitchell Gates, the Johnson Laboratories, the Barr Smith Library, and the Benham Laboratories. The [[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] [[Cloister]]s were built in 1929 as a [[war memorial]] to the 470 University of Adelaide members who served during [[World War I]], of which 64 had died during the war.{{Cite web |title=Union Cloisters |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7332 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707131648/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7332 |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=6 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |last=Larsen |first=Marie |date=25 April 2016 |title=Building the University of Adelaide War Memorial |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25414 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240722063235/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25414 |archive-date=22 July 2024 |access-date=6 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Adelaide University Union Cloisters |url=https://monumentaustralia.org.au/themes/conflict/ww1/display/50018-adelaide-university-union-cloisters |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240405014510/https://monumentaustralia.org.au/themes/conflict/ww1/display/50018-adelaide-university-union-cloisters |archive-date=5 April 2024 |access-date=6 September 2024 |website=Monument Australia |language=en-AU}} There are three plaques on the site, with the latest added in 2015 to mark the centenary of the [[Gallipoli campaign|Gallipoli landing]]. The university also has a presence in the adjacent [[Lot Fourteen]] precinct, that is also home to the national headquarters of the [[Australian Space Agency]] among other institutions in the fields of science and technology.{{Cite web |title=Contact us |url=https://www.space.gov.au/contact-us |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240504233401/https://www.space.gov.au/contact-us |archive-date=4 May 2024 |access-date=14 July 2024 |website=[[Australian Space Agency]] |publisher=[[Australian Government]] ([[Department of Industry, Science, Energy and Resources]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |date= |title=Lot Fourteen (Old Royal Adelaide Hospital) Innovation Area Development Plan Amendment |url=https://plan.sa.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0004/856849/Lot_Fourteen_Old_Royal_Adelaide_Hospital_Innovation_Area_DPA_-_approval.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240411151548/https://plan.sa.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0004/856849/Lot_Fourteen_Old_Royal_Adelaide_Hospital_Innovation_Area_DPA_-_approval.pdf |archive-date=11 April 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[City of Adelaide|The Corporation of the City of Adelaide]] via PlanSA |publisher=[[Government of South Australia]] (Department of Housing and Urban Development) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Directory Archive |url=https://lotfourteen.com.au/directory/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240331052414/https://www.lotfourteen.com.au/directory/ |archive-date=31 March 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[Lot Fourteen]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It also operates the [[National Wine Centre of Australia|National Wine Centre]] further along of the terrace and adjacent to the [[Adelaide Botanic Garden]]. On the west end of North Terrace, the Adelaide Health and Medical Sciences Building is surrounded by the [[South Australian Health and Medical Research Institute]] and the [[Royal Adelaide Hospital]] forming part of the Adelaide BioMed City precinct.{{Cite web |title=Adelaide Health and Medical Sciences Building |url=https://aecom.com/sa/projects/adelaide-health-medical-sciences-building/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181204083511/https://aecom.com/sa/projects/adelaide-health-medical-sciences-building/ |archive-date=4 December 2018 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[AECOM]] |language=en-US |publication-place=[[Dallas]], [[Texas]]}}{{Cite web |title=Adelaide Health and Medical Sciences |url=https://www.lyonsarch.com.au/project/adelaide-health-and-medical-sciences/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240519102544/https://www.lyonsarch.com.au/project/adelaide-health-and-medical-sciences/ |archive-date=19 May 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[Lyons (architecture firm)|Lyons]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Melbourne]], [[Victoria (state)|Victoria]]}} The {{AUD|246 million}} biomedical teaching and research facility was completed in 2017 and is home to various clinical and simulation facilities in the fields of healthcare and medicine. ===Waite=== [[File:Waite Institute, University of Adelaide.jpg|thumb|The Waite campus is home to the Waite Research Institute|alt=This is a photograph of the main building at the Waite campus, which is home to the Waite Research Institute.]] The Waite campus specialises in [[agricultural science]], [[viticulture]], [[oenology]], [[plant breeding]], food research and [[biotechnology]]. It is located in the suburb of [[Urrbrae, South Australia|Urrbrae]] in Adelaide's eastern foothills, adjacent to the [[Urrbrae Agricultural High School]], on {{Convert|184|ha|acre|abbr=off}} of which a large amount was donated through the will of Scottish-born pastoralist [[Peter Waite (philanthropist)|Peter Waite]].{{Cite web |date=25 October 2013 |title=Original School Building/Headmaster's Residence, Urrbrae Agricultural High School |url=https://data.environment.sa.gov.au/Content/Publications/26306_Research.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240409183402/https://data.environment.sa.gov.au/Content/Publications/26306_Research.pdf |archive-date=9 April 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=28 July 2024 |website=[[South Australian Heritage Register|South Australian Heritage Council]] |publisher=[[Department for Environment and Water (South Australia)|Department for Environment and Water]] ([[Government of South Australia]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |last1=Gardner |first1=J.A. |last2=Delaporte |first2=K.L. |date=24 March 2023 |title=Waite Arboretum – An enduring gift |url=https://data.environment.sa.gov.au/Content/Publications/JABG30P069_Gardner.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240529004833/https://data.environment.sa.gov.au/Content/Publications/JABG30P069_Gardner.pdf |archive-date=29 May 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=3 July 2024 |website=Enviro Data SA ([[Government of South Australia]]) |publisher=Board of the Botanic Gardens & State Herbarium |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Approximately half of the land donated was dedicated for studies in agriculture and the remainder as a public park. The [[Urrbrae, South Australia#Waite Research Precinct|Waite Research Precinct]] is home to several research centres.{{Cite web |title=The WAITE Research Precinct |url=https://www.businesseventsadelaide.com.au/choose-adelaide/adelaides-innovation-expertise/the-waite-research-precinct/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231204005348/https://www.businesseventsadelaide.com.au/choose-adelaide/adelaides-innovation-expertise/the-waite-research-precinct/ |archive-date=4 December 2023 |access-date=28 July 2024 |website=Business Events Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The Waite Agricultural Research Institute was established in 1924.{{Cite web |title=Waite Research Institute |url=https://www.thewaite.org/waite-partners/waite-research-institute/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240115084433/https://www.thewaite.org/waite-partners/waite-research-institute/ |archive-date=15 January 2024 |access-date=28 July 2024 |website=Waite Research Precinct |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=About The Waite |url=https://www.thewaite.org/about/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240808102234/https://www.thewaite.org/about/ |archive-date=8 August 2024 |access-date=20 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Its first director was [[A. E. V. Richardson|Arnold E. V. Richardson]]. Later renamed to the Waite Research Institute, it produces approximately 70% of Australia's research output in [[viticulture]] and [[oenology]] and around 80% of cereal varieties used in southern Australia were created there. A Soil Research Centre was founded in 1929 with a donation of £10,000 from [[John Darling and Son#Harold Gordon Darling|Harold Darling]] of [[John Darling and Son|J. Darling and Son]], grain merchants. In 2004, State Premier [[Mike Rann]] opened the A$9.2 million Plant Genomics Centre at the campus. In 2010, he opened The Plant Accelerator, a A$30 million research facility which is the largest and most advanced of its kind in the world. [[File:The Plant Accelerator.jpg|alt=This is a photograph of "The Plant Accelerator" on the Waite campus in Urrbrae, a plant phenotyping facility.|thumb|The Plant Accelerator is a plant phenotyping facility]] A number of other organisations are co-located in the precinct including the [[South Australian Research and Development Institute]] (or ''SARDI'', part of [[Primary Industries and Regions SA]] which is also headquartered at the campus), Australian Grain Technologies, [[Australian Wine Research Institute]] and the [[CSIRO|Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organisation]] (CSIRO).{{Cite web |date=1997 |title=Waite Accompli |url=https://www.pir.sa.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0008/49508/Waite_Collocation.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120407094359/https://www.pir.sa.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0008/49508/Waite_Collocation.pdf |archive-date=7 April 2012 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=28 July 2024 |website=[[Primary Industries and Regions SA|Department of Primary Industries and Regions]] |publisher=[[Government of South Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=History |url=https://www.agtbreeding.com.au/about/history |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240701061251/https://www.agtbreeding.com.au/about/history |archive-date=1 July 2024 |access-date=28 July 2024 |website=Australian Grain Technologies |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Roseworthy, South Australia|Roseworthy]], [[South Australia]]}} The Urrbrae House built in 1891, now a museum, served as the home of Peter and Matilda Waite who purchased the land with support from [[Thomas Elder]].{{Cite web |title=The History |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/waite-historic/urrbrae-house-precinct/the-history |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231209022832/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/waite-historic/urrbrae-house-precinct/the-history |archive-date=9 December 2023 |access-date=20 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Its interior & Co is designed by Aldam Heaton & Co, who was also responsible for designing interiors for the [[Titanic]]. The campus is also home to the Waite Arboretum and Conservation Reserve. The Waite Arboretum is a tree museum which is home to over 2,500 tree specimens from over 1,000 taxa, many of which are endangered in the wild.{{Cite web |title=History and Public Spaces |url=https://www.thewaite.org/history/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240304081437/https://www.thewaite.org/history/ |archive-date=4 March 2024 |access-date=28 July 2024 |website=Waite Research Precinct |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |last=Gardner |first=Jennifer A. |title=The Waite Arboretum – Science, Trees and Technology |url=https://www.foresthistory.org.au/2015_conference_papers/01-Gardner.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240316171414/https://www.foresthistory.org.au/2015_conference_papers/01-Gardner.pdf |archive-date=16 March 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=28 July 2024 |website=Australian Forest History Society |language=en-AU}} The Waite Conservation Reserve, also co-located on the campus, is home to native plants and wildlife.{{Cite web |title=Friends of Waite Conservation Reserve |url=https://friendsofwaitereserve.org.au/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707233631/https://friendsofwaitereserve.org.au/ |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=28 July 2024 |website=Friends of Waite Conservation Reserve |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} ===Roseworthy=== {{Main|Roseworthy College}} [[File:4. Roseworthy College.jpg|alt=This is a photograph of the main building on the Roseworthy campus which was built in 1883 and now serves as a student hub.|thumb|The Roseworthy College Hall, built in 1884, is a student hub]] Located north of the city, the [[Roseworthy, South Australia|Roseworthy]] campus comprises {{Convert|16|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} of [[Farmland (farming)|farmland]] and is a large centre for [[agriculture|agricultural research]] and [[Veterinary medicine|veterinary sciences]].{{Cite web |title=Roseworthy Campus |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/campuses/roseworthy |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240801143339/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/campuses/roseworthy |archive-date=1 August 2024 |access-date=4 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Agricultural Science Research |url=https://set.adelaide.edu.au/agriculture-food-wine/research/agricultural-science |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240228070602/https://set.adelaide.edu.au/agriculture-food-wine/research/agricultural-science |archive-date=28 February 2024 |access-date=4 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It was the site of the former [[Roseworthy College|Roseworthy Agricultural College]] which was established in 1883 as the first agricultural college in Australia.{{Cite web |title=Roseworthy Agricultural College |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/records/university-archives/related-institutions/roseworthy-agricultural-college |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240514072024/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/records/university-archives/related-institutions/roseworthy-agricultural-college |archive-date=14 May 2024 |access-date=22 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The Roseworthy College Hall, now the student hub, is the main building on the campus and was built in 1884.{{Cite web |title=The University of Adelaide Roseworthy Student Hub |url=https://sarah.com.au/projects/roseworthy-student-hub/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240523211008/https://sarah.com.au/projects/roseworthy-student-hub/ |archive-date=23 May 2024 |access-date=6 September 2024 |website=Sarah Constructions |publisher=Sarah Group |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Its clock tower features a Swiss precision clock that is synced via [[Global Positioning System|GPS]] with [[Greenwich Mean Time]].{{Cite web |title=Main Building, Roseworthy |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7372 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240724014109/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7372 |archive-date=24 July 2024 |access-date=6 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The clock tower was missing a clock for more than 120 years until 2003, when the mechanism was finally added following a donation. The colleges' teaching and research in [[oenology]] and [[viticulture]] were transferred to the Waite campus, along with the bulk of its work in [[plant breeding]].{{Cite web |title=Wine course move from Roseworthy to Waite campus puts students within Wine Industry Cluster research core |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/wine-course-move-from-roseworthy-to-waite-campus-make-students-part-of-wine-industry-cluster-research-core- |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240519201928/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/wine-course-move-from-roseworthy-to-waite-campus-make-students-part-of-wine-industry-cluster-research-core- |archive-date=19 May 2024 |access-date=4 August 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |date=2024-04-17 |title=Centennial celebration: 100 years of scientific discovery at Waite Campus |url=https://winetitles.com.au/centennial-celebration-100-years-of-scientific-discovery-at-waite-campus/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240519201928/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/wine-course-move-from-roseworthy-to-waite-campus-make-students-part-of-wine-industry-cluster-research-core- |archive-date=19 May 2024 |access-date=4 August 2024 |website=Winetitles Media |publisher=Provincial Press Group |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite book |last=Zeitz |first=Lynette D. |date=2014 |title=The Waite: A Social and Scientific History of the Waite Agricultural Research Institute |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/press/titles/waite |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240716184803/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/press/titles/waite |archive-date=16 July 2024 |access-date=4 August 2024 |publisher=Barr Smith Press |pages=82–85 |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]] |doi=10.20851/waite |isbn=9781922064615 |issn=0312-9640}} Before studies in oenology were transferred to the Waite campus, the college had produced a number of highly regarded and awarded winemakers and wine critics.{{Cite book |last=Bishop |first=Geoffrey C. |title=Australian Winemaking, the Roseworthy Influence: the contribution of Alan R. Hickinbotham and Roseworthy Agricultural College to winemaking in Australia [1879-1970] |publisher=Investigator Press |year=1980 |isbn=9780859640404 |location=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]] |language=en-AU }} [[File:Roseworthy Ag college chapel.JPG|alt=This is a photograph of the Roseworthy Memorial Chapel, which was built to memorialise fallen soldiers from the college.|thumb|The Roseworthy Memorial Chapel was built to memorialise fallen soldiers from the college]] Following the merger, the campus expanded its focus in [[Dryland farming|dryland agriculture]], [[natural resource management]] and [[Animal husbandry|animal production]] by the mid-1990s.{{Cite web |title=Roseworthy, Australia's first agricultural college in 1883, grows from viticulture to veterinary science |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/roseworthy--australia-s-first-agricultural-college-from-1883--starts-the-tradition-of-research |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240506040300/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/roseworthy--australia-s-first-agricultural-college-from-1883--starts-the-tradition-of-research |archive-date=6 May 2024 |access-date=4 August 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The campus is also now home to South Australia's first [[Veterinary medicine|veterinary science]] training program, which commenced in 2008.{{Cite web |title=VETERINARY SCIENCE PROJECT |url=https://www8.austlii.edu.au/au/other/SAHAStaCPW/2008/12.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240804154329/https://www8.austlii.edu.au/au/other/SAHAStaCPW/2008/12.pdf |archive-date=4 August 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=4 August 2024 |website=[[Australasian Legal Information Institute]] |publisher=[[Parliament of South Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The Veterinary Science Centre houses teaching facilities including a surgical skills suite, a public [[Veterinary medicine|veterinary clinic]] offering general practice as well as emergency and specialist veterinary services for pet animals.{{Cite web |title=Roseworthy Veterinary Hospital |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/vet/roseworthy-veterinary-hospital |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240407063100/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/vet/roseworthy-veterinary-hospital |archive-date=7 April 2024 |access-date=4 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} There are also specialised pathology laboratories at the centre for teaching, research and diagnostics. In 2013, the veterinary science facilities were expanded with the opening of the Equine Health and Performance Centre, a specialised facility for equine surgery, internal medicine, sports medicine and reproduction. The Memorial Chapel is a notable building on the Roseworthy campus.{{Cite web |title=Memorial Chapel |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25421 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240723124617/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25421 |archive-date=23 July 2024 |access-date=6 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |last=Elder |first=Bruce |title=Roseworthy, SA |url=https://www.aussietowns.com.au/town/roseworthy-sa |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240518231825/https://www.aussietowns.com.au/town/roseworthy-sa |archive-date=18 May 2024 |access-date=4 August 2024 |website=Aussie Towns |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}} It was built in 1955 to memorialise students from the former college who died during [[World War I]], [[World War II]] and the [[Military history of South Africa|Boer Wars]].{{Cite web |date=2004 |title=Heritage Survey of the Light Regional Council |url=https://data.environment.sa.gov.au/Content/heritage-surveys/2-Light-Regional-Survey-Vol-2-Pt-C-2004.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240405161241/https://data.environment.sa.gov.au/Content/heritage-surveys/2-Light-Regional-Survey-Vol-2-Pt-C-2004.pdf |archive-date=5 April 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=6 September 2024 |website=Enviro Data SA ([[Government of South Australia]]) |publisher=McDougall & Vines |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The entrance features a limestone statue of a young soldier "discarding his uniform in readiness to return to the land". The [[Organ (music)|organ]] of the chapel was donated by the mother of a student that died in [[New Guinea]] during World War II. There is a [[time capsule]] from 1976 located near the chapel. It is expected to be opened in 2026. In 2021, the {{AUD|7 million}} Roseworthy Solar and Energy Storage Project was opened on the campus. It included a solar farm with an output of 1.2MW with a 420/1200kWh hybrid battery.{{Cite web |last=Carroll |first=David |date=20 May 2021 |title=Roseworthy solar farm looms as high achiever for Adelaide university |url=https://www.pv-magazine-australia.com/2021/05/20/roseworthy-solar-farm-looms-as-high-achiever-for-adelaide-university/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230401193955/https://www.pv-magazine-australia.com/2021/05/20/roseworthy-solar-farm-looms-as-high-achiever-for-adelaide-university/ |archive-date=1 April 2023 |access-date=10 July 2024 |website=pv magazine Australia |publisher=pv magazine |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Berlin]], [[Germany]]}} Its 3,200 solar panels are estimated to produce 42% of the campus' energy requirements.{{Cite web |last=Bradbrook |first=Sam |date=21 August 2019 |title=Roseworthy solar farm project begins |url=https://www.bunyippress.com.au/news/page/roseworthy-solar-farm-project-begins |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240710185811/https://www.bunyippress.com.au/news/roseworthy-solar-farm-project-begins |archive-date=10 July 2024 |access-date=10 July 2024 |website=[[The Bunyip]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} ==Governance and structure== [[File:Bonython Hall detail, University of South Australia, North Terrace, Adelaide, South Australia (5103643265).jpg|alt=This is a photograph of the entrance to Bonython Hall, the great hall of the university and venue for graduation ceremonies.|thumb|Entrance to [[Bonython Hall]], a great hall of the university, and venue for graduation events]] === Faculties and departments === Research and teaching is organised into three faculties, each of which contains a number of constituent schools, departments and institutes.{{cite web |date= |title=Academic Faculties |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/about/leadership-structure/faculties-divisions/academic-faculties |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240218161151/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/about/leadership-structure/faculties-divisions/academic-faculties |archive-date=18 February 2024 |access-date=1 July 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |publisher= |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The current faculties at the university were developed over a series of mergers,{{Cite web |title=Faculty of Medicine |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7389 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707212641/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7389 |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=24 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} the latest of which were in 2022 following a merger between the Arts and Professions faculties and the Faculty of Sciences into the Faculty of Engineering, Computer and Mathematical Sciences.{{Cite web |last=Richards |first=Stephanie |date=24 August 2021 |title=Adelaide Uni Council votes for faculty mergers |url=https://indaily.com.au/news/2021/08/24/adelaide-uni-council-votes-for-faculty-mergers |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210926063448/https://indaily.com.au/news/2021/08/24/adelaide-uni-council-votes-for-faculty-mergers/ |archive-date=26 September 2021 |access-date=2 September 2022 |website=InDaily |publisher=[[Solstice Media]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The establishment of faculties and academic departments is formally the responsibility of the University Council.{{Cite web |date=12 December 2017 |title=Statutes of the University |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/policies/233?dsn=policy.document;field=data;id=227;m=view |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221204071804/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/policies/233?dsn=policy.document;field=data;id=227;m=view |archive-date=4 December 2022 |access-date=1 July 2022 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} {{Collapsible list | title = Faculty of Health and Medical Sciences|[[Adelaide Dental School]]|Adelaide Medical School|Adelaide Nursing School|Adelaide Rural Clinical School|School of Allied Health Science and Practice|School of Biomedicine|School of Psychology|School of Public Health }} {{Collapsible list | title = Faculty of Sciences, Engineering and Technology|School of Agriculture, Food and Wine|School of Architecture and Civil Engineering|School of Biological Sciences|School of Chemical Engineering|School of Computer and Mathematical Sciences|School of Electrical and Mechanical Engineering|School of Physics, Chemistry and Earth Sciences|School of Animal and Veterinary Sciences }} {{Collapsible list | title = Faculty of Arts, Business, Law and Economics|Adelaide Business School|[[Adelaide Law School]]|[[Elder Conservatorium of Music]]|School of Economics and Public Policy|School of Education|School of Humanities|School of Social Sciences| }} [[File:Catherine Branson 2011.jpeg|thumb|[[Catherine Branson]] at a ceremony to present the [[Human Rights Awards (Australia)|2010 Human Rights Medal]] to [[Thérèse Rein|Therese Rein]] prior to her chancellorship|alt=This is a photograph of Catherine Branson at a ceremony to present the 2010 Human Rights Medal to Therese Rein before her chancellorship at the University of Adelaide.]] === University Council === The main governing body of the institution is its Council. It is the executive committee responsible for managing operations, setting policies and appointing the chancellor and vice-chancellor. The Council comprises the chancellor, vice-chancellor, a member of the academic staff, a member of the professional staff, an undergraduate student, a postgraduate student, at least one member with a commercial background, two members with prior experience in financial management and other members appointed by the selection committee. The selection committee, which comprises the chancellor and six other appointed members, can appoint members to the Council to serve for between 2 and 4 years. This excludes elected staff and student members, which have a term limit of 2 years. ==== Chancellor and Vice-Chancellor ==== The [[Chancellor (education)|chancellor]] of the university is a limitless term position that is mainly ceremonial and is held by former [[Federal Court of Australia|Federal Court]] [[List of judges of the Federal Court of Australia|judge]] [[Catherine Branson]] who succeeded [[Kevin Scarce]] following his retirement in May 2020.{{Cite web |last=Marsh |first=Walter |date=15 July 2020 |title=University of Adelaide names new Chancellor |url=https://www.adelaidereview.com.au/latest/news/2020/07/15/university-of-adelaide-names-new-chancellor/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230315121940/https://www.adelaidereview.com.au/latest/news/2020/07/15/university-of-adelaide-names-new-chancellor/ |archive-date=15 March 2023 |access-date=4 August 2024 |website=[[The Adelaide Review]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |last1=McGuire |first1=Michael |last2=Bond |first2=Caleb |date=5 May 2020 |title=Adelaide Uni breaks silence on leaders' departure |url=https://www.adelaidenow.com.au/education/tertiary/former-south-australian-governor-kevin-scarce-quits-adelaide-uni-post-six-months-early/news-story/a8c89f185bc75d95c0620885f79cff37s-story/a8c89f185bc75d95c0620885f79cff37 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230320175247/https://www.adelaidenow.com.au/education/tertiary/former-south-australian-governor-kevin-scarce-quits-adelaide-uni-post-six-months-early/news-story/a8c89f185bc75d95c0620885f79cff37 |archive-date=20 March 2023 |access-date=1 July 2024 |website=[[The Advertiser (Adelaide)|The Advertiser]] |publisher=[[News Corp]]oration (which owns "[[News Corp Australia]]") |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Branson was appointed by the University Council. The current vice-chancellor is [[Danes|Danish]] [[biochemist]] [[Peter Høj]], who began his role in February 2021 following similar roles at the [[University of South Australia]] and the [[University of Queensland]].{{Cite web |title=Professor Peter Høj |url=https://www.ipgroupanz.com/about-us/our-people/steeringgroup/peter-hoj |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240804150847/https://www.ipgroupanz.com/about-us/our-people/steeringgroup/peter-hoj |archive-date=4 August 2024 |access-date=4 August 2024 |website=[[IP Group]] |language=en |publication-place=[[London]], [[United Kingdom]]}} While the chancellor's office is ceremonial, the [[Chancellor (education)#Vice-chancellor|vice-chancellor]] serves as the university's de facto principal administrative officer.{{Cite web |date=1 July 2021 |title=University of Adelaide Act 1971 |url=https://www.legislation.sa.gov.au/__legislation/lz/c/a/university%20of%20adelaide%20act%201971/current/1971.41.auth.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231128124618/https://www.legislation.sa.gov.au/__legislation/lz/c/a/university%20of%20adelaide%20act%201971/current/1971.41.auth.pdf |archive-date=28 November 2023 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=1 July 2024 |website=South Australian Legislation |publisher=[[Government of South Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The university's internal governance is carried out by the University Council formed through the University of Adelaide Act 1971. The legislation, which superseded the Adelaide University Act 1874, is scheduled to be superseded by the Adelaide University Act 2023.{{Cite web |date=1874 |title=Adelaide University Act 1874 |url=https://www.legislation.sa.gov.au/home/historical-numbered-as-made-acts/1874/0020-Adelaide-University-Act-No-20-of-37-and-38-Vic,-1874.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220707202837/https://www.legislation.sa.gov.au/home/historical-numbered-as-made-acts/1874/0020-Adelaide-University-Act-No-20-of-37-and-38-Vic,-1874.pdf |archive-date=7 July 2022 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=1 July 2024 |website=South Australian Legislation |publisher=[[Parliament of South Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |date=1 July 2024 |title=Adelaide University Act 2023 |url=https://www.legislation.sa.gov.au/__legislation/lz/c/a/adelaide%20university%20act%202023/current/2023.32.auth.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240508134414/https://www.legislation.sa.gov.au/__legislation/lz/c/a/adelaide%20university%20act%202023/current/2023.32.auth.pdf |archive-date=8 May 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=1 July 2024 |website=South Australian Legislation |publisher=[[Government of South Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} === Finances and endowment === In 2023, the University of Adelaide had a total revenue of {{AUD|1.13 billion}} (2022 – {{AUD|1 billion}}) and a total expenditure of {{AUD|1.09 billion}} (2022 – {{AUD|995.46 million}}). Key sources of income included {{AUD|228.2 million}} from research grants and fees (2022 – {{AUD|190.97 million}}), {{AUD|105.95 million}} from other research funding (2022 – {{AUD|93.22 million}}), {{AUD|350.71 million}} from tuition fees and grants (2022 – {{AUD|318.44 million}}), {{AUD|313.91 million}} from [[Higher Education Support Act 2003|HESA]] funding (2022 – {{AUD|305.91 million}}) and {{AUD|134.47 million}} from donations and investments (2022 – {{AUD|86.48 million}}). At year-end the university had endowments of {{AUD|393.4 million}} (2022 – {{AUD|366.3 million}}) and total net assets of {{AUD|2.19 billion}} (2022 – {{AUD|2.15 billion}}).{{Cite web |date=May 2024 |title=2023 Endowment Fund Investment Report |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/give/ua/media/1923/final_2023-endowment-fund-investment-report.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240701171059/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/give/ua/media/1923/final_2023-endowment-fund-investment-report.pdf |archive-date=1 July 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=1 July 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |date=June 2023 |title=2022 Endowment Fund Investment Report |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/give/ua/media/1808/2022-endowment-fund-investment-report.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240516162534/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/give/ua/media/1808/2022-endowment-fund-investment-report.pdf |archive-date=16 May 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=1 July 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |date=April 2024 |title=2023 Annual Report |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/publications/ua/media/504/annual-report-2023.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240701170818/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/publications/ua/media/504/annual-report-2023.pdf |archive-date=1 July 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=1 July 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} === Heraldry and insignia === The university uses a number of symbols to represent the institution. (of learning) under the (Southern) Cross". The official coat of arms, in [[Blazon|heraldic terminology]], is:{{Cite web |date=23 March 2024 |title=2015 Calendar, Professional and Continuing Education |url=https://calendar.adelaide.edu.au/pdf/calendar2015-pce.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240323085634/https://calendar.adelaide.edu.au/pdf/calendar2015-pce.pdf |archive-date=23 March 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=20 June 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} {{Emblem table|name=the University of Adelaide|image=University-of-Adelaide-Crest.svg|year_granted=in 1925 by the [[College of Arms]], [[London]]|escutcheon=Per pale Or and Argent an Open Book proper edged Gold on a Chief Azure five Mullets, one of eight, two of seven, one of six and one of five points of the second, representing the Constellation of the Southern Cross}}As part of the merger, the coat of arms has been ''de facto'' retired for new students and will only be available to University of Adelaide alumni and continuing students who started their programs on or before 2024.{{Cite web |title=What parchment will I receive? |url=https://adelaideuni.edu.au/about/faqs/answer//4244 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240923082733/https://adelaideuni.edu.au/about/faqs/answer//4244 |archive-date=23 September 2024 |access-date=23 September 2024 |website=[[Adelaide University]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} ==== University mace ==== Many universities possess [[Ceremonial mace#Universities|ceremonial maces]] used during graduation ceremonies.{{Cite web |title=University Muniments - The Mace. |url=https://uma.recollectcms.com/nodes/view/442125 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240620150650/https://uma.recollectcms.com/nodes/view/442125 |archive-date=20 June 2024 |access-date=20 June 2024 |website=[[University of Melbourne|The University of Melbourne]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Melbourne]], [[Victoria (state)|Victoria]]}}{{Cite web |last= |first= |date=23 February 2024 |title=University Archives – 70 Years, 70 Stories – Week 5 |url=https://university-history.sydney.edu.au/university-archives-70-years-70-stories-week-5/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240620150746/https://university-history.sydney.edu.au/university-archives-70-years-70-stories-week-5/ |archive-date=20 June 2024 |access-date=20 June 2024 |website=[[University of Sydney|The University of Sydney]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}} The University of Adelaide Mace was forged by silversmiths using [[silver-gilt]] under the supervision of [[F. Millward Grey|Frederick Millward Grey]]. It features the coat of arms on an orb symbolising the world protruding from an open book representing learning with gum leaves-inspired design. Grey was a designer based at the School of Fine Arts in Adelaide which later became an antecedent institution of the [[University of South Australia]].{{Cite web |title=Millward Grey expands from fine arts school to making a wider mark on Adelaide and South Australian image |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/millward-grey-goes-behind-running-fine-arts-school-to-make-wider-mark-on-adelaide-south-australian-image |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230927182239/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/millward-grey-goes-behind-running-fine-arts-school-to-make-wider-mark-on-adelaide-south-australian-image |archive-date=27 September 2023 |access-date=20 June 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |last=Sergeant |first=Kate |title=Guides: UniSA Calendars: Art Schools (1856-1972) |url=https://guides.library.unisa.edu.au/UniSACalendars/ArtSchools |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240327073035/https://guides.library.unisa.edu.au/UniSACalendars/ArtSchools |archive-date=27 March 2024 |access-date=20 June 2024 |website=[[University of South Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The first mace bearer was KH Boykett in 1926 who carried it during the 50th anniversary jubilee of the first classes at [[St Peter's Cathedral, Adelaide|St Peter's Cathedral]]. The mace symbolises protection of the chancellor and the mace bearer, who is usually a student of the university, carries it in front of the chancellor during ceremonies.{{Cite web |last=Ross |first=Edward |date=17 July 2020 |title=Mace-Bearer |url=https://skeptric.com/mace-bearer/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210303050917/https://skeptric.com/mace-bearer/ |archive-date=3 March 2021 |access-date=20 June 2024 |website=skeptric |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} ==Academic profile== The university is a member of the [[Group of Eight (Australian universities)|Group of Eight]], a coalition of research-led Australian universities.{{Cite web |title=Go8 Members |url=https://go8.edu.au/about/members |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240904110612/https://go8.edu.au/about/members |archive-date=4 September 2024 |access-date=23 September 2024 |website=[[Group of Eight (Australian universities)|Group of Eight]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} It is also a member of the [[Association of Pacific Rim Universities]], an international association of research-intensive universities, and the former Academic Consortium 21.{{Cite web |title=The University of Adelaide |url=https://apru.org/members/the_university_of_adelaide/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240621093953/https://apru.org/members/the_university_of_adelaide/ |archive-date=21 South Australian Health and Medical Research Institute building in North Terrace.]] In 2023, the University of Adelaide had a total research income of {{AUD|261.59 million}}, of which {{AUD|121.62 million}} was from the National Competitive Grants Program; {{AUD|65.48 million}} from other public sector research; {{AUD|8.04 million}} from Cooperative Research Centres; and {{AUD|66.44 million}} from industry and other research. Additionally, it also received {{AUD|49.59 million}} from the Research Support Program and {{AUD|53.24 million}} from the Research Training Program as research block grants.{{Cite web |date=June 2024 |title=2024 Pocket Statistics |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/planning/ua/media/1614/pocket-stats-2024_web.pdf |url-status=live [[Australian Research Council]] evaluated work produced between 2014 and 2018.{{Cite web |date=2019 |title=State of Australian University Research |url=https://dataportal.arc.gov.au/ERA/NationalReport/2018/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240502140017/https://dataportal.arc.gov.au/ERA/NationalReport/2018/ world standard" (4*) or "well above world standard" (5*).{{Cite web |title=Our Performance |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/research/about-us/our-performance |url-status=live |archive-date=25 March 2024 |access-date=3 July 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=Adelaide}} which was obtained with the support of South Australian Senator [[Stirling Griff]]. It is jointly resourced by the federal [[Department of Health and Aged Care]], the Central Adelaide Local Health Network, and the University of Adelaide.{{cite web |title=About SAIGENCI |website=South Australian immunoGENomics Cancer Institute (SAiGENCI) |date=6 August 2024 |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/saigenci/about-saigenci |access-date=3 December 2024}} SAiGENCI is located in the Adelaide Health and Medical Sciences Building, in between the [[University of South Australia]]'s Bradley Building and the [[South Australian Health and Medical Research Institute]], adjacent to the [[Royal Adelaide Hospital]]. ====Stretton Institute==== {{confuse|Stretton Centre}} The Stretton Institute{{cite web |date=16 October 2024 |title=Home |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/stretton/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241021114813/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/stretton/ |archive-date=21 October 2024 |access-date=3 December 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} is named in honour of [[Hugh Stretton]], who was professor and reader at the university from 1954 until he retired in 1989.{{cite web |date=29 October 2021 |title=Professor Hugh Stretton AC. |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/stretton/our-people/professor-hugh-stretton-ac |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240521003914/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/stretton/our-people/professor-hugh-stretton-ac |archive-date=21 May 2024 |access-date=3 December 2024 |website=Stretton Institute |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It was established in 2020 under director Adam Graycar "to conduct research on [[public policy]] issues in a multi-disciplinary and multi-method manner and to contribute to policy agendas locally and beyond". No research was undertaken in the first year as it focused on engaging with stakeholders and building recognition. The [[COVID-19 pandemic in South Australia|COVID-19 pandemic]] delayed some of its plans, such as the inaugural ''Hugh Stretton Oration''. In 2020, through online meetings and webinars with stakeholders, the institute established five initiatives: [[Food industry|Agrifood]] Policy; Building a City for the Future; Climate Change and Energy Transition; Democracy, Security, Trust and Integrity; and Economic Productivity through Population.{{cite web |date=2020 |title=Stretton Institute Annual Report 2020 |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/stretton/ua/media/405/stretton-institute-annual-report-2020-final.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211102154013/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/stretton/ua/media/405/stretton-institute-annual-report-2020-final.pdf |archive-date=2 November 2021 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=3 December 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The inaugural Hugh Stretton Oration was held in 2021, with [[Glyn Davis]] as the speaker, after being postponed from 2020. Since then, speakers included [[Natasha Stott Despoja]], [[Mark Butler]] and [[Danielle Wood (economist)|Danielle Wood]].{{cite web |title=Hugh Stretton Oration |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/stretton/policy-engagement/hugh-stretton-oration#2022-australia-fair-is-the-playing-field-fair-for-women-and-girls-natasha-stott-despoja-ao |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240714141844/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/stretton/policy-engagement/hugh-stretton-oration#2022-australia-fair-is-the-playing-field-fair-for-women-and-girls-natasha-stott-despoja-ao |archive-date=14 July 2024 |access-date=3 December 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} ==== Waite Research Institute ==== The Waite Research Institute specialises and conducts research in [[agricultural science]], [[viticulture]], [[oenology]], [[plant breeding]], food research and [[biotechnology]].{{Cite web |title=Our Research |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/waite-research-institute/our-research |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240303140615/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/waite-research-institute/our-research |archive-date=3 March 2024 |access-date=3 July 2024 |website=Waite Research Institute |publisher=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It is located on the Waite campus in Adelaide's south-eastern foothills, in the suburb of [[Urrbrae, South Australia|Urrbrae]] on {{Convert|184|ha|acre|abbr=off}}, a large amount of which was donated through the will of Scottish-born pastoralist [[Peter Waite (philanthropist)|Peter Waite]]. It was established in 1924 as the Waite Agricultural Research Institute. The institute produces approximately 70% of Australia's research output in viticulture and oenology and around 80% of cereal varieties used in southern Australia were created there.{{Cite web |title=Plant Breeding |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/waite-research-institute/our-research/plant-breeding |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230926214725/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/waite-research-institute/our-research/plant-breeding |archive-date=26 September 2023 |access-date=3 July 2024 |website=Waite Research Institute |publisher=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} A Soil Research Centre was founded in 1929 with a donation of £10,000 from [[Harold Gordon Darling|Harold Darling]] of [[John Darling and Son|J. Darling and Son]], grain merchants.{{cite news |date=5 June 1929 |title=GIFT OF £10,000 TO THE UNIVERSITY. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article35736252 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220822055310/https://trove.nla.gov.au/newspaper/article/35736252 |archive-date=22 August 2022 |access-date=30 November 2011 |newspaper=[[The Advertiser (Adelaide)|The Advertiser]] |location=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]] |page=8 |language=en-AU |via=[[National Library of Australia]]}} In 2004, [[Premier of South Australia|State Premier]] [[Mike Rann]] opened the {{AUD|9.2 million}} Plant Genomics Centre at the campus.{{Cite web |date=3 April 2004 |title=Pivotal role for new Waite research centre |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/script/adelaidean/archive/2004/issue3_april04.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070418152517/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/script/adelaidean/archive/2004/issue3_april04.pdf |archive-date=18 April 2007 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=3 July 2024 |website=Adelaidean |publisher=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} In 2010, he opened ''The Plant Accelerator'', a {{AUD|30 million}} research facility which is the largest and most advanced of its kind in the world.{{Cite web |date=28 January 2010 |title=$30m super greenhouse to help sustain global agriculture |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/news/news37761.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230531042714/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/news/news37761.html |archive-date=31 May 2023 |access-date=3 July 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} === Academic reputation === {{Infobox Australian university ranking|QS_W==82|USNWR_W=99|ARWU_W=151–200|ARWU_W_year=2025|CWTS_W=205{{efn|name=a}}|CWTS_W_year=2024|QS_W_year=2025|QS_W_Employability=171–180|QS_W_Employability_year=2022|THE_W==128|THE_W_year=2025|USNWR_W_year=25/26|ARWU_N=8|ARWU_N_year=2025|CWTS_N=7{{efn|name=a}}|CWTS_N_year=2024|ERA_N=8|ERA_N_year=2018|QS_N=8|QS_N_year=2025|THE_N=7|THE_N_year=2025|USNWR_N=9|USNWR_N_year=25/26|AFR_N=5|AFR_N_year=2024|type=University|THE_W_Reputation=151–200|THE_W_Reputation_year=2025}} In the 2024 ''Aggregate Ranking of Top Universities'', which measures aggregate performance across the QS, THE and ARWU rankings, the university attained a position of #95 (8th nationally).{{Cite web |title=University Results |url=https://research.unsw.edu.au/artu/artu-results |website=Aggregate Ranking of Top Universities |publisher=[[University of New South Wales]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}} ; National publications In the [[Australian Financial Review|''Australian Financial Review'' Best Universities Ranking]] 2024, the university was ranked #5 amongst Australian universities.{{Cite web |title=Best Universities Ranking |url=https://www.afr.com/lists-and-awards/best-universities-ranking |website=[[Australian Financial Review]] |date=14 November 2024 |publisher=[[Nine Entertainment]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}} ; Global publications In the 2025 ''[[Quacquarelli Symonds]]'' [[QS World University Rankings|World University Rankings]] (published 2024), the university attained a tied position of #82 (8th nationally).{{cite web |title=QS World University Rankings 2025: Top Global Universities |url=https://www.topuniversities.com/world-university-rankings |website=[[QS World University Rankings]] |publisher=[[Quacquarelli Symonds]] |language=en-GB |publication-place=[[London]], [[United Kingdom]]}} In the [[Times Higher Education World University Rankings|''Times Higher Education'' World University Rankings]] 2025 (published 2024), the university attained a tied position of #128 (7th nationally).{{cite web |title=World University Rankings |url=https://www.timeshighereducation.com/world-university-rankings |work=[[Times Higher Education]] |publisher=Inflexion |language=en-GB |publication-place=[[London]], [[United Kingdom]]}} In the 2025 ''[[Academic Ranking of World Universities]]'', the university attained a position of #151–200 (8th nationally).{{cite web |title=ShanghaiRanking's Academic Ranking of World Universities |url=https://www.shanghairanking.com/rankings |website=[[Academic Ranking of World Universities]] |publisher=Shanghai Ranking Consultancy |language=en |publication-place=[[Shanghai]], [[China]]}} In the 2025–2026 [[U.S. News & World Report Best Global Universities Ranking|''U.S. News & World Report'' Best Global Universities]], the university attained a position of #99 (9th nationally).{{Cite web |title=Best Global Universities Rankings |url=https://www.usnews.com/education/best-global-universities/rankings |website=[[U.S. News & World Report]] |language=en-US |publication-place=[[Washington, D.C.]], [[United States]]}} In the ''[[CWTS Leiden Ranking]]'' 2024,{{efn|The [[CWTS Leiden Ranking]] is based on P (top 10%).|name=a}} the university attained a position of #205 (7th nationally).{{Cite web |title=CWTS Leiden Ranking |url=https://www.leidenranking.com/ranking/2024/list |website=[[CWTS Leiden Ranking]] (Centre for Science and Technology Studies) |publisher=[[Leiden University]] |language=en |publication-place=[[Leiden]], [[Netherlands]]}} === Student outcomes === The Australian Government's QILT{{Efn|Abbreviation for Quality Indicators for Learning and Teaching.}} conducts national surveys documenting the student life cycle from enrolment through to employment. These surveys place more emphasis on criteria such as student experience, graduate outcomes and employer satisfaction{{Cite web |title=About |url=https://www.qilt.edu.au/About |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250102224528/https://www.qilt.edu.au/About |archive-date=2 January 2025 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=14 January 2025 |website=Quality Indicators for Learning and Teaching |publisher=[[Australian Government]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} than perceived reputation, research output and citation counts.{{Cite web |last=Bridgestock |first=Laura |date=19 April 2021 |title=World University Ranking Methodologies Compared |url=https://www.topuniversities.com/university-rankings-articles/world-university-rankings/world-university-ranking-methodologies-compared |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250102224525/https://www.topuniversities.com/university-rankings-articles/world-university-rankings/world-university-ranking-methodologies-compared |archive-date=2 January 2025 |access-date=14 January 2025 |website=[[Quacquarelli Symonds]] |language=en-GB |publication-place=[[London]], [[United Kingdom]]}} In the 2023 Employer Satisfaction Survey, graduates of the university had an overall employer satisfaction rate of 83.8%.{{Cite web |date=May 2024 |title=2023 Employer Satisfaction Survey |url=https://www.qilt.edu.au/docs/default-source/default-document-library/2023-ess-national-report.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250102224527/https://www.qilt.edu.au/docs/default-source/default-document-library/2023-ess-national-report.pdf |archive-date=2 January 2025 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=14 January 2025 |website=Quality Indicators for Learning and Teaching |publisher=[[Australian Government]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} In the 2023 Graduate Outcomes Survey, graduates of the university had a full-time employment rate of 75.1% for undergraduates and 89.5% for postgraduates.{{Cite web |date=May 2024 |title=2023 Graduate Outcomes Survey: National Report |url=https://www.qilt.edu.au/docs/default-source/default-document-library/2023-gos-national-report.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241220062418/https://www.qilt.edu.au/docs/default-source/default-document-library/2023-gos-national-report.pdf |archive-date=20 December 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=14 January 2025 |website=Quality Indicators for Learning and Teaching |publisher=[[Australian Government]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} The initial full-time salary was {{AUD|70,000}} for undergraduates and {{AUD|85,000}} for postgraduates. In the 2023 Student Experience Survey, undergraduates at the university rated the quality of their entire educational experience at 78.1% meanwhile postgraduates rated their overall education experience at 72.7%.{{Cite web |date=May 2024 |title=2023 Student Experience Survey |url=https://www.qilt.edu.au/docs/default-source/default-document-library/ses-national-report.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250102224530/https://www.qilt.edu.au/docs/default-source/default-document-library/ses-national-report.pdf |archive-date=2 January 2025 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=14 January 2025 |website=Quality Indicators for Learning and Teaching |publisher=[[Australian Government]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} === Accreditation === The university possesses self-accrediting authority from the [[Tertiary Education Quality and Standards Agency]]{{Cite web |title=The University of Adelaide |url=https://www.teqsa.gov.au/provider/university-adelaide |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250410041817/https://www.teqsa.gov.au/provider/university-adelaide |archive-date=10 April 2025 |access-date=11 January 2025 |website=[[Tertiary Education Quality and Standards Agency]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Melbourne]], [[Victoria (state)|Victoria]]}} and also has numerous additional specialised accreditations for its programs. Its teacher education programs are accredited by the Teachers Registration Board of South Australia.{{Cite web |date=31 May 2023 |title=Initial Teacher Education Programs |url=https://www.trb.sa.edu.au/Qualifications-to-Teach/Initial-Teacher-Education-Programs |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250316103547/https://www.trb.sa.edu.au/Qualifications-to-Teach/Initial-Teacher-Education-Programs |archive-date=16 March 2025 |access-date=13 June 2025 |website=Teachers Registration Board of South Australia |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Its law programs are accredited by the Legal Practitioners' Education and Admission Council.{{Cite web |date=February 2024 |title=Law |url=https://international.adelaide.edu.au/agency-partners/ua/media/937/law-2025-flyer.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250403075631/https://international.adelaide.edu.au/agency-partners/ua/media/937/law-2025-flyer.pdf |archive-date=3 April 2025 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=13 June 2025 |website=The University of Adelaide |page=2 |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Its medical school are accredited by the [[Liaison Committee on Medical Education|Australian Medical Council]],{{Cite web |title=Accredited medical schools |url=https://www.amc.org.au/accredited-organisations/medical-schools/accredited-medical-schools/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241018070414/https://www.amc.org.au/accredited-organisations/medical-schools/accredited-medical-schools/ |archive-date=18 October 2024 |access-date=28 November 2024 |website=[[Australian Medical Council]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} and other healthcare programs with the relevant National Board of the [[Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency]] or their affiliated agencies.{{Cite web |title=Accreditation authorities |url=https://www.ahpra.gov.au/Accreditation/Accreditation-Authorities.aspx |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241203060042/https://www.ahpra.gov.au/Accreditation/Accreditation-Authorities.aspx |archive-date=3 December 2024 |access-date=11 January 2025 |website=[[Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Melbourne]], [[Victoria (state)|Victoria]]}} Its engineering programs are accredited by [[Engineers Australia]]{{Cite web |title=Engineers Australia accredited programs |url=https://www.engineersaustralia.org.au/publications/engineers-australia-accredited-programs |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250420041700/https://www.engineersaustralia.org.au/publications/engineers-australia-accredited-programs |archive-date=20 April 2025 |access-date=13 June 2025 |website=[[Engineers Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} and its information technology programs are accredited by the [[Australian Computer Society]].{{Cite web |title=Accredited Courses |url=https://www.acs.org.au/cpd-education/accredited-courses.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250522085301/https://www.acs.org.au/cpd-education/accredited-courses.html |archive-date=22 May 2025 |access-date=13 June 2025 |website=[[Australian Computer Society]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}} Most programs are also recognised in other states, territories and New Zealand through reciprocal arrangements.{{Cite web |title=AQF qualifications |url=https://www.aqf.edu.au/framework/aqf-qualifications |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241215064344/https://www.aqf.edu.au/framework/aqf-qualifications |archive-date=15 December 2024 |access-date=11 January 2025 |website=[[Australian Qualifications Framework]] |publisher=[[Department of Education (Australia)|Department of Education]] ([[Australian Government]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}}{{Cite web |date=2014 |title=A User's Guide: To the Mutual Recognition Agreement (MRA) and the Trans-Tasman Mutual Recognition Arrangement (TTMRA) |url=https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/1513-users-guide-to-the-trans-tasman-mutual-recognition-arrangement-pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240713104744/https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/1513-users-guide-to-the-trans-tasman-mutual-recognition-arrangement-pdf |archive-date=13 July 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=15 December 2024 |website=[[Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment]] ([[New Zealand Government]]) |publisher=[[Australian Government]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} === Admissions process === The admissions process is managed by the [[South Australian Tertiary Admissions Centre]].{{Cite web |title=How to Apply or Change Your Preferences |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/study/undergraduate/how-to-apply-or-change-your-preferences |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240621151622/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/study/undergraduate/how-to-apply-or-change-your-preferences |archive-date=21 June 2024 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=How to Apply for a Coursework Degree |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/study/postgraduate/coursework-degrees/how-to-apply |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240401030408/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/study/postgraduate/coursework-degrees/how-to-apply |archive-date=1 April 2024 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Established in 1977, it is the main administrative body processing applications for tertiary institutions in [[South Australia]] and the [[Northern Territory]].{{Cite web |title=SATAC's role |url=https://satac.edu.au/satacs-role |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240317121719/https://satac.edu.au/satacs-role |archive-date=17 March 2024 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=[[South Australian Tertiary Admissions Centre]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=What is SATAC? |url=https://future.ask.adelaide.edu.au/app/answers/detail/a_id/3422/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240915144821/https://future.ask.adelaide.edu.au/app/answers/detail/a_id/3422/ |archive-date=15 September 2024 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Qualifications that can be used for consideration include [[Senior Secondary Certificate of Education|Australian]] and [[National Certificate of Educational Achievement|New Zealand]] [[High school diploma|high school certificates]] or international equivalent, a [[Grading in education|Grade Point Average]] from prior higher education, [[TAFE South Australia|TAFE]] and other [[Registered training organisation|RTO]] qualifications at [[Australian Qualifications Framework|Certificate IV or higher]], competitive scores from a [[Special Tertiary Admissions Test|Skills for Tertiary Admissions Test]] and prior work experience or military service with the [[Australian Defence Force]].{{Cite web |title=Undergraduate admission pathways |url=https://satac.edu.au/undergraduate-admission-pathways |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240314061407/https://satac.edu.au/undergraduate-admission-pathways |archive-date=14 March 2024 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=[[South Australian Tertiary Admissions Centre]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Vocational Education and Training (VET) Study |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/study/undergraduate/entry-pathways/vocational-education-and-training-vet-study |url-status=live
