|url=https://satac.edu.au/postgraduate-entry-requirements |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240729192753/https://satac.edu.au/postgraduate-entry-requirements |archive-date=29 July 2024 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=[[South Australian Tertiary Admissions Centre]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Competitive courses, including in [[List of healthcare occupations|healthcare]] and [[medicine]], have additional requirements.{{Cite web |title=Admission and Application Guides |url=https://health.adelaide.edu.au/study-with-us/admission-and-application-guides |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240313004615/https://health.adelaide.edu.au/study-with-us/admission-and-application-guides |archive-date=13 March 2024 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Information for nursing and midwifery applicants |url=https://satac.edu.au/information-for-nursing-and-midwifery-applicants |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240711164443/https://satac.edu.au/information-for-nursing-and-midwifery-applicants |archive-date=11 July 2024 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=[[South Australian Tertiary Admissions Centre]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Additionally, the [[University of Adelaide College]] and Eynesbury College offer pathways into university programs.{{Cite web |title=Higher Education Study |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/study/undergraduate/entry-pathways/higher-education-study |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240917080614/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/study/undergraduate/entry-pathways/higher-education-study |archive-date=17 September 2024 |access-date=14 October 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} === Tuition, loans and financial aid === For international students starting in 2025, tuition fees range from {{AUD|19000}} to {{AUD|94000}} per academic year depending on the field of study.{{Cite web |date=February 2024 |title=2025 International guide |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/publications/ua/media/480/international-guide.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241110045517/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/publications/ua/media/480/international-guide.pdf |archive-date=10 November 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=8 March 2025 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Domestic students{{Efn|name=Domestic students|According to the [[Higher Education Support Act 2003]], domestic students include permanent residents and New Zealand citizens in addition to Australian citizens.{{Cite web |date=16 November 2023 |title=5. Domestic and overseas students |url=https://www.education.gov.au/higher-education-publications/higher-education-administrative-information-providers-october-2021/5-domestic-and-overseas-students |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240921075810/https://www.education.gov.au/higher-education-publications/higher-education-administrative-information-providers-october-2021/5-domestic-and-overseas-students |archive-date=21 September 2024 |access-date=12 November 2024 |website=[[Department of Education (Australia)|Department of Education]] |publisher=[[Australian Capital Territory]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}}}} may be offered a federally-subsidised Commonwealth Supported Place (CSP) which substantially decreases the student contribution amount billed to the student.{{Cite web |date=30 July 2024 |title=Commonwealth supported places (CSPs) |url=https://www.studyassist.gov.au/financial-and-study-support/commonwealth-supported-places-csps |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250118030734/https://www.studyassist.gov.au/financial-and-study-support/commonwealth-supported-places-csps |archive-date=18 January 2025 |access-date=7 February 2025 |website=Study Assist |publisher=[[Department of Education (Australia)|Department of Education]] ([[Australian Government]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} The maximum student contribution amount limits that can be applied to CSP students are dependent on the field of study.{{Cite web |date=7 January 2025 |title=Student contribution amounts |url=https://www.studyassist.gov.au/financial-and-study-support/commonwealth-supported-places/student-contribution-amounts |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250121194609/https://www.studyassist.gov.au/financial-and-study-support/commonwealth-supported-places/student-contribution-amounts |archive-date=21 January 2025 |access-date=7 February 2025 |website=Study Assist |publisher=[[Department of Education (Australia)|Department of Education]] ([[Australian Government]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} Since 2021, Commonwealth Supported Places have also been limited to 7 years of equivalent full-time study load (EFTSL), calculated in the form of Student Learning Entitlement (SLE).{{Cite web |date=21 November 2024 |title=Student learning entitlement (SLE) |url=https://www.studyassist.gov.au/financial-and-study-support/student-learning-entitlement-sle |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241221002359/https://www.studyassist.gov.au/financial-and-study-support/student-learning-entitlement-sle |archive-date=21 December 2024 |access-date=3 January 2025 |website=Study Assist |publisher=[[Department of Education (Australia)|Department of Education]] ([[Australian Government]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} Students may accrue additional SLE under some circumstances (e.g. starting a ''separate'' one-year honours program) or every 10 years. Domestic students are also able to access the HECS-HELP student loans scheme offered by the federal government.{{Cite web |date=10 January 2025 |title=HECS-HELP |url=https://www.studyassist.gov.au/financial-and-study-support/hecs-help |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250129101829/https://www.studyassist.gov.au/financial-and-study-support/hecs-help |archive-date=29 January 2025 |access-date=7 February 2025 |website=Study Assist |publisher=[[Department of Education (Australia)|Department of Education]] ([[Australian Government]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} These are indexed to the [[Consumer price index|Consumer]] or [[Wage growth|Wage Price Index]], whichever is lower, and repayments are voluntary unless the recipient passes an income threshold. The university also offers several [[scholarship]]s, which come in the form of [[Bursary|bursaries]] or tuition fee remission.{{Cite web |title=Home |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/scholarships/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241014111210/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/scholarships/ |archive-date=14 October 2024 |access-date=8 March 2025 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Domestic students studying full-time may also receive social security payments for the duration of their studies{{Cite web |date=21 January 2025 |title=Payments you can get while studying |url=https://my.gov.au/en/services/education/help-when-studying/financial-help-while-studying/payments-you-can-get-while-studying |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250325212428/https://my.gov.au/en/services/education/help-when-studying/financial-help-while-studying/payments-you-can-get-while-studying |archive-date=25 March 2025 |access-date=27 March 2025 |website=myGov |publisher=[[Australian Government]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} and there is a Relocation Scholarship for students moving to or from a regional areas in Australia.{{Cite web |date=5 April 2022 |title=Relocation Scholarship |url=https://www.servicesaustralia.gov.au/relocation-scholarship |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250329230319/https://www.servicesaustralia.gov.au/relocation-scholarship |archive-date=29 March 2025 |access-date=3 April 2025 |website=[[Services Australia]] |publisher=[[Department of Social Services (Australia)|Department of Social Services]] ([[Australian Government]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} === Academic calendar === The academic year is divided into two semesters and optional summer and winter schools.{{Cite web |title=Academic Year Dates 2024 |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/student/dates/academic/2024 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240708171555/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/student/dates/academic/2024 |archive-date=8 July 2024 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=Important University Dates |publisher=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The weeks of term are called "teaching weeks" and there are also study without teaching, or ''SWOT'', weeks before examinations. All terms start on a Monday. Additionally, there is an "Orientation" week, informally known as O' Week, for first year students prior to the start of the two main semesters.{{Cite web |title=O'Week |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/orientation/oweek |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240412124327/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/orientation/oweek |archive-date=12 April 2024 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=O'Week |url=https://youx.org.au/welcome-week/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240823235158/https://youx.org.au/welcome-week/ |archive-date=23 August 2024 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] (trading as [[YouX]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Certain courses operate on trimesters with the model expected to eventually replace semesters.{{Cite web |title=Strategic Ambition and Direction 2024-2034 |url=https://adelaideuni.edu.au/content/dam/adelaideuniversity/documents/about/pdfs/our-journey/au-strategic-ambition-direction.pdf.coredownload.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240715153025/https://adelaideuni.edu.au/content/dam/adelaideuniversity/documents/about/pdfs/our-journey/au-strategic-ambition-direction.pdf.coredownload.pdf |archive-date=15 July 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=[[Adelaide University]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} == Libraries and archives == [[File:Mortlock Wing, State Library of South Australia.jpg|alt=This is a photograph of the State Library of South Australia, located adjacent to the university, where studies were initially conducted.|thumb|220x220px|The university is co-located with the [[State Library of South Australia|State Library]] where studies were initially conducted]]The library system comprises four libraries located across its three campuses.{{Cite web |title=Locations & Hours |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/locations-hours |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240420011330/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/locations-hours |archive-date=20 April 2024 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The largest library is the [[Barr Smith Library]] which is located with the Sir John Salmond Law Library on the main campus and the two satellite campuses each have their own libraries.{{Cite web |title=Barr Smith Library |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/locations-hours/barr-smith-library |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240513231300/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/locations-hours/barr-smith-library |archive-date=13 May 2024 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Sir John Salmond Law Library |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/locations-hours/sir-john-salmond-law-library |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231204002315/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/locations-hours/sir-john-salmond-law-library |archive-date=4 December 2023 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Roseworthy Campus Library |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/locations-hours/roseworthy-campus-library |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240516102540/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/locations-hours/roseworthy-campus-library |archive-date=16 May 2024 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Waite Campus Library |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/locations-hours/waite-campus-library |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240526213743/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/locations-hours/waite-campus-library |archive-date=26 May 2024 |access-date=18 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Additionally, the university also has several [[open access]] [[Institutional repository|repositories]] and university members have subscriptions to online [[academic journal]]s and databases.{{Cite web |title=Open Access Repositories |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/library-services/services-for-researchers/open-access/open-access-repositories |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230620082819/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/library-services/services-for-researchers/open-access/open-access-repositories |archive-date=20 June 2023 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Library Search |url=https://librarysearch.adelaide.edu.au/discovery/search |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210525055741/https://librarysearch.adelaide.edu.au/discovery/search |archive-date=25 May 2021 |access-date=15 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The [[State Library of South Australia]] is also co-located with the university. === Barr Smith Library === {{Main article|Barr Smith Library}} [[File:The Barr Smith Library Corinthian Pillars.jpg|alt=This is a photograph of the Barr Smith Library which was built in the Georgian Revival style with Corinthian pillars.|thumb|[[Corinthian order|Corinthian pillars]] incorporated into the [[Barr Smith Library]]]] The [[Barr Smith Library]] is the third-oldest university library in Australia and was originally located in the Mitchell Building.{{Cite web |title=Barr Smith Library, The University of Adelaide |url=https://www.experienceadelaide.com.au/heritage-places/barr-smith-library-the-university-of-adelaide-north-terrace-adelaide/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240818062204/https://www.experienceadelaide.com.au/heritage-places/barr-smith-library-the-university-of-adelaide-north-terrace-adelaide/ |archive-date=18 August 2024 |access-date=18 August 2024 |website=Experience Adelaide |publisher=[[City of Adelaide|The Corporation of the City of Adelaide]] (Adelaide Economic Development Agency) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Tom Elder Barr Smith adds to family legacy by funding 1932 Adelaide University library as a Walter Bagot classic |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/tom-elder-barr-smith-add-to-family-legacy-by-funding-new-adelaide-university-library-opened-in-1935-to-walter-bagot-s-classic-design |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240228134248/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/tom-elder-barr-smith-add-to-family-legacy-by-funding-new-adelaide-university-library-opened-in-1935-to-walter-bagot-s-classic-design |archive-date=28 February 2024 |access-date=18 August 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The library purchased its first book in 1877 for £11, prior to its formal establishment in 1882. It was later named after its founder [[Robert Barr Smith]] who throughout his life had donated £9,000 to purchase books towards the struggling library, which previously had no librarian and an annual budget of £200 of which £150 were spent on books.{{Cite web |date=1996 |title=BARR SMITH LIBRARY |url=https://d31atr86jnqrq2.cloudfront.net/heritage-places/heritage-place-information-sheet-north-terrace-barr-smith-library.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240818062937/https://d31atr86jnqrq2.cloudfront.net/heritage-places/heritage-place-information-sheet-north-terrace-barr-smith-library.pdf |archive-date=18 August 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=18 August 2024 |website=Heritage of the City of Adelaide: An Illustrated Guide 1996 |publisher=[[City of Adelaide|The Corporation of the City of Adelaide]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |last= |last2= |first2= |last3= |last4= |title=Robert Barr Smith & The University of Adelaide |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/special/stories/barr-smith/university/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240415134621/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/special/stories/barr-smith/university/ |archive-date=15 April 2024 |access-date=17 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} [[William Barlow (vice-chancellor)|William Barlow]], the registrar, acted as the ''de facto'' first librarian of the then-small library and R. J. M. Clucas was the first ''official'' librarian in 1900. Following Robert's death in 1915, an additional endowment of £11,000 in 1920 was made by his family. In 1928, his son [[Tom Elder Barr Smith|Tony Elder Barr Smith]] donated almost £35,000 towards a new building for the library to reduce congestion at its original site.{{Cite web |title=Barr Smith Library |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7318 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707145057/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7318 |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=17 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Reading room of Barr Smith library created as a beautiful scholarly space by Walter Bagot at Adelaide University |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/reading-room-of-adelaide-university-s-barr-smith-library-a-beautiful-scholarly-space-created-by-walter-bagot |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240226085312/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/reading-room-of-adelaide-university-s-barr-smith-library-a-beautiful-scholarly-space-created-by-walter-bagot |archive-date=26 February 2024 |access-date=17 August 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Robert's granddaughter Christine Margaret Mcgregor also donated almost 5,000 books in 1974. The building was designed in the [[Georgian architecture|Georgian Revival architecture]] style by [[Walter Bagot (architect)|Walter Hervey Bagot]] of the Adelaide-based architecture firm [[Woods Bagot|Woods, Bagot & Laybourne Smith]] and was inspired by [[Kensington Palace]] in [[London]]. Following its completion, its collection was transferred from the Mitchell Building through a [[zip line]]. The building features red-brick exteriors with an entrance with [[Corinthian order|Corinthian pillars]] below an inscription reading "''The Barr Smith Library''".{{Cite web |last=Chizea |first=Chukwuebuka |date=2 June 2022 |title=The Barr Smith Library by Woods Bagot |url=https://www.re-thinkingthefuture.com/case-studies/a7050-the-barr-smith-library-by-woods-bagot/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231206052126/https://www.re-thinkingthefuture.com/case-studies/a7050-the-barr-smith-library-by-woods-bagot/ |archive-date=6 December 2023 |access-date=18 August 2024 |website=Rethinking The Future |language=en |publication-place=[[New Delhi]], [[India]]}} It was later expanded twice to increase capacity, reaching a peak of 2 million books in 1999. [[File:A Hall of Learning.jpg|thumb|The Reading Room in the library features gilded ivory arches and tall pillars|alt=This is a photograph of the Reading Room inside the Barr Smith Library. It features gold and ivory arches, pillars and a high ceiling.]] The Barr Smith Reading Room is a notable feature of the library on Level 2. It features oak flooring and furniture with white pillars holding the gilded and ivory arches that form the rounded ceiling. Between the pillars and the arches are two large [[Latin]] inscriptions that run across both sides of the room in gold and commemorate the donations from Robert and his family who played a major role in its development. In mid-2023, over 61 paper planes were found in ledges around the ceiling of the reading room, including one made using a university brochure dating back to 1991.{{Cite web |last=Francis |first=Ben |date=9 August 2023 |title=Library restoration unearths unusual hidden history |url=https://glamadelaide.com.au/library-restoration-unearths-unusual-hidden-history/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240717051119/https://glamadelaide.com.au/library-restoration-unearths-unusual-hidden-history/ |archive-date=17 July 2024 |access-date=18 August 2024 |website=Glam Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The library is also home to a collection of rare books, the archives documenting the development of the university among other collections across various subject areas.{{Cite web |title=Special Collections and Archives |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/collections-archives/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240412154253/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/collections-archives/ |archive-date=12 April 2024 |access-date=18 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} This includes books belonging to [[Samuel Way]]'s collection, who had donated 16,000 books. === Sir John Salmond Law Library === Established in 1883, the Sir John Salmond Law Library holds a collection of legal works from Australian and overseas sources including the [[Law of the United Kingdom|United Kingdom]], [[Law of Canada|Canada]], [[Law of New Zealand|New Zealand]] and the [[Law of the United States|United States]].{{Cite web |last=Babie |first=Paul |date=June 2010 |title=125 YEARS OF LEGAL EDUCATION IN SOUTH AUSTRALIA |url=https://classic.austlii.edu.au/au/journals/AdelLawRw/2010/6.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240818092733/https://classic.austlii.edu.au/au/journals/AdelLawRw/2010/6.pdf |archive-date=18 August 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=18 August 2024 |website=[[Australasian Legal Information Institute]] |publisher=[[Adelaide Law School]] (which owns "[[Adelaide Law Review]]") |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=History Of The Library |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/about-the-library/history-of-the-library |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240418203744/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/about-the-library/history-of-the-library |archive-date=18 April 2024 |access-date=18 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} In 1967, the law library moved to the Ligertwood Building, which was among those that replaced the demolished [[Jubilee Exhibition Building]].{{Cite web |last=Gavaskar |first=Tanvi |date=21 February 2021 |title=Jubilee Exhibition Building in Adelaide: Building lost in time |url=https://www.re-thinkingthefuture.com/case-studies/a3391-jubilee-exhibition-building-in-adelaide-building-lost-in-time/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220520200026/https://www.re-thinkingthefuture.com/case-studies/a3391-jubilee-exhibition-building-in-adelaide-building-lost-in-time/ |archive-date=20 May 2022 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=Rethinking The Future |language=en |publication-place=[[New Delhi]], [[India]]}} It was renamed two years later after [[John Salmond (judge)|John William Salmond]] who was the third Professor of Law at the [[Adelaide Law School]]. === Roseworthy Library === {{See also|Roseworthy Agricultural College}} The Roseworthy Campus Library, formerly the Roseworthy Agricultural College Library, is located on the Roseworthy campus.{{Cite web |last=Emery |first=Margaret |date=November 1990 |title=Roseworthy Agricultural College and its Library 1883 – 1990 |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/ua/media/58/history.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240521013619/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/ua/media/58/history.pdf |archive-date=21 May 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=18 August 2024 |website=[[Roseworthy Agricultural College]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Roseworthy, South Australia|Roseworthy]], [[South Australia]]}} It dates back to the former Tassie Memorial Library which was funded by John Tassie in 1920. It was built as a memorial to his son, also named John Tassie, who was a student at the then [[Roseworthy College]] who died during [[World War I]] in [[France]]. It was later expanded in 1945 through donations by A Lowrie, the widow of former principal [[William Lowrie]]. The William Lowrie Memorial Annexe, which connected to the previous library in a T-shape, was completed in 1947. The library moved to its current site in 1974 due to increasing size constraints with the former site now used as a gymnasium. === Waite Woolhouse Library === The Waite Campus Library, also known as the Woolhouse Library, is located on the Waite campus in [[Urrbrae, South Australia|Urrbrae]]. ==Museums and collections== === Tate Museum === [[File:Mawson Laboratories.JPG|alt=This is a photograph of the Mawson Laboratories to where the Tate Museum moved from the former Prince of Wales building in 1952|thumb|The Tate Museum, located in the [[Mawson Building]] ]] The Tate Museum is a large [[natural history museum|geological museum]] housed in the Mawson Building. It was established in 1902 following the death of its namesake [[Botany|botanist]] and [[geologist]] [[Ralph Tate]], foundation Elder Professor of Natural Sciences from 1875 until 1901. The museum had existed informally since 1881, when Tate first began the collection. In 1952, it moved from the former Prince of Wales Building to the Mawson Laboratories (now Mawson Building; named after geologist and explorer [[Douglas Mawson]]).{{Cite web |date=September 2018 |title=Tate Museum Brochure |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25426 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707121626/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25426 |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=15 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It is one of the largest geological museums in Australia, with a collection of approximately 29,000 [[Rock (geology)|rocks]] and [[fossil]]s including [[meteorite]]s, [[tektite]]s, and [[Earliest known life forms|specimens of early life]].{{Cite web |last1=Milnes |first1=Tony |last2=Gostin |first2=Vic |date=16 May 2018 |title=Tate Museum attracts young scientists |url=https://earthnotes.blog/2018/05/16/tate-museum-attracts-young-scientists/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230531093326/https://earthnotes.blog/2018/05/16/tate-museum-attracts-young-scientists/ |archive-date=31 May 2023 |access-date=15 August 2024 |website=earthnotes |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |last=Heruc |first=Mirna |date=March 2009 |title=Art & Heritage Collections: Tate Museum |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/adelaidean/issues/31921/news31932.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707110307/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/adelaidean/issues/31921/news31932.html |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=15 August 2024 |website=Adelaidean |publisher=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |last=McKay |first=Tayla |date=22 March 2022 |title=The Tate Museum |url=https://issuu.com/adelaideuniversityunion/docs/issue_2-issuu/s/15182738 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240815162257/https://issuu.com/adelaideuniversityunion/docs/issue_2-issuu/s/15182738 |archive-date=15 August 2024 |access-date=15 August 2024 |website=[[On Dit]] |publisher=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Notable specimens include [[Ediacaran biota|some of earth's earliest organisms]] from the [[Ediacaran]], a number of which are from up to 550 million years ago, as well as [[Acraman impact structure|a display on an asteroid impact]] that smashed into the state's [[Gawler Ranges]] around 580 million years ago, and its associated widespread ejecta layer.{{Cite web |title=Tate Museum |url=https://set.adelaide.edu.au/physics-chemistry-earth-sciences/disciplines/earth-sciences/tate-museum |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240419054143/https://set.adelaide.edu.au/physics-chemistry-earth-sciences/disciplines/earth-sciences/tate-museum |archive-date=19 April 2024 |access-date=15 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The museum also hosts artefacts from Mawson's various expeditions to [[Antarctica|the Antarctic]], including one of his original sleds, bequeathed to the university by Mawson's widow [[Paquita Mawson]] after his death in 1958, and officially named the Douglas Mawson Antarctic Collection. It also houses Mawson Institute for Antarctic Research (founded 1959). The aim of the institute was to foster Antarctic study and research, by the maintenance of a library and collection, and by the delivery of occasional public lectures. [[George Ligertwood]], deputy chancellor of the university, was the inaugural chairman of the management committee.{{cite journal | title=The Mawson Institute for Antarctic Research | journal=[[Polar Record]] | publisher=Cambridge University Press (CUP) | volume=10 | issue=67 | year=1961 | issn=0032-2474 | doi=10.1017/s0032247400051706 | pages=408 | bibcode=1961PoRec..10Q.408. }} The museum's first official curator is Tony Milnes, who had worked to restore and document the large collection. [[File:Urrbrae House Adelaide.jpg|thumb|Urrbrae House, built in 1891, now a museum|alt=This is a photograph of Urrbrae House, now a museum forming part of the Waite Historic Precinct.]] ===Waite Historic Precinct=== The Waite Historic Precinct includes the Urrbrae House museum, Waite Arboretum, and Waite Conservation Reserve. The museum resides in the former home of [[Peter Waite (philanthropist)|Peter Waite]] and was built in 1891. Its interior is designed by Aldam Heaton & Co, who was also responsible for designing interiors for the [[Titanic]]. The Waite Arboretum, a tree museum, is home to over 2,500 tree specimens{{Cite web |title=Waite Campus |url=https://adelaideuni.edu.au/why-adelaide/campuses/waite-campus/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241002073055/https://adelaideuni.edu.au/why-adelaide/campuses/waite-campus/ |archive-date=2 October 2024 |access-date=7 October 2024 |website=[[Adelaide University]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} from over 1,000 taxa, many of which are endangered in the wild. The annual rainfall at the arboretum is 622mm.{{Cite web |title=Waite Agricultural Research Institute |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7384 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707211658/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7384 |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=6 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The Waite Conservation Reserve is home to native plants and fauna. == Other divisions == Other notable divisions of the university include: === Elder Conservatorium === {{Main article|Elder Conservatorium of Music}} [[File:Elder Hall interior, Adelaide.jpg|thumb|The Elder Hall was inspired by [[Florence|Florentine]] [[Gothic Revival architecture|Gothic]] architecture|alt=This is a photograph of the interior of the Elder Hall, which was inspired by Florentine Gothic architecture.]] Established in 1883, the [[Elder Conservatorium of Music]] is the oldest tertiary [[Music school|music academy]] in Australia.{{Cite web |date=December 2018 |title=Elder Conservatorium Handbook |url=https://able.adelaide.edu.au/music/ua/media/66/Elder%20Conservatorium%20Handbook.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240411114946/https://able.adelaide.edu.au/music/ua/media/66/Elder%20Conservatorium%20Handbook.pdf |archive-date=11 April 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=16 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It offers study and research programs in [[jazz]], classical performance, [[musical theatre]], classical voice, [[pop music]], sonic arts, music production, [[Songwriter|song-writing]], [[Musical composition|music composition]], [[conducting]], [[Music education|teaching]], ensembles and performance studies.{{Cite web |title=Elder Conservatorium of Music |url=https://able.adelaide.edu.au/music/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240524014545/https://able.adelaide.edu.au/music/ |archive-date=24 May 2024 |access-date=15 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It is also home to the [[Australian String Quartet]], [[Sia|Sia Furler Institute]] and the [[Centre for Aboriginal Studies in Music]].{{Cite web |date=29 March 2016 |title=Sia lends star power to university |url=https://adelaidenow.com.au/entertainment/confidential/south-australianborn-singer-sia-furler-gives-her-name-to-a-new-adelaide-university-music-institute/news-story/e945c7b44f4de4fca175c4601b303865 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240120123716/https://adelaidenow.com.au/entertainment/confidential/south-australianborn-singer-sia-furler-gives-her-name-to-a-new-adelaide-university-music-institute/news-story/e945c7b44f4de4fca175c4601b303865 |archive-date=20 January 2024 |access-date=16 August 2024 |website=[[The Advertiser (Adelaide)|The Advertiser]] |publisher=[[News Corp]]oration (which owns "[[News Corp Australia]]") |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite news |last= |first= |date=31 March 2016 |title=Sia lends her name to new music institute at University of Adelaide |url=https://www.theguardian.com/music/australia-culture-blog/2016/mar/31/sia-lends-her-name-to-new-music-institute-at-university-of-adelaide |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240715161548/https://www.theguardian.com/music/australia-culture-blog/2016/mar/31/sia-lends-her-name-to-new-music-institute-at-university-of-adelaide |archive-date=15 July 2024 |access-date=15 August 2024 |work=[[The Guardian]] |publisher=[[Scott Trust Limited|The Scott Trust]] (which owns "[[Guardian Media Group]]") |language=en |publication-place=[[London]], [[United Kingdom]] |issn=0261-3077}} The conservatorium also offers theatre performances and ''Lunchtime'' and ''After Hours'' concert series.{{Cite web |title=Elder Conservatorium Music Theatre |url=https://able.adelaide.edu.au/music/engage/elder-conservatorium-music-theatre |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240524015333/https://able.adelaide.edu.au/music/engage/elder-conservatorium-music-theatre |archive-date=24 May 2024 |access-date=15 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Concert Series |url=https://able.adelaide.edu.au/music/engage/concert-series |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240605144903/https://able.adelaide.edu.au/music/engage/concert-series |archive-date=5 June 2024 |access-date=15 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The academy's first Professor of Music was [[Cambridge]] graduate [[Joshua Ives]], also the first professor of music in Australia.{{Cite web |title=Elder Conservatorium of Music |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7382 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707133133/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7382 |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=7 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Controversial Joshua Ives starts Australia's first music doctorates at Adelaide University's Elder Conservatorium |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/controversial-joshua-ives-starts-conservatorium-as-australia-s-first-music-academy-with-doctorates |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240229150632/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/controversial-joshua-ives-starts-conservatorium-as-australia-s-first-music-academy-with-doctorates |archive-date=29 February 2024 |access-date=15 August 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} [[E. Harold Davies|Edward Harold Davies]] was the first Australian to graduate with a [[Doctor of Music]] in 1902 and [[Ruby Claudia Davy]] was the first Australian woman to earn the doctorate.{{Cite web |last=Elton |first=Jude |title=Elder Hall |url=https://sahistoryhub.history.sa.gov.au/places/elder-hall |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240229223734/https://sahistoryhub.history.sa.gov.au/places/elder-hall |archive-date=29 February 2024 |access-date=15 August 2024 |website=SA History Hub |publisher=[[Government of South Australia]] ([[History Trust of South Australia]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite news |last=Newman |first=G. G. |date=25 December 1918 |title=Australia's First Lady Doctor of Music. |url=https://trove.nla.gov.au/newspaper/article/212479175 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240714010627/https://trove.nla.gov.au/newspaper/article/212479175 |archive-date=14 July 2024 |access-date=15 August 2024 |work=[[The Critic (Adelaide)|The Critic]] |page=7 |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The Elder Hall used by the conservatorium was built following the death of its namesake and music lover [[Thomas Elder]] who left £20,000 towards its construction. The founding stone, made from nearby gumtree, was placed on 26 September 1898 by then-state governor Thomas Fowell Buxton. The building was officially opened exactly two years later on 26 September 1900 in a formal ceremony despite having been already used for months.{{Cite web |title=Elder Hall |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7320 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707130817/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7320 |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=15 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} ''[[South Australian Register|The South Australian Register]]'' reported the next day that the great hall was opened by [[Hallam Tennyson, 2nd Baron Tennyson|Lord Tennyson]] "''positively for the last time''" and that "''the majority of those who were present had already attended at two more or less appropriate ceremonial openings of the Elder Hall''".{{Cite news |date=27 September 1900 |title=ELDER HALL. |url=https://trove.nla.gov.au/newspaper/article/54534233 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707124615/https://trove.nla.gov.au/newspaper/article/54534233 |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=15 August 2024 |work=[[South Australian Register|The South Australian Register]] |page=4 |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite news |date=27 September 1900 |title=THE UNIVERSITY SPECIAL CONGREGATION. |url=https://trove.nla.gov.au/newspaper/article/54534252 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240815204302/https://trove.nla.gov.au/newspaper/article/54534252 |archive-date=15 August 2024 |access-date=15 August 2024 |work=[[South Australian Register|The Register]] |page=4 |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Its current organ is built by [[Casavant Frères|Casavant Fréres]] of [[Quebec]] in 1979, having replaced the previous Dodd organ that later was purchased by [[St Mark's Cathedral, Port Pirie|St Mark's Cathedral]] in [[Port Pirie]]. The [[great hall]], which was previously used for graduation ceremonies until the construction of [[Bonython Hall]] in 1936, was constructed in the [[Florence|Florentine]] [[Gothic Revival architecture|Gothic]] architectural style integrating freestone from [[Mount Gambier]].{{Cite web |title=Adelaide University Elder Hall's rich and varied feast – classical, jazz, experimental – at Friday lunch and more |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/elder-hall-presents-rich-and-varied-feast-of-music-at-friday-lunchtime-and-nights |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240227011850/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/elder-hall-presents-rich-and-varied-feast-of-music-at-friday-lunchtime-and-nights |archive-date=27 February 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} === National Wine Centre === {{main|National Wine Centre of Australia}} [[File:National Wine Centre.jpg|alt=This is a photograph of the National Wine Centre of Australia on North Terrace.|thumb|The [[National Wine Centre of Australia]] on North Terrace]] Located in the [[Adelaide Park Lands]] at the eastern end of North Terrace, the [[National Wine Centre of Australia|National Wine Centre]] offers some of the university's [[oenology]] courses.{{Cite web |title=The History & Architecture Of Adelaide SA's National Wine Centre Of Australia |url=https://www.nationalwinecentre.com.au/architecture-and-history |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240315092755/https://www.nationalwinecentre.com.au/architecture-and-history |archive-date=15 March 2024 |access-date=29 July 2024 |website=[[National Wine Centre of Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Opened in 2001, the facility also hosts public exhibitions about winemaking and its [[South Australian wine|industry in South Australia]].{{cite web |date=16 May 2006 |title=National Wine Centre of Australia |url=http://www.samemory.sa.gov.au/site/page.cfm?u=264 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140130013838/http://www.samemory.sa.gov.au/site/page.cfm?u=264 |archive-date=30 January 2014 |access-date=15 November 2013 |work=[[State Library of South Australia]] |publisher=[[Arts South Australia]] ([[Government of South Australia]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It contains an interactive permanent exhibition of [[winemaking]], introducing visitors to the technology, varieties and styles of wine. It also has [[wine tasting]] areas, giving visitors the opportunity to taste and compare wines from across Australia. The building, which is adjacent to the [[Adelaide Botanic Gardens]], was designed by [[Philip Cox|Phillip Cox]] and Grieve Gillett and uses building materials to reflect items used in making wine.{{cite web |title=National Wine Centre |url=http://dynamic.architecture.com.au/awards_search?option=showaward&entryno=20025028 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304031350/http://dynamic.architecture.com.au/awards_search?option=showaward&entryno=20025028 |archive-date=4 March 2016 |access-date=15 November 2013 |website=[[Australian Institute of Architects|The Royal Australian Institute of Architects]] |publisher= |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Melbourne]], [[Victoria (state)|Victoria]]}} ==Student life== ===Student association=== {{Main|Adelaide University Union|}} {{Infobox legislature | name = YouX SRC Political Groups | election1 = 28 March 2024 (by-election) | political_groups1 = {{plainlist | * {{Color box|C2223E|border=darkgray}} [[Socialist Alternative (Australia)|Left Action]] (5) * {{Color box|green|border=darkgray}} [[Australian Greens|Grassroots]] (2) * {{Color box|F323CF|border=darkgray}} [[Labor Right|UNITE]] (4) * {{Color box|FCDE01|border=darkgray}} Progress (12) * {{Color box|DD3633|border=darkgray}} [[Labor Left|Activate]] (1) * {{Color box|888888|border=darkgray}} [[Independent politician|Independent]] (2) * {{Color box|#ff9a9a|border=darkgray}} Vacant (0)* }} | website = {{url|https://youx.org.au/voice/src/}} | footnotes =Accurate as of 23 July 2024 }} Founded in 1895, the Adelaide University Union, trading as [[YouX]], is one of the oldest students' unions in Australia.{{Cite web |title=Our history |url=https://youx.org.au/about/aboutus/history/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240408052618/https://youx.org.au/about/aboutus/history/ |archive-date=8 April 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] (trading as [[YouX]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |date=10 September 2012 |title=ADELAIDE UNIVERSITY UNION CONSTITUTION |url=https://youx.org.au/pageassets/about/governance/rules/Constitution-of-the-Adelaide-University-Union.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230923184911/https://youx.org.au/pageassets/about/governance/rules/Constitution-of-the-Adelaide-University-Union.pdf |archive-date=23 September 2023 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] (trading as [[YouX]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The union operates both as the representative voice for university students and as a provider of a wide range of services.{{Cite web |title=About YouX |url=https://youx.org.au/about/aboutus/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240703224203/https://youx.org.au/about/aboutus/ |archive-date=3 July 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] (trading as [[YouX]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It is democratically controlled through its Board and [[YouX Students Representatives Council|Students Representatives Council]] and is run by elected student officers.{{Cite web |title=Governance |url=https://youx.org.au/about/governance/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240704003635/https://youx.org.au/about/governance/ |archive-date=4 July 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] (trading as [[YouX]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The union also supports a range of services, including numerous clubs and societies, social events and an advice service.{{Cite web |date=2023 |title=YouX Clubs Guide 2023 |url=https://youx.org.au/pageassets/interests/clubs/YOUX-CLUBS-GUIDE-2023(1).pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240430022005/https://youx.org.au/pageassets/interests/clubs/YOUX-CLUBS-GUIDE-2023(1).pdf |archive-date=30 April 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] (trading as [[YouX]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Events |url=https://youx.org.au/interests/events/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240323233937/https://youx.org.au/interests/events/ |archive-date=23 March 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] (trading as [[YouX]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Student Care |url=https://youx.org.au/support/studentcare/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240703232107/https://youx.org.au/support/studentcare/ |archive-date=3 July 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] (trading as [[YouX]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Union members also receive various discounts including at the UniBar, cafes and shops.{{Cite web |title=Member Discounts |url=https://youx.org.au/membership/discounts/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240227204405/https://youx.org.au/membership/discounts/ |archive-date=27 February 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] (trading as [[YouX]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} As of 2024, there are over 175 clubs and societies under the umbrella of the union.{{Cite web |title=Clubs |url=https://youx.org.au/interests/clubs/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240422093519/https://youx.org.au/interests/clubs/ |archive-date=22 April 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] (trading as [[YouX]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} These include the Adelaide University Sciences Association (the oldest society at the union not related to sports), the Adelaide Medical Students' Society established in 1889 and formerly the [[Adelaide University Sport|Adelaide University Sports Association]] whose [[Adelaide University Sport#Founding sporting clubs|founding clubs]] predate and ultimately founded the union.{{Cite web |title=Adelaide University Sciences Association |url=https://youx.org.au/interests/clubs/join/sciences/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240401104121/https://youx.org.au/interests/clubs/join/sciences/ |archive-date=1 April 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] (trading as [[YouX]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Adelaide Medical Students Society |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25445 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707130806/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25445 |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=14 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The sports association, which was founded in 1896, became directly affiliated with the university in 2010. In September 2024, YouX and the [[University of South Australia Student Association]] announced their intention to merge.{{Cite web |date=24 September 2024 |title=USASA & YouX All-Student Merger Survey |url=https://www.thewaite.org/usasa-youx-all-student-merger-survey/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241008052441/https://www.thewaite.org/usasa-youx-all-student-merger-survey/ |archive-date=8 October 2024 |access-date=8 October 2024 |website=Waite Research Precinct |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} === Annual Prosh === {{Main|Prosh (University of Adelaide)}} [[File:Prosh1905.jpg|alt=This is a photograph from the first Prosh Parade in Adelaide following its formalisation in 1905.|thumb|The inaugural Prosh Parade following its 1905 formalisation]] The student union also organises the annual [[Prosh (University of Adelaide)|''Prosh'']] week events inspired by the [[Ancient university|medieval]] tradition of ''ragging'' or "an extensive display of noisy disorderly conduct, carried on in defiance of authority or discipline".{{Cite web |last= |last2= |first2= |last3= |last4= |title=Student raggers and radicals: Student activity and activism from 1880 |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/special/exhibitions/student-raggers/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240626041422/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/library/special/exhibitions/student-raggers/ |archive-date=26 June 2024 |access-date=29 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Student raggers and radicals: Student activity and activism from 1880 |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25407 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230318091356/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25407 |archive-date=18 March 2023 |access-date=6 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The annual ''Procesh'' procession began in 1905 as a means for students to poke fun at established South Australian institutions, though ragging at the university dates back to the late 19th century. In one example, multiple alarm clocks set and hid by students behind books made constant interruptions during a 1896 ceremony at the former Mitchell Building library. The Prosh parade has in modern times included live band performances on flatbed trucks, student club-made floats and booze cruisers transporting inebriated students.{{Cite web |title=PROSH Week |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7472 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240708024032/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7472 |archive-date=8 July 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Among notable pranks, students suspended [[Holden FJ|a Holden car]] on Adelaide University Footbridge in 1971 above the [[River Torrens]] as part of ''Operation Bridge-hang''.{{Cite web |title=Adelaide Uni's Prosh Week 1966 uproar after Ernie Sigley's broadcast from beyond Kangaroo Island jammed in city |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/adelaide-uni-s-prosh-week-1966-starts-uproar-with-ernie-sigley-s-offshore-radio-broadcast-from-kangaroo-island |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240829192029/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/adelaide-uni-s-prosh-week-1966-starts-uproar-with-ernie-sigley-s-offshore-radio-broadcast-from-kangaroo-island |archive-date=29 August 2024 |access-date=29 August 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=PMG jams Adelaide Uni students' Prosh pirate radio, using Ernie Sigley to broadcast off Kangaroo Island in 1966 |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/federal-pmg-jams-adelaide-uni-students--prosh-pirate-radio--using-ernie-sigley-to-broadcast-in-international-waters-off-kangaroo-island-in-1966 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240301231751/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/federal-pmg-jams-adelaide-uni-students--prosh-pirate-radio--using-ernie-sigley-to-broadcast-in-international-waters-off-kangaroo-island-in-1966 |archive-date=1 March 2024 |access-date=29 August 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} [[File:1905 Prosh Procession.jpg|alt=This is a photograph of students on horse cart taking part in the annual Prosh event IN 1905, poking fun at Nobel Prize laureates Bragg and Bragg. A poster reads "Do not Bragg about radium".|thumb|Students on horse cart during the 1905 Prosh Parade with a poster that reads "DO NOT [[University of Adelaide#Notable people|BRAGG]] ABOUT RADIUM"]] Since 1954, the event has also involved the sale of satirical newspapers in public settings.{{Cite web |date=15 December 2018 |title=On Dit (1975) "Prosh" |url=https://digital.library.adelaide.edu.au/dspace/handle/2440/24761 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181215174040/https://digital.library.adelaide.edu.au/dspace/handle/2440/24761 |archive-date=15 December 2018 |access-date=15 December 2018 |website=[[On Dit]] |publisher=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |date=1965 |title=Austrine |url=http://www.samemory.sa.gov.au/site/page.cfm?c=2580 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240612165047/http://www.samemory.sa.gov.au/site/page.cfm?c=2580 |archive-date=12 June 2024 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=SA Memory |publisher=[[State Library of South Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The ''Prosh Rag'', later an annual issue of the [[On Dit]] student magazine, contains humorous references to various well-known persons of the day.{{Cite web |date=28 August 2016 |title=Adelaide University Prosh Day pranks of old |url=https://www.adelaidenow.com.au/news/south-australia/adelaide-university-prosh-day-pranks-of-old/news-story/014f7b11c4c1d2fde588b033e632dbc0 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210212222655/https://www.adelaidenow.com.au/news/south-australia/adelaide-university-prosh-day-pranks-of-old/news-story/014f7b11c4c1d2fde588b033e632dbc0 |archive-date=12 February 2021 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=[[The Advertiser (Adelaide)|The Advertiser]] |publisher=[[News Corp]]oration (which owns "[[News Corp Australia]]") |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |date=July 2001 |title=PROSH efforts lead to fundraising record by Adelaide Uni students |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/script/adelaidean/archive/backissues/Adelaidean-2001-07.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220814224241/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/script/adelaidean/archive/backissues/Adelaidean-2001-07.pdf |archive-date=14 August 2022 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=28 July 2024 |website=Adelaidean |publisher=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It has been sold on city streets by students to raise funds for charity as an attempt to legitimise the event following attempts to ban it in the early 1950s. Since at least the 1960s, the event and magazine has often been involved in controversial topics including [[Military history of Australia during the Vietnam War|Australia's involvement in the Vietnam War]], [[apartheid]], [[1971–74 French nuclear tests|nuclear warfare]], [[Indigenous rights|Aboriginal rights]] and the general administration of the university. Following the end of free university education, the event has toned down in more recent times as a result of increased work commitments by students and the [[Internet activism|rise of social media for activism]]. Prosh week winds up with the Prosh After Dark social event in the UniBar which has its origins from the Prosh Ball.{{Cite web |date=31 July 1970 |title=On Dit (Aug 1970) "Prosh Ball" |url=http://digital.library.adelaide.edu.au/dspace/bitstream/2440/24753/1/OnDit1970Aug_ProshBall.pdf |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131228202112/http://digital.library.adelaide.edu.au/dspace/bitstream/2440/24753/1/OnDit1970Aug_ProshBall.pdf |archive-date=28 December 2013 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=28 December 2013 |website=[[On Dit]] |publisher=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} ===Student newspapers and radio=== {{Main|On Dit}}[[File:Adelaide. Autumn walk. (20016607286).jpg|alt=This is a photograph of a footpath along the southern fence on War Memorial Drive in winter.|thumb|A footpath along the southern fence on [[War Memorial Drive]]]] ''[[On Dit]]'' (pronounced ''on-dee''), the second-oldest student-run print media in Australia, was established by the student union in 1932.{{Cite web |title=On Dit |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7315 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240720130204/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7315 |archive-date=20 July 2024 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |date=20 October 2022 |title=About |url=https://onditmagazine.com.au/about/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240717212627/https://onditmagazine.com.au/about/ |archive-date=17 July 2024 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=[[On Dit]] |publisher=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] (trading as [[YouX]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It is named after the French expression "we say" and operates independently of the university. It also occasionally uses the name "Hearsay" as a creative writing edition of the magazine that dates back to 1972 when the then-newspaper changed its name to "Heresay" in Volume 14 as part of a protest against [[1971–74 French nuclear tests|nuclear testing in the Pacific]] by [[Overseas France|France]].{{Cite web |date=1 December 2021 |title=Love, death, and science fiction: Our 2021 Hearsay creative writing awards |url=https://onditmagazine.com.au/2021/12/01/love-death-and-science-fiction-our-2021-hearsay-creative-writing-awards/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231025143508/https://onditmagazine.com.au/2021/12/01/love-death-and-science-fiction-our-2021-hearsay-creative-writing-awards/ |archive-date=25 October 2023 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=[[On Dit]] |publisher=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] (trading as [[YouX]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |date=June 1972 |title=On Dit. Volume 40, Issue 14 |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/2414 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707234318/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/2414?keywords=on%20dit%201972&type=all&highlights=WyJkaXQiLCIxOTcyIl0=&lsk=a5f7c531cbbdcb26535d3a0b4fe847f7 |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=[[On Dit]] |publisher=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The ''Prosh Rag'', which contains satirical content, is another special issue that is sold by students to the public to raise funds for charities. On Dit often publishes content on national and global politics and is an example of student activism at the university.{{Cite web |title=On Dit |url=https://youx.org.au/development/studentmedia/ondit/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240723060132/https://youx.org.au/development/studentmedia/ondit/ |archive-date=23 July 2024 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=[[YouX|Adelaide University Union]] (trading as [[YouX]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Former writers of the newspaper include politicians [[Julia Gillard]], [[Christopher Pyne]], [[Penny Wong]], [[Nick Xenophon]] and [[John Bannon]].{{Cite web |title=Significant figures in politics, education, media and more as editors of Adelaide University's 'On Dit' |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/significant-figures-in-politics--education--media-and-more-as-editors-of-adelaide-university--on-dit--student-newspaper |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240420041042/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/significant-figures-in-politics--education--media-and-more-as-editors-of-adelaide-university--on-dit--student-newspaper |archive-date=20 April 2024 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It is the successor to the ''Varsity Ragge'' which was founded in 1929.{{Cite web |title=Varsity Ragge |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7316 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240723111532/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7316 |archive-date=23 July 2024 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title='Varsity Ragge' 1928-31 the ragged predecessor of Adelaide University's boldly activist students newspaper 'On Dit' |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/-varsity-ragge--1928-34-the-predecessor-of-the-adelaide-university-s-student-newspaper--on-dit- |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240226232439/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/-varsity-ragge--1928-34-the-predecessor-of-the-adelaide-university-s-student-newspaper--on-dit- |archive-date=26 February 2024 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It also published news, poetry and comedic works from various student clubs and societies, including the neighbouring Adelaide Teachers College which later became part of the university campus. According to On Dit, the original newspaper failed due to student apathy. The oldest student-run newspaper dates back to 1889 as the ''Review'', which was previously ran by the Adelaide Medical Students' Society.{{Cite web |title=About the AMSS |url=https://www.amss.org.au/about/about-the-amss |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240321144015/https://www.amss.org.au/about/about-the-amss |archive-date=21 March 2024 |access-date=6 September 2024 |website=Adelaide Medical Students' Society |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The ''Adelaide University Magazine'' was another student-run magazine that began printing in 1918.{{Cite web |title=The Adelaide university magazine. |url=https://www.awm.gov.au/collection/LIB109748 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240906055554/https://www.awm.gov.au/collection/LIB109748 |archive-date=6 September 2024 |access-date=6 September 2024 |website=[[Australian War Memorial]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} Following the establishment of On Dit as the official organ of the student union, it renamed to the ''Phoenix'' in 1935 as a [[Modernism|modernist]] cultural and artistic magazine with literary works including [[poetry]].{{Cite web |last=Larsen |first=Marie |date=21 January 2019 |title=100 Years On - Adelaide University Magazine |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25410 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240708013037/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25410 |archive-date=8 July 2024 |access-date=6 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |last=Hoskin |first=Cheryl |date=May 2013 |title=A Genius About the Place: The Phoenix Magazine and Australian modernism |url=https://digital.library.adelaide.edu.au/dspace/bitstream/2440/129739/1/A%20Genius%20About%20the%20Place.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230530175648/https://digital.library.adelaide.edu.au/dspace/bitstream/2440/129739/1/A%20Genius%20About%20the%20Place.pdf |archive-date=30 May 2023 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=6 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Following funding cuts in 1940, its editors founded the ''Angry Penguins'' which was influential in the then-isolationist Australia as a socially-progressive magazine promoting [[Internationalism (politics)|internationalism]].{{Cite journal |last=Yates |first=Gavin |date=6 April 2023 |title="Black Swan of Trespass": Surrealism, Angry Penguins Journal, and the "Ern Malley" Experiment |url=https://pubs.lib.uiowa.edu/dadasur/article/id/31892/ |journal=Dada/Surrealism |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Melbourne]], [[Victoria (state)|Victoria]] |volume=24 |issue=1 |doi=10.17077/0084-9537.31892 |issn=2372-6725 |access-date=6 September 2024 |via=[[University of Iowa|The University of Iowa]]}}{{Cite web |last=Miles |first=John |date=12 July 2000 |title=Lost Angry Penguins: D.B. Museum of Modern Art]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Melbourne]], [[Victoria (state)|Victoria]]}} The magazine ceased operations after six years, following the [[Ern Malley hoax]] that set back modernism in the country.{{Cite web |last=Freeland |first=Lucy |date=7 February 2016 |title=Australias Avant-Garde Angry Penguins: From Art To Literature |archive-date=9 August 2024 |access-date=6 September 2024 |website=[[Tate Britain]] |language=en-GB |publication-place=[[London]], [[United Kingdom]]}} The magazine was later revived on-and-off the following years as the ''Phoenix'' but published its final issue in 1949. Later attempts by the student union in the 1950s to revive the Adelaide University Magazine as a staff and graduate magazine, rather than one focussed on artistic and literary works, failed. The University of Adelaide also runs its own official university-run newspapers including ''Lumen'' and formerly ''The Adelaidean'' between 1991 and 2016.{{Cite web |title=Previous Issues Established in 1896, [[Adelaide University Sport]] has 37 sports clubs, including some that predate its establishment. Its historical motto is ''Mobilitate Vigemus'' translated "we thrive by mobility".{{Cite web |last=Coppin |first=Sue |date=28 April 2011 |title=University Sporting Colours: Why the Blacks?
