Boat Club]] was founded in 1881 and operates from the [[River Torrens]] in the [[Adelaide city centre]] and from [[West Lakes Shore, South Australia#A.M. Ramsay Regatta Course|West Lakes]].{{Cite web |title=Adelaide University Boat Club |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25658 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707130724/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25658 |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=10 July 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Its primary [[University of Adelaide#Sports and athletics|boat shed]] was donated by [[Robert Barr Smith]] in 1909. The club claims to be the second-oldest in Australia, though this claim is disputed with the [[Sydney University Boat Club]] who uses the founding date of the [[Sydney Rowing Club]]. Each year the club competes in a number of events including the [[Oxford and Cambridge Cup]], which was donated by [[Blue (university sport)|Old Blues]] of the Universities of [[University of Oxford|Oxford]] and [[University of Cambridge|Cambridge]] in the 1890s.{{Cite web |last=Guerin |first=Andrew |date=22 June 2007 |title=Australian University Championships: History of these Championships |url=http://www.rowinghistory-aus.info/university-championships/index.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070622061733/http://www.rowinghistory-aus.info/university-championships/index.php |archive-date=22 June 2007 |access-date=10 July 2024 |website=The History of Australian Rowing |language=en-AU}} Several [[Olympic Games|Olympians]] have previously been members of the club including [[Collier Cudmore]] who later became Australia's [[Rowing at the 1908 Summer Olympics – Men's coxless four|first gold medalist in rowing]] at the [[1908 Summer Olympics|1908 London Olympics]]. The [[Adelaide University Sport#Adelaide University Tennis Club|Adelaide University Tennis Club]] was founded in 1885 and is a lawn tennis club.{{Cite web |title=Adelaide University Lawn Tennis Club |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25661 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707131338/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25661 |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=10 July 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The [[Adelaide University Lacrosse Club]] was founded in 1889 by future [[List of Nobel laureates|Nobel laureate]] [[William Henry Bragg]] and is one of the oldest in Australia.{{Cite web |title=A Brief History of the North Adelaide Lacrosse Club |url=https://lambtonmountlacrosse.com/artifacts/69529865.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240710231941/https://lambtonmountlacrosse.com/artifacts/69529865.pdf |archive-date=10 July 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=10 July 2024 |website=Lambton Mount Lacrosse Club |language=en-AU }} It is long-time rivals with the [[Melbourne University Lacrosse Club]].{{Cite news |date=12 August 1905 |title=LACROSSE. |url=https://trove.nla.gov.au/newspaper/article/9870541 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180919062143/https://trove.nla.gov.au/newspaper/article/9870541 |archive-date=19 September 2018 |access-date=8 September 2024 |work=[[The Argus (Melbourne)|The Argus]] |pages=19 |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Melbourne]], [[Victoria (state)|Victoria]]}}{{Cite web |title=Adelaide University Lacrosse Club |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25659 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707131928/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/25659 |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=8 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |last= |first= |title=About |url=https://www.revolutionise.com.au/aulacrosse/about |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240908055350/https://www.revolutionise.com.au/aulacrosse/about |archive-date=8 September 2024 |access-date=8 September 2024 |website=Adelaide University Lacrosse Club |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Following the 1895 establishment of the [[YouX|Adelaide University Union]], the student association, the three clubs co-founded the [[Adelaide University Sport#Adelaide University Sports Association|Adelaide University Sports Association]] in 1896. Additional sports clubs such as the [[Adelaide University Football Club]] were established [[Adelaide University Sport#Other sporting clubs|throughout the 20th century]].{{Cite web |title=Adelaide University Sports Association Inc |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7379 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240707202101/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7379 |archive-date=7 July 2024 |access-date=10 July 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Clubs |url=https://www.adelaideunisport.com.au/clubs/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240314112853/https://www.adelaideunisport.com.au/clubs/ |archive-date=14 March 2024 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=[[Adelaide University Sport]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The sports association was administered by the student union for over 100 years before becoming directly affiliated with the university in 2010. The Adelaide University Sports Association renamed to ''Adelaide University Sport'' that same year. === Residential colleges === The university did not set any land aside on its main campus for student accommodation due to ideological opposition to the culture of live-in students at the time but also influenced by the small size of the original campus.{{Cite book |last=Walkley |first=Gavin |title=St Mark's College: The Buildings and Grounds |date=1985 |publisher=Lutheran Publishing House |isbn=9780958881302 |location=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]] |language=en-AU}} However, demand for residential college accommodation led to the establishment of private colleges affiliated to the university. [[File:Newland Building.jpg|thumb|Newland Building of [[St Mark's College (University of Adelaide)|St Mark's College]], one of several private residential colleges|alt=This is a photograph of the Newland Building at St Mark's College, one of several private residential colleges near the university.]] [[St Mark's College (University of Adelaide)|St Mark's College]] was founded in 1925 by the [[Anglican Diocese of Adelaide]] and is the oldest of the colleges.{{Cite web |last= |first= |date=14 December 2020 |title=Preparing for our centenary: steps towards the founding of St Mark's College in 1925 |url=https://stmarkscollege.com.au/news/preparing-for-our-centenary-steps-towards-the-founding-of-st-marks-college-in-1925/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231109083423/https://stmarkscollege.com.au/news/preparing-for-our-centenary-steps-towards-the-founding-of-st-marks-college-in-1925/ |archive-date=9 November 2023 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=[[St Mark's College (University of Adelaide)|St Mark's College]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It was developed by some former residents of the Universities of [[University of Oxford|Oxford]] and [[University of Cambridge|Cambridge]] among others with the goal of developing a similar collegiate lifestyle. [[Aquinas College, Adelaide|Aquinas College]] was founded as a men's college in 1950 by the [[Catholic Church in Australia|Catholic Church]] at Montefiore House, the former residence of [[Samuel Way]] who was a chancellor and vice-chancellor at the university.{{Cite web |date= 21 December 2018|title=College History |url=https://www.aquinas.edu.au/about-aquinas/college-history/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240801054535/https://www.aquinas.edu.au/about-aquinas/college-history/ |archive-date=1 August 2024 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=[[Aquinas College, Adelaide|Aquinas College]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Citation |last=Bray |first=John Jefferson |title=Sir Samuel James Way (1836–1916) |date=1990 |work=Australian Dictionary of Biography |volume=12 |url=https://adb.anu.edu.au/biography/way-sir-samuel-james-9014 |access-date=13 August 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240813172513/https://adb.anu.edu.au/biography/way-sir-samuel-james-9014 |archive-date=13 August 2024 |url-status=live |place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]] |publisher=[[Australian National University]] (which owns "National Centre of Biography") |language=en-AU |author-link=John Jefferson Bray}} It later expanded to surrounding sites and became co-residential in 1975. [[File:Lincoln College Abraham House.jpg|thumb|Abraham House, one of several heritage buildings that are part of [[Lincoln College (University of Adelaide)|Lincoln College]]|alt=This is a photograph of Abraham House, one of a number of heritage-listed buildings at Lincoln College, a residential college for university students in Adelaide.]] [[St Ann's College]] was founded as a women's college in 1947.{{Cite web |date=6 December 2023 |title=ABOUT US |url=https://stannscollege.edu.au/about-us/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240801015329/https://stannscollege.edu.au/about-us |archive-date=1 August 2024 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=[[St Ann's College]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The college's honorary founder is politician [[Josiah Symon]] who in 1924 suggested that female students at the university should have somewhere to live. It became co-educational in 1973. [[Lincoln College (University of Adelaide)|Lincoln College]] was founded in 1952 by the [[Uniting Church in Australia|Methodist Church]] and named after the [[Lincoln College, Oxford|Lincoln College]] at the University of Oxford.{{Cite web |title=About Us |url=https://lincoln.edu.au/about-us/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240719134338/https://lincoln.edu.au/about-us/ |archive-date=19 July 2024 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=[[Lincoln College (University of Adelaide)|Lincoln College]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Originally established as a men's college, it became co-residential in 1973.{{Cite web |title=Our History |url=https://lincoln.edu.au/our-history/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240603181032/https://lincoln.edu.au/our-history/ |archive-date=3 June 2024 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=[[Lincoln College (University of Adelaide)|Lincoln College]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It features several [[Lincoln College (University of Adelaide)#Buildings and grounds|heritage-listed buildings]].{{Cite web |title=Lincoln College |url=https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7399 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027214318/https://connect.adelaide.edu.au/nodes/view/7399 |archive-date=27 October 2022 |access-date=6 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} There are also other private student accommodation providers in the city centre and in suburban Adelaide.{{Cite web |date=June 2023 |title=Student Accommodation |url=https://study.unisa.edu.au/SysSiteAssets/docs/brochures/2024/2023-accommodation-brochure_web.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929130459/https://study.unisa.edu.au/SysSiteAssets/docs/brochures/2024/2023-accommodation-brochure_web.pdf |archive-date=29 September 2023 |access-date=7 October 2024 |website=[[University of South Australia]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The university also manages the University Village, Mattanya Student Residences and the Roseworthy Residential College.{{Cite web |title=Accommodation |url=https://college.adelaide.edu.au/student-life/accommodation/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240419162758/https://college.adelaide.edu.au/student-life/accommodation/ |archive-date=19 April 2024 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=[[University of Adelaide College|The University of Adelaide College]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Mattanya Student Residences |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/accommodation/university-managed-student-accommodation/mattanya-student-residences |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230922030650/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/accommodation/university-managed-student-accommodation/mattanya-student-residences |archive-date=22 September 2023 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}{{Cite web |title=Roseworthy Residential College |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/accommodation/university-managed-student-accommodation/roseworthy-residential-college |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240804154329/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/accommodation/university-managed-student-accommodation/roseworthy-residential-college |archive-date=4 August 2024 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} ==== Former colleges ==== [[Kathleen Lumley College]] was a postgraduate college founded by the university in 1965.{{Cite web |date= |title=About Us |url=https://kathleenlumleycollege.com.au/about-us/ |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220311131604/https://kathleenlumleycollege.com.au/about-us/ |archive-date=11 March 2022 |access-date=13 August 2024 |website=[[Kathleen Lumley College]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} It closed in 2022 following a drop in overseas students during the [[COVID-19 pandemic]].{{Cite web |last=Lydia |first=Kellner |date=24 November 2023 |title=Kathleen Lumley College in North Adelaide to be sold off |url=https://www.realcommercial.com.au/news/kathleen-lumley-college-in-north-adelaide-to-be-sold-off Paralympian and state politician File:Sir Edward Holden.jpeg|alt=Edward Holden was an industrialist and founder of GM Holden.|[[Edward Holden]]{{Cite web |title=Holden, Edward Wheewall 1885-1947 |url=https://beta.samemory.sa.gov.au/site/page.cfm?u=438&c=2428 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240715215327/https://beta.samemory.sa.gov.au/site/page.cfm?u=438&c=2428 |archive-date=15 July 2024 |access-date=15 July 2024 |website=[[State Library of South Australia]] (which owns "SA Memory") |publisher=[[Government of South Australia]] ([[Department of the Premier and Cabinet (South Australia)|Department of the Premier and Cabinet]]) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}}
Industrialist and founder of [[Holden|GM Holden]] The university has had a significant impact on the public life of South Australia, having educated many of the state's earliest businesspeople, lawyers, medical professionals and politicians. It has also produced 117 [[Rhodes Scholarship|Rhodes scholars]],{{Cite web |title=Rhodes Scholars |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/alumni/recognised/rhodes-scholars |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240120152421/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/alumni/recognised/rhodes-scholars |archive-date=20 January 2024 |access-date=8 May 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]] }} 168 [[Fulbright Program|Fulbright scholars]]{{Cite web |title=Fulbright Scholars |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/alumni/recognised/fulbright-scholars |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240120152445/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/alumni/recognised/fulbright-scholars |archive-date=20 January 2024 |access-date=8 May 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]] }} and is associated with five [[List of Nobel laureates|Nobel laureates]] which constitute [[List of Australian Nobel laureates|one-third]] of Australia's total Nobel laureates.{{Cite web |title=Nobel Laureates |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/research/about-us/nobel-laureates |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240205125448/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/research/about-us/nobel-laureates |archive-date=5 February 2024 |access-date=8 May 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]] }} The university throughout its history has been associated with the development of [[penicillin]], [[space exploration]], [[sunscreen]], the [[Tank|military tank]], [[Wi-Fi]], [[polymer banknote]]s and [[X-ray crystallography]], and the study of [[viticulture]] and [[oenology]]. [[List of University of Adelaide people|Notable alumni of the university]] include the first female Australian prime minister [[Julia Gillard]], Singaporean presidents [[Tony Tan]] and [[Ong Teng Cheong]], the first Australian-born astronaut [[Andy Thomas]], the first demonstrator of nuclear fusion [[Mark Oliphant]], singer-songwriter [[Guy Sebastian]], the industrialist [[Edward Holden]] who founded Australian automobile manufacturer [[Holden|GM Holden]], the speech therapist [[Lionel Logue]] who helped [[George VI|King George VI]] manage his stammer, the inventor of modern sunscreen [[Milton Blake (chemist)|Milton Blake]]{{Cite web |title=History |url=http://hamiltonlabs.chimo.com.au/site/page.cfm?u=10 |url-status=bot: unknown |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240630180926/https://webarchive.nla.gov.au/awa/20070831103818/http://hamiltonlabs.chimo.com.au/site/page.cfm?u=10 |archive-date=30 June 2024 |access-date=19 September 2024 |website=Hamilton Laboratories |publisher=[[National Library of Australia]] (which owns "[[Trove]]") |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]] }}{{cbignore|bot=medic}} and [[Neil Weste]] whose advancements in [[Wi-Fi|wireless communications]] are widely used.{{Cite web |title=Professor Neil Weste |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/records/ua/media/103/Neil_Weste.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240630180922/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/records/ua/media/103/Neil_Weste.pdf |archive-date=30 June 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=19 September 2024 |website=The University of Adelaide |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} [[List of University of Adelaide people#Sports|Several Olympians and Paralympians]] have also studied at the university including [[Matt Cowdrey|Matthew Cowdrey]]. |website=[[Australian National University|The Australian National University]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Canberra]], [[Australian Capital Territory]]}} and [[Cabinet of Australia|several federal cabinet ministers]]. === Women's education === [[File:Student of 1885.jpg|alt=This is a mural to Edith Emily Dornwell who was concurrently Australia's first Bachelor of Science graduate and the first female graduate of the university.|thumb|Mural to [[Edith Emily Dornwell]], Australia's first [[Bachelor of Science|BSc]] graduate]] Following a [[royal charter]] granted by [[Queen Victoria]] in 1881, the university became the second university in the [[English-speaking world]] to formally admit women to degree-conferring programs on equal terms as men, contributing to a number of firsts in the history of [[Female education|women's education]] in Australia. Its first female graduate was [[Edith Emily Dornwell]] who concurrently became the first person in Australia to receive the degree of [[Bachelor of Science]] in 1885.{{Cite web |title=From Advanced School, Emily Dornwell: first Adelaide University female graduate, first for science in Australia |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/adelaide-unis-first-female-graduate-australias-first-science-graduate-in-1885_copy |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240710223557/https://adelaideaz.com/articles/adelaide-unis-first-female-graduate-australias-first-science-graduate-in-1885_copy |archive-date=10 July 2024 |access-date=10 July 2024 |website=Adelaide AZ |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The university also graduated Australia's first female surgeon [[Laura Margaret Hope|Laura Margaret Fowler]] in 1891.{{Cite web |title=Laura Fowler, South Australia's first female doctor in 1891, ends exclusion by British Medical Association |url=https://adelaideaz.com/articles/laura-fowler--first-female-medical-graduate-from-adelaide-university--blocked-from-south-australian-association-membership |url-status=live Pathologist{{Cite web |date=11 June 2014 |title=Emeritus Professor J Robin Warren |url=https://www.uts.edu.au/current-students/managing-your-course/graduation/past-speakers-and-speeches/2009-and-older/emeritus-professor-j-robin-warren |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230315000235/https://www.uts.edu.au/current-students/managing-your-course/graduation/past-speakers-and-speeches/2009-and-older/emeritus-professor-j-robin-warren |archive-date=15 March 2023 |access-date=15 July 2024 |website=[[University of Technology Sydney]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}} | align = right | header = Nobel laureates associated with the university: }} Nobel laureates associated with the university include alumni [[Lawrence Bragg]], who held the record for the youngest laureate ever until 2014, co-recipient with his father [[William Henry Bragg]] for their work in [[x-ray crystallography]] in 1915.{{Cite web |last=Bragg |first=William Lawrence |author-link=Lawrence Bragg |date=1967 |orig-date=6 September 1922 |title=The diffraction of X-rays by crystals |url=https://www.nobelprize.org/uploads/2018/06/wl-bragg-lecture.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221021090852/https://www.nobelprize.org/uploads/2018/06/wl-bragg-lecture.pdf |archive-date=21 October 2022 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=21 June 2024 |website=[[Nobel Prize|Les Prix Nobel]] ([[Nobel Foundation|The Nobel Foundation]]) via Nobel Lectures, Physics 1901-1921 |publisher=[[Elsevier|Elsevier Publishing Company]] |place=[[Stockholm]], [[Sweden]] |language=en |publication-place=[[Amsterdam]], [[Netherlands]] |publication-date=1967}}{{Cite web |last=Bragg |first=William Henry |author-link=William Henry Bragg |date=1967 |orig-date=6 September 1922 |title=William Bragg – Biographical - |website=[[Nobel Prize|Les Prix Nobel]] ([[Nobel Foundation|The Nobel Foundation]]) via The Nobel Prizes 2005 |publisher= |language=en |format= |publication-place=[[Stockholm]], [[Sweden]] }} == Controversies == === University merger === ==== Initial merger discussions ==== The University of Adelaide and the [[University of South Australia]] had previously engaged to discuss a merger in 2018 but failed due to disagreements from the latter about the post-merger leadership structure. ==== Opinion polling on staff ==== The [[National Tertiary Education Union]] SA conducted a survey of 1,100 university staff and found that three-quarters of respondents were against the merger.{{Cite web |last= |first= |date=16 June 2023 |title=Union demands evidence that SA university merger will deliver better quality education and research |url=https://www.nationaltribune.com.au/union-demands-evidence-that-sa-university-merger-will-deliver-better-quality-education-and-research/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230704132754/https://www.nationaltribune.com.au/union-demands-evidence-that-sa-university-merger-will-deliver-better-quality-education-and-research/ |archive-date=4 July 2023 |access-date=4 July 2023 |website=The National Tribune |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Toowoomba]], [[Queensland]]}} In addition, the state government has been accused of coercing the universities to agree to merge, indicating that a commission of inquiry would be established to find ways to compel the two universities to merge had their councils refused to do so, with less financial support available.{{Cite web |last=Kelsall |first=Thomas |date=30 June 2023 |title=Govt's carrot-and-stick approach to uni merger |url=https://indaily.com.au/news/2023/06/30/govts-carrot-and-stick-approach-to-uni-merger/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230710102931/https://indaily.com.au/news/2023/06/30/govts-carrot-and-stick-approach-to-uni-merger/ |archive-date=10 July 2023 |access-date=4 July 2023 |website=InDaily |publisher=[[Solstice Media]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Andrew Miller, the state secretary of the union, raised concerns that staff were under "extreme psychosocial pressure" to meet the 2026 launch deadline.{{Cite web |last=Kelsall |first=Thomas |date=30 May 2024 |title='Game of Thrones' culture claim in SA university merger |url=https://www.indaily.com.au/news/2024/05/30/game-of-thrones-culture-claim-in-sa-university-merger |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240714010615/https://www.indaily.com.au/news/2024/05/30/game-of-thrones-culture-claim-in-sa-university-merger |archive-date=14 July 2024 |access-date=21 August 2024 |website=InDaily |publisher=[[Solstice Media]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} Backing his claims with communications from the ''Integration Management Office'' staff responsible for merging the two institutions, he added that the "''[[Game of Thrones]]''" perception among staff competing "for the final spots of the new Adelaide University" was causing tensions, breakdowns and disharmony. The institutions' vice-chancellors [[David Lloyd (chemist)|David Lloyd]] and [[Peter Høj]] criticised the claims, referring to them as "whispers of Little Birds or Littlefingers",{{Cite web |last1=Lloyd |first1=David |author-link=David Lloyd (chemist) |last2=Høj |first2=Peter |author-link2=Peter Høj |date=21 August 2024 |title=Adelaide University merger is no Game of Thrones |url=https://www.timeshighereducation.com/blog/adelaide-university-merger-no-game-thrones |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240821074221/https://www.timeshighereducation.com/blog/adelaide-university-merger-no-game-thrones |archive-date=21 August 2024 |access-date=21 August 2024 |website=Times Higher Education |publisher=Inflexion |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[London]], [[United Kingdom]]}} though they had previously admitted that the "two-by-two approach across the board" was "not as linear as first conceived". In 2025, a [[Freedom of Information Act 1982|FOI]] document obtained by ''[[The Australian]]'' found an increase in bullying and harassment reports at the university following the announcement of the merger.{{Cite web |last=Willis |first=Belinda |date=22 January 2025 |title=Bullying and harassment spike at Adelaide University as Uni SA merger fears revealed in Freedom of Information laws report |url=https://www.adelaidenow.com.au/news/south-australia/bullying-and-harassment-spike-at-adelaide-university-as-uni-sa-merger-fears-revealed-in-freedom-of-information-laws-report/news-story/74c613eecdb09222237900a942e9a9f7 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250613215414/https://www.adelaidenow.com.au/remote/check_cookie.html?url=https%3a%2f%2fwww.adelaidenow.com.au%2fnews%2fsouth-australia%2fbullying-and-harassment-spike-at-adelaide-university-as-uni-sa-merger-fears-revealed-in-freedom-of-information-laws-report%2fnews-story%2f74c613eecdb09222237900a942e9a9f7 |archive-date=13 June 2025 |access-date=13 June 2025 |website=[[The Australian]] |publisher=[[News Corp Australia]] ([[News Corp]]oration) |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}} It added concerns from staff that the merger would result in a "meat grinder producing poorly educated students" that would be seen as "walking dollar signs". The post-merger plan to switch to a trimester academic calendar has also been criticised by the union whose internal poll showed that more than 4 in 5 members were against the move.{{Cite web |last=Kelsall |first=Thomas |date=4 July 2024 |title=New Adelaide University academic calendar under fire |url=https://www.indaily.com.au/news/2024/07/04/new-adelaide-university-academic-calendar-under-fire |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240705025457/https://www.indaily.com.au/news/2024/07/04/new-adelaide-university-academic-calendar-under-fire |archive-date=5 July 2024 |access-date=11 July 2024 |website=InDaily |publisher=[[Solstice Media]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} ==== Land re-development ==== [[File:Sunset reflection at Mawson Lakes.jpg|alt=This is a photograph of the University of South Australia's Mawson Lakes campus, around half of which will be sold for housing and commercial development as part of the merger.|thumb|Around half of UniSA's [[Mawson Lakes, South Australia|Mawson Lakes]] campus will be sold for development]] In February 2024, the [[Government of South Australia|State Government]] drew criticism for its plans to convert land it had purchased from two [[University of South Australia]] campuses for housing and commercial re-development. As part of the merger agreement, the land was to be sold to the [[Government of South Australia|South Australian Government]] for {{AUD|114.5 million}} and leased back to the university for a period of up to 10 years. Following the release of several internal [[Freedom of Information Act 1982|FOI]] documents retrieved by [[Solstice Media|''InDaily'']] from the [[Malinauskas ministry|Premier's Office]], it was later revealed that the land was "earmarked for future development" for residential and commercial purposes. The original media release replaced the phrase with "short-term transitional lease to university", referring to the leaseback period of 10 years, following concerns from [[University of South Australia|UniSA]] vice-chancellor [[David Lloyd (chemist)|David Lloyd]] that the original draft would "create enormous community reaction which will be particularly unhelpful at this time".{{Cite web |last=Kelsall |first=Thomas |date=16 February 2024 |title=FOI documents shed light on how to spin Adelaide's university merger |url=https://www.indaily.com.au/news/2024/02/16/foi-documents-shed-light-on-how-to-spin-adelaides-university-merger |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240216052304/https://www.indaily.com.au/news/2024/02/16/foi-documents-shed-light-on-how-to-spin-adelaides-university-merger |archive-date=16 February 2024 |access-date=7 May 2024 |website=InDaily |publisher=[[Solstice Media]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} The land sales account for the entirety of the [[Magill, South Australia|Magill]] campus and approximately 50% of the [[Mawson Lakes, South Australia|Mawson Lakes]] campus. === Staff conduct === In May 2020, then vice-chancellor [[Peter Rathjen]] commenced an indefinite leave of absence after chancellor [[Kevin Scarce]] resigned without public explanation the previous day.{{cite news |last=Siebert |first=Bension |date=5 May 2020 |title=No explanation as University of Adelaide's two top leaders step down |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2020-05-05/adelaide-university-vice-chancellor-leave-chancellor-resigns/12215942 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200827023500/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2020-05-05/adelaide-university-vice-chancellor-leave-chancellor-resigns/12215942 |archive-date=27 August 2020 |access-date=6 May 2020 |work=[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]] |publisher=[[Australian Broadcasting Corporation]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}} Later in the week, the [[Independent Commission Against Corruption (South Australia)|Independent Commissioner Against Corruption]] (ICAC) confirmed he was investigating allegations of improper conduct by the vice-chancellor of the University of Adelaide.{{cite news |last=Harmsen |first=Nick |date=7 May 2020 |title=ICAC investigating alleged 'improper conduct' by University of Adelaide vice-chancellor |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2020-05-07/icac-investigating-alleged-improper-conduct-by-vice-chancellor/12224370 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200901115459/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2020-05-07/icac-investigating-alleged-improper-conduct-by-vice-chancellor/12224370 |archive-date=1 September 2020 |access-date=7 May 2020 |work=[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}} Rathjen, accused of engaging in "a personal relationship with a staff member",{{cite web |last1=Dodd |first1=Tim |last2=Penberthy |first2=David |date=8 May 2020 |title=Uni chief faces misconduct probe |url=https://www.theaustralian.com.au/higher-education/uni-chief-faces-improper-conduct-probe/news-story/5cea3529cdd316509927c82a3c8979fb |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231208043503/https://www.theaustralian.com.au/higher-education/uni-chief-faces-improper-conduct-probe/news-story/5cea3529cdd316509927c82a3c8979fb |archive-date=8 December 2023 |access-date=27 August 2020 |website=[[The Australian]] |publisher=[[News Corp]]oration (which owns "[[News Corp Australia]]") |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}} was succeeded by acting vice-chancellor Mike Brooks. Rathjen formally resigned in July 2020,{{cite web |last=Penberthy |first=David |date=17 July 2020 |title=University of Adelaide in global hunt for new vice-chancellor as talk of merger rekindled |url=https://www.theaustralian.com.au/higher-education/university-of-adelaide-in-global-hunt-for-new-vicechancellor-as-talk-of-merger-rekindled/news-story/35a22ef0825ed265b6f7a481c552f630 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240715155848/https://www.theaustralian.com.au/higher-education/university-of-adelaide-in-global-hunt-for-new-vicechancellor-as-talk-of-merger-rekindled/news-story/35a22ef0825ed265b6f7a481c552f630 |archive-date=15 July 2024 |access-date=27 August 2020 |website=[[The Australian]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}} "due to ill health".{{Cite web |date=20 July 2020 |title=Adelaide University vice-chancellor, investigated by ICAC, resigns 'due to ill health' |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2020-07-20/adelaide-university-peter-rathjen-resigns-due-to-ill-health/12472954 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200827023456/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2020-07-20/adelaide-university-peter-rathjen-resigns-due-to-ill-health/12472954 |archive-date=27 August 2020 |access-date=27 August 2020 |website=[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]] |publisher=[[Australian Broadcasting Corporation]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}} In August 2020, the ICAC found that Rathjen had committed "serious misconduct" by sexually harassing two University of Adelaide colleagues, had lied to the then-chancellor Kevin Scarce, and also lied to the Commissioner in his evidence with respect to an investigation of sexual misconduct with a postgraduate student when he was employed at the University of Melbourne.{{Cite web |date=26 August 2020 |title='Egregious disrespect': University vice-chancellor sexually harassed colleagues, ICAC finds |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2020-08-26/vice-chancellor-peter-rathjen-mistreated-women-icac-finds/12597974 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200828030254/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2020-08-26/vice-chancellor-peter-rathjen-mistreated-women-icac-finds/12597974 |archive-date=28 August 2020 |access-date=26 August 2020 |website=[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Sydney]], [[New South Wales]]}} The ICAC Commissioner [[Bruce Lander]] acknowledged there were "further issues" in the full 170-page report on the investigation which he chose not to release due to privacy concerns surrounding the victims, instead releasing an abridged 12-page version 'Statement about an Investigation: Misconduct by the Vice-Chancellor of the University of Adelaide'.{{Cite web |date=26 August 2020 |title=Statement about an Investigation: Misconduct by the Vice-Chancellor of the University of Adelaide |url=https://icac.sa.gov.au/system/files/ICAC_Statement_About_an_Investigation-University_of_Adelaide.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240619155646/https://icac.sa.gov.au/system/files/ICAC_Statement_About_an_Investigation-University_of_Adelaide.pdf |archive-date=19 June 2024 |archive-format=PDF |access-date=27 August 2020 |website=[[Independent Commission Against Corruption (South Australia)|Independent Commission Against Corruption]] |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[Adelaide]], [[South Australia]]}} In determining his findings, the Commissioner relied in part on the personal blog{{cite news |last=Balter |first=Michael |date=16 May 2020 |title=Peter Rathjen serial sexual predator |url=https://michael-balter.blogspot.com/2020/05/peter-rathjen-serial-sexual-predator.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200830073300/https://michael-balter.blogspot.com/2020/05/peter-rathjen-serial-sexual-predator.html |archive-date=30 August 2020 |access-date=31 August 2020 |language=en-US}} of US journalist [[Michael Balter]], who documented Rathjens prior history of sexual harassment, and was largely responsible for bringing the matter to the public's attention, and ultimately ICAC's.{{Cite web |date=30 August 2020 |title=Sexual harassment: university's culture under scrutiny after damning findings against vice-chancellor |url=https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/2020/aug/30/peter-rathjen-why-his-sexual-harassment-scandal-was-no-surprise-to-south-australian-academia |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200831011034/https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/2020/aug/30/peter-rathjen-why-his-sexual-harassment-scandal-was-no-surprise-to-south-australian-academia |archive-date=31 August 2020 |access-date=31 August 2020 |website=[[The Guardian]] |publisher=[[Scott Trust Limited|The Scott Trust]] (which owns "[[Guardian Media Group]]") |language=en-AU |publication-place=[[London]]}} Claiming ill-health, Rathjen formally resigned in July 2020 and, despite the ICAC Commissioner's findings, received a large payout from the university.{{Cite news |date=8 September 2020 |title=Adelaide University defends $326,400 payout to disgraced former vice-chancellor Peter Rathjen |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2020-09-08/adelaide-university-defends-payment-to-former-vc-peter-rathjen/12640972 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240120201841/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2020-09-08/adelaide-university-defends-payment-to-former-vc-peter-rathjen/12640972 |archive-date=20 January 2024 {{short description|none}} {{multiple issues| {{peacock|date=July 2015}} {{original research|date=July 2015}} {{more citations needed|date=July 2019}} {{Very long|date=June 2025}} }} '''Varèse Sarabande''' is an American record label founded in 1977 and currently owned by [[Concord Music Group|Concord Music]]. The label is best known for preserving the history of [[Film score|film scores]], [[television]] soundtracks, and original [[Cast recording|cast recordings]]. During the 1980s and 1990s it became one of the most active soundtrack publishers, releasing hundreds of titles across LP, cassette, and CD formats. Its catalogue includes [[music]] from a wide range of film and television productions, and some classical works.
==VC/VX series (1978)== Starting in 1978, Varèse Sarabande released both [[Classical music|classical]] works and motion picture [[soundtrack]]s on [[Phonograph record|vinyl]] (LP). Some of the titles were reissued by [[Intrada Records]], Kritzerland Records, and [[Citadel Records]].<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.soundtrackgeek.com/v2/tag/varese-sarabande-2/ |title=Varese Sarabande | Soundtrack Geek V2 |access-date=2019-08-05 |archive-date=2019-08-24 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190824151328/http://www.soundtrackgeek.com/v2/tag/varese-sarabande-2/ |url-status=usurped }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/label/57951-Varèse-Sarabande|title=Varèse Sarabande|website=Discogs.com}}</ref>

==STV and CTV series (1979–1987)== In 1979, Varèse Sarabande introduced a new cataloguing system to differentiate its television and film soundtrack releases from its classical music catalogue. This was achieved using a distinctive [[STV (TV channel)|"STV]]" prefix to avoid confusion between the two lines. Many of these early soundtrack titles were later reissued on CD–either by Varèse Sarabande itself or by other specialty labels such as [[Intrada Records]], [[Citadel Records]], and Kritzerland Records. These reissues are often catalogued under the label's 47000 series or as part of its CD Club releases.
From 1984 to 1990 most soundtrack titles were issued in both [[LP record|LP]] and [[Compact disc|CD]] formats during a transitional period when compact discs were gradually replacing vinyl records due to their larger storage capacity–approximately 80 minutes compared to the average 45 minutes of a standard LP. Many titles were also released on cassette, identified by a "CTV" prefix, to distinguish them from their LP and CD counterparts.
In 1987, Varèse Sarabande entered into a distribution agreement with [[MCA Distributing Corporation|MCA Distributing]]. This move reflected a broader industry trend at the time, during which major record companies acquired or partnered with smaller independent labels amidst a growing stock market. Other labels involved in similar acquisitions include [[A&M Records]], [[Island Records]], and later [[GRP Records]].{{Citation needed|date=July 2025|reason=Need factual references to historical trend described}} The partnership with MCA significantly expanded Varèse Sarabande’s distributive reach and led to a notable increase in its soundtrack release output. * STV-81108 ''[[Fedora (1978 film)|Fedora]]'' - [[Miklós Rózsa]] * STV-81109 ''[[A Little Romance]]'' - [[Georges Delerue]] * STV-81115 ''[[The Young Lions (film)|The Young Lions]]'' - [[Hugo Friedhofer]] * STV-81116 ''[[Island in the Sky (1953 film)|Island in the Sky]]'' - [[Alfred Newman (composer)|Alfred Newman]] / ''[[The Song of Bernadette (film)|The Song of Bernadette]]'' - Hugo Friedhofer, [[Emil Newman]], [[Herbert W. ==704/1000 digital LP series (1979–1988)== Starting in the late 1970s, the label wanted to distinguish its digital LP releases from their standard edition LPs, featuring a unique codex starting with either 1000 or 704 in their later issues. Most of these applied to their earlier classical releases, with the label later pivoting to focus on their digital soundtrack recordings, which were becoming standard during the mid-1980s. This series lasted until mid-1988, when the label officially joined MCA to distribute and release their soundtracks and future Broadway, jazz and vocal albums.
Amongst this series was the DBX Recording Technology Showcase Series,<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xyQEAAAAMBAJ&q=DBX+Recording+Technology+Showcase+Series&pg=PT67|title=Billboard|date=27 October 1979}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PAEAAAAAMBAJ&q=DBX+Recording+Technology+Showcase+Series&pg=RA8-PA4|title=Popular Science|date=December 1979}}</ref> which was the label's attempt, in conjunction with the new technology of LPs played on a specialised no noise system, to make the music louder sonically with the [[dbx (noise reduction)|dbx]] encoded LPs. Chalfont, Varèse's LP offshoot, was amongst the group of record labels involved along with themselves personally. Their entry into this technology produced the album, ''Beyond the Sound Barrier: The Spectacular Sound of Digital DBX Discs'', a compilation of digitally recorded tracks from the albums ''Digital Space'' by [[Morton Gould]] and ''Boy With Goldfish'' and ''Lazarus and His Beloved (Symphonic Suite from the Opera)'' by [[Lee Holdridge]].
The soundtrack titles also corresponded in sequence to their CD releases also starting with the identical number or a different number than previously assigned. These were very scarce in production, unlike the LPs which were more widely available. However, a few of these LPs did not have a CD counterpart and some were cancelled, possibly due to the poor theatrical performances of the film. Examples of these are ''[[Bad Dreams (film)|Bad Dreams]]'' and ''[[Dead Heat (1988 film)|Dead Heat]],'' in which CD counterparts were announced and then cancelled, relegating them to LP and cassette releases only. Since these vinyl albums were not released with a suffix (i.e. STV, VSD, etc.) unlike the original classical releases (VCDM), VCDM has been added to avoid confusion between the 704 series CD as Varèse Sarabande Digital LP.

==5200 LP series (1988–1991)== After the label completed its deal with [[MCA Records|MCA Distributing]], they continued to produce LPs of their soundtracks, despite the fact that sales of vinyl were plummeting due to the successful emergence of the CD format which allowed for a more sonic experience, as well as putting more music on a CD.
The label reassigned the number of their LPs to try and match their CD counterparts under the prefix of VSD (Varèse Sarabande Disc). The LPs were simply assigned VS and followed by the catalogue number. All LPs featured the exact same artwork as the CDs as well as musical content. However, not all of the CD releases had an LP counterpart which was largely due to reissues from other record labels such as [[Arista Records]], MCA, Sony and others which were not produced by the label. Their releases concentrated on everything they produced and released which included hit films such as ''[[Die Hard 2]]'', ''[[Total Recall (1990 film)|Total Recall]]'', ''[[Back to the Future Part III]]'', ''[[Terminator 2: Judgment Day]]'' and ''[[Ghost (1990 film)|Ghost]]'', which is one of the label's all-time best selling albums.
The final LPs produced by the label were in late 1991, when the label switched strictly to CDs and cassettes only. The thrilling action score, ''[[Ricochet (1991 film)|Ricochet]],'' composed by Academy Award nominee [[Alan Silvestri]], was the final LP release on the label.

==47000 series (1983–1987)== Varèse Sarabande first began producing compact discs roughly around 1983, almost at the dawn of the compact disc, which also included labels such as [[GRP Records]], [[Telarc]], [[Mobile Fidelity]] and [[Polydor]]. Their initial releases in this series are often held in high regard by collectors, especially those without a [[barcode]] which all discs were printed and produced in Japan by the [[Japan Victor Company]] (JVC).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/label/295720-Victor-Company-Of-Japan-Ltd|title=Victor Company Of Japan, Ltd.|website=Discogs.com}}</ref> These discs feature exceptional sound quality and in particular, the classical releases feature exceptional artwork which have made these titles extremely valuable.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.digitaltrends.com/features/the-history-of-the-cds-rise-and-fall/|title=Remembering the rise (and final fall) of the late, great Compact Disc|date=7 February 2018|website=Digital Trends|access-date=7 January 2022}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.gizmodo.com.au/2012/08/the-history-of-the-compact-disc/|title=The History Of The Compact Disc|date=17 August 2012|website=Gizmodo Australia|access-date=7 January 2022}}</ref> Their first run of CDs pressed in Japan by JVC for US market began with the best selling ''[[Star Wars]] Trilogy'' CD (47201) and went up until ''[[Peggy Sue Got Married]]'' (47275). At that point the label switched over to [[LaserDisc|Laser Video]] for its releases, starting with ''[[Tai-Pan (film)|Tai-Pan]]'' (VCD-47274) in late 1986 until its final distributed release for that company with ''[[No Way Out (1987 film)|No Way Out]]'' (VCD-47301) in 1987.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/lists/CDs-manufactured-by-LaserVideo-Disctronics-Disc-Mfg-USA-my-collection-only/437456|title=CDs manufactured by LaserVideo / Disctronics / Disc Mfg. (USA) (my collection only) by mjb | Discogs Lists|website=Discogs.com}}</ref> After that, the label's releases were exclusively pressed by JVC America.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/label/288312-JVC-Disc-America|title=JVC Disc America|website=Discogs.com}}</ref>
This series was started and supervised by Tom Null, who oversaw the classical releases and the soundtracks along with co-producer Chris Kuchler. Richard Kraft came on board around 1986 and co-ran the label with Null until the end of 1988 to ease the label's transition after they reached a deal with MCA Distributing in 1987, which didn't take full effect until mid-1988.<ref name="latimes.com">{{Cite web |date=July 28, 1989 |title=Polygram Will Buy Island Records for About $300 Million |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1989-07-28-fi-294-story.html |website=Los Angeles Times}}</ref> Null would remain with the label until 1993 where he would eventually reactivate Citadel Records, formerly a subsidiary, and reissued many of Varèse's LP and out-of-print Varèse CDs.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/label/66642-Citadel-3|title=Citadel (3)|website=Discogs.com}}</ref> Under new producer Robert Townson, they released three titles during this period, including ''[[Omen III: The Final Conflict|The Final Conflict]]'', which was his first ever release as producer (and under his label Masters Film Music from Canada) in 1986, as well as ''[[Lionheart (1987 film)|Lionheart]],'' a little-seen adventure film which was released by the label in two separate volumes ''in 1987''. Townson would take over and oversee the label in early 1989 after Kraft's departure.
This series was notorious for many catalog number gaps especially during late 1986 and the entirety of 1987, which many speculate were titles that were released as LPs and cassettes during this period. One such title, ''[[King Kong Lives]],'' was actually produced by the label but acquired by MCA Records in late 1986 as the film was figured to be a big holiday release late that year and was released through them instead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/John-Scott-Graunke-Symphony-Orchestra-King-Kong-Lives-Original-Motion-Picture-Soundtrack/release/3536887|title=John Scott / Graunke Symphony Orchestra* - King Kong Lives (Original Motion Picture Soundtrack)|website=Discogs|year=1987 }}</ref> Some titles that were released theatrically during this period, did receive a CD counterpart to go along with the LP and cassette formats (i.e. ''[[Aliens (film)|Aliens]]'', ''[[The Fly (1986 film)|The Fly]]'', ''[[Link (film)|Link]]'', ''[[Enemy Mine (film)|Enemy Mine]]'', etc.) and others that were either slated to be released as a CD and were cancelled would be reissued later on by the label through the Varèse Sarabande Club or in some cases as part of the regular catalog (i.e. ''[[Vamp (film)|Vamp]]'', ''[[Firewalker (film)|Firewalker]]'', ''[[52 Pick-Up]]'',<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/Gary-Chang-52-Pick-Up-Original-Motion-Picture-Soundtrack/release/8490775|title=Gary Chang - 52 Pick-Up (Original Motion Picture Soundtrack) (2016, CD) | Discogs|website=[[Discogs]]|date=15 April 2016 }}</ref> ''[[F/X]]'', etc.).
Townson with the help of Kraft and Null would go on to start a Limited Edition Club of mail order only releases that would finally come to fruition during the spring of 1989 after being promoted in late 1987 and throughout 1988 after long delays.<ref name="ReferenceA">{{Cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/label/332651-Varèse-Sarabande-CD-Club|title=Varèse Sarabande CD Club|website=Discogs}}</ref> The club was intended for titles for new or older scores for past films that were released that did not receive a soundtrack release or a score album while the film was released theatrically and would later include many titles from the many gaps in their CD discography decades later. Townson's label, Masters Film Music, would get the first releases which included reissues which included ''[[Obsession (1976 film)|Obsession]]'', ''[[Bernard Herrmann]] Concert Suites,'' an elaborate four-disc reissue set of the original [[Decca Records|Decca]] LPs featuring artwork and a full-fledged production, and ''[[Jerry Goldsmith]] Suites and Themes'', the first live concert performance on CD by the famed Oscar winning composer which also featured artwork by [[Bob Peak]]. The official club titles started by Kraft and Null would soon be released not long after with ''[[Cherry 2000]]''<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/Basil-Poledouris-Cherry-2000-Original-Motion-Picture-Soundtrack/release/4226114|title=Basil Poledouris - Cherry 2000 (Original Motion Picture Soundtrack)|website=Discogs.com|date=March 1989 |access-date=16 May 2019}}</ref> by [[Basil Poledouris]], which at one point was the most valuable soundtrack on the market, going for as much as $2500 at a hotly contested auction. Null would leave Varèse in 1993 and start his own label Citadel Records and reissued a lot of Varèse's older out-of-print soundtrack LPs, as well as some of the classical releases he put out during his time with the label. Also during this period, Varèse reached an agreement with [[Columbia House]] under the Columbia Record Club to release a handful classical releases that included Scheherazade, The Four Seasons, and Dvořák Symphony No. 9 in E Minor, Op. 95, amongst others. The album covers had different color fonts and on the back insert and CD had the Columbia Record Club moniker "CRC" on it to distinguish itself from the original Varèse produced editions.
''[[Star Wars original trilogy|The Star Wars Trilogy]]'', ''[[The Man from Snowy River (soundtrack)|The Man from Snowy River]]'', ''[[Halloween (franchise)|Halloween]]'' and ''[[Witness (1985 film)|Witness]]'' are amongst the label's all-time top ten best selling releases<ref name="auto">{{cite web|url=https://variety.com/2018/music/news/varese-sarabandes-top-10-sellers-list-led-by-ghost-t2-star-wars-albums-1203084914/|title=Varèse Sarabande's Top 10 Sellers List Led by 'Ghost,' 'T2,' 'Star Wars' Albums|first1=Jon|last1=Burlingame|date=8 December 2018|website=Variety.com|access-date=16 May 2019}}</ref> from this period along with other titles such as ''[[The Emerald Forest]]'', ''[[Escape from New York]]'', ''[[Aliens (film)|Aliens]]'', ''[[The Fly (1986 film)|The Fly]]'', ''[[The Right Stuff (film)|The Right Stuff]]/[[North and South (miniseries)|North and South]]'', ''[[Pee-wee's Big Adventure]] / [[Back to School]]'', ''[[Starman (film)|Starman]]'' and ''[[Mad Max 2|The Road Warrior]]''. Many of these titles stayed in print for at least two decades or more and others quickly went out of print.
The very last title produced by the label for this run, ''[[The Serpent and the Rainbow (film)|The Serpent and the Rainbow]],'' (VCD-47362) was one of the rarest titles in this series due to its low print run of an estimated 400 copies. This title was probably caught under the label's transition with MCA Distributing and was barely released throughout the country. For this reason, this title was very valuable for a long period of time and was recently reissued as part of Varèse's ''Little Box of Horrors'' 12-CD collection in 2016 in an LP replica styled slipcase featuring the same artwork as the original CD. This also goes for the label's strange handling of reissues of ''[[Phantasm (film)|Phantasm]]'', ''[[Liquid Sky]],'' and ''[[Dawn of the Dead (1978 film)|Dawn of the Dead]]'' which also were supposed to be released during or after the MCA transition and received very limited runs and also became collector's items as a result of this.

==70400 series (1988)== With this batch of a dozen titles between the 47000 series and the new mainline series starting from 5200 to 7500 - which started right after this after Varèse's newly minted distribution deal with MCA (aka UNI later on [[Universal Music Group]] (UMG)) in late 1987 - the label finally instituted a barcode system which included this series of titles with a limited press run of over 500 copies or more during the label's transition with MCA into their distribution cycle. This numbering system stems from their digital LP series of titles featuring a few soundtracks (e.g. ''[[Suspect (1987 film)|Suspect]]'') and also could be seen as 470 rearranged as 704. These titles featured specific lettering and package design not unlike the 47000 series and this lettering and package design would run until the first batch of the 5200 series, which would later re-institute the font lettering they used during the 47000 series permanently until 1999.
This series features a few cancellations including one rumoured title of ''[[Dead Heat (1988 film)|Dead Heat]]'' by Ernest Troost and one confirmed but announced title, ''[[Bad Dreams (film)|Bad Dreams]]''<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/Jay-Ferguson-Bad-Dreams/release/8133981|title=Jay Ferguson - Bad Dreams|website=Discogs|date=15 February 2016 }}</ref> in which collectors were willing to pay top prices for until the label revealed and confirmed its cancellation when they finally released it on CD as part of their LP to CD Subscription Series that ran from June 2015 to May 2016.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/label/864273-LP-to-CD-Subscription-Series|title=LP to CD Subscription Series|website=Discogs}}</ref>

(1988 film)|D.O.A.]]'' - [[Chaz Jankel]] * VCD-70462 ''[[Poltergeist III]]'' - [[Joe Renzetti]] * VCD-70470 ''[[Betrayed (1988 film)|Betrayed]]'' - [[Bill Conti]]
==5200–7500 series (1988–2018)== When Varèse Sarabande began its partnership with [[Universal Music Distribution|MCA Distribution]] in 1988, the benefits of this deal were nationwide availability of Varèse Sarabande CDs, LPs and cassettes. The label adopted the MCA catalogue numbering system with the prefix VSD (which stood for Varèse Sarabande Disc and VSC for Varèse Sarabande cassette) and a subsequent number to denote multiple-disc sets (VSD2, VSD3, etc.) or a video release (VSV) if any as that was required for all labels in the MCA distribution system. When the MCA and [[PolyGram]] families merged in 1999, which created [[Universal Music Group|Universal Music]], the newly merged company used PolyGram's catalogue-numbering system, which used the main six digits of the [[Universal Product Code|UPC]] barcode (numbers 5 through 10 in the standard 12-number UPC set) as the basis for the catalogue number and since the original MCA numbering was already based on part of the UPC number (specifically digits 7 through 10), the number sequence was not changed. This run starting from VSD-5200 ended during the 7500 series as [[Concord Records]] assumed control of the company during the middle of 2018 and started a new numbering system as a result. Catalogue numbers without a title are usually assigned to a release that is not ready to be officially announced by the label. In some cases, older unassigned numbers are the result of a production delay, but usually it is because they have been canceled. For [[Record collecting|collectors]], the curiosity of knowing what a number has been assigned to is part of the demand for a complete discography.
Also as Varèse Sarabande grew, it branched into other musical venues and imprints including Varèse Spotlight, which focused on original cast recordings;<ref name="discogs.com">{{cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/label/925052-Varèse-Sarabande-Spotlight-Series|title=Varèse Sarabande Spotlight Series|website=Discogs.com|access-date=16 May 2019}}</ref> Varèse Jazz,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/label/766187-Varèse-Sarabande-Jazz|title=Varèse Sarabande Jazz|website=Discogs.com|access-date=16 May 2019}}</ref> which focused on jazz interpretations featuring the Trotter Trio and others; Varèse Vintage, which re-issued all genres of oldies;<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.varesesarabande.com/collections/varese-vintage|title=Varѐse Vintage|website=Varesesarabande|access-date=16 May 2019}}</ref> Water Music, which specialised in [[electronica]]; Fuel 2000, which focused on popular music;<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/label/10743-Fuel-2000|title=Fuel 2000|website=Discogs.com|access-date=16 May 2019}}</ref> and Wildcat, which focused on rock recordings. All divisions share the same catalog numbering system, but only the Varèse Sarabande, Spotlight, and Vintage imprints share the same number sequence. There have also been some soundtracks released as [[Music download|digital download]] titles that are not currently on CD at the moment.
Colosseum Records, which is featured here as well on this listing, was the label's international division located in [[Nuremberg]], Germany, which released all US soundtrack releases as well as European films that were able to release under their banner. Like the US versions of these soundtracks, the German pressings featured the same artwork, but instead of the black [[JVC]] design on the label side, they feature a silver label side with the JVC design in red. The artwork also featured "LC" which stood for a Colosseum version. The label released titles that were only available through imports that were not released here and many of those titles featured numbers to what were cancelled releases to movies here in the US. (i.e. ''[[John Carpenter]]'s Greatest Hits'' VSD-5336 / ''[[Dutch (1991 film)|Dutch]]'' VSD-5336 US, ''[[The Evil Dead|Evil Dead]]'' VSD-5362 / ''[[Rock-a-Doodle]]'' VSD-5362 US). They also released titles for US films that did not receive a US equivalent which included ''[[Dust Devil (film)|Dust Devil]]'' by [[Simon Boswell]], ''[[The Evil Dead]]'' by [[Joseph LoDuca]], ''[[Goodbye Bafana]]'' by [[Dario Marianelli]] and a few more. They also kept in print many of the out of print 47000 and 70400 series titles for a long period of time which included ''[[D.O.A. (1988 film)|D.O.A.]]'', ''[[Poltergeist III]]'', ''[[Cocoon: The Return]]'', ''[[The Fog|John Carpenter's The Fog]]'', ''[[The Thief of Baghdad (1978 film)|The Thief of Baghdad]] / [[Rudyard Kipling's Jungle Book|The Jungle Book]]'', and ''[[Lionheart (1987 film)|Lionheart: Volumes 1 & 2]]''. The label would later close in 2015 and Varèse does not have a European distributor at the moment.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/label/19526-Colosseum-Schallplatten-GmbH|title=Colosseum Schallplatten GmbH|website=Discogs.com|access-date=16 May 2019}}</ref>
Robert Townson, who produced his first title which was ''[[Omen III: The Final Conflict|The Final Conflict]]'' aka ''Omen III'' under his Masters Film Music label in conjunction with the label in 1986, took over in early 1989 and oversaw the label's growth into one of the greatest soundtrack labels in the world with a yearly output of approximately 50 titles from newly released films and restorations and reissues from other labels. These partnerships started with reissues of [[Sony Music|Sony]] LP titles (i.e. ''[[The Chase (1994 film)|The Chase]]'', ''[[The Lion in Winter (1968 film)|The Lion in Winter]]'', ''[[The Guns of Navarone (film)|The Guns of Navarone]]'', etc.) in 1989, then titles from the MCA catalog (i.e. ''[[MacArthur (1977 film)|MacArthur]]'', ''[[Ghost Story (1981 film)|Ghost Story]]'', ''[[Dracula (1979 film)|Dracula]]'', etc.) starting in 1990, which lasted until 2001 along with [[Arista Records]] (i.e. ''[[Close Encounters of the Third Kind (soundtrack)|Close Encounters of the Third Kind]]'', ''[[Taxi Driver]]'', ''[[The Fury (film)|The Fury]]'', etc.), the [[Decca Records|Decca]] catalog (i.e. ''[[The Robe (film)|The Robe]]'', ''[[Airport (1970 film)|Airport]]'', ''[[Anastasia (1997 film)|Anastasia]]'', etc.), and Bay Cities in 1997 (i.e. 1941, ''[[The Private Lives of Elizabeth and Essex]]'', etc.). It also included reissues from their LP catalog that appeared as regular releases (i.e. ''[[Lifeforce (film)|Lifeforce]]'', ''[[The Manhattan Project (film)|The Manhattan Project]]'', ''[[Videodrome (soundtrack)|Videodrome]]'', etc.) and as Soundtrack Club releases (i.e. ''[[F/X]]: The Deluxe Edition'', ''[[Eye of the Needle (film)|Eye of the Needle]]'', ''[[Last Embrace]]'', ''[[Vamp (film)|Vamp]]'', ''[[Silver Bullet (film)|Silver Bullet]]'', etc.). He also created a re-recording program which brought new audiences to older soundtrack recordings from composers who he had worked with personally on the label which included [[John Williams]], [[Jerry Goldsmith]], [[John Barry (composer)|John Barry]], [[Alex North]], [[Elmer Bernstein]], [[Alan Silvestri]], [[Stu Phillips (composer)|Stu Phillips]] and [[James Horner]] (i.e. ''[[Midway (1976 film)|Midway]]'', ''[[Body Heat]]'', ''[[To Kill a Mockingbird (film)|To Kill a Mockingbird]]'', ''[[Patton (film)|Patton]]'', ''[[Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf? (film)|Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?]]'', etc.) as well as produced new and complete re-recording of scores by Golden Age composers such as [[Bernard Herrmann]], [[Franz Waxman]] and [[Miklós Rózsa]] (i.e. ''[[Psycho (1960 film)|Psycho]]'', ''[[Vertigo (film)|Vertigo]]'', ''[[Sunset Boulevard (film)|Sunset Boulevard]]'', etc.). His first re-recording of a film score was a historic event in that it was for Alex North's rejected score for [[Stanley Kubrick]]'s sci-fi classic, ''[[2001: A Space Odyssey (film)|2001: A Space Odyssey]]'' which the famed director completely threw out. Conducting the event was North's best friend and Oscar-winning composer Jerry Goldsmith with his favourite orchestra, the [[National Philharmonic Orchestra|National Philharmonic]] of London with North's wife, Abby, present during the recording. Before this event took place, no one outside of North or Goldsmith had heard this seemingly lost score until North's passing in late 1991 and this project came to light. His most important release was ''[[Spartacus (film)|Spartacus]]'' by his favourite composer Alex North, for whom he was finally able to release his epic score (his personal all-time favourite) in 2010 to commemorate the film's 50th anniversary with an elaborate six-CD set. Coming in a close second and surprisingly one of the best-selling soundtracks of all-time in 1990 was the Oscar winning film, ''[[Ghost (1990 film)|Ghost]]'', which featured a score by Oscar winner [[Maurice Jarre]] and the hit song "[[Unchained Melody]]" sung by [[the Righteous Brothers]] which Alex North co-wrote and which featured in North's full orchestral composition. The album sold over one million copies.<ref name="auto" /> Townson left the label in late 2018 after Concord Music bought the label to concentrate on live concerts throughout the U.S. and Europe.
[[Bruce Kimmel]], who was asked to be a part of the label<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.haineshisway.com/2013/05/THE-REAL-HISTORY-OF-VARESE-SARABANDE/|title=The Real History of Varese Sarabande|publisher=Haines His Way}}</ref> when it started in 1978, which was intended to be solely for classical music and reissues of older Golden Age soundtracks that were really successful, released two soundtracks to films he had personally worked on ''[[The First Nudie Musical]]'' and ''Stages''. He joined the label in 1993 after a successful run at the then defunct Bay Cities soundtrack label and much like Robert Townson for soundtracks, was given the full responsibility of the Broadway Musical, Spotlight and Jazz imprints of the label. These divisions were dedicated to releasing or reissuing soundtracks to musicals that were performing on and off Broadway, which was a huge success for the label throughout the 1990s as they dominated the marketplace with stellar releases with many being best sellers and featured favourable reviews. Kimmel also showcased performers from these shows on the Varèse Spotlight<ref name="discogs.com" /> banner, which included [[Michelle Nicastro]], [[Christiane Noll]], [[Judy Kaye]], [[Paige O'Hara]], [[Mary Cleere Haran]], [[Liz Callaway]] and many others. Kimmel also produced a few compilations dedicated to film music as well such as the [[Ennio Morricone]] jazz ensemble album ''Once Upon a Time in Cinema'', ''Toonful'' and its sequel, ''Toonful Too'' dedicated to popular Disney songs performed by Nicastro, and also dedicated himself to jazz as well with the label's Varèse Jazz imprint which featured wonderful recordings by the [[Terry Trotter]] Trio producing albums inspired by movies, artists and Broadway shows by [[Stephen Sondheim]]. These include ''The Star Wars Album'', ''The [[Michel Legrand]] Album'', ''[[Follies]]'', ''Passion... in Jazz'' and many others. He also produced two of accomplished jazz pianist [[Fred Hersch]]'s albums, ''I Never Told You: Fred Hersch Plays the Music of [[Johnny Mandel]]'' and a popular collaboration between Hersch and jazz singer [[Janis Siegel]] entitled ''Slow Hot Wind''. Sadly, his divisions were closed down by 2001 by the label as the company was no longer interested in producing or releasing Broadway or jazz releases at that point in time. He would leave to work with Fynsworth Alley, a label dedicated to Broadway and off-Broadway theatre shows as well as vocals just as they had been doing at Varèse Sarabande in 2000, which ended abruptly in late 2001 as he and the label had completely different visions.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/label/113481-Fynsworth-Alley|title=Fynsworth Alley|website=Discogs.com|access-date=16 May 2019}}</ref> Kimmel would later form his own label, Kritzerland Records, which is dedicated to movie soundtracks, musicals and some jazz albums, in the late 2000s.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kritzerland.com/|title=Kritzerland Records - hard-to-find classics, reissues and new releases|website=Kritzerland.com|access-date=16 May 2019}}</ref>
The biggest curiosity of this series is the aborted release of the score album to that 1993 ''[[Blood In Blood Out|Bound By Honor]]'' which was originally titled ''Blood In Blood Out''. This soundtrack which was publicly announced at the time of the films' release, and abruptly cancelled because of Disney's last-minute decision to change the films' title due to a test screening that led to violence at a time of turmoil in Los Angeles at time. The label was part way through the run (an unspecified number of these CDs were produced) when the change was made. Proving it would be too costly to reprint and produce another run of CDs under the films' new title, the CD was never officially released despite its announcements in the film, the film's advertising and subsequent video releases along with the song laden soundtrack album released by [[Hollywood Records]] at the same time. This was an officially produced album that was never released but a number of copies of it have surfaced around Hollywood insiders and throughout the soundtrack community and has been dubbed ''[[The Caine Mutiny (1954 film)|The Caine Mutiny]]'' of soundtrack CDs due to its rarity. This title has recently appeared on eBay as a counterfeit (bootleg) which features the same contents. However, the disc itself was not officially pressed by JVC America as the official Varèse release and the inner ring features more silver in the centre which is not on the original JVC pressing. The artwork is a colour copy of the original with the exception of the lettering on the bottom in reference to the VSD-5396 catalog number which the font is incorrect on the booklet and it also omits liner notes by the films' writer Jimmy Santiago Baca and the films' director Taylor Hackford which is featured inside the booklet. The original release also featured a butterfly silver foiled holographic sticker on the jewel case like all Varèse Sarabande titles as well as all Universal Music Group distributed titles during this period of time.<ref name="discogs.com1">{{cite web|url=https://www.discogs.com/Bill-Conti-Blood-In-Blood-Out-Music-From-The-Original-Motion-Picture-Score/release/10492663|title=Bill Conti - Blood In Blood Out (Music From The Original Motion Picture Score)|website=Discogs.com|year=1993 |access-date=16 May 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.ebay.com/itm/164853063450?hash=item2662021f1a:g:xIcAAOSwIQRglC8u |title=BLOOD IN BLOOD OUT ORIGINAL SOUNDTRACK VARESE SARABANDE CD OOP BILL CONTI | eBay |access-date=2021-07-02 |archive-date=2021-07-09 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210709181840/https://www.ebay.com/itm/164853063450?hash=item2662021f1a:g:xIcAAOSwIQRglC8u |url-status=dead }}</ref>
The very last "official" title released under the old Varèse numerical system that had started in mid to late 1988 was Jeff Beal's score to the Rob Reiner film "Shock And Awe".

==The New VSD Series (2018–present)== The label was acquired by [[Concord Music]] in 2018 and abandoned the PolyGram/Universal numbering system. A new series was started as a result of Concord's UPC prefix. The rest of the UPC does not necessarily match the catalogue number of the release, and all have the VSD prefix regardless of which format it was released on. All LP versions released have not been included and should be in a separate section. This list concentrates on the CD portion and not digital releases.
