{{lang-zh|s=习近平|p=Xí Jìnpíng}}, pronounced {{IPAc-cmn|AUD|Xi Jinping.ogg|x|i|2|-|j|in|4|.|p|ing|2}}}} (born 15 June 1953) is a Chinese politician who has been the [[general secretary of the Chinese Communist Party]] (CCP) and [[Chairman of the Central Military Commission (China)|chairman of the Central Military Commission]] (CMC), and thus the [[paramount leader]] of China, since 2012. Since 2013, Xi has also served as the [[president of China]]. As a member of the fifth [[Generations of Chinese leadership|generation of Chinese leadership]], Xi is the first CCP general secretary born after the [[Proclamation of the People's Republic of China|establishment of the People's Republic of China]] (PRC). The son of Chinese communist veteran [[Xi Zhongxun]], Xi was exiled to rural [[Yanchuan County]], Shaanxi Province, as a teenager following his father's purge during the [[Cultural Revolution]]. He lived in a [[yaodong]] in the village of [[Liangjiahe]], where he joined the CCP after several failed attempts and worked as the local [[Chinese Communist Party Committee Secretary|party secretary]]. After studying [[chemical engineering]] at [[Tsinghua University]] as a [[Worker-Peasant-Soldier student|worker-peasant-soldier student]], Xi rose through the ranks politically in China's coastal provinces. Xi was [[governor of Fujian]] from 1999 to 2002, before becoming governor and party secretary of neighboring [[Politics of Zhejiang|Zhejiang]] from 2002 to 2007. Following the dismissal of the party secretary of Shanghai, [[Chen Liangyu]], Xi was transferred to replace him for a brief period in 2007. He subsequently joined the [[Politburo Standing Committee of the Chinese Communist Party|Politburo Standing Committee]] (PSC) of the CCP the same year and was the first-ranking member of the [[Secretariat of the Chinese Communist Party|Central Secretariat]] in October 2007. In 2008, he was designated as [[Hu Jintao]]'s presumed [[Succession of power in China|successor]] as paramount leader. Towards this end, Xi was appointed the [[Vice President of China|vice president]] and [[Vice Chairman of the Central Military Commission|vice chairman of the CMC]]. He officially received the title of [[leadership core]] from the CCP in 2016. While overseeing China's domestic policy, Xi has introduced far-ranging measures to enforce party discipline and strengthen internal unity. His [[Anti-corruption campaign under Xi Jinping|anti-corruption campaign]] led to the downfall of prominent incumbent and retired CCP officials, including former PSC member [[Zhou Yongkang]]. For the sake of promoting "[[common prosperity]]", Xi has enacted a series of policies designed to increase equality, overseen [[Targeted Poverty Alleviation|targeted poverty alleviation]] programs, and directed a broad crackdown in 2021 against the tech and tutoring sectors. Furthermore, he has expanded support for [[State-owned enterprises of China|state-owned enterprises]] (SOEs), emphasized advanced manufacturing and tech development, advanced [[military-civil fusion]], and attempted to reform China's property sector. Following the onset of the [[COVID-19 pandemic in mainland China]], he initially presided over a [[zero-COVID]] policy from January 2020 to December 2022 before ultimately shifting towards a [[mitigation strategy]] after [[2022 COVID-19 protests in China|COVID-19 protests]] occurred in China. Xi has pursued a more aggressive foreign policy particularly with regards to [[China–United States relations|China's relations with the United States]], the [[nine-dash line]] in the [[Territorial disputes in the South China Sea|South China Sea]], and the [[Sino-Indian border dispute]]. Additionally, for the sake of advancing Chinese economic interests abroad, Xi has sought to expand China's influence in Africa and Eurasia by championing the [[Belt and Road Initiative]]. Xi presided over a deterioration in relations between Beijing and Taipei under Taiwanese president [[Tsai Ing-wen]], successor of [[Ma Ying-jeou]] whom Xi met in 2015. In 2020, Xi oversaw the passage of a [[2020 Hong Kong national security law|national security law in Hong Kong]], which clamped down on political opposition in the city, especially [[Pro-democracy camp (Hong Kong)|pro-democracy activists]]. Since coming to power, Xi's tenure has witnessed a significant increase in [[Censorship in China|censorship]] and [[Mass surveillance in China|mass surveillance]], a deterioration in [[Human rights in China|human rights]] (including the [[Persecution of Uyghurs in China|persecution of Uyghurs]]), the rise of a [[Xi Jinping's cult of personality|cult of personality]], and the [[2018 amendment to the Constitution of China|removal]] of [[term limits]] for the presidency in 2018. Xi's political ideas and principles, known as [[Xi Jinping Thought]], have been incorporated into the [[Constitution of the Chinese Communist Party|party]] and [[Constitution of China|national constitutions]]. As the central figure of the fifth generation of leadership of the PRC, Xi has centralized institutional power by taking on multiple positions, including new CCP committees on national security, economic and social reforms, military restructuring and modernization, and the internet. In October 2022, Xi secured a third term as CCP General Secretary, and was re-elected state president for an unprecedented third term in March 2023. {{TOC limit|3}} ==Early life and education== Xi Jinping was born on 15 June 1953 in [[Beijing]],{{sfn|Wong|2023|p=21}} the third child of [[Xi Zhongxun]] and his second wife [[Qi Xin]]. After the [[Proclamation of the People's Republic of China|founding of the PRC]] in 1949, Xi's father held a series of posts, including the chief of the [[Publicity Department of the Chinese Communist Party]], [[Vice Premier of China|vice-premier]], and [[Vice Chairperson of the Standing Committee of the National People's Congress|vice chairperson of the National People's Congress]].{{Cite news|url=https://www.rferl.org/a/china-profile-xi-jinping/24764283.html|title=Profile: Chinese Vice President Xi Jinping|date=7 November 2012|publisher=[[Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty]]|access-date=26 August 2013|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131019083336/http://www.rferl.org/content/china-profile-xi-jinping/24764283.html|archive-date=19 October 2013}} Xi has two older sisters, [[Qi Qiaoqiao]] (齐桥桥), born in 1949 and Qi An'an (齐安安), born in 1952.{{cite news |url=http://news.xinhuanet.com/book/2009-04/28/content_11270872.htm |script-title=zh:與丈夫習仲勛相伴58年 齊心：這輩子無比幸福 |date=28 April 2009 |publisher=[[Xinhua News Agency]] |language=zh|trans-title=With her husband Xi Zhongxun for 58 years: very happy in this life|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130128102212/http://news.xinhuanet.com/book/2009-04/28/content_11270872.htm|archive-date=28 January 2013|access-date=18 March 2013}} Xi's father was from [[Fuping County, Shaanxi|Fuping County]], Shaanxi.{{Cite book |last=Chan |first=Alfred L. |url=https://academic.oup.com/book/41435/chapter/352774284 |title=Xi Jinping: Political Career, Governance, and Leadership, 1953–2018 |date=2022-03-24 |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]] |isbn=978-0-19-761525-6 |chapter=Childhood and Youth: Privilege and Trauma, 1953–1979 |chapter-url=https://academic.oup.com/book/41435/chapter/352774284 |access-date=12 January 2024 |archive-date=27 February 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240227172004/https://academic.oup.com/book/41435/chapter/352774284 |url-status=live }} Xi went to [[Beijing Bayi School]],{{cite web|last=Takahashi|first=Tetsushi|url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Spotlight/Beijing-Diary/Connecting-the-dots-of-the-Hong-Kong-law-and-veneration-of-Xi|title=Connecting the dots of the Hong Kong law and veneration of Xi|newspaper=[[Nikkei Shimbun]]|date=1 June 2002|access-date=23 October 2020|archive-date=3 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211203074351/https://asia.nikkei.com/Spotlight/Beijing-Diary/Connecting-the-dots-of-the-Hong-Kong-law-and-veneration-of-Xi|url-status=live}} and then [[Beijing No. 25 Middle School|Beijing No. 25 School]],{{cite web |last1=Li |first1=Cheng |year=2014|title=Xi Jinping's Inner Circle (Part 2: Friends from Xi's Formative Years) |url=https://www.hoover.org/sites/default/files/research/docs/clm44cl.pdf |access-date=15 July 2020 |website=[[Hoover Institution]] |publisher= |pages=6–22 |archive-date=26 September 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200926055927/https://www.hoover.org/sites/default/files/research/docs/clm44cl.pdf |url-status=live}} in the 1960s. He became friends with [[Liu He (politician)|Liu He]], who attended [[Beijing 101 Middle School|Beijing No. 101 School]] in the same district, and who later became China's vice premier and a close advisor to Xi after he became China's paramount leader.{{Cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2019/dec/29/power-behind-the-thrones-10-movers-and-shakers-for-2020|title=The power behind the thrones: 10 political movers and shakers who will shape 2020|last=Tisdall|first=Simon|date=29 December 2019|work=[[The Guardian]]|access-date=16 January 2020|issn=0029-7712|author-link=Simon Tisdall|archive-date=5 November 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211105032127/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2019/dec/29/power-behind-the-thrones-10-movers-and-shakers-for-2020|url-status=live}}{{Cite news|url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/blessed-by-xi-jinping-the-new-captain-of-chinas-economy-1519688592|title=Who Is 'Uncle He?' The Man in Charge of China's Economy|last=Wei|first=Lingling|date=27 February 2018|work=[[The Wall Street Journal]]|access-date=16 January 2020|archive-date=3 October 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211003102625/https://www.wsj.com/articles/blessed-by-xi-jinping-the-new-captain-of-chinas-economy-1519688592|url-status=live}} In 1963, when Xi was ten years old, his father was purged from the CCP and sent to work in a factory in [[Luoyang]], Henan.{{sfn|Bouée|2010|p=93}} In May 1966, the [[Cultural Revolution]] cut short Xi's secondary education when all secondary classes were halted for students to criticise and fight their teachers. Student militants ransacked the Xi family home.{{cite web |date=14 October 2013 |script-title=zh:习近平：＂我坚信我的父亲是一个大英雄＂ |trans-title=Xi Jinping: "I firmly believe my father is a great hero" |url=http://www.gapp.gov.cn/contents/2091/157500.shtml |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180305150646/http://www.gapp.gov.cn/contents/2091/157500.shtml |archive-date=5 March 2018 |access-date=6 March 2018 |publisher=[[State Administration of Press, Publication, Radio, Film and Television]] |language=zh |script-quote=zh:女儿习和平……在＂文化大革命＂中被迫害致死，是习仲勋难以抹去的心痛。|trans-quote=His daughter Xi Heping... was persecuted to death during the "Cultural Revolution", which is a heartache that Xi Zhongxun could not erase.}}{{cite news |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2015/09/25/world/asia/xi-jinping-china-cultural-revolution.html |title=Cultural Revolution Shaped Xi Jinping, From Schoolboy to Survivor |last1=Buckley|first1=Chris |last2=Tatlow|first2=Didi Kirsten|date=24 September 2015 |newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=6 March 2018|url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180305150638/https://www.nytimes.com/2015/09/25/world/asia/xi-jinping-china-cultural-revolution.html |archive-date=5 March 2018 |url-access=limited}} Also during the Cultural Revolution, Xi's sister Heping hanged herself at her military academy.{{Cite book |last=Torigian |first=Joseph |title=The Party's Interests Come First: The Life of Xi Zhongxun, Father of Xi Jinping |date=2025 |publisher=[[Stanford University Press]] |isbn=9781503634756 |location=Stanford, California}}{{Reference page|pages=238-239}} Later, his mother was forced to publicly denounce his father, as he was paraded before a crowd as an enemy of the revolution. His father was later imprisoned in 1968 when Xi was aged 15. In 1968, Xi submitted an application to the Bayi School's Reform Committee and insisted on leaving Beijing for the countryside.{{cite book |last=Shi |first=Z. C. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MKSRswEACAAJ |script-title=zh:习近平的七年知青岁月 |publisher=China Central Party School Press |year=2017 |isbn=978-7-5035-6163-4 |page={{page needed|date=November 2024}} |language=zh |trans-title=Xi Jinping's Seven Years as an Educated Youth |access-date=2024-08-18}} On 13 January 1969, they left Beijing and arrived in [[Liangjiahe|Liangjiahe Village]], [[Yan'an]], Shaanxi, alongside [[Chairman of the Chinese Communist Party|CCP Chairman]] [[Mao Zedong]]'s [[Down to the Countryside Movement]].{{cite web |url=http://v.youku.com/v_show/id_XMzM2NTc1MjAwOA==.html |script-title=zh:不忘初心：是什么造就了今天的习主席?|date=30 January 2018|website=[[Youku]]|language=zh-Hans |trans-title=What Were His Original Intentions? The President Xi of Today |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180130204306/http://v.youku.com/v_show/id_XMzM2NTc1MjAwOA==.html |archive-date=30 January 2018 |access-date=30 January 2018}} The rural areas of Yan'an were very backward,{{Cite news|last=Page|first=Jeremy|date=23 December 2020|title=How the U.S. Misread China's Xi: Hoping for a Globalist, It Got an Autocrat|language=en-US|work=[[The Wall Street Journal]]|url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/xi-jinping-globalist-autocrat-misread-11608735769|access-date=25 December 2020|issn=0099-9660|archive-date=24 December 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201224233237/https://www.wsj.com/articles/xi-jinping-globalist-autocrat-misread-11608735769|url-status=live}} which created a big gap for Xi as a teenager. He once recalled that he had to overcome "five hurdles" ([[flea]], food, life, labor and thought hurdle),{{cite news |date=2022-05-23 |script-title=zh:《足迹》第1集：过＂五关＂有多难 |trans-title="Footprints" Episode 1: How difficult is it to pass the "Five Gates" |url=http://www.news.cn/politics/leaders/2022-05/23/c_1128675005.htm |access-date=2024-08-18 |website=[[Xinhua News Agency]] |language=zh |archive-date=18 August 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240818143438/http://www.news.cn/politics/leaders/2022-05/23/c_1128675005.htm |url-status=live }} and the experience led him to feel affinity with the rural poor. After a few months, unable to stand rural life, he ran away to Beijing. He was arrested during a crackdown on deserters from the countryside and sent to a work camp to dig ditches. He later returned to the village, under the persuasion of his aunt Qi Yun and uncle Wei Zhenwu.{{cite news |date=2018-07-01 |script-title=zh:习近平的七年知青岁月 |trans-title=Xi Jinping's Seven Years as an Educated Youth |url=https://www.chinanews.com.cn/m/gn/2018/07-01/8552619.shtml |access-date=2024-08-18 |website=[[China News Service]] |language=zh |archive-date=18 August 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240818143603/https://www.chinanews.com.cn/m/gn/2018/07-01/8552619.shtml |url-status=live }} He worked as the [[Chinese Communist Party Committee Secretary|party secretary]] of Liangjiahe, where he lived in a [[yaodong|cave house]].{{Cite news |last=Lim |first=Louisa |date=9 November 2012 |title=For China's Rising Leader, A Cave Was Once Home |work=[[NPR]] |url=https://www.npr.org/2012/11/09/164684418/for-chinas-rising-leader-a-cave-was-once-home?t=1578589261050 |url-status=live |access-date=27 October 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180616053635/https://www.npr.org/2012/11/09/164684418/for-chinas-rising-leader-a-cave-was-once-home |archive-date=16 June 2018}} He then spent a total of seven years in [[Yanchuan County|Yanchuan]].{{cite news|last1=Demick|first1=Barbara|last2=Pierson|first2=David|date=14 February 2012|title=China's political star Xi Jinping is a study in contrasts|newspaper=[[Toronto Star]]|url=https://www.thestar.com/news/world/2012/02/14/chinas_political_star_xi_jinping_is_a_study_in_contrasts.html|url-status=live|access-date=24 October 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171013225355/https://www.thestar.com/news/world/2012/02/14/chinas_political_star_xi_jinping_is_a_study_in_contrasts.html|archive-date=13 October 2017}}{{cite news |first=Matt | last=Rivers |date=19 March 2018 |title=This entire Chinese village is a shrine to Xi Jinping |work=[[CNN]] |url=https://www.cnn.com/2018/03/19/asia/china-xi-jinping-village-intl/index.html |access-date=11 February 2021 |archive-date=26 February 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210226054335/https://www.cnn.com/2018/03/19/asia/china-xi-jinping-village-intl/index.html |url-status=live}} In 1973, Yanchuan County assigned Xi Jinping to Zhaojiahe Village in Jiajianping Commune to lead social education efforts. Due to his effective work and strong rapport with the villagers, the community expressed a desire to keep him there. However, after Liangjiahe Village advocated for his return, Xi went back in July that same year. Liang Yuming ({{lang|zh|梁玉明}}) and Liang Youhua ({{lang|zh|梁有华}}), the village branch secretaries, supported his application to the Chinese Communist Party.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4_4pDQAAQBAJ&pg=PA12 |script-title=zh:習近平如何改變中國 |publisher=Linghuo wenhua shiye youxiang gongsi |year=2014 |isbn=978-986-5721-05-3 |series=China Interpretation Series |page=12 |language=zh |trans-title=How Xi Jinping is changing China |access-date=2024-08-23}} Yet, due to his father, Xi Zhongxun, still facing political persecution, the application was initially blocked by higher authorities.{{cite news |last1=Osnos |first1=Evan |author-link=Evan Osnos |date=30 March 2015 |title=Born Red |magazine=[[The New Yorker]] |url=http://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2015/04/06/born-red |url-status=live |access-date=11 September 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191004153259/https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2015/04/06/born-red |archive-date=4 October 2019}} Despite submitting ten applications, it wasn't until the new commune secretary, Bai Guangxing ({{lang|zh|白光兴}}), recognized Xi's capabilities that his application was forwarded to the CCP Yanchuan County Committee and approved in early 1974.{{cite book | last=Chan | first=A. L. | title=Xi Jinping: Political Career, Governance, and Leadership, 1953–2018 | publisher=Oxford University Press | year=2022 | isbn=978-0-19-761522-5 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HH1ZEAAAQBAJ&pg=PA33 | access-date=2024-08-23 | page=33}} Around that time, as Liangjiahe village underwent leadership changes, Xi was recommended to become the Party branch chairman of the Liangjiahe Brigade.{{cite web |url=https://www.brookings.edu/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/china_20180318_xi_jinping_profile.pdf |title=Xi Jinping 习近平 |publisher=[[Brookings Institution]] |access-date=14 October 2021 |archive-date=27 September 2021 |url=https://docs.google.com/viewer?a=v&q=cache:Zy479_jx11YJ:www.aerospaceindia.org/Issue%2520Briefs/2010/25%2520October%25202010%2520-%2520CHINA%25E2%2580%2599S%2520NEXT%2520CHAIRMAN%25E2%2580%2594XI%2520JINPING.pdf+&hl=en&pid=bl&srcid=ADGEESjpQjUAtfkdEgw0cxv3UMunFfoLBcy0_po2aPN4xvPoQ-K9lFpIdgtf7PAw5ebYoTd8NwaRSswzfWmws278UPehKckL3Zb0TXlci2R0HsZGceCYRpBiD6EfBrspdjUh8CZzwcMg&sig=AHIEtbRjgFp8BCiHybWUtaP2uVF8BVuVZA |title=China's Next Chairman – Xi Jinping |last=Ranade|first=Jayadva|date=25 October 2010|publisher=Centre for Air Power Studies|access-date=27 May 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130724125447/https://docs.google.com/viewer?a=v&q=cache%3AZy479_jx11YJ%3Awww.aerospaceindia.org%2FIssue%2520Briefs%2F2010%2F25%2520October%25202010%2520-%2520CHINA%25E2%2580%2599S%2520NEXT%2520CHAIRMAN%25E2%2580%2594XI%2520JINPING.pdf+&hl=en&pid=bl&srcid=ADGEESjpQjUAtfkdEgw0cxv3UMunFfoLBcy0_po2aPN4xvPoQ-K9lFpIdgtf7PAw5ebYoTd8NwaRSswzfWmws278UPehKckL3Zb0TXlci2R0HsZGceCYRpBiD6EfBrspdjUh8CZzwcMg&sig=AHIEtbRjgFp8BCiHybWUtaP2uVF8BVuVZA |archive-date=24 July 2013|url-status=live}} After taking office, Xi noted that [[Mianyang]], Sichuan was using [[biogas]] technology and, given the fuel shortages in his village, he traveled to Mianyang to learn about biogas digesters.{{cite book | last=Chan | first=A. L. | title=Xi Jinping: Political Career, Governance, and Leadership, 1953–2018 | publisher=Oxford University Press | year=2022 | isbn=978-0-19-761522-5 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HH1ZEAAAQBAJ&pg=PA34 | access-date=2024-08-23 | page=34}} Upon returning, he successfully implemented the technology in Liangjiahe, marking a breakthrough in Shaanxi that soon spread throughout the region.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QjItAAAAMAAJ |script-title=zh:延川县志 |publisher=Shaanxi People's Publishing House |year=1999 |isbn=978-7-224-05262-6 |series=Shaanxi Local Chronicles Series |page=41 |language=zh |trans-title=Yanchuan County Annals |access-date=2024-08-23}} Additionally, he led efforts to drill wells for water supply, establish iron industry cooperatives, reclaim land, plant flue-cured tobacco, and set up sales outlets to address the village's production and economic challenges.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=r6NgAAAAIAAJ |script-title=zh:红墙内的子女们 |publisher=Yanbian University Press |year=1998 |isbn=978-7-5634-1080-4 |page=468 |language=zh |trans-title=Children Within the Red Wall |access-date=2024-08-23}}{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZQbWEAAAQBAJ&pg=PA76 |script-title=zh:缙麓别调（三） |publisher=Chongqing daxue dianzi yinxiang chubanshe |year=2021 |isbn=978-7-5689-2545-7 |page=76 |language=zh |trans-title=Jinlu Special Tune (Part 3) |access-date=2024-08-23}} In 1975, when Yanchuan County was allocated a spot at [[Tsinghua University]], the CCP Yanchuan County Committee recommended Xi for admission.{{cite book |last=Pengpeng |first=Z. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LqRV8BYikI0C&pg=PA619 |script-title=zh:谜一样的人生 |publisher=Zhu Peng Peng |isbn=978-0-9787999-2-2 |page=619 |language=zh |trans-title=A Mysterious Life |access-date=2024-08-23}} From 1975 to 1979, Xi studied [[chemical engineering]] at Tsinghua University as a [[Worker-Peasant-Soldier student|worker-peasant-soldier student]] in Beijing.{{cite book |author=Zhong |first1=Wen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ImNcEAAAQBAJ&pg=PA3 |script-title=zh:习近平传 |last2=Zhang |first2=Jie |publisher=Bouden House |year=2022 |isbn=978-1-034-94892-6 |page=3 |language=zh |trans-title=Biography of Xi Jinping |access-date=2024-08-30}}{{sfn|Simon|Cong|pp=28–29|2009}} ==Early political career== === Central Military Commission === After graduating in April 1979, Xi was assigned to the [[General Office of the State Council]] and the [[General Office of the Central Military Commission|General Office of the CCP Central Military Commission]], where he served as one of three secretaries to [[Geng Biao]],{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yI1KAQAAIAAJ |title=耿飚传 |publisher=People's Liberation Army Press |year=2009 |isbn=978-7-5065-5904-1 |page= |language=zh |trans-title=Biography of Geng Biao |access-date=2024-08-30 |issue=v. 2}} a member of the [[Politburo of the Chinese Communist Party|CCP Politburo]] and [[Minister of National Defense (China)|Minister of Defense]].{{cite book | last=Guo | first=X. | title=The Politics of the Core Leader in China: Culture, Institution, Legitimacy, and Power | publisher=Cambridge University Press | year=2019 | isbn=978-1-108-48049-9 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hm2MDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA363 | access-date=2024-08-30 | page=363}} === Hebei === On 25 March 1982, Xi was appointed deputy party secretary of [[Zhengding County]] in Hebei.{{cite book | last=Li | first=C. | title=Chinese Politics in the Xi Jinping Era: Reassessing Collective Leadership | publisher=Brookings Institution Press | year=2016 | isbn=978-0-8157-2694-4 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3NKACwAAQBAJ&pg=PA338 | access-date=2024-08-30 | page=338}}{{Cite news |last=Johnson |first=Ian |date=2012-09-30 |title=Elite and Deft, Xi Aimed High Early in China |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2012/09/30/world/asia/aiming-for-top-xi-jinping-forged-ties-early-in-china.html |access-date=2023-04-04 |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=30 September 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120930091232/http://www.nytimes.com/2012/09/30/world/asia/aiming-for-top-xi-jinping-forged-ties-early-in-china.html |url-status=live}} Together with Lü Yulan ({{lang|zh|吕玉兰}}), the other deputy party secretary of Zhengding, Xi wrote a letter to the central government addressing the excessive requisitions that burdened local farmers.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1Y36DwAAQBAJ&pg=PT59 |title=精准扶贫的辩证法 |publisher=Xiamen University Press |year=2018 |isbn=978-7-5615-6916-0 |page=59 |language=zh |trans-title=The Dialectics of Targeted Poverty Alleviation |access-date=2024-08-30}} Their efforts successfully convinced the central government to reduce the annual requisition amount by 14 million kilograms.{{cite book |author= |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MZFBEAAAQBAJ&pg=PA61 |title=習近平扶貧故事 |publisher=Sino United Electronic Publishing Limited |year=2021 |isbn=978-988-8758-27-2 |pages=19–61 |language=zh |trans-title=Xi Jinping's Poverty Alleviation Story |access-date=2024-08-30}} In 1983, Zhengding adjusted its agricultural structure, leading to a significant increase in farmers' incomes from 148 yuan to over 400 yuan in 1984,{{cite news |date=2018-02-08 |title=习近平同志当县委书记时就被认为是栋梁之才 |trans-title=Comrade Xi Jinping was considered a pillar of talent when he was the county party secretary |url=http://www.xinhuanet.com/politics/2018-02/08/c_1122386142.htm |access-date=2024-08-30 |website=[[Xinhua News Agency]] |language=zh |archive-date=30 August 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240830144357/http://www.xinhuanet.com/politics/2018-02/08/c_1122386142.htm |url-status=live }} thoroughly solving the county's economic issues.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=57EXEAAAQBAJ&pg=PT12 |title=决战2020：拒绝贫困 |publisher=China Democracy and Legal System Publishing House |year=2016 |isbn=978-7-5162-1125-0 |page=12 |language=zh |trans-title=Decisive Battle of 2020: Say No to Poverty |access-date=2024-08-30}} As the secretary of the CCP Zhengding County Committee in July 1983,{{cite book |author= |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SRMnAAAAMAAJ |title=正定縣志 |publisher=China City Press |year=1992 |isbn=978-7-5074-0610-8 |pages=73–548 |language=zh |trans-title=Zhengding Chronicle |access-date=2024-08-30}} Xi initiated several development projects, including the development of "Nine Articles of Zhengding talents", the construction of Changshan Park,{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AZBJAAAAMAAJ |title=青春岁月: 当代青年小报告文学选 |publisher=[[People's Literature Publishing House]] |year=1986 |page=62 |language=zh |trans-title=Youth Years: A Selection of Contemporary Youth Reportage |access-date=2024-08-30}} the restoration of the [[Longxing Temple]], the formation of a tourism company, and the establishment of the Rongguo Mansion and Zhengding Table Tennis Base.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z-sXEQAAQBAJ&pg=PA3 |title=河北社会主义核心价值观培育践行报告 |publisher=[[Social Sciences Literature Press]] |year=2023 |isbn=978-7-5228-1618-0 |page=3 |language=zh |trans-title=Hebei's Report on the Cultivation and Practice of Socialist Core Values |access-date=2024-08-30}} He also persuaded the China Teleplay Production Center to set the filming base of [[Dream of the Red Chamber (1987 TV series)|Dream of the Red Mansions]] in Zhengding and secured 3.5 million yuan to build Rongguo Mansion,{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Vn9pAAAAIAAJ |title=紅色后代 |publisher=Chengdu Publishing House |year=1996 |isbn=978-7-80575-946-3 |page=286 |language=zh |trans-title=Red Offspring |access-date=2024-08-30}} which significantly boosted the county's tourism industry, generating 17.61 million yuan in revenue that year.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YqFPAAAAIAAJ |title=血脉总相连: 中国名人后代大寻踪 |publisher=Beijing Yanshan Publishing House |year=1993 |isbn=978-7-5402-0658-1 |page=363 |language=zh |trans-title=Blood is Always Connected: Tracing the Descendants of Chinese Celebrities |access-date=2024-08-30}} Additionally, Xi invited prominent figures such as [[Hua Luogeng]], [[Yu Guangyuan]], Pan Chengxiao to visit Zhengding,{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gx8zAQAAIAAJ |title=河北青年 |publisher=Hebei Youth Magazine |year=1984 |page=5 |language=zh |trans-title=Hebei Youth |access-date=2024-08-30 |issue=nos. 1–9}} which eventually led to the development of the county's "semi-urban" strategy, leveraging its proximity to Shijiazhuang for diverse business growth.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pq8XEAAAQBAJ&pg=PT141 |title=决策论 |publisher=Beijing Book Co. Inc. |year=2018 |isbn=978-7-226-05307-2 |page=141 |language=zh |trans-title=Decision Theory |access-date=2024-08-30}}{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SxsaEQAAQBAJ&pg=PT17 |title=社会的逻辑 |publisher=Peking University Press |year=2017 |isbn=978-7-301-26916-9 |page=17 |language=zh |trans-title=The Logic of Society |access-date=2024-08-30}} In September 1984, during a briefing session chaired by He Zai, the secretary-general of the [[Organization Department of the Chinese Communist Party|CCP Central Organization Department]], Xi Jinping's strategic vision and comprehensive understanding of Zhengding County's development were highlighted.{{cite news |date=2018-06-15 |title=习近平与人民日报的10个故事 |trans-title=10 stories about Xi Jinping and People's Daily |url=https://opinion.haiwainet.cn/n/2018/0615/c456318-31335689.html |access-date=2024-08-30 |website=Haiwai Net |language=zh |archive-date=30 August 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240830150708/https://opinion.haiwainet.cn/n/2018/0615/c456318-31335689.html |url-status=live }} He Zai, along with [[Wei Jianxing]], deputy head of the CCP Central Organization Department, communicated these findings to [[Hu Yaobang]], describing Xi as a leader with a strategic outlook and a strong alliance ideology between workers and peasants.{{cite news |date=2023-11-17 |title=中共元老何載逝世 曾薦習近平「棟樑之才」 |trans-title=He Zai, the veteran of the CCP, passed away. He once recommended Xi Jinping as a "pillar of talent" |url=https://www.chinatimes.com/realtimenews/20231117002407-260409 |access-date=2024-08-30 |work=[[China Times]] |language=zh |archive-date=30 August 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240830150706/https://www.chinatimes.com/realtimenews/20231117002407-260409 |url-status=live }}{{cite news |date=2023-11-16 |title=104岁中共元老何载逝世 曾荐习近平"栋梁之才" |trans-title=He Zai, a 104-year-old CCP veteran, passed away. He once recommended Xi Jinping as a "pillar of talent" |url=https://www.singtao.ca/6452821/2023-11-16/news-104%E6%AD%B2%E4%B8%AD%E5%85%B1%E5%85%83%E8%80%81%E4%BD%95%E8%BC%89%E9%80%9D%E4%B8%96++%E6%9B%BE%E8%96%A6%E7%BF%92%E8%BF%91%E5%B9%B3%E3%80%8C%E6%A3%9F%E6%A8%91%E4%B9%8B%E6%89%8D%E3%80%8D/?variant=zh-cn&appedition=toronto |access-date=2024-08-30 |website=[[Sing Tao Daily (Canada)|Sing Tao Daily]] |language=zh |archive-date=30 August 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240830150707/https://www.singtao.ca/6452821/2023-11-16/news-104%E6%AD%B2%E4%B8%AD%E5%85%B1%E5%85%83%E8%80%81%E4%BD%95%E8%BC%89%E9%80%9D%E4%B8%96%20%20%E6%9B%BE%E8%96%A6%E7%BF%92%E8%BF%91%E5%B9%B3%E3%80%8C%E6%A3%9F%E6%A8%91%E4%B9%8B%E6%89%8D%E3%80%8D/?variant=zh-cn&appedition=toronto |url-status=live }} In 1985, Xi participated in a study tour on corn processing and traveled to [[Iowa]], the United States,{{cite book | last=Aoyagi | first=W.S.A. | title=History of Biodiesel – with Emphasis on Soy Biodiesel (1900–2017): Extensively Annotated Bibliography and Sourcebook | publisher=Soyinfo Center | year=2017 | isbn=978-1-928914-97-6 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FFU8DwAAQBAJ&pg=PA262 | access-date=2024-08-30 | page=262}} to study agricultural production and corn processing technology.{{cite news |date=2015-09-23 |title=˭谁能想到石家庄食品协会主席成了中国领导人 |trans-title=Who would have thought that the chairman of Shijiazhuang Food Association would become the Chinese leader? |url=https://news.cnr.cn/native/gd/20150923/t20150923_519945574.shtml |access-date=2024-08-30 |website=[[China National Radio]] |language=zh |archive-date=30 August 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240830150923/https://news.cnr.cn/native/gd/20150923/t20150923_519945574.shtml |url-status=live }} During his visit to the U.S., the CCP Central Organization Department decided to transfer him to [[Xiamen]] as a member of the Standing Committee of the CCP Xiamen Municipal Committee and as vice mayor. === Fujian === Arriving in Xiamen as vice-mayor in June 1985, Xi drafted the first strategic plan for the development of the city, the Xiamen Economic and Social Development Strategy for 1985–2000.{{cite book |author-last2=Zhang|author-first2=Qizi|author-last1=Huang|author-first1=Qunhui|display-authors=etal|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0dsEAAAQBAJ&pg=PP9 |title=厦门城市治理体系和治理能力现代化研究 |publisher=[[Social Sciences Literature Press]] |year=2016 |isbn=978-7-5097-9562-0 |series=A series of achievements of inter-academy cooperation of Chinese Academy of Social Sciences: Xiamen |page=9 |language=zh |trans-title=Research on the modernization of Xiamen's urban governance system and governance capacity |access-date=2024-09-19}} From August, Along with helping to prepare [[Xiamen Airlines]],{{cite news |date=2024-07-25 |title=白鹭振翅 向风而行 |trans-title=Egrets flap their wings and fly into the wind |url=http://www.news.cn/politics/20240725/cd4283ccd3464fda884c56e0645a2b44/c.html |access-date=2024-09-19 |website=[[Xinhua News Agency]] |language=zh |archive-date=24 September 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240924011244/http://www.news.cn/politics/20240725/cd4283ccd3464fda884c56e0645a2b44/c.html |url-status=live }} the Xiamen Economic Information Center,{{cite news |date=2023-05-26 |title=习近平同志推动厦门经济特区建设发展的探索与实践 |trans-title=Comrade Xi Jinping's exploration and practice in promoting the construction and development of Xiamen Special Economic Zone |url=https://www.gov.cn/xinwen/2018-06/23/content_5300679.htm |access-date=2024-09-19 |website=State Council of the People's Republic of China |language=zh}} and the Xiamen Special Administrative Region Road Project, etc., he oversaw the resolution for Yundang Lake's comprehensive treatments.{{cite news |date=2024-02-21 |title=时政长镜头丨筼筜回响 |trans-title=Long shot of current affairs丨Yuanlong Echo |url=https://www.stdaily.com/index/yaowen/202402/ba6fc81376314bb4b909bd7af8d64fd7.shtml |access-date=2024-09-19 |website=[[Science and Technology Daily]] |language=zh |archive-date=24 September 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240924011242/https://www.stdaily.com/index/yaowen/202402/ba6fc81376314bb4b909bd7af8d64fd7.shtml |url-status=live }} He married [[Peng Liyuan]] then in Xiamen.{{cite book | last=Kuhn | first=R.L. | title=How China's Leaders Think: The Inside Story of China's Past, Current and Future Leaders | publisher=Wiley | year=2011 | isbn=978-1-118-10425-5 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4j5UoOoQZCIC&pg=PA420 | access-date=2024-09-19 | page=420}}{{cite book | last=Dillon | first=M. | title=China in the Age of Xi Jinping | publisher=Taylor & Francis | year=2021 | isbn=978-1-000-37096-6 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-EEiEAAAQBAJ&pg=PT93 | access-date=2024-09-19 | page=93}} He started serving as the head of a region after being appointed just as the secretary of [[Ningde]] in September 1988.{{cite book | last=Brown | first=W.N. | title=Chasing the Chinese Dream: Four Decades of Following China's War on Poverty | publisher=Springer Nature Singapore | year=2021 | isbn=978-981-16-0654-0 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OzsxEAAAQBAJ&pg=PA9 | access-date=2024-09-19 | page=9}} Ningde's economy was far worse at that time than that of Fuzhou and Xiamen.{{cite book | last=Brown | first=W.N. | title=The Evolution of China's Anti-Poverty Strategies: Cases of 20 Chinese Changing Lives | publisher=Springer Nature Singapore | year=2022 | isbn=978-981-19-7281-2 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eVWbEAAAQBAJ&pg=PR6 | access-date=2024-09-19 | page=6}} Xi organized his work log and experience during his Ningde period into his book ''Getting out of Poverty'',{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=y5j-EAAAQBAJ&pg=PT74 |title=中国脱贫攻坚精神 |publisher=Huazhong University of Science and Technology Press |year=2021 |isbn=978-7-5680-6816-1 |page=74 |language=zh |trans-title=China's Spirit of Poverty Alleviation |access-date=2024-09-19}} and handled the local poverty eradicating efforts and local CCP building projects.{{cite book |author=Feng |first=Hexia |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D5n-EAAAQBAJ&pg=PT11 |title=中国的贫困治理 |publisher=[[Social Sciences Literature Press]] |year=2023 |isbn=978-7-5228-1509-1 |page=11 |language=zh |trans-title=Poverty Governance in China |access-date=2024-09-19}} The CCP Fujian Provincial Committee decided in May 1990 to assign Xi to [[Fuzhou City]] as the Municipal Committee Secretary.{{cite book | last=Lam | first=W. | title=Xi Jinping: The Hidden Agendas of China's Ruler for Life | publisher=Taylor & Francis | year=2023 | isbn=978-1-000-92583-8 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZELGEAAAQBAJ&pg=PA21 | access-date=2024-09-19 | page=21}} In 1997, he was named an alternate member of the [[15th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party|15th CCP Central Committee]]. In 1999, he was promoted to the office of Vice Governor of Fujian, and became governor a year later. Xi proposed the concept of the Golden Triangle at Min River ({{lang-zh|闽江口金三角经济圈}}) and oversaw the construction of the Fuzhou 3820 Project Master Plan, which outlines Fuzhou City's growth strategy for 3, 8, and 20 years.{{cite book | author=《福州年鉴》编辑委员会 | title=福州年鉴 | publisher=中国统计出版社 | year=1995 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3YmzAAAAIAAJ | language=zh | access-date=2024-11-07 | page=16}} He concentrated on the development of [[Changle International Airport]], the Min River Water Transfer Project, the Fuzhou Telecommunication Hub, and [[Fuzhou Port]], among others. He concentrated on attracting Taiwanese and foreign investment,{{cite news|url=https://thediplomat.com/2014/11/from-fujian-chinas-xi-offers-economic-olive-branch-to-taiwan/|title=From Fujian, China's Xi Offers Economic Olive Branch to Taiwan|last=Tiezzi|first=Shannon|date=4 November 2014|newspaper=[[The Diplomat (magazine)|The Diplomat]]|language=en-US|url-access=limited|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160610145941/https://thediplomat.com/2014/11/from-fujian-chinas-xi-offers-economic-olive-branch-to-taiwan/|archive-date=10 June 2016|access-date=17 March 2016}} establishing Southwest TPV Electronics and Southeast Automobile in Fuzhou, and fostering [[Fuyao Glass]], [[Newland Digital Technology]] and other manufacturing firms. Furthermore, he rehabilitated local cultural landmarks, including as the [[Sanfang Qixiang]] in Fuzhou, advanced urban renewal initiatives, and effectively addressed the issue of poverty alleviation on [[Pingtan Island]]. In 1995, Xi Jinping was elevated to deputy secretary of the [[Fujian Provincial Committee of the Chinese Communist Party]] and served as [[Governor of Fujian]] from 1999 to 2002, during which he presented the notion of "[[Megalopolises in China|Megalopolises]]" and advocated for the inter-island growth strategy of Fuzhou and Xiamen, which motivated local officials to swiftly overcome the repercussions of the [[Lai Changxing|Yuanhua smuggling case]] ({{lang-zh|远华走私案}}) and adopt a new development strategy.{{cite book | title=驼铃: 大众文学| publisher=驼铃杂志社 | issue=172–177 | year=2000 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7No2AAAAIAAJ | language=zh | access-date=2024-11-07 | page=45}} Xi also oversaw the development of "Digital Fujian", including the province's complaint hotline into the "[[12345 hotline|12345 Citizen Service Platform]]", so enhancing organizational efficiency.{{cite book | last=Chan | first=A.L. | title=Xi Jinping: Political Career, Governance, and Leadership, 1953–2018 | publisher=Oxford University Press | year=2022 | isbn=978-0-19-761522-5 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HH1ZEAAAQBAJ&pg=PA547 | access-date=2024-11-07 | page=547}} === Zhejiang === In 2002, Xi left Fujian and took up leading political positions in neighbouring Zhejiang. He eventually took over as [[Fujian Provincial Committee of the Chinese Communist Party|provincial Party Committee]] secretary after several months as acting governor, occupying a top provincial office for the first time in his career. In 2002, he was elected a full member of the [[16th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party|16th Central Committee]], marking his ascension to the national stage. While in Zhejiang, Xi presided over reported growth rates averaging 14% per year.{{Cite news|url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/article/1068825/chinas-leadership-transition-xi-jinpings-time-zhejiang|title=Xi Jinping's time in Zhejiang: doing the business|last=Ho|first=Louise|date=25 October 2012|newspaper=[[South China Morning Post]]|language=en|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160429160349/http://www.scmp.com/news/china/article/1068825/chinas-leadership-transition-xi-jinpings-time-zhejiang|archive-date=29 April 2016|access-date=9 December 2019}} During this period, Zhejiang increasingly transitioned away from heavy industry.{{Cite book |last=Chatwin |first=Jonathan |title=The Southern Tour: Deng Xiaoping and the Fight for China's Future |publisher=[[Bloomsbury Academic]] |year=2024 |isbn=978-1-350-43571-1 |pages=}}{{Rp|page=121}} Xi's career in Zhejiang was marked by a tough and straightforward stance against corrupt officials. This earned him a name in the national media and drew the attention of China's top leaders.{{cite news|url=http://opinion.dwnews.com/news/2014-12-25/59626005.html |script-title=zh:习近平为官之道 拎着乌纱帽干事|last1=Wang|first1=Lei|date=25 December 2014|work=[[Duowei News]]|access-date=7 September 2019|language=zh-hans|trans-title=Xi Jinping's Governmental Path – Carries Official Administrative Posts|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150203225014/http://opinion.dwnews.com/news/2014-12-25/59626005.html|archive-date=3 February 2015}} Between 2004 and 2007, [[Li Qiang]] acted as Xi's chief of staff through his position as secretary-general of the [[Zhejiang Provincial Committee of the Chinese Communist Party|Zhejiang Party Committee]], where they developed close mutual ties.{{Cite news |last1=Tian |first1=Yew Lun |last2=Chen |first2=Laurie |last3=Cash |first3=Joe |date=11 March 2023 |title=Li Qiang, Xi confidant, takes reins as China's premier |work=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/li-qiang-xi-confidant-takes-reins-chinas-premier-2023-03-11/ |access-date=24 May 2023 |archive-date=24 May 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230524163326/https://www.reuters.com/world/china/li-qiang-xi-confidant-takes-reins-chinas-premier-2023-03-11/ |url-status=live}} During this period, Xi and Li drafted the [[Double Eight Strategy]], which listed eight comparative advantages of Zhejiang and eight corresponding actions to improve the province.{{Cite news |last1=Zheng |first1=William |last2=Ren |first2=Daniel |date=23 October 2022 |title=China's Li Qiang shakes off Shanghai Covid chaos to enter Communist Party inner circle |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3196959/chinas-li-qiang-shakes-shanghai-covid-chaos-enter-communist-party-inner-circle |access-date=27 June 2025 |work=[[South China Morning Post]]}} === Shanghai === Following the dismissal of [[Party Secretary of Shanghai|Shanghai Party secretary]] [[Chen Liangyu]] in September 2006 due to a [[Shanghai pension scandal|social security fund scandal]], Xi was transferred to Shanghai in March 2007, where he was the party secretary there for seven months.{{cite news|url=http://news.sohu.com/20070324/n248946143.shtml|script-title=zh:习近平任上海市委书记 韩正不再代理市委书记|date=24 March 2007|work=[[Sohu]]|access-date=7 September 2019|language=zh-Hans|trans-title=Xi Jinping is Secretary of Shanghai Municipal Party Committee – Han Zheng is No Longer Acting Party Secretary|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071016061557/http://news.sohu.com/20070324/n248946143.shtml|archive-date=16 October 2007|url-status=live}}{{Cite news |date=15 November 2012 |title=China new leaders: Xi Jinping heads line-up for politburo |language=en-GB |work=[[BBC News]] |publisher= |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-20322288 |url-status=live |access-date=25 August 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190909102154/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-20322288 |archive-date=9 September 2019}} While in Shanghai, he worked on preserving unity of the local party organisation. He pledged there would be no 'purges' during his administration, despite the fact many local officials were thought to have been implicated in the Chen Liangyu corruption scandal.{{sfn|Lam|2015|p=56}} In Shanghai, Xi avoided controversy and was known for strictly observing party discipline. For example, Shanghai administrators attempted to earn favour with him by arranging a special train to shuttle him between Shanghai and [[Hangzhou]] for him to complete handing off his work to his successor as Zhejiang party secretary [[Zhao Hongzhu]]. However, Xi reportedly refused to take the train, citing a loosely enforced party regulation that stipulated that special trains can only be reserved for "national leaders".{{cite news|url=http://www.boxun.com/news/gb/china/2013/07/201307110449.shtml#.VTxXVvnF_tU|script-title=zh:从上海到北京 习近平贴身秘书只有钟绍军|date=11 July 2013|work=[[Mingjing News]]|access-date=12 September 2019|language=zh-hans|trans-title=From Shanghai to Beijing, Zhong Shaojun Has Been Xi Jinping's Only Personal Secretary|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180612141343/http://www.boxun.com/news/gb/china/2013/07/201307110449.shtml#.VTxXVvnF_tU|archive-date=12 June 2018|url-status=live}} On most issues, Xi largely echoed the line of the central leadership.{{cite news|url=http://www.caijing.com.cn/2007-10-22/100034588.html |script-title=zh:新晋政治局常委: 习近平|date=22 October 2007|work=[[Caijing]]|access-date=12 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151222175301/http://www.caijing.com.cn/2007-10-22/100034588.html|archive-date=22 December 2015|language=zh-hans|trans-title=Newly Appointed Member of Politburo Standing Committee: Xi Jinping}} He developed a reputation in his early career for avoiding controversy and not making political opponents.{{Cite book |last=Borst |first=Nicholas |title=The Bird and the Cage: China's Economic Contradictions |date=2025 |publisher=[[Palgrave Macmillan]] |isbn=978-981-96-3996-0 |location=Singapore}}{{Reference page|page=75}} ==Politburo Standing Committee== [[File:President George W. Bush with Vice President Xi Jinping.jpg|thumb|Xi Jinping greeting U.S. president [[George W. Bush]] in August 2008|left]] [[File:Dmitry Medvedev in China 28 September 2010-6.jpeg|thumb|Xi Jinping with Russian president [[Dmitry Medvedev]] on 28 September 2010|left]] Xi was appointed to the nine-man PSC after the [[17th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party|17th Party Congress]] in October 2007. He was ranked above [[Li Keqiang]], an indication that he was going to succeed Hu Jintao as China's [[Succession of power in China|next leader]]. In addition, Xi served in the CCP's [[Secretariat of the Chinese Communist Party|Central Secretariat]]. This assessment was further supported at the first session of the [[11th National People's Congress]] in March 2008, when Xi was elected as vice president of China.{{Cite news |date=16 March 2008 |title=Wen Jiabao re-elected China PM |url=https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2008/3/16/wen-jiabao-re-elected-china-pm |access-date=13 August 2022 |website=[[Al Jazeera English|Al Jazeera]] |language=en |archive-date=13 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813171805/https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2008/3/16/wen-jiabao-re-elected-china-pm |url-status=live}} Following his elevation, Xi held a broad range of portfolios. He was put in charge of the comprehensive preparations for the [[2008 Summer Olympics]] in Beijing, as well as being the central government's leading figure in Hong Kong and Macau affairs. In addition, he also became the [[president of the Central Party School]], the CCP's icadre-training and ideological education school. In the wake of the [[2008 Sichuan earthquake]], Xi visited disaster areas in [[Shaanxi]] and [[Gansu]]. After the Olympics, Xi was assigned the post of committee chair for the preparations of the [[60th anniversary of the People's Republic of China|60th Anniversary Celebrations]] of the founding of the PRC. He was also reportedly at the helm of a top-level CCP committee dubbed the [[6521 Project]], which was charged with ensuring social stability during a series of politically sensitive anniversaries in 2009.{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2009/03/10/world/asia/10china.html|title=China's Leaders See a Calendar Full of Anniversaries, and Trouble|last=Wines|first=Michael|date=9 March 2009|work=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=8 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160721045913/http://www.nytimes.com/2009/03/10/world/asia/10china.html|archive-date=21 July 2016|language=en-US|issn=0362-4331|url-access=limited}} Xi's position as the apparent successor to become the paramount leader was threatened with the rapid rise of [[Bo Xilai]], the party secretary of [[Chongqing]] at the time. Bo was expected to join the PSC after the [[18th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party|18th Party Congress]], with most expecting that he would try to eventually maneuver himself into replacing Xi.{{Cite news|url=https://www.ft.com/content/d67b90f0-d140-11e1-8957-00144feabdc0|archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221211221204/https://www.ft.com/content/d67b90f0-d140-11e1-8957-00144feabdc0|archive-date=11 December 2022|url-access=subscription|url-status=live|title=Bo Xilai: power, death and politics|last=Anderlini|first=Jamil|date=20 July 2012|work=[[Financial Times]]|access-date=11 January 2020}} Bo's policies in Chongqing inspired imitations throughout China and received praise from Xi himself during Xi's visit to Chongqing in 2010. Records of praises from Xi were later erased after he became paramount leader. Bo's downfall would come with the [[Wang Lijun incident]], which opened the door for Xi to come to power without challengers.{{Cite news|url=https://foreignpolicy.com/2017/10/19/the-resistible-rise-of-xi-jinping/|title=The Resistible Rise of Xi Jinping|last=Palmer|first=James|date=19 October 2017|work=[[Foreign Policy (magazine)|Foreign Policy]]|access-date=11 January 2020|archive-date=6 February 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200206195505/https://foreignpolicy.com/2017/10/19/the-resistible-rise-of-xi-jinping/|url-status=live}} On 18 October 2010, at the fifth plenary session of the 17th Central Committee, Xi was elected to be a vice chairman of the CCP Central Military Commission,{{Cite news |last=Branigan |first=Tania |date=2010-10-18 |title=Chinese vice-president Xi Jinping appointed to key military post |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2010/oct/18/china-xi-jinping-military-commission |access-date=2025-10-27 |work=[[The Guardian]] |language=en-GB |issn=0261-3077}} followed by his appointment as vice chairman of the state CMC on 28 October by the Standing Committee of the National People's Congress.{{Cite news |date=28 October 2010 |title=全国人大常委会表决决定习近平为中华人民共和国中央军事委员会副主席 |trans-title=The Standing Committee of the National People's Congress voted to appoint Xi Jinping as Vice Chairman of the Central Military Commission of the People's Republic of China |url=http://news.xinhuanet.com/politics/2010-10/28/c_12712309.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101201023918/http://news.xinhuanet.com/politics/2010-10/28/c_12712309.htm |archive-date=1 December 2010 |access-date=27 October 2025 |work=[[Xinhua News Agency]]}} Xi is considered one of the most successful members of the [[Princelings]], a quasi-clique of politicians who are descendants of early Chinese Communist revolutionaries. Former prime minister of Singapore, [[Lee Kuan Yew]], when asked about Xi, said he felt he was "a thoughtful man who has gone through many trials and tribulations."{{Cite news|url=https://www.newsweek.com/xi-jinping-chinas-new-boss-and-l-word-95021|title=Xi Jinping: China's New Boss And The 'L' Word|last=Ansfield|first=Jonathan|date=22 December 2007|work=[[Newsweek]]|access-date=20 October 2010|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100330064319/http://www.newsweek.com/id/81558|archive-date=30 March 2010}} Lee also commented: "I would put him in the [[Nelson Mandela]] class of persons. A person with enormous emotional stability who does not allow his personal misfortunes or sufferings affect his judgment. In other words, he is impressive."{{cite news|url=https://world.time.com/2007/11/19/chinas_nelson_mandela/|title=China's Nelson Mandela|last=Elegant|first=Simon|date=19 November 2007|magazine=[[Time (magazine)|Time]]|access-date=8 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090728171010/http://china.blogs.time.com/2007/11/19/chinas_nelson_mandela/|archive-date=28 July 2009|url-status=live}} Former U.S. Treasury Secretary [[Henry Paulson]] described Xi as "the kind of guy who knows how to get things over the goal line."{{cite news|url=https://www.bloomberg.com/apps/news?pid=20601087&sid=aDpLoVnlKn6E&refer=home|title=China Appoints Xi Vice President, Heir Apparent to Hu|last=Elegant|first=Simon|date=15 March 2008|newspaper=[[Bloomberg News]]|url-access=limited|access-date=20 October 2010|archive-date=21 October 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121021142400/https://www.bloomberg.com/apps/news?pid=20601087&sid=aDpLoVnlKn6E&refer=home|url-status=live}} Australian prime minister [[Kevin Rudd]] said that Xi "has sufficient reformist, party and military background to be very much his own man."{{cite news|url=http://www.theaustralian.com.au/national-affairs/rudd-seeks-to-pre-empt-pms-china-white-paper-with-his-own-version/story-fn59niix-1226488505695|title=Rudd seeks to pre-empt PM's China white paper with his own version|last=Uren|first=David|date=5 October 2012|work=[[The Australian]]|access-date=8 September 2019|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121122083821/http://www.theaustralian.com.au/national-affairs/rudd-seeks-to-pre-empt-pms-china-white-paper-with-his-own-version/story-fn59niix-1226488505695|archive-date=22 November 2012}} ===Trips as Vice President=== Xi made his first foreign trip as vice president to North Korea, Mongolia, Saudi Arabia, Qatar and Yemen from 17 to 25 June 2008.{{cite web |date=5 June 2008 |title=Vice-President Xi Jinping to Visit DPRK, Mongolia, Saudi Arabia, Qatar and Yemen |url=http://www.mfa.gov.cn/eng/wjdt/wsrc/t462294.htm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723214209/http://www.mfa.gov.cn/eng/wjdt/wsrc/t462294.htm |archive-date=23 July 2011 |access-date=20 October 2010 |website=[[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (China)|Ministry of Foreign Affairs]] |publisher=}} In February 2009, in his capacity as vice president, Xi embarked on a tour of Latin America, visiting Mexico, Jamaica,{{cite news |date=15 February 2009 |title=Chinese VP Receives Key to the City of Montego Bay |publisher=Jamaica Information Service |url=http://jis.gov.jm/chinese-vp-receives-key-to-the-city-of-montego-bay/ |url-status=live |access-date=7 September 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190714065907/https://jis.gov.jm/chinese-vp-receives-key-to-the-city-of-montego-bay/ |archive-date=14 July 2019}} Colombia, Venezuela,{{cite web|url=http://www.cctv.com/english/20090218/112381.shtml|title=Chinese VP praises friendly cooperation with Venezuela, Latin America|agency=Xinhua|date=18 February 2009|work=CCTV|access-date=20 October 2022|archive-date=21 October 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221021011922/http://www.cctv.com/english/20090218/112381.shtml|url-status=live}} Brazil,{{cite web|url=http://www.china.org.cn/international/2009-02/20/content_17311463.htm|title=Xi Jinping proposes efforts to boost cooperation with Brazil |date=20 February 2009|website=[[China Daily]]|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110206063031/http://china.org.cn/international/2009-02/20/content_17311463.htm|archive-date=6 February 2011|access-date=11 September 2019}} and Malta, after which he returned to China.{{Cite news|url=https://timesofmalta.com/articles/view/chinese-vice-president-begins-official-visit.245968|title=Chinese vice president begins official visit|date=22 February 2009|newspaper=[[Times of Malta]]|language=en-gb|access-date=11 September 2019|archive-date=3 February 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200203050934/https://timesofmalta.com/articles/view/chinese-vice-president-begins-official-visit.245968|url-status=live}} On 11 February 2009, while visiting Mexico, Xi spoke in front of a group of [[overseas Chinese]] and explained China's contributions during the international financial crisis, saying that it was "the greatest contribution towards the whole of human race, made by China, to prevent its 1.37 billion people from hunger."{{efn|Original {{lang-zh|s=在国际金融风暴中, 中国能基本解决13亿人口吃饭的问题, 已经是对全人类最伟大的贡献|t=在國際金融風暴中, 中國能基本解決13億人口吃飯的問題, 已經是對全人類最偉大的貢獻}}|}} He went on to remark: "There are some bored foreigners, with full stomachs, who have nothing better to do than point fingers at us. First, China doesn't export revolution; second, China doesn't export hunger and poverty; third, China doesn't come and cause you headaches. What more is there to be said?"{{efn|Original: {{lang-zh|s=有些吃饱没事干的外国人, 对我们的事情指手画脚.中国一不输出革命, 二不输出饥饿和贫困, 三不折腾你们, 还有什么好说的?|t=有些吃飽沒事干的外國人, 對我們的事情指手畫腳.中國一不輸出革命, 二不輸出飢餓和貧困, 三不折騰你們, 還有什麽好說的?}}}}{{cite news|url=http://news.asiaone.com/News/the%2BStraits%2BTimes/Story/A1Story20090214-121872.html|title=Chinese VP blasts meddlesome foreigners|last=Sim|first=Chi Yin|date=14 February 2009|newspaper=[[AsiaOne]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110711171035/http://news.asiaone.com/News/the%2BStraits%2BTimes/Story/A1Story20090214-121872.html|archive-date=11 July 2011|access-date=20 October 2010}} The story was reported on some local television stations. The news led to a flood of discussions on Chinese Internet forums and it was reported that the Chinese Ministry of Foreign Affairs was caught off-guard by Xi's remarks, as the actual video was shot by some accompanying Hong Kong reporters and broadcast on Hong Kong TV, which then turned up on various Internet video websites.{{Cite news|url=http://udn.com/NEWS/MAINLAND/MAI1/4742366.shtml |script-title=zh:習近平出訪罵老外 外交部捏冷汗|last1=Lai|first1=Jinhong|date=18 February 2009|newspaper=[[United Daily News]]|access-date=27 February 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221023838/http://udn.com/NEWS/MAINLAND/MAI1/4742366.shtml|archive-date=21 February 2009|language=zh-hans|trans-title=Xi Jinping Goes and Scolds at Foreigners, Ministry of Foreign Affairs in Cold Sweat}} [[File:Xi Jinping Iowa house.jpg|thumb|right|The Muscatine, Iowa house where Xi stayed with an American family during a 1985 agricultural visit to the United States. The home was turned into a museum decades later.]] In the [[European Union]], Xi visited Belgium, Germany, Bulgaria, Hungary and Romania from 7 to 21 October 2009.{{cite web|url=http://www.china-un.ch/eng/xwdt/t622138.htm|title=A Journey of Friendship, Cooperation and Culture – Vice Foreign Minister Zhang Zhijun Sums Up Chinese Vice President Xi Jinping's Trip to 5 European Countries|date=21 October 2009|publisher=[[Permanent Representative of China to the United Nations]]|access-date=11 September 2019|archive-date=3 February 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200203050934/http://www.china-un.ch/eng/xwdt/t622138.htm|url-status=live}} He visited Japan, South Korea, Cambodia, and Myanmar on his Asian trip from 14 to 22 December 2009.{{cite web|url=http://www.southasiaanalysis.org/papers36/paper3566.html|title=China's Cousin-Cousin Relations with Myanmar|last=Raman|first=B.|date=25 December 2009|publisher=South Asia Analysis Group|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100317074826/http://www.southasiaanalysis.org/papers36/paper3566.html|archive-date=17 March 2010|url-status=usurped|access-date=14 February 2012}} He later visited the United States, Ireland and Turkey in February 2012. This visit included meeting with then U.S. president [[Barack Obama]] at the White House and vice president [[Joe Biden]] (with Biden as the official host);{{Cite news |last1=Bull |first1=Alister |author2=Chris Buckley |date=24 January 2012 |title=China leader-in-waiting Xi to visit White House next month |work=[[Reuters]] |publisher= |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-china-xi-idUSTRE80N06E20120124 |access-date=28 October 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029201614/https://www.reuters.com/article/2012/01/24/us-usa-china-xi-idUSTRE80N06E20120124 |archive-date=29 October 2013}} and stops in California and [[Iowa]]. In Iowa, he met with the family that previously hosted him during his 1985 tour as a [[Hebei]] provincial official.{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2012/02/16/world/asia/xi-jinping-of-china-makes-a-return-trip-to-iowa.html|title=Xi Jinping of China Makes a Return Trip to Iowa|last=Johnson|first=Kirk|date=15 February 2012|work=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=12 September 2019|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190621114147/https://www.nytimes.com/2012/02/16/world/asia/xi-jinping-of-china-makes-a-return-trip-to-iowa.html|archive-date=21 June 2019|language=en-US|issn=0362-4331|url-access=limited}} ===Accession to top posts=== {{See also|Generations of Chinese leadership|Succession of power in China}} A few months before his ascendancy to the party leadership, Xi disappeared from official media coverage and cancelled meetings with foreign officials for several weeks beginning on 1 September 2012, causing rumors. He then reappeared on 15 September.{{Cite magazine |last=Beech |first=Hannah |date=15 September 2012 |title=China's Heir Apparent Xi Jinping Reappears in Public After a Two-Week Absence |url=https://world.time.com/2012/09/15/chinas-heir-apparent-xi-jinping-reappears-in-public-after-a-two-week-absence/ |magazine=[[Time (magazine)|Time]] |language=en-US |issn=0040-781X |access-date=20 August 2022 |archive-date=14 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220814104509/https://world.time.com/2012/09/15/chinas-heir-apparent-xi-jinping-reappears-in-public-after-a-two-week-absence/ |url-status=live}} On 15 November 2012, Xi was elected to the posts of general secretary of the CCP and chairman of the CMC by the [[18th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party|18th Central Committee of the CCP]]. This made him, formally the [[Leader of the Chinese Communist Party|CCP leader]], and informally, the [[paramount leader]] and also the first to be born after the founding of the PRC. The following day Xi led the new line-up of the PSC onto the stage in their first public appearance.{{cite news |date=17 November 2012 |title=China Confirms Leadership Change |work=[[BBC News]] |publisher= |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-china-20322288 |access-date=15 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160729201558/http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-china-20322288 |archive-date=29 July 2016}} The PSC was reduced from nine to seven, with Xi and Li Keqiang retaining their seats; the other five members were new.{{Cite news |date=15 November 2012 |title=Xi Jinping: China's 'princeling' new leader |newspaper=[[Hindustan Times]] |url=https://www.hindustantimes.com/world/xi-jinping-china-s-princeling-new-leader/story-uKjI60ipqpRSEew3Zi7jzL.html |url-status=live |access-date=28 October 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181028230023/https://www.hindustantimes.com/world/xi-jinping-china-s-princeling-new-leader/story-uKjI60ipqpRSEew3Zi7jzL.html |archive-date=28 October 2018}}{{cite news |last1=Wong |first1=Edward |author-link=Edward Wong |date=14 November 2012 |title=Ending Congress, China Presents New Leadership Headed by Xi Jinping |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2012/11/15/world/asia/communists-conclude-party-congress-in-china.html?ref=asia&_r=0 |url-status=live |url-access=limited |access-date=16 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121208103006/http://www.nytimes.com/2012/11/15/world/asia/communists-conclude-party-congress-in-china.html?ref=asia&_r=0 |archive-date=8 December 2012}}{{cite news |last1=FlorCruz |first1=Jaime A |last2=Mullen |first2=Jethro |date=16 November 2012 |title=After months of mystery, China unveils new top leaders |work=[[CNN]] |publisher= |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2012/11/14/world/asia/china-leadership-transition/index.html |url-status=live |access-date=16 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121115195006/http://edition.cnn.com/2012/11/14/world/asia/china-leadership-transition/index.html |archive-date=15 November 2012}} In a marked departure from the common practice of Chinese leaders, Xi's first speech as general secretary was plainly worded and did not include any political slogans or mention his predecessors.{{cite news |last1=Johnson |first1=Ian |date=15 November 2012 |title=A Promise to Tackle China's Problems, but Few Hints of a Shift in Path |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2012/11/16/world/asia/new-chinese-leader-offers-few-hints-of-a-shift-in-direction.html?pagewanted=all |url-status=live |url-access=limited |access-date=16 July 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140726121506/http://www.nytimes.com/2012/11/16/world/asia/new-chinese-leader-offers-few-hints-of-a-shift-in-direction.html?pagewanted=all |archive-date=26 July 2014}} Xi mentioned the aspirations of the average person, remarking, "Our people ... expect better education, more stable jobs, better income, more reliable social security, medical care of a higher standard, more comfortable living conditions, and a more beautiful environment." Xi also vowed to tackle corruption at the highest levels, alluding that it would threaten the CCP's survival; he was reticent about far-reaching economic reforms.{{cite news |date=15 November 2012 |title=Full text: China's new party chief Xi Jinping's speech |work=[[BBC News]] |publisher= |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-20338586 |url-status=live |access-date=7 September 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190914232334/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-20338586 |archive-date=14 September 2019}} In December 2012, Xi visited [[Guangdong]] in his first trip outside Beijing since taking the general secretaryship. The overarching theme of the trip was to call for further economic reform and a strengthened military. Xi visited the statue of Deng Xiaoping and his trip was described as following in the footsteps of [[Deng Xiaoping's southern tour|Deng's own southern trip in 1992]], which provided the impetus for further economic reforms in China after conservative party leaders stalled many of Deng's reforms in the aftermath of the [[1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre]]. On his trip, Xi consistently alluded to his signature slogan, the "Chinese Dream". "This dream can be said to be the dream of a strong nation. And for the military, it is a dream of a strong military," Xi told sailors.{{cite news |last=Page |first=Jeremy |date=13 March 2013 |title=New Beijing Leader's 'China Dream' |newspaper=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/SB10001424127887324128504578348774040546346 |url-status=live |access-date=8 September 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191011195356/https://www.wsj.com/articles/SB10001424127887324128504578348774040546346 |archive-date=11 October 2019}} Xi's trip was significant in that he departed from the established convention of Chinese leaders' travel routines in multiple ways. Rather than dining out, Xi and his entourage ate regular hotel buffet. He travelled in a large van with his colleagues rather than a fleet of limousines, and did not restrict traffic on the parts of the highway he travelled.{{cite news |last1=Chen |first1=Zhuang |date=10 December 2012 |title=The symbolism of Xi Jinping's trip south |work=[[BBC News]] |publisher= |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-20662947 |url-status=live |access-date=22 July 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140708094339/http://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-20662947 |archive-date=8 July 2014}} Xi was elected president on 14 March 2013, in a confirmation vote by the [[12th National People's Congress]] in Beijing. He received 2,952 for, one vote against, and three abstentions. He replaced Hu Jintao, who retired after serving two terms.{{cite news |last=Demick |first=Barbara |author-link=Barbara Demick |date=3 March 2013 |title=China's Xi Jinping formally assumes title of president |newspaper=[[Los Angeles Times]] |url=http://www.latimes.com/news/world/worldnow/la-fg-wn-china-xi-presidency-20130313,0,1240600.story |url-status=live |access-date=16 March 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130316021753/http://www.latimes.com/news/world/worldnow/la-fg-wn-china-xi-presidency-20130313,0,1240600.story |archive-date=16 March 2013}} He subsequently nominated Li Keqiang to be the premier, who was then appointed by the NPC.{{cite news |date=15 March 2013 |title=China confirms Li Keqiang as premier |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-china-21796641 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130315212159/http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-china-21796641 |archive-date=15 March 2013 |access-date=15 March 2013 |work=[[BBC News]]}} ==General Secretaryship== {{Main|General secretaryship of Xi Jinping}} ===Consolidation of power=== {{See also|Xi Jinping faction}}Political observers have called Xi the most powerful Chinese leader since Chairman [[Mao Zedong]], especially since the ending of presidential two-term limits in 2018.{{Cite news |last=Phillips |first=Tom |date=24 October 2017 |title=Xi Jinping becomes most powerful leader since Mao with China's change to constitution |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2017/oct/24/xi-jinping-mao-thought-on-socialism-china-constitution |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171024053607/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2017/oct/24/xi-jinping-mao-thought-on-socialism-china-constitution |archive-date=24 October 2017 |access-date=24 October 2017 |work=[[The Guardian]] |issn=0261-3077}}{{cite news |date=24 October 2017 |title=China elevates Xi to most powerful leader in decades |url=http://www.cbc.ca/news/world/china-congress-xi-jinping-1.4368588 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180501062515/http://www.cbc.ca/news/world/china-congress-xi-jinping-1.4368588 |archive-date=1 May 2018 |access-date=22 June 2018 |work=[[CBC News]] |publisher=}}{{cite news |date=24 October 2017 |title=China elevates Xi Jinping's status, making him the most powerful leader since Mao |url=https://www.independent.ie/world-news/china-elevates-xi-jinpings-status-making-him-the-most-powerful-leader-since-mao-36256294.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180622220327/https://www.independent.ie/world-news/china-elevates-xi-jinpings-status-making-him-the-most-powerful-leader-since-mao-36256294.html |archive-date=22 June 2018 |access-date=22 June 2018 |work=[[Irish Independent]]}}{{cite news |last1=Collins |first1=Stephen |date=9 November 2017 |title=Xi's up, Trump is down, but it may not matter |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2017/11/08/politics/president-donald-trump-xi-jinping-china/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180623004701/https://edition.cnn.com/2017/11/08/politics/president-donald-trump-xi-jinping-china/index.html |archive-date=23 June 2018 |access-date=22 June 2018 |work=[[CNN]] |publisher=}} Xi has departed from the [[collective leadership]] practices of post-Mao predecessors. He has centralised his power and created working groups with himself at the head to subvert government bureaucracy, making himself become the unmistakable central figure of the administration.{{cite news |last1=Holtz |first1=Michael |date=28 February 2018 |title=Xi for life? China turns its back on collective leadership. |url=https://www.csmonitor.com/World/Asia-Pacific/2018/0228/Xi-for-life-China-turns-its-back-on-collective-leadership |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180624035940/https://www.csmonitor.com/World/Asia-Pacific/2018/0228/Xi-for-life-China-turns-its-back-on-collective-leadership |archive-date=24 June 2018 |access-date=23 June 2018 |work=[[The Christian Science Monitor]]}} In the opinion of at least one political scientist, Xi "has surrounded himself with cadres he met while stationed on the coast, Fujian and [[Shanghai]] and in Zhejiang."{{cite book |last1=Andrésy |first1=Agnès |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=F6jTCgAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 |title=Xi Jinping: Red China, The Next Generation |date=2015 |publisher=UPA |isbn=978-0-7618-6601-5 |page=88 |access-date=22 August 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240711081521/https://books.google.com/books?id=F6jTCgAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 |archive-date=11 July 2024 |url-status=live}} Observers have said that Xi has seriously diluted the influence of the once-dominant "[[Tuanpai]]", also called the Youth League Faction, which were CCP officials who rose through the Communist Youth League (CYLC).{{Cite news |last=Pollard |first=Martin |date=26 October 2022 |title=China's Xi deals knockout blow to once-powerful Youth League faction |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/chinas-xi-deals-knockout-blow-once-powerful-youth-league-faction-2022-10-26/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230524163327/https://www.reuters.com/world/china/chinas-xi-deals-knockout-blow-once-powerful-youth-league-faction-2022-10-26/ |archive-date=24 May 2023 |access-date=24 May 2023 |work=[[Reuters]]}} He criticized the cadres of the CYLC, saying that "[these cadres] can't talk about science, literature and art, work or life [with young people]. All they can do is just repeat the same old bureaucratic, stereotypical talk."{{cite news |last=Gan |first=Nectar |date=23 September 2017 |title=Latest Xi Jinping book gives clues on decline of Communist Party's youth wing |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/policies-politics/article/2112461/latest-xi-jinping-book-gives-clues-decline-communist?mc_cid=1f9bc1ec42&mc_eid=1f9d28130a |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171014163041/http://www.scmp.com/news/china/policies-politics/article/2112461/latest-xi-jinping-book-gives-clues-decline-communist?mc_cid=1f9bc1ec42&mc_eid=1f9d28130a |archive-date=14 October 2017 |access-date=8 October 2017 |newspaper=[[South China Morning Post]]}} In 2018, the [[National People's Congress]] (NPC) passed [[2018 amendment to the Constitution of China|constitutional amendments]] including removal of term limits for the president and vice president, the creation of a [[National Supervisory Commission]], as well as enhancing the central role of the CCP.{{Cite news |last1=Shi |first1=Jiangtao |last2=Huang |first2=Kristin |date=26 February 2018 |title=End to term limits at top 'may be start of global backlash for China' |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy-defence/article/2134791/end-term-limits-top-may-be-start-global-backlash-china |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180227155138/http://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy-defence/article/2134791/end-term-limits-top-may-be-start-global-backlash-china |archive-date=27 February 2018 |access-date=28 February 2018 |work=[[South China Morning Post]]}}{{cite news |last=Phillips |first=Tom |date=4 March 2018 |title=Xi Jinping's power play: from president to China's new dictator? |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2018/mar/04/xi-jinping-from-president-to-china-new-dictator |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180304005848/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2018/mar/04/xi-jinping-from-president-to-china-new-dictator |archive-date=4 March 2018 |access-date=4 March 2018 |website=[[The Guardian]]}} Xi was reappointed as president, now without term limits,{{cite news |last=Wen |first=Philip |date=17 March 2018 |title=China's parliament re-elects Xi Jinping as president |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-parliament-xi/chinas-parliament-re-elects-xi-jinping-as-president-idUSKCN1GT03C |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180317042406/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-parliament-xi/chinas-parliament-re-elects-xi-jinping-as-president-idUSKCN1GT03C |archive-date=17 March 2018 |access-date=17 March 2018 |publisher=[[Reuters]]}}{{cite news |last1=Bodeen |first1=Christopher |date=17 March 2018 |title=Xi reappointed as China's president with no term limits |url=https://apnews.com/78ea5b0646564a6eb98d9d20d5fbcbb6 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180317190611/https://apnews.com/78ea5b0646564a6eb98d9d20d5fbcbb6 |archive-date=17 March 2018 |access-date=17 March 2018 |publisher=[[Associated Press]]}} while Li Keqiang was reappointed premier.{{cite news |last1=Zhou |first1=Xin |date=18 March 2018 |title=Li Keqiang endorsed as China's premier; military leaders confirmed |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/economy/article/2137707/li-keqiang-endorsed-chinas-premier-while-military-commission |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190827155325/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/economy/article/2137707/li-keqiang-endorsed-chinas-premier-while-military-commission |archive-date=27 August 2019 |access-date=7 September 2019 |newspaper=[[South China Morning Post]]}} According to the ''[[Financial Times]]'', Xi expressed his views of constitutional amendment at meetings with Chinese officials and foreign dignitaries. Xi explained the decision in terms of needing to align two more powerful posts—general secretary of the CCP and chairman of the CMC—which have no term limits. However, Xi did not say whether he intended to be party general secretary, CMC chairman and state president, for three or more terms.{{cite news |last1=Mitchell |first1=Tom |date=7 September 2019 |title=China's Xi Jinping says he is opposed to life-long rule |url=https://www.ft.com/content/2b449400-413a-11e8-803a-295c97e6fd0b |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180417192157/https://www.ft.com/content/2b449400-413a-11e8-803a-295c97e6fd0b |archive-date=17 April 2018 |access-date=17 April 2018 |newspaper=[[Financial Times]] |quote=President insists term extension is necessary to align government and party posts}} In its sixth plenary session in November 2021, CCP adopted the ''[[Resolution on the Major Achievements and Historical Experience of the Party over the Past Century]]'', a kind of document that evaluated the party's history. This was the third of historical resolution after ones adopted by Mao Zedong and Deng Xiaoping.{{Cite web |last=Wang |first=Cat |date=7 November 2021 |title=The significance of Xi Jinping's upcoming 'historical resolution' |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3154996/xi-jinpings-historical-resolution-what-it-and-why-it-important |url-status=live
