|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220810013800/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3154996/xi-jinpings-historical-resolution-what-it-and-why-it-important |archive-date=10 August 2022 |access-date=11 August 2022 |website=[[South China Morning Post]] |language=en}}{{Cite web |last=Ni |first=Vincent |date=11 November 2021 |title=Chinese Communist party elevates Xi's status in 'historical resolution' |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2021/nov/11/chinese-communist-party-elevates-xi-jinping-status-historical-resolution |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220625192901/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2021/nov/11/chinese-communist-party-elevates-xi-jinping-status-historical-resolution |archive-date=25 June 2022 |access-date=11 August 2022 |website=[[The Guardian]] |language=en}} In comparison with the other historical resolutions, Xi's one did not herald a major change in how the CCP evaluated its history.{{Cite news |author1=Wong, Chun Han |author2=Zhai, Keith |date=17 November 2021 |title=How Xi Jinping Is Rewriting China's History to Put Himself at the Center |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/how-xi-jinping-is-rewriting-chinas-history-to-put-himself-at-the-center-11637157022 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220810135113/https://www.wsj.com/articles/how-xi-jinping-is-rewriting-chinas-history-to-put-himself-at-the-center-11637157022 |archive-date=10 August 2022 |access-date=11 August 2022 |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |language=en-US |issn=0099-9660}} To accompany the historical resolution, the CCP promoted the terms [[Two Establishes]] and [[Two Upholds]], calling the CCP to unite around and protect Xi's core status within the party.{{Cite news |last=Bandurski |first=David |date=8 February 2022 |title=Two Establishes |url=https://chinamediaproject.org/the_ccp_dictionary/two-establishes/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220729191420/https://chinamediaproject.org/the_ccp_dictionary/two-establishes/ |archive-date=29 July 2022 |access-date=12 August 2022 |website=China Media Project |language=en-US}}[[File:Xi Jinping leads CPC leadership in meeting the press.webm|thumb|Xi Jinping and other members of the [[20th Politburo Standing Committee of the Chinese Communist Party|20th Politburo Standing Committee]] meeting the press, October 2022]]The [[20th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party]], held between 16 and 22 October 2022, has overseen amendments in the [[Constitution of the Chinese Communist Party|CCP constitution]] and the re-election of Xi as general secretary of the CCP and chairman of the CMC for a third term, with the overall result of the Congress further strengthening Xi's power.{{Cite news |last1=Davidson |first1=Helen |last2=Graham-Harrison |first2=Emma |date=23 October 2022 |title=China's leader Xi Jinping secures third term and stacks inner circle with loyalists |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/oct/23/xi-jinping-to-rule-china-for-precedent-breaking-third-term |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221023041913/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/oct/23/xi-jinping-to-rule-china-for-precedent-breaking-third-term |archive-date=23 October 2022 |access-date=23 October 2022 |work=[[The Guardian]]}} Xi's re-election made him the first [[Leader of the Chinese Communist Party|party leader]] since Mao Zedong to be chosen for a third term, though Deng Xiaoping ruled the country informally for a longer period (1978–1989).{{Cite news |last=Wingfield-Hayes |first=Rupert |date=23 October 2022 |title=Xi Jinping's party is just getting started |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-63225277 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230317004249/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-63225277 |archive-date=17 March 2023 |access-date=23 October 2022 |work=[[BBC News]] |language=en-GB}} The new Politburo Standing Committee elected just after the CCP Congress was filled almost completely with people close to Xi, with four out of the seven members of the previous PSC stepping down.{{Cite web |date=22 October 2022 |title=Shake-up at the top of China's Communist Party as Xi Jinping starts new term |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3196911/shake-top-chinas-communist-party-xi-jinping-starts-new-term |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221022234719/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3196911/shake-top-chinas-communist-party-xi-jinping-starts-new-term |archive-date=22 October 2022 |access-date=23 October 2022 |website=[[South China Morning Post]] |language=en}} Xi was further unanimously re-elected as the PRC president and chairman of the PRC Central Military Commission on 10 March 2023 during the opening of the [[14th National People's Congress]], and surpassed his predecessors [[Jiang Zemin]] and [[Hu Jintao]] in [[List of voting results of the National People's Congress|voting results]].{{Cite news |title=Xi Jinping unanimously elected Chinese president, PRC CMC chairman |url=https://english.news.cn/20230310/0fb19441ee4145a588911118edd300a3/c.html |date=10 March 2023 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310033619/https://english.news.cn/20230310/0fb19441ee4145a588911118edd300a3/c.html |archive-date=10 March 2023 |access-date=10 March 2023 |work=[[Xinhua News Agency]]}} At the same time, Xi ally [[Li Qiang]] succeeded Li Keqiang as the Premier. ===Cult of personality=== {{Main|Xi Jinping's cult of personality}} [[File:抗战胜利70周年阅兵演练.jpg|thumb|Portrait of Xi in Beijing, September 2015]] Xi has had a [[cult of personality]] constructed around himself since entering office{{Cite news |date=18 September 2014 |title=The power of Xi Jinping |url=https://www.economist.com/news/china/21618882-cult-personality-growing-around-chinas-president-what-will-he-do-his-political |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171107055514/https://www.economist.com/news/china/21618882-cult-personality-growing-around-chinas-president-what-will-he-do-his-political |archive-date=7 November 2017 |access-date=20 September 2017 |newspaper=[[The Economist]]}}{{Cite news |last1=Jiayang |first1=Fan |last2=Taisu |first2=Zhang |author-link2=Taisu Zhang |last3=Ying |first3=Zhu |date=8 March 2016 |title=Behind the Personality Cult of Xi Jinping |url=https://foreignpolicy.com/2016/03/08/the-personality-cult-of-xi-jinping-china-leader-communist-party/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190726173241/https://foreignpolicy.com/2016/03/08/the-personality-cult-of-xi-jinping-china-leader-communist-party/ |archive-date=26 July 2019 |access-date=26 July 2019 |newspaper=[[Foreign Policy (magazine)|Foreign Policy]] |language=en-US}} with books, cartoons, pop songs and dance routines honouring his rule.{{Cite news |last=Phillips |first=Tom |date=19 September 2015 |title=Xi Jinping: Does China truly love 'Big Daddy Xi' – or fear him? |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2015/sep/19/xi-jinping-does-china-truly-love-big-daddy-xi-or-fear-him |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190427001038/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2015/sep/19/xi-jinping-does-china-truly-love-big-daddy-xi-or-fear-him |archive-date=27 April 2019 |access-date=31 August 2017 |work=[[The Guardian]] |issn=0261-3077}} Following Xi's ascension to the [[leadership core]] of the CCP, he had been referred to as ''Xi Dada'' ({{lang-zh|s=习大大|labels=no}}, Uncle or Papa Xi),{{cite news |last1=Blanchard |first1=Ben |date=28 October 2016 |title=All hail the mighty uncle – Chinese welcome Xi as the 'core' |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-politics-xi-idUSKCN12S0AA |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180624010629/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-politics-xi-idUSKCN12S0AA |archive-date=24 June 2018 |access-date=23 June 2018 |work=[[Reuters]] |publisher=}} though this stopped in April 2016.{{Cite news |date=28 April 2015 |title=Xi's Nickname Becomes Out of Bounds for China's Media |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2016-04-28/no-more-big-daddy-xi-as-china-s-spin-doctors-revise-playbook |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221012225210/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2016-04-28/no-more-big-daddy-xi-as-china-s-spin-doctors-revise-playbook |archive-date=12 October 2022 |access-date=11 August 2022 |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |language=en}} The village of Liangjiahe, where Xi was sent to work, is decorated with propaganda and murals extolling the formative years of his life.{{cite news |last1=Rivers |first1=Matt |date=19 March 2018 |title=This entire Chinese village is a shrine to Xi Jinping |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2018/03/19/asia/china-xi-jinping-village-intl/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180624010733/https://edition.cnn.com/2018/03/19/asia/china-xi-jinping-village-intl/index.html |archive-date=24 June 2018 |access-date=23 June 2018 |work=[[CNN]] |publisher=}} The CCP's Politburo named Xi Jinping ''lingxiu'' ({{lang-zh|s=领袖|labels=no}}), a reverent term for "leader" and a title previously only given to Mao Zedong and his immediate successor [[Hua Guofeng]].{{Cite news |last=Gan |first=Nectar |date=28 December 2017 |title=Why China is reviving Mao's grandiose title for Xi Jinping |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/policies-politics/article/2117421/xi-jinpings-latest-grandiose-title-aims-take |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190724182232/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/policies-politics/article/2117421/xi-jinpings-latest-grandiose-title-aims-take |archive-date=24 July 2019 |access-date=26 July 2019 |newspaper=[[South China Morning Post]] |language=en}}{{Cite news |date=17 February 2018 |title=Xi Jinping is no longer any old leader |url=https://www.economist.com/china/2018/02/17/xi-jinping-is-no-longer-any-old-leader |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190724182222/https://www.economist.com/china/2018/02/17/xi-jinping-is-no-longer-any-old-leader |archive-date=24 July 2019 |access-date=26 July 2019 |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |issn=0013-0613}}{{Cite news |last1=Shepherd |first1=Christian |last2=Wen |first2=Philip |date=20 October 2017 |title=With tears and song, China welcomes Xi as great, wise leader |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-congress-idUSKBN1CO0AV |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190726171857/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-congress-idUSKBN1CO0AV |archive-date=26 July 2019 |access-date=26 July 2019 |work=[[Reuters]] |publisher= |language=en}} He is also sometimes called the "pilot at the helm" ({{lang-zh|s=领航掌舵|labels=no}}).{{Cite web |last=Qian |first=Gang |date=2 November 2020 |title=A Brief History of the Helmsmen |url=https://chinamediaproject.org/2020/11/02/a-brief-history-of-the-helmsman/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220816101848/https://chinamediaproject.org/2020/11/02/a-brief-history-of-the-helmsman/ |archive-date=16 August 2022 |access-date=16 August 2022 |website=China Media Project |language=en-US}} On 25 December 2019, the Politburo officially named Xi as "People's Leader" ({{lang-zh|s=人民领袖|labels=no|c=|t=|p=rénmín lǐngxiù}}), a title only Mao had held previously.{{Cite news |last=Nakazawa |first=Katsuji |date=9 January 2020 |title=China crowns Xi with special title, citing rare crisis |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Editor-s-Picks/China-up-close/China-crowns-Xi-with-special-title-citing-rare-crisis |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200109211043/https://asia.nikkei.com/Editor-s-Picks/China-up-close/China-crowns-Xi-with-special-title-citing-rare-crisis |archive-date=9 January 2020 |access-date=10 January 2020 |work=[[Nikkei Asian Review]]}} ===Anti-corruption campaign=== {{main|Anti-corruption campaign under Xi Jinping}} {{Quote box | quote = "To speak the truth" means to focus on the nature of things, to speak frankly, and follow the truth. This is an important embodiment of a leading official's characteristics of truth seeking, embodying justice, devotion to public interests, and uprightness. Moreover, he highlighted that the premise of telling the truth is to listen to the truth. | source = — Xi Jinping during a speech in 2012{{cite book | title=Narrating China's Governance | chapter=Stories of Incorrupt Government: "The Corruption and Unjustness of Officials Give Birth to the Decline of Governance" | publisher=Springer Singapore | publication-place=Singapore | date=20 Nov 2019 | isbn=978-981-329-177-5 | doi=10.1007/978-981-32-9178-2_1 | pages=3–39 | last1=People's Daily | first1=Department of Commentary}} | align = right | width = 25em }} Xi vowed to crack down on corruption immediately after he ascended to power. In his inaugural speech as general secretary, Xi mentioned that fighting corruption was one of the toughest challenges for the party.{{cite news |date=15 November 2012 |title=Xi Jinping's inaugural Speech |work=[[BBC News]] |publisher= |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-20338586 |url-status=live |access-date=7 September 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190914232334/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-20338586 |archive-date=14 September 2019}} A few months into his term, Xi outlined the [[Eight-point Regulation]], listing rules intended to curb corruption and waste during official party business; it aimed at stricter discipline on the conduct of officials. Xi vowed to root out "tigers and flies", that is, high-ranking officials and ordinary party functionaries.{{cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/28/world/asia/xi-jinping-imposes-austerity-measures-on-chinas-elite.html?pagewanted=all&_r=0|title=Elite in China Face Austerity Under Xi's Rule|last=Jacobs|first=Andrew|date=27 March 2013|work=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=7 September 2019|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181130054920/https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/28/world/asia/xi-jinping-imposes-austerity-measures-on-chinas-elite.html?pagewanted=all&_r=0|archive-date=30 November 2018|author-link=Andrew Jacobs (journalist)}} Xi initiated cases against former CMC vice chairmen [[Xu Caihou]] and [[Guo Boxiong]], former PSC member and security chief [[Zhou Yongkang]] and former Hu Jintao chief aide [[Ling Jihua]].{{cite news|url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/2014-03-03/china-s-xi-broadens-graft-crackdown-to-boost-influence.html|title=President Xi's Anti-Corruption Campaign Biggest Since Mao|last=Oster|first=Shai|date=4 March 2014|publisher=[[Bloomberg News]]|access-date=7 September 2019|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141209164917/http://www.bloomberg.com/news/2014-03-03/china-s-xi-broadens-graft-crackdown-to-boost-influence.html|archive-date=9 December 2014|author-link=Shai Oster}} Along with new disciplinary chief [[Wang Qishan]], Xi's [[General secretaryship of Xi Jinping|administration]] spearheaded the formation of "centrally-dispatched inspection teams". These were cross-jurisdictional squads whose task was to gain understanding of the operations of provincial and local party organizations, and enforce party discipline mandated by Beijing. Work teams had the effect of identifying and initiating investigations of high-ranking officials. Over one hundred provincial-ministerial level officials were implicated during a nationwide anti-corruption campaign. These included former and current regional officials, leading figures of state-owned enterprises and central government organs, and generals. Within the first two years of the campaign alone, over 200,000 officials received warnings, fines, and demotions.{{sfn|Heilmann|2017|pp=62–75}} The campaign has led to the downfall of prominent incumbent and retired CCP officials, including members of the PSC.{{cite news|url=http://www.jamestown.org/programs/chinabrief/single/?tx_ttnews%5Btt_news%5D=43160&tx_ttnews%5BbackPid%5D=25&cHash=d5d24d2f82c5981963c0180d300e62cd#.VOOBN_nF9uM|title=China's Soft-Power Deficit Widens as Xi Tightens Screws Over Ideology|date=5 December 2014|access-date=7 September 2019|publisher=[[Brookings Institution]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304041925/http://www.jamestown.org/programs/chinabrief/single/?tx_ttnews%5Btt_news%5D=43160&tx_ttnews%5BbackPid%5D=25&cHash=d5d24d2f82c5981963c0180d300e62cd#.VOOBN_nF9uM|archive-date=4 March 2016|url-status=live}} Xi's anti-corruption campaign is seen by critics, such as ''[[The Economist]]'', as a political tool to remove potential opponents and consolidate power.{{Cite news |date=22 October 2017 |title=Charting China's 'great purge' under Xi |language=en-GB |work=[[BBC News]] |publisher= |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-41670162 |access-date=7 January 2022 |archive-date=6 June 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210606041942/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-41670162 |url-status=live}}{{Cite news |date=11 December 2014 |title=Tiger in the net |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |url=https://www.economist.com/china/2014/12/11/tiger-in-the-net |access-date=17 August 2022 |issn=0013-0613 |archive-date=17 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220817202459/https://www.economist.com/china/2014/12/11/tiger-in-the-net |url-status=live}} Xi's establishment of a new anti-corruption agency, the National Supervision Commission, ranked higher than the supreme court, has been described by [[Amnesty International]] as a "systemic threat to human rights" that "places tens of millions of people at the mercy of a secretive and virtually unaccountable system that is above the law."{{cite news |title=China's former military chief of staff jailed for life for corruption |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2019/feb/20/chinas-former-military-chief-of-staff-jailed-for-life-for-corruption |access-date=13 September 2019 |work=[[The Guardian]]|date=20 February 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191010131232/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2019/feb/20/chinas-former-military-chief-of-staff-jailed-for-life-for-corruption |archive-date=10 October 2019 |url-status=live}}{{cite news |date=20 March 2018 |title=China's anti-corruption campaign expands with new agency |work=[[BBC News]] |publisher= |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-china-43453769 |url-status=live |access-date=13 September 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190924060145/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-china-43453769 |archive-date=24 September 2019}} As of 2023, approximately 2.3 million government officials have been prosecuted.{{Cite book |last=Jin |first=Keyu |author-link=Keyu Jin |title=The New China Playbook: Beyond Socialism and Capitalism |date=2023 |publisher=Viking |isbn=978-1-9848-7828-1 |location=New York}}{{Rp|page=129}} In January 2018, Xi launched a three-year [[Special Campaign to Crack Down on Organized Crime and Eliminate Evil]] that lasted until 2020.{{Cite web |last=Greitens |first=Sheena Chestnut |date=2020-09-02 |title=The Saohei Campaign, Protection Umbrellas, and China's Changing Political-Legal Apparatus |url=https://www.prcleader.org/post/the-saohei-campaign-protection-umbrellas-and-china-s-changing-political-legal-apparatus |access-date=2025-03-19 |website=China Leadership Monitor |language=en}} After the special campaign exposed problems in the legal system, the CCP announced a campaign to [[Education and Rectification of the Political and Legal Teams|educate and rectify the political and legal teams]] in July 2020.{{Cite news |last=Zheng |first=William |date=17 July 2020 |title=Chinese official leading security purge 'may be on fast track to promotion', analysts say |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3093711/chinese-official-leading-security-purge-may-be-fast-track |access-date=17 December 2023 |work=[[South China Morning Post]]}}{{Cite web |date=2019-10-13 |title=把扫黑除恶专项斗争不断推向深入 |trans-title=Continue to deepen the special campaign against organized crime and evil |url=http://www.legaldaily.com.cn/zt/content/2019-10/13/content_8015733.htm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200930193710/http://www.legaldaily.com.cn/zt/content/2019-10/13/content_8015733.htm |archive-date=2020-09-30 |accessdate=2020-10-27 |website=[[Legal Daily]] |publisher=}} Xi has overseen significant reforms of the Central Commission for Discipline Inspection (CCDI), CCP's highest [[internal control]] institution.{{cite news |date=13 June 2014 |title=31个省级纪委改革方案获批复 12省已完成纪委"重建" |trans-title=31 Provincial Commission for Discipline Inspection Reform Plans Approved 12 Provinces Have Completed "Reconstruction" |work=[[Xinhua News Agency]] |url=http://news.xinhuanet.com/politics/2014-06/13/c_126612534.htm |access-date=7 January 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140703204141/http://news.xinhuanet.com/politics/2014-06/13/c_126612534.htm |archive-date=3 July 2014}} He and CCDI Secretary Wang Qishan further institutionalized CCDI's independence from the day-to-day operations of the CCP, improving its ability to function as a ''bona fide'' control body. According to ''The Wall Street Journal'', anti-corruption punishment to officials at or above the vice ministerial level need approval from Xi.{{Cite news |last=Chin |first=Josh |date=15 December 2021 |title=Xi Jinping's Leadership Style: Micromanagement That Leaves Underlings Scrambling |language=en-US |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/xi-jinpings-leadership-style-micromanagement-that-leaves-underlings-scrambling-11639582426 |access-date=13 August 2022 |issn=0099-9660 |archive-date=13 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813143342/https://www.wsj.com/articles/xi-jinpings-leadership-style-micromanagement-that-leaves-underlings-scrambling-11639582426 |url-status=live}} ''The Wall Street Journal'' said that when he wants to neutralize a political rival, he asks inspectors to prepare pages of evidence. It also said he authorizes investigations on close associates of a high-ranking politician, to replace them with his proteges and puts rivals in less important positions to separate them from their political bases. Reportedly, these tactics have even been used against Wang Qishan, Xi's close friend.{{Cite news |last=Wong |first=Chun Han |date=16 October 2022 |title=Xi Jinping's Quest for Control Over China Targets Even Old Friends |language=en-US |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/xi-jinping-china-anticorruption-11665925166 |access-date=17 October 2022 |issn=0099-9660 |archive-date=16 October 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221016230010/https://www.wsj.com/articles/xi-jinping-china-anticorruption-11665925166 |url-status=live}} Especially since 2023, Xi has also overseen significant anti-corruption efforts in the military, with some targets involving those that rose to prominence under his leadership.{{Cite news |last=Buckley |first=Chris |date=2025-08-10 |title=Xi Looks to Tighten Grip After Scandals Shake China’s Military Elite |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2025/08/10/world/asia/china-military-corruption.html |access-date=2025-10-27 |work=[[The New York Times]] |language=en}} Those targeted included former defense ministers [[Li Shangfu]] and [[Wei Fenghe]],{{Cite news |last1=Xiao |first1=Josh |last2=Choong Wilkins |first2=Rebecca |date=18 July 2024 |title=China Ousts Ex-Foreign Minister Qin Gang From Central Committee |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2024-07-18/china-ousts-ex-foreign-minister-qin-gang-from-central-commitee |access-date=18 July 2024 |work=[[Bloomberg News]]}} CMC vice chairman [[He Weidong]], and CMC member [[Miao Hua]],{{Cite news |last=Dang |first=Yuanyue |last2=Wang |first2=Amber |last3=Zhen |first3=Liu |date=17 October 2025 |title=China expels He Weidong, Miao Hua and 7 other generals from party and military |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/military/article/3329429/chinas-no-2-general-he-weidong-expelled-communist-party |access-date=27 October 2025 |work=[[South China Morning Post]]}} Bloomberg News estimated that as October 2025, at least 14 generals out of 79 appointed under Xi's leadership have been officially ousted.{{Cite news |date=23 October 2025 |title=China Military Purge Targets Group Who Undermined Xi’s Authority |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-10-23/china-military-purge-targets-group-who-undermined-xi-s-authority |access-date=27 October 2025 |work=[[Bloomberg News]]}} According to [[sinologist]] [[Wang Gungwu]], Xi inherited a party that was faced with pervasive corruption.{{Cite web |date=19 March 2021 |title=China re-connects: joining a deep-rooted past to a new world order |url=https://www.jesus.cam.ac.uk/articles/china-re-connects-joining-deep-rooted-past-new-world-order |access-date=2023-02-11 |website=[[Jesus College, Cambridge]] |language=en |archive-date=11 February 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230211132318/https://www.jesus.cam.ac.uk/articles/china-re-connects-joining-deep-rooted-past-new-world-order |url-status=live}}{{Cite news |last=Wang |first=Gungwu |author-link=Wang Gungwu |date=7 September 2019 |title=Ancient past, modern ambitions: historian Wang Gungwu's new book on China's delicate balance |work=[[South China Morning Post]] |url=https://www.scmp.com/week-asia/politics/article/3026041/ancient-past-modern-ambitions-historian-wang-gungwus-new-book |access-date=11 February 2023 |archive-date=11 February 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230211175223/https://www.scmp.com/week-asia/politics/article/3026041/ancient-past-modern-ambitions-historian-wang-gungwus-new-book |url-status=live}} Xi believed corruption at the higher levels of the CCP put the party and country at risk of collapse. Wang adds that Xi has a belief that only the CCP is capable of governing China, and that its collapse would be disastrous for the Chinese people. Xi and the new generational leaders reacted by launching the anti-corruption campaign to eliminate corruption at the higher levels of the government. ===Censorship=== {{Main|Censorship in China|Internet censorship in China}} Since Xi became general secretary, censorship has stepped up.{{Cite news|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/chinas-communist-party-unveils-new-leadership-with-no-obvious-successor-to-xi-jinping/2017/10/25/efe67876-b8fc-11e7-9b93-b97043e57a22_story.html|title=China's Xi Jinping unveils his top party leaders, with no successor in sight|last=Denyer|first=Simon|date=25 October 2017|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|access-date=25 October 2017|quote=Censorship has been significantly stepped up in China since Xi took power.|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190813203127/https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/chinas-communist-party-unveils-new-leadership-with-no-obvious-successor-to-xi-jinping/2017/10/25/efe67876-b8fc-11e7-9b93-b97043e57a22_story.html|archive-date=13 August 2019|url-status=live}}{{cite news|ref=none|url=https://www.theguardian.com/news/2018/jun/29/the-great-firewall-of-china-xi-jinpings-internet-shutdown|title=The great firewall of China: Xi Jinping's internet shutdown|last=Economy|first=Elizabeth|author-link=Elizabeth Economy|date=29 June 2018|newspaper=[[The Guardian]]|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191010172129/https://www.theguardian.com/news/2018/jun/29/the-great-firewall-of-china-xi-jinpings-internet-shutdown|archive-date=10 October 2019|access-date=4 November 2019|quote=Before Xi Jinping, the internet was becoming a more vibrant political space for Chinese citizens. But today the country has the largest and most sophisticated online censorship operation in the world.}} Xi has overseen the increased coordination and consolidation and of censorship authorities, raising their efficiency, and under his leadership censorship practices have tightened.{{cite journal |last1=Shirk |first1=Susan L. |author-link1=Susan Shirk |date=April 2018 |title=China in Xi's 'New Era': The Return to Personalistic Rule |url=https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/articles/china-in-xis-new-era-the-return-to-personalistic-rule/ |url-status=live |journal=Journal of Democracy |volume=29 |issue=2 |pages=22–24 |doi=10.1353/jod.2018.0022 |s2cid=158867019 |url-access=subscription |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200804092600/https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/articles/china-in-xis-new-era-the-return-to-personalistic-rule/ |archive-date=4 August 2020 |access-date=22 August 2020}} At the [[Symposium on News Reporting and Public Opinion]] in 2016, Xi stated that "[[Party media takes the party's last name|party and government-owned media must hold the family name of the party]]" and that the state media "must embody the party's will, safeguard the party's authority".{{Cite web |last=Zhuang |first=Pinghui |date=19 February 2016 |title=China's top party mouthpieces pledge 'absolute loyalty' as president makes rare visits to newsrooms |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/policies-politics/article/1914136/chinas-top-party-mouthpieces-pledge-absolute-loyalty |access-date=14 August 2022 |website=[[South China Morning Post]] |language=en |archive-date=31 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230331170900/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/policies-politics/article/1914136/chinas-top-party-mouthpieces-pledge-absolute-loyalty |url-status=live}} Chairing the 2018 China Cyberspace Governance Conference, Xi committed to "fiercely crack down on criminal offenses including hacking, telecom fraud, and violation of citizens' privacy."{{cite news |date=21 April 2018 |title=Xi outlines blueprint to develop China's strength in cyberspace |url=http://www.xinhuanet.com/english/2018-04/21/c_137127374.htm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180421162040/http://www.xinhuanet.com/english/2018-04/21/c_137127374.htm |archive-date=21 April 2018 |access-date=22 April 2018 |work=[[Xinhua News Agency]] |publisher=}} Xi's administration has overseen more Internet restrictions imposed, and is described as being "stricter across the board" on speech than previous administrations.{{Cite news|url=https://www.usnews.com/news/articles/2014/06/03/tiananmen-censorship-reflects-crackdown-under-xi-jinping|title=Tiananmen Censorship Reflects Crackdown Under Xi Jinping|last=Risen|first=Tom|date=3 June 2014|work=[[U.S. News & World Report]]|access-date=8 September 2019|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190411190520/https://www.usnews.com/news/articles/2014/06/03/tiananmen-censorship-reflects-crackdown-under-xi-jinping|archive-date=11 April 2019}}{{Cite news |last=Economy |first=Elizabeth C. |date=2018-06-29 |title=The great firewall of China: Xi Jinping's internet shutdown |url=https://www.theguardian.com/news/2018/jun/29/the-great-firewall-of-china-xi-jinpings-internet-shutdown |access-date=2025-08-03 |work=[[The Guardian]] |language=en-GB |issn=0261-3077}} A law enacted in 2013 authorized a three-year prison term for bloggers who shared more than 500 times any content considered "defamatory".{{cite news |last=Grigg |first=Angus |date=4 July 2015 |title=How China stopped its bloggers |url=http://www.afr.com/technology/social-media/how-china-stopped-its-bloggers-20150701-gi34za |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180626001441/https://www.afr.com/technology/social-media/how-china-stopped-its-bloggers-20150701-gi34za |archive-date=26 June 2018 |access-date=8 September 2019 |newspaper=[[The Australian Financial Review]]}} The State Internet Information Department summoned influential bloggers to a seminar to instruct them to avoid writing about politics, the CCP, or making statements contradicting official narratives. Many bloggers stopped writing about controversial topics, and [[Weibo]] went into decline, with much of its readership shifting to [[WeChat]] users speaking to limited social circles. Xi has taken a strong stand to control internet usage inside China, including [[Google]] and [[Facebook]],{{sfn|Bougon|pp=157–65|2018}} advocating Internet censorship under the concept of [[Network sovereignty|internet sovereignty]].{{Cite news|last=Tiezzi|first=Shannon|date=24 June 2014|title=China's 'Sovereign Internet'|language=en-US|work=[[The Diplomat (magazine)|The Diplomat]]|url=https://thediplomat.com/2014/06/chinas-sovereign-internet/|url-status=live|access-date=4 August 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170804053110/https://thediplomat.com/2014/06/chinas-sovereign-internet/|archive-date=4 August 2017}}{{Cite news|last=Ford|first=Peter|date=18 December 2015|title=On Internet freedoms, China tells the world, 'leave us alone'|work=[[The Christian Science Monitor]]|url=https://www.csmonitor.com/World/Asia-Pacific/2015/1218/On-Internet-freedoms-China-tells-the-world-leave-us-alone|url-status=live|access-date=4 August 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170804055927/https://www.csmonitor.com/World/Asia-Pacific/2015/1218/On-Internet-freedoms-China-tells-the-world-leave-us-alone|archive-date=4 August 2017|issn=0882-7729}} The [[censorship of Wikipedia]] has been stringent; in April 2019, all versions of Wikipedia were blocked.{{cite news|date=14 May 2019|title=Wikipedia blocked in China in all languages|publisher=[[BBC News]]|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/technology-48269608|url-status=live|access-date=15 May 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190515001341/https://www.bbc.com/news/technology-48269608|archive-date=15 May 2019}} Likewise, the situation for users of Weibo has been described as a change from fearing one's account would be deleted, to fear of arrest.{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2015/aug/06/china-xi-jinping-crackdown-liberal-academics-minor-cultural-revolution|title='It's getting worse': China's liberal academics fear growing censorship|last=Phillips|first=Tom|date=6 August 2015|work=[[The Guardian]]|access-date=8 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190918070042/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2015/aug/06/china-xi-jinping-crackdown-liberal-academics-minor-cultural-revolution|archive-date=18 September 2019|url-status=live}} Under his administration, China started enforcing an [[Internet real-name system in China|Internet real-name system]] for online platforms, requiring them to collect users' real names, ID numbers, and other information when providing services.{{Cite web |last=Carnap |first=Kai von |date=2024-02-15 |title=The increasing challenge of obtaining information from Xi's China |url=https://merics.org/en/report/increasing-challenge-obtaining-information-xis-china |access-date=2025-08-03 |website=[[Mercator Institute for China Studies]] |language=en}} === Economy and technology === {{Main|Economy of China}} {{See also|Xi Jinping Thought on Economy}} Xi was initially seen as a market reformist,{{Cite journal |last=Whyte |first=Martin K. |date=15 March 2021 |title=China's economic development history and Xi Jinping's "China dream:" an overview with personal reflections |journal=Chinese Sociological Review |volume=53 |issue=2 |pages=115–134 |doi=10.1080/21620555.2020.1833321 |s2cid=228867589 |issn=2162-0555|doi-access=free}} and a Central Committee under him announced "market forces" would begin to play a "decisive" role in allocating resources. This meant that the state would gradually reduce its involvement in the distribution of capital, and restructure [[State-owned enterprises of China|state-owned enterprises]] (SOEs) to allow further competition, potentially by attracting foreign and private sector players in industries previously highly regulated. This policy aimed to address the bloated state sector that had unduly profited from re-structuring by purchasing assets at below-market prices, assets no longer being used productively. Xi launched the [[Shanghai Free-Trade Zone]] in 2013, which was seen as part of the economic reforms.{{Cite news |last=Denyer |first=Simon |date=25 August 2013 |title=Creeping reforms as China gives Shanghai Free Trade Zone go-ahead |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/creeping-reforms-as-china-gives-shanghai-free-trade-zone-go-ahead/2013/08/25/95f66472-0bf7-11e3-89fe-abb4a5067014_story.html |access-date=4 January 2023 |archive-date=3 June 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230603015413/https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/creeping-reforms-as-china-gives-shanghai-free-trade-zone-go-ahead/2013/08/25/95f66472-0bf7-11e3-89fe-abb4a5067014_story.html |url-status=live}} However, by 2017, Xi's promise of economic reforms was said to have stalled by experts.{{Cite news |last1=Buckley |first1=Chris |last2=Bradsher |first2=Keith |date=4 March 2017 |title=Xi Jinping's Failed Promises Dim Hopes for Economic Change in 2nd Term |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/03/04/world/asia/china-xi-jinping-economic-reform.html |access-date=22 August 2022 |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=22 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220822135411/https://www.nytimes.com/2017/03/04/world/asia/china-xi-jinping-economic-reform.html |url-status=live}} In 2015, the [[2015–2016 Chinese stock market turbulence|Chinese stock market bubble popped]], which led Xi to use state forces to fix it.{{Sfn|Wong|2023|p=146}} China's economy has grown under Xi, more than doubling from $8.7 trillion in 2012 to $18.7 trillion in 2024,{{Cite web |title=GDP (current US$) – China {{!}} Data |url=https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/NY.GDP.MKTP.CD?locations=CN |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190906052638/https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/NY.GDP.MKTP.CD?locations=CN |archive-date=6 September 2019 |access-date=13 August 2022 |website=[[World Bank]]}} while China's nominal GDP per capita surpassed the world average in 2021,{{cite web |author= |date=April 2023 |title=World Economic Outlook Database, April 2023 |url=https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/WEO/weo-database/2023/April |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230413194731/https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/WEO/weo-database/2023/April |archive-date=13 April 2023 |access-date=16 May 2023 |website=[[International Monetary Fund]] |publisher=}} though growth has slowed from 7.9% in 2012 to 5% in 2024.{{Cite web |title=GDP growth (annual %) – China {{!}} Data |url=https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/NY.GDP.MKTP.KD.ZG?locations=CN |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813205801/https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/NY.GDP.MKTP.KD.ZG?locations=CN |archive-date=13 August 2022 |access-date=13 August 2022 |website=[[World Bank]]}} Xi has stressed the importance of "high-quality growth" rather than "inflated growth".{{Cite web |last=Hui |first=Mary |date=16 February 2022 |title=What China means when it says it wants "high quality" GDP growth |url=https://qz.com/2119395/what-china-means-by-high-quality-economic-growth/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813205819/https://qz.com/2119395/what-china-means-by-high-quality-economic-growth/ |archive-date=13 August 2022 |access-date=13 August 2022 |website=[[Quartz (publication)|Quartz]] |language=en}} He has stated China has abandoned a growth-at-all-costs strategy which Xi refers to as "GDP heroism".{{Sfn|Marquis|Qiao|2022|p=22}} Instead, Xi said other social issues such as environmental protection are important.{{Sfn|Marquis|Qiao|2022|p=22}} Xi has increased state control over the economy, voicing support for SOEs,{{Cite news|url=https://www.scmp.com/economy/china-economy/article/2166261/chinese-president-xi-jinpings-show-support-state-owned-firms|title=Chinese President Xi Jinping's show of support for state-owned 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201109022556/https://www.scmp.com/economy/china-economy/article/2166261/chinese-president-xi-jinpings-show-support-state-owned-firms|url-status=live}} while also supporting the private sector.{{Cite news|url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/2166108/xi-jinping-reassures-chinas-state-owned-enterprises-and-private|title=Xi says it's wrong to 'bad mouth' China's state firms... but country needs private sector as well|last=Gan|first=Nectar|date=28 September 2018|work=[[South China Morning Post]]|access-date=31 January CCP control of SOEs has increased, while limited steps towards market liberalization, such as increasing mixed ownership of SOEs were undertaken.{{sfn|Pieke|Hofman|2022|p=48}} From 2012 to 2022, the share of the market value of private sector firms in China's top listed companies increased from 10% to over 40%.{{Cite news |last1=Orlik |first1=Tom |last2=Hancock |first2=Tom |date=3 March 2023 |title=What Wall Street Gets Wrong About Xi Jinping's New Money Men |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2023-03-03/xi-s-new-money-men-worry-investors-but-could-push-chinese-economic-reform?srnd=premium&leadSource=uverify%20wall |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240711081522/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2023-03-03/xi-s-new-money-men-worry-investors-but-could-push-chinese-economic-reform?srnd=premium&leadSource=uverify%20wall |archive-date=11 July 2024 |access-date=4 March 2023 |work=[[Bloomberg News]]}} He has overseen the relaxation of restrictions on [[foreign direct investment]] (FDI) and increased cross-border holdings of stocks and bonds. His administration made it easier for banks to issue [[Mortgage loan|mortgages]], increased foreign participation in the bond market, and increased the national currency [[Renminbi|renminbi's]] global role, helping it to join [[International Monetary Fund|IMF's]] basket of [[Special drawing rights|special drawing right]].{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/03/04/business/china-xi-jinping-economic-reform-scorecard.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170305071055/https://www.nytimes.com/2017/03/04/business/china-xi-jinping-economic-reform-scorecard.html |archive-date=5 March 2017 |url-access=limited |url-status=live|title=China and Economic Reform: Xi Jinping's Track Record|last=Bradsher|first=Keith|date=4 March 2017|work=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=31 January 2020|language=en-US|issn=0362-4331}} In 2018, he promised to continue reforms but warned nobody "can dictate to the Chinese people."{{Cite news|url=https://www.ft.com/content/658e78ce-0287-11e9-99df-6183d3002ee1|archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221211221207/https://www.ft.com/content/658e78ce-0287-11e9-99df-6183d3002ee1|archive-date=11 December 2022|url-access=subscription|url-status=live|title=Xi says no one can 'dictate to the Chinese people'|last=Wildau|first=Gabriel|date=18 December 2018|work=[[Financial Times]]|access-date=31 January 2020}} Xi has overseen regional economic development initiatives within China such as the [[Coordinated Development of the Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei Region]],{{Cite news |last=Huang |first=Cary |date=25 June 2014 |title=Integration of Beijing, Tianjin and Hebei set to be signature project of Xi Jinping's administration |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/article/1539886/integration-beijing-tianjin-and-hebei-set-be-signature-project-xi |access-date=27 October 2025 |work=[[South China Morning Post]]}} [[Strategy for Integrated Development of the Yangtze River Delta]]{{Cite news |date=15 October 2023 |title=China’s Slowdown Casts Pall Over Xi’s Yangtze Delta Project |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-10-15/china-s-slowdown-casts-pall-over-xi-s-yangtze-delta-project |access-date=27 October 2025 |work=[[Bloomberg News]]}} and the [[Guangdong–Hong Kong–Macao Greater Bay Area]]. Xi has been involved in the development of [[Xiong'an]], a [[New areas|new area]] announced in 2017, planned to become a major metropolis near Beijing; the relocation aspect is estimated to last until 2035 while it is planned to developed into a "modern socialist city" by 2050.{{Cite news |date=18 May 2023 |title=Xiongan is Xi Jinping's pet project |url=https://www.economist.com/china/2023/05/18/xiongan-is-xi-jinpings-pet-project |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230519023705/https://www.economist.com/china/2023/05/18/xiongan-is-xi-jinpings-pet-project |archive-date=19 May 2023 |access-date=19 May 2023 |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |issn=0013-0613}} Xi has made eradicating extreme poverty through [[Targeted Poverty Alleviation|targeted poverty alleviation]] a key goal.{{Cite news |date=25 February 2021 |title=Xi Puts His Personal Stamp on China's Fight Against Poverty |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2021-02-25/china-s-xi-puts-personal-stamp-on-nation-s-fight-against-poverty |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220218100001/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2021-02-25/china-s-xi-puts-personal-stamp-on-nation-s-fight-against-poverty |archive-date=18 February 2022 |access-date=14 August 2022 |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |language=en}} In 2015, he launched the [[battle against poverty]].{{Cite web |last=Bandurski |first=David |date=2021-01-29 |title=Propaganda Soars Into Orbit |url=https://chinamediaproject.org/2021/01/29/propaganda-soars-into-orbit/ |access-date=2025-03-29 |website=China Media Project |language=en-US}} The campaign concluded by 2021, when Xi declared a "complete victory" over extreme poverty, saying nearly 100 million have been lifted out of poverty under his tenure, though some experts said China's [[poverty threshold]] was lower than that of the [[World Bank Group|World Bank]].{{Cite news |date=25 February 2021 |title=China's Xi declares victory in ending extreme poverty |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-56194622 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220728095225/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-56194622 |archive-date=28 July 2022 |access-date=14 August 2022 |work=[[BBC News]] |language=en-GB}} In 2020, premier Li Keqiang, citing the [[National Bureau of Statistics of China|National Bureau of Statistics]] (NBS) said that China still had 600 million people living with less than 1000 yuan ($140) a month, although ''The Economist'' said the methodology NBS used was flawed.{{Cite news |date=18 June 2020 |title=China's poverty line is not as stingy as commentators think |url=https://www.economist.com/finance-and-economics/2020/06/18/chinas-poverty-line-is-not-as-stingy-as-commentators-think |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220814221948/https://www.economist.com/finance-and-economics/2020/06/18/chinas-poverty-line-is-not-as-stingy-as-commentators-think |archive-date=14 August 2022 |access-date=14 August 2022 |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |location=Hong Kong |issn=0013-0613}} When Xi took office in 2012, 58% of people in China were living on less than $8.30 per day, in 2021 this had fallen to 21%.{{cite web |title=World Bank Open Data |url=https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/SI.POV.UMIC?locations=CN |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240414183532/https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/SI.POV.UMIC |archive-date=14 April 2024 |access-date=12 January 2024}} Xi supports [[dual circulation]], a reorientation of the economy towards domestic consumption while remaining open to foreign trade and investment.{{Cite news |last=Wei |first=Lingling |date=12 August 2020 |title=China's Xi Speeds Up Inward Economic Shift |language=en-US |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/chinas-xi-speeds-up-inward-economic-shift-11597224602 |access-date=13 August 2022 |issn=0099-9660 |archive-date=13 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813205801/https://www.wsj.com/articles/chinas-xi-speeds-up-inward-economic-shift-11597224602 |url-status=live}} Xi has prioritised boosting [[productivity]].{{Cite news |date=14 August 2021 |title=China's future economic potential hinges on its productivity |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |url=https://www.economist.com/briefing/2021/08/14/chinas-future-economic-potential-hinges-on-its-productivity |access-date=13 August 2022 |issn=0013-0613 |archive-date=12 August 2022 reform the property sector to combat the steep increase in prices and cut the economy's dependence on it.{{Cite news |date=27 July 2021 |title=China's Escalating Property Curbs Point to Xi's New Priority |language=en |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2021-07-27/china-s-escalating-property-curbs-underline-xi-s-new-priority |access-date=11 August 2022 |url-status=live}} In the 19th CCP National Congress, Xi declared "[[houses are for living, not for speculation]]."{{Cite news |date=18 October 2017 |title=Housing Should Be for Living In, Not for Speculation, Xi Says |language=en |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2017-10-18/xi-renews-call-housing-should-be-for-living-in-not-speculation |access-date=11 August |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220425013308/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2017-10-18/xi-renews-call-housing-should-be-for-living-in-not-speculation |url-status=live}} In 2020, Xi's government formulated the "[[three red lines]]" policy that aimed to deleverage the heavily indebted property sector.{{Cite news |last1=Lin |first1=Andy |last2=Hale |first2=Thomas |last3=Hudson |first3=Hudson |date=8 October 2021 |title=Half of China's top developers crossed Beijing's 'red lines' |work=[[Financial Times]] |url=https://www.ft.com/content/d5803d64-5cc5-46f0-bed0-1bc207440f9c |access-date=11 August 2022}} Xi has supported a [[property tax]], for which he has faced resistance from members of the CCP.{{Cite news |last=Wei |first=Lingling |date=19 October 2021 |title=In |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/in-tackling-chinas-real-estate-bubble-xi-jinping-faces-resistance-to-property-tax-plan-11634650751 |access-date=11 August 2022 |issn=0099-9660 |archive-date=8 August |url-status=live}} His administration pursued a debt-deleveraging campaign, seeking to slow and cut the unsustainable amount of debt China has accrued during its growth.{{Cite web |last=Tran |first=Hung |date=14 March 2023 |title=China's debt-reduction campaign is making progress, but at a cost |url=https://www.atlanticcouncil.org/blogs/new-atlanticist/chinas-debt-reduction-campaign-is-making-progress-but-at-a-cost/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230314200320/https://www.atlanticcouncil.org/blogs/new-atlanticist/chinas-debt-reduction-campaign-is-making-progress-but-at-a-cost/ |archive-date=14 March 2023 |access-date=14 March 2023 |website=[[Atlantic Council]]}} Since 2021, China has faced a [[Chinese property sector crisis (2020–present)|property sector crisis]], with decreasing house prices, shrinking of the real estate sector and bankruptcies of many property developers, partially as a result of Xi's efforts to decrease the sector's role in the Chinese economy.{{Cite news |date=23 August 2023 |title=China's Debt-Fueled Housing Market Is Having a Meltdown, Again |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-08-23/china-real-estate-market-crisis-is-another-mess-for-xi-jinping |access-date=1 February 2025 |work=[[Bloomberg News]]}} Xi has heavily emphasized the role of advanced manufacturing and technology development to drive China's future economic growth.{{Cite news |last1=White |first1=Edward |last2=Leng |first2=Cheng |date=2024-03-27 |title=Will Xi's manufacturing plan be enough to rescue China's economy? |url=https://www.ft.com/content/ae517907-0244-4344-ad0a-1d029c03555b |access-date=2025-02-01 |work=[[Financial Times]]}} Since shortly after taking office as General Secretary, Xi emphasised the use of industrial policy to increase domestic innovation and reduce reliance on foreign tehchnology.{{Reference page|page=105}} In 2015, Xi's administration launched the [[Double First-Class Construction]], a higher education development and sponsorship scheme intended to replace the previous programs [[Project 211]] and [[Project 985]].{{Citation |last1=Li |first1=Jian |title=The Policy Analysis of Creating World-Class Universities in China |date=2021 |work=Creating World-Class Universities in China : Ideas, Policies, and Efforts |pages=1–33 |editor-last=Li |editor-first=Jian |isbn=978-981-16-6725-1 |s2cid=240467383 |last2=Xue |first2=Eryong |editor2-last=Xue |editor2-first=Eryong |url-access=subscription}} Xi's administration has promoted [[Made in China 2025]] plan that aims to make China self-reliant in key technologies, although publicly China de-emphasised this plan due to the outbreak of a [[China–United States trade war]]. Since the outbreak of the trade war in 2018, Xi has revived calls for "self-reliance", especially on technology.{{Cite news |last=Fifield |first=Anna |date=2 November 2018 |title=As China settles in for trade war, leader Xi emphasizes 'self reliance' |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/asia_pacific/as-china-settles-in-for-trade-war-leader-xi-emphasizesself-reliance/2018/11/01/2961b2b2-d8de-11e8-9559-712cbf726d1c_story.html |access-date=31 January 2020 |archive-date=31 January 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200131193728/https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/asia_pacific/as-china-settles-in-for-trade-war-leader-xi-emphasizesself-reliance/2018/11/01/2961b2b2-d8de-11e8-9559-712cbf726d1c_story.html |url-status=live}} Domestic spending on R&D has significantly increased, surpassing the European Union (EU) and reaching a record $564 billion in 2020. The Chinese government has supported technology companies like [[Huawei]] through grants, tax breaks, credit facilities and other assistance, enabling their rise, leading to US countermeasures.{{Cite news |last=Yap |first=Chuin-Wei |date=25 December 2019 |title=State Support Helped Fuel Huawei's Global Rise |language=en-US |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/state-support-helped-fuel-huaweis-global-rise-11577280736 |access-date=16 September 2022 |issn=0099-9660 |archive-date=25 December 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191225174935/https://www.wsj.com/articles/state-support-helped-fuel-huaweis-global-rise-11577280736 |url-status=live}} In 2023, Xi put forward [[new productive forces]], this refers to a new form of productive forces derived from continuous sci-tech breakthroughs and innovation that drive strategic emerging and future industries in a more intelligent [[Information Age|information era]].{{Cite news |last=Yu |first=Evelyn |date=5 March 2024 |title=Xi Wants 'New Productive Forces' to Fit Local Conditions |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2024-03-05/xi-wants-new-productive-forces-to-be-based-on-local-conditions |access-date=1 May 2024 |work=[[Bloomberg News]]}} Under Xi, China made rapid advances in key technological areas, becoming a world leader in tech such as [[Plug-in electric vehicles in China|electric vehicles]], [[Lithium batteries in China|lithium batteries]] and solar panels.{{Cite news |date=30 October 2024 |title=US Efforts to Contain Xi's Push for Tech Supremacy Are Faltering |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/graphics/2024-us-china-containment/ |access-date=1 February 2025 |work=[[Bloomberg News]]}} {{Quote box | quote = Common prosperity is an essential requirement of socialism and a key feature of Chinese-style modernization. The common prosperity we are pursuing is for all, affluence both in material and spiritual life, but not for a small portion nor for uniform egalitarianism. | source = — Xi Jinping during a speech in 2021 | align = right | width = 25em }} In 2020, ''[[The Wall Street Journal]]'' reported that Xi ordered a halt to [[Ant Group]]'s [[initial public offering]] (IPO), in reaction to its founder [[Jack Ma]] criticizing government regulation in finance.{{Cite news |last=Wei |first=Jing Yang and Lingling |date=12 November 2020 |title=China's President Xi Jinping Personally Scuttled Jack Ma's Ant IPO |language=en-US |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/china-president-xi-jinping-halted-jack-ma-ant-ipo-11605203556 |access-date=11 August 2022 |issn=0099-9660 |archive-date=13 November 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201113061215/https://www.wsj.com/articles/china-president-xi-jinping-halted-jack-ma-ant-ipo-11605203556 |url-status=live}} Under Xi, [[China Government Guidance Fund|government guidance funds]], public-private investment funds set up by or for government bodies, have raised more than $900 billion for early funding to companies that work in sectors the government deems as strategic.{{Cite news |last=Lockett |first=Hudson |date=2022-06-12 |title=How Xi Jinping is reshaping China's capital markets |url=https://www.ft.com/content/d5b81ea0-5955-414c-b2eb-886dfed4dffe |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221211221237/https://www.ft.com/content/d5b81ea0-5955-414c-b2eb-886dfed4dffe |archive-date=11 December 2022 |access-date=2022-11-21 |work=[[Financial Times]]}} Xi's administration has overseen a decrease in offshore IPOs by Chinese companies, with most Chinese IPOs taking place either in Shanghai or Shenzhen {{As of|2022|lc=y}}, and has increasingly directed funding to IPOs of companies that works in sectors it deems as strategic, including electric vehicles, [[Biotechnology industry in China|biotechnology]], [[Renewable energy in China|renewable energy]], [[Artificial intelligence industry in China|artificial intelligence]], [[Semiconductor industry in China|semiconductors]] and other high-technology manufacturing. Since 2021, Xi has promoted the term [[common prosperity]], which he defined as an "essential requirement of socialism", described as affluence for all and said entailed reasonable adjustments to excess incomes.{{Cite web |date=19 October 2021 |title=Full Text: Xi Jinping's Speech on Boosting Common Prosperity – Caixin Global |url=https://www.caixinglobal.com/2021-10-19/full-text-xi-jinpings-speech-on-boosting-common-prosperity-101788302.html |access-date=11 August 2022 |website=[[Caixin Global]] |language=en |archive-date=30 October 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221030001458/https://www.caixinglobal.com/2021-10-19/full-text-xi-jinpings-speech-on-boosting-common-prosperity-101788302.html |url-status=live}}{{Cite news |last=Hass |first=Ryan |date=9 September 2021 |title=Assessing China's "common prosperity" campaign |language=en-US |work=[[Brookings Institution]] |url=https://www.brookings.edu/blog/order-from-chaos/2021/09/09/assessing-chinas-common-prosperity-campaign/ |access-date=11 August 2022 |archive-date=3 July 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220703091105/https://www.brookings.edu/blog/order-from-chaos/2021/09/09/assessing-chinas-common-prosperity-campaign/ |url-status=live}} Common prosperity has been used as the justification for [[2020–2021 Xi Jinping Administration reform spree|large-scale crackdowns and regulations]] towards the perceived "excesses" of several sectors, most prominently tech and tutoring industries.{{Cite news |last1=Shen |first1=Samuel |last2=Ranganathan |first2=Vidya |date=3 November 2021 |title=China stock pickers reshape portfolios on Xi's 'common prosperity' |language=en |work=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/technology/china-stock-pickers-reshape-portfolios-xis-common-prosperity-2021-11-02/ |access-date=11 August 2022 |archive-date=8 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220808115133/https://www.reuters.com/technology/china-stock-pickers-reshape-portfolios-xis-common-prosperity-2021-11-02/ |url-status=live}} Actions taken include fining large tech companies{{cite news |last1=Moore |first1=Elena |date=10 April 2021 |title=China Fines Alibaba $2.8 Billion For Breaking Anti-Monopoly Law |language=en |publisher=[[NPR]] |url=https://www.npr.org/2021/04/10/986112628/china-fines-alibaba-2-8-billion-for-breaking-anti-monopoly-law |access-date=16 September 2021 |archive-date=16 September 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210916024333/https://www.npr.org/2021/04/10/986112628/china-fines-alibaba-2-8-billion-for-breaking-anti-monopoly-law |url-status=live}} and passing laws such as the [[Data Security Law of the People's Republic of China|Data Security Law]]. China introduced severe restrictions on private tutoring in the name of promoting social equality, effectively eliminating the private education industry and enacting the [[Double Reduction Policy]].{{Cite news |date=12 August 2021 |title=China's Education Crackdown Pushes Costly Tutors Underground |language=en |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2021-08-12/china-s-education-crackdown-pushes-costly-tutors-underground |access-date=13 August 2022 |archive-date=24 March 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220324173146/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2021-08-12/china-s-education-crackdown-pushes-costly-tutors-underground |url-status=live}}{{Reference page|page=|pages=131, 173-174}} Xi opened a [[Beijing Stock Exchange|new stock exchange]] in Beijing targeted for small and medium enterprises (SMEs).{{Cite news |date=15 November 2021 |title=Trading kicks off on Beijing Stock Exchange, 10 stocks surge |language=en |work=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/trading-kicks-off-beijing-stock-exchange-10-stocks-surge-2021-11-15/ |access-date=22 August 2022 |archive-date=27 November 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211127204552/https://www.reuters.com/world/china/trading-kicks-off-beijing-stock-exchange-10-stocks-surge-2021-11-15/ |url-status=live}} There have been other cultural regulations including restrictions on minors playing video games and crackdowns on celebrity culture.{{Cite news |last1=Stevenson |first1=Alexandra |last2=Chien |first2=Amy Chang |last3=Li |first3=Cao |date=27 August 2021 |title=China's Celebrity Culture Is Raucous. The Authorities Want to Change That. |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2021/08/27/business/media/china-celebrity-culture.html |access-date=13 August 2022 |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=12 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220812152735/https://www.nytimes.com/2021/08/27/business/media/china-celebrity-culture.html |url-status=live}}{{Cite news |last=Buckley |first=Chris |date=30 August 2021 |title=China Tightens Limits for Young Online Gamers and Bans School Night Play |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2021/08/30/business/media/china-online-games.html |access-date=13 August 2022 |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=18 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220818212528/https://www.nytimes.com/2021/08/30/business/media/china-online-games.html |url-status=live}} The push for common prosperity has also included salary and bonus cuts, especially across the financial sector,{{Cite news |date=15 November 2022 |title=China Bankers Face Deeper Pay Cuts in 'Common Prosperity' Push |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2022-11-15/china-bankers-face-deeper-pay-cuts-in-common-prosperity-push |access-date=1 February 2025 |work=[[Bloomberg News]]}}{{Cite news |date=27 February 2024 |title=Xi Crackdown on 'Hedonistic' Bankers Fuels Industry Brain Drain |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2024-02-26/xi-crackdown-on-hedonistic-bankers-fuels-industry-brain-drain |access-date=1 February 2025 |work=[[Bloomberg News]]}} as well as crackdowns on wealth flaunting.{{Cite news |date=20 June 2024 |title=China doesn't want people flaunting their wealth |url=https://www.economist.com/china/2024/06/20/china-doesnt-want-people-flaunting-their-wealth |access-date=2025-02-01 |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |issn=0013-0613}} ===Reforms=== In November 2013, at the conclusion of the [[Third plenary session of the 18th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party|third plenum of the 18th Central Committee]], the Communist Party delivered the ''[[Decision on Several Major Issues Concerning Comprehensively Deepening Reform]]'', a far-reaching reform agenda that alluded to changes in both economic and social policy. Xi signaled at the plenum that he was consolidating control of the massive internal security organization that was formerly the domain of Zhou Yongkang.{{cite news |last1=Kroeber |first1=Arthur R. |date=17 November 2013 |title=Xi Jinping's Ambitious Agenda for Economic Reform in China |work=[[Brookings Institution]] |publisher= |url=http://www.brookings.edu/research/opinions/2013/11/17-xi-jinping-economic-agenda-kroeber |url-status=live |access-date=21 July 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140727211348/http://www.brookings.edu/research/opinions/2013/11/17-xi-jinping-economic-agenda-kroeber |archive-date=27 July 2014}} The [[Central Leading Group for Comprehensively Deepening Reforms]]—another ''ad hoc'' policy coordination body led by Xi upgraded to a commission in 2018—was also formed to oversee the implementation of the reform agenda.{{Cite web |last=Jun |first=Mai |date=21 March 2018 |title=China unveils bold overhaul to tighten Communist Party control |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/policies-politics/article/2138291/china-unveils-bold-overhaul-tighten-communist-party |access-date=13 August 2022 |website=[[South China Morning Post]] |language=en |archive-date=13 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813171805/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/policies-politics/article/2138291/china-unveils-bold-overhaul-tighten-communist-party |url-status=live}}{{cite news |last1=Keck |first1=Zachary |date=7 January 2014 |title=Is Li Keqiang Being Marginalized? |url=https://thediplomat.com/2014/01/is-li-keqiang-being-marginalized/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181029152046/https://thediplomat.com/2014/01/is-li-keqiang-being-marginalized/ |archive-date=29 October 2018 |access-date=7 September 2019 |work=[[The Diplomat (magazine)|The Diplomat]]}} Termed "comprehensive deepening reforms", they were said to be the most significant since Deng Xiaoping's 1992 Southern Tour. The plenum also announced economic reforms and resolved to abolish the ''[[laogai]]'' system of "[[Re-education through labor|re-education through labour]]", which was largely seen as a blot on China's human rights record. The system has faced significant criticism for years from domestic critics and foreign observers. In 2015, Xi announced the [[Four Comprehensives]], namely comprehensively build a [[moderately prosperous society]], deepening reform, governing the nation according to [[Chinese law|law]], and strictly governing the Party;{{Cite news |date=2015-02-25 |title=China's Xi Jinping unveils new 'four comprehensives' slogans |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-31622571 |access-date=2025-10-27 |work=[[BBC News]] |language=en-GB}}{{Sfn|Tsang|Cheung|2024|p=23}} in 2021, at the 100th anniversary of the CCP, Xi declared that China achieved its goal of building a moderately prosperous society in all respects.{{Cite news |date=31 December 2021 |title=Look to the future and stay focused, Xi tells China in New Year's address |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/look-future-stay-focused-xi-tells-china-new-years-address-2021-12-31/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231108102024/https://www.reuters.com/world/china/look-future-stay-focused-xi-tells-china-new-years-address-2021-12-31/ |archive-date=8 November 2023 |access-date=2025-02-06 |work=[[Reuters]] |language=en-US |url-status=live }} In January 2016, a [[two-child policy]] replaced the [[one-child policy]],{{cite news|last=Sridahan|first=Vasudevan|date=27 December 2015|title=China formally abolishes decades-old one-child policy|newspaper=[[International Business Times]]|url=http://www.ibtimes.co.uk/china-formally-abolishes-decades-old-one-child-policy-1535006|url-status=live|access-date=7 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190828224908/https://www.ibtimes.co.uk/china-formally-abolishes-decades-old-one-child-policy-1535006|archive-date=28 August 2019}} which was in turn was replaced with a [[three-child policy]] in May 2021.{{Cite news |last=Wee |first=Sui-Lee |date=31 May 2021 |title=China Says It Will Allow Couples to Have 3 Children, Up From 2 |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2021/05/31/world/asia/china-three-child-policy.html |access-date=11 August 2022 |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=4 November 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211104064050/https://www.nytimes.com/2021/05/31/world/asia/china-three-child-policy.html |url-status=live}} In July 2021, all family size limits as well as penalties for exceeding them were removed.{{Cite web |last=Cheng |first=Evelyn |date=21 July 2021 |title=China scraps fines, will let families have as many children as they'd like |url=https://www.cnbc.com/2021/07/21/china-scraps-fines-for-families-violating-childbirth-limits.html |access-date=29 April 2022 |website=[[CNBC]] |language=en |archive-date=28 January 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230128131149/https://www.cnbc.com/2021/07/21/china-scraps-fines-for-families-violating-childbirth-limits.html |url-status=live}} ==== Political reforms ==== {{See also|Neoauthoritarianism (China)}} Xi's administration taken a number of changes to the structure of the CCP and state bodies, especially in a large overhaul in 2018. Beginning in 2013, the CCP under Xi has created a series of Central Leading Groups: supra-ministerial steering committees, designed to bypass existing institutions when making decisions, and ostensibly make policy-making a more efficient process. Xi was also believed to have diluted the authority of premier Li Keqiang, taking authority over the economy which has generally been considered to be the domain of the premier.{{Cite news |date=28 September 2020 |title=Li Keqiang: China's marginalised premier |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-19870221 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221017104619/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-19870221 |archive-date=17 October 2022 |access-date=17 October 2022 |work=[[BBC News]] |language=en-GB}}{{Cite news |last=Wei |first=Lingling |date=11 May 2022 |title=China's Forgotten Premier Steps Out of Xi's Shadow as Economic Fixer |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/china-premier-li-keqiang-xi-jinping-11652277107 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230531130648/https://www.wsj.com/articles/china-premier-li-keqiang-xi-jinping-11652277107 |archive-date=31 May 2023 |access-date=17 October 2022 |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |language=en-US |issn=0099-9660}} February 2014 oversaw the creation of the [[Central Cyberspace Affairs Commission|Central Leading Group for Cybersecurity and Informatization]] with Xi as its leader. The State Internet Information Office (SIIO), previously under the [[State Council Information Office]] (SCIO), was transferred to the central leading group and renamed in English into the Cyberspace Administration of China.{{Cite web |last1=Attrill |first1=Nathan |last2=Fritz |first2=Audrey |date=24 November 2021 |title=China's cyber vision: How the Cyberspace Administration of China is building a new consensus on global internet governance |url=https://ad-aspi.s3.ap-southeast-2.amazonaws.com/2021-11/Chinas%20cyber%20vision.pdf?VersionId=M0ePH4Ij3w7WRJrLlhyxVvt269MWSYOs |access-date=29 December 2022 |website=[[Australian Strategic Policy Institute]] |archive-date=29 December 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221229145556/https://ad-aspi.s3.ap-southeast-2.amazonaws.com/2021-11/Chinas%20cyber%20vision.pdf?VersionId=M0ePH4Ij3w7WRJrLlhyxVvt269MWSYOs |url-status=live}} As part of managing the financial system, the [[Financial Stability and Development Committee]], a State Council body, was established in 2017. Chaired by vice premier [[Liu He (politician)|Liu He]] during its existence, the committee was disestablished by the newly established Central Financial Commission during the 2023 Party and state reforms.{{Cite news |last1=Chen |first1=Laurie |last2=Tang |first2=Ziyi |date=16 March 2023 |title=China to create powerful financial watchdog run by Communist Party |work=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/china-create-powerful-financial-watchdog-run-by-communist-party-2023-03-16/ |access-date=17 March 2023 |archive-date=16 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230316162813/https://www.reuters.com/world/china/china-create-powerful-financial-watchdog-run-by-communist-party-2023-03-16/ |url-status=live}} Xi has increased the role of the [[Central Financial and Economic Affairs Commission]] at the expense of the [[State Council of China|State Council]].{{Cite news |last=Mitchell |first=Tom |date=25 July 2016 |title=Xi's China: The rise of party politics |url=https://www.ft.com/content/57371736-4b69-11e6-88c5-db83e98a590a |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221211221251/https://www.ft.com/content/57371736-4b69-11e6-88c5-db83e98a590a |archive-date=11 December 2022 |access-date=31 January 2020 |work=[[Financial Times]]}} 2018 has seen the [[deepening the reform of the Party and state institutions]]. In that year, several [[Leading Small Group|central leading groups]] including [[Central Comprehensively Deepening Reforms Commission|reform]], cyberspace affairs, [[Central Financial and Economic Affairs Commission|finance and economics]], and [[Central Foreign Affairs Commission|foreign affairs]] were upgraded to commissions.{{Cite news |last=Buckley |first=Chris |date=2018-03-21 |title=China Gives Communist Party More Control Over Policy and Media |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/03/21/world/asia/china-communist-party-xi-jinping.html |access-date=2022-12-29 |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=12 November 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211112211222/https://www.nytimes.com/2018/03/21/world/asia/china-communist-party-xi-jinping.html |url-status=live}} The powers of the Central Propaganda Department was strengthened, which now oversaw the newly established [[China Media Group]] (CMG). Two State Council departments. one dealing with [[Overseas Chinese Affairs Office|overseas Chinese]], and other one dealing with [[State Administration for Religious Affairs|religious affairs]], were merged into the [[United Front Work Department]] of the CCP while another commission dealing with [[National Ethnic Affairs Commission|ethnic affairs]] was brought under formal UFWD leadership. In 2020, all elections at all levels of the [[System of people's congress|people's congress system]] and NPC were mandated to adhere to the leadership of the CCP.{{Cite journal |last1=Hao |first1=Mingsong |last2=Ke |first2=Xiwang |date=5 July 2023 |title=Personal Networks and Grassroots Election Participation in China: Findings from the Chinese General Social Survey |journal=[[Journal of Chinese Political Science]] |language=en |volume=29 |issue=1 |pages=159–184 |doi=10.1007/s11366-023-09861-3 |issn=1080-6954 |doi-access=free}} 2023 has seen further reforms to the CCP and state bureaucracy called the [[plan on reforming Party and state institutions]], which included the strengthening of Party control over the financial and technology domains.{{Cite news |last1=Wong |first1=Chun Han |last2=Zhai |first2=Keith |date=16 March 2023 |title=China's Communist Party Overhaul Deepens Control Over Finance, Technology |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/chinas-communist-party-overhaul-deepens-control-over-finance-technology-6acdd43c?mod=world_lead_pos5 |access-date=16 March 2023 |archive-date=16 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230316221649/https://www.wsj.com/articles/chinas-communist-party-overhaul-deepens-control-over-finance-technology-6acdd43c?mod=world_lead_pos5 |url-status=live}} This included the creation of two CCP bodies for overseeing finance; the [[Central Financial Commission]] (CFC), as well as the revival of the [[Central Financial Work Commission]] (CFWC) that was previously dissolved in 2002. Additionally, a new CCP [[Central Science and Technology Commission]] would be established to broadly oversee the technology sector, while a newly created [[Society Work Department]] was tasked with CCP interactions with several sectors, including civic groups, chambers of commerce and industry groups, as well as handling public petition and grievance work. Regulatory bodies saw large overhauls.{{Cite news |date=7 March 2023 |title=China Overhauls Financial Regulatory Regime to Control Risks |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-03-07/china-to-set-up-enlarged-financial-regulatory-authority |access-date=17 March 2023 |archive-date=4 April 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230404200523/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-03-07/china-to-set-up-enlarged-financial-regulatory-authority |url-status=live}} Several regulatory responsibilities were also transferred from the [[People's Bank of China]] (PBC) to another regulatory body, while the PBC reopened offices around the country that were closed in a previous reorganization.{{Cite news |last1=Bradsher |first1=Keith |last2=Che |first2=Chang |date=2023-03-10 |title=Why China Is Tightening Its Oversight of Banking and Tech |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/03/09/business/china-peoples-congress-financial-regulation.html |access-date=2023-03-16 |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=16 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230316221649/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/03/09/business/china-peoples-congress-financial-regulation.html |url-status=live}} In 2024, the CCP's role was strengthened further with the [[Organic Law of the State Council]] amended to add a clause about following CCP ideology and policies.{{Cite news |date=11 March 2024 |title=China passes law granting Communist Party more control over cabinet |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/china-passes-law-granting-communist-party-more-control-over-cabinet-2024-03-11/ |access-date=11 March 2024 |work=[[Reuters]]|author-first1=Mei Mei|author-last1=Chu|author-first2=Laurie|author-last2=Chen}} ====Legal reforms==== {{see also|Xi Jinping Thought on the Rule of Law}}{{Quote box | quote = Efforts should be made to enable the people to see that justice is served in every judicial case. | source = — Xi Jinping during a speech in November 2020{{cite web | title=习近平出席中央全面依法治国工作会议并发表重要讲话 | website=Chinadaily.com.cn | date=18 Nov 2020 | url=https://language.chinadaily.com.cn/a/202011/18/WS5fb4ad35a31024ad0ba94eb8.html | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210119151727/https://language.chinadaily.com.cn/a/202011/18/WS5fb4ad35a31024ad0ba94eb8.html | archive-date=19 Jan 2021 | url-status=live | access-date=21 Sep 2023}} | align = right | width = 25em }} The party under Xi announced a raft of [[Law reform|legal reforms]] at the Fourth Plenum held in the fall 2014, and he called for "[[Rule of law in China|Chinese socialistic rule of law]]" immediately afterwards. The party aimed to reform the legal system, which had been perceived as ineffective at delivering justice and affected by corruption, local government interference and lack of constitutional oversight.
