The plenum, while emphasizing the absolute leadership of the party, also called for a greater role of the constitution in the affairs of state and a strengthening of the role of the National People's Congress Standing Committee in interpreting the constitution.{{cite news|url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/article/1623286/communist-party-pledges-greater-role-constitution-rights-fourth-plenum|title=Communist Party pledges greater role for constitution, rights in fourth plenum|last1=Zhou|first1=Laura|date=24 October 2014|work=[[South China Morning Post]]|access-date=7 September 2019|last2=Huang|first2=Cary|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181030035405/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/article/1623286/communist-party-pledges-greater-role-constitution-rights-fourth-plenum|archive-date=30 October 2018|url-status=live}} It also called for more transparency in legal proceedings, more involvement of ordinary citizens in the legislative process, and an overall "professionalization" of the legal workforce. The party also planned to institute cross-jurisdictional circuit legal tribunals as well as giving provinces consolidated administrative oversight over lower level legal resources, which is intended to reduce local government involvement in legal proceedings.{{cite news |last1=Doyon |first1=Jerome |last2=Winckler |first2=Hugo |date=20 November 2014 |title=The Fourth Plenum, Party Officials and Local Courts |work=[[Jamestown Foundation]] |publisher= |url=https://jamestown.org/program/the-fourth-plenum-party-officials-and-local-courts/ |access-date=7 September 2019 |archive-date=18 May 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200518221059/https://jamestown.org/program/the-fourth-plenum-party-officials-and-local-courts/ |url-status=live}} There have been several reforms to the court system under Xi, including transferring the authority over local court finance and personnel from local governments to the provincial-level , establishing a personnel quota system, emphasizing "lifetime accountability" for judges for their judicial decisions, and specification of case transferring procedures between various courts.{{Cite web |last=Li |first=Sitao |date=2021-11-17 |title=The irony and efficacy of China’s judicial reforms |url=https://eastasiaforum.org/2021/11/17/the-irony-and-efficacy-of-chinas-judicial-reforms/ |access-date=2025-10-31 |website=East Asia Forum |language=en}} In 2014, China started efforts to pass a unified [[Civil Code of the People's Republic of China|Civil Code]], which was eventually adopted in 2020.{{Cite news |last=Zhai |first=Keith |last2=Crossley |first2=Gabriel |last3=Tian |first3=Yew Lun |date=21 May 2020 |title=China set to implement its first civil code, as private investment slows |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/business/china-set-to-implement-its-first-civil-code-as-private-investment-slows-idUSKBN22X0T9/ |access-date=31 October 2025 |work=[[Reuters]]}} In 2015, Xi's administration oversaw the establishment of two circuit courts under the Supreme People's Court, followed by four other circuit courts being established in 2016.{{Cite web |last=Rudolf |first=Moritz |title=Xi Jinping Thought on the Rule of Law |url=https://www.swp-berlin.org/publikation/xi-jinping-thought-on-the-rule-of-law |access-date=2025-10-31 |website=Stiftung Wissenschaft und Politik (SWP) |language=de |doi=10.18449/2021c28/}} Under, an increasing share of laws passed by the NPC explicitly affirmed the leadership of the CCP, with the share increasing from 4% in 2018 to nearly 70% in 2024.{{Cite news |last=Xiao |first=Josh |date=16 October 2024 |title=Xi's Stronger Grip on Legislature Shows Lack of Checks on Power |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2024-10-15/xi-s-stronger-grip-on-legislature-shows-lack-of-checks-on-power |access-date=3 August 2025 |work=[[Bloomberg News]]}} ====Military reforms==== {{see also|Modernization of the People's Liberation Army|Deepening National Defense and Military Reform|Xi Jinping Thought on Strengthening the Military}}Since taking power in 2012, Xi has undertaken an overhaul of the [[People's Liberation Army]], including both political reform and its modernization.{{Cite news|url=https://www.reuters.com/investigates/special-report/china-army-xi/|title=How China is replacing America as Asia's military titan|last1=Lague|first1=David|date=23 April 2019|work=[[Reuters]]|access-date=10 January 2020|last2=Lim|first2=Benjamin Kang|archive-date=21 January 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121184056/https://www.reuters.com/investigates/special-report/china-army-xi/|url-status=live}} [[Military-civil fusion]] has advanced under Xi.{{Cite journal |last=Bitzinger |first=Richard A. |date=2021 |title=China's Shift from Civil-Military Integration to Military-Civil Fusion |url= |journal=Asia Policy |language=en |volume=28 |issue=1 |pages=5–24 |doi=10.1353/asp.2021.0001 |s2cid=234121234 |issn=1559-2960}}{{cite journal |last1=B. Kania |first1=Elsa |last2=Laskai |first2=Lorand |date=28 January 2021 |title=Myths and Realities of China's Military-Civil Fusion Strategy |url=https://www.cnas.org/publications/reports/myths-and-realities-of-chinas-military-civil-fusion-strategy |website=[[Center for a New American Security]] |jstor=resrep28654 |jstor-access=free |access-date=30 August 2022 |archive-date=16 February 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230216154559/https://www.cnas.org/publications/reports/myths-and-realities-of-chinas-military-civil-fusion-strategy |url-status=live}} Xi has been active in his participation in military affairs, taking a direct hands-on approach to military reform. In addition to being the chairman of the CMC and leader of the [[Central Leading Group for Military Reform]] founded in 2014 to oversee comprehensive military reforms, Xi has delivered numerous high-profile pronouncements vowing to clean up malfeasance and complacency in the military. Xi has repeatedly warned that the depoliticization of the PLA from the CCP would lead to a collapse similar to that of the Soviet Union.{{Cite news |last=Fifield |first=Anna |author-link=Anna Fifield |date=29 September 2019 |title=China's Communist Party has one more reason to celebrate – a year longer in power than the U.S.S.R. |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/asia_pacific/chinas-communist-party-has-one-more-reason-to-celebrate--a-year-longer-in-power-than-the-soviet-union/2019/09/28/993bb832-dfc0-11e9-be7f-4cc85017c36f_story.html |access-date=31 January 2020 |archive-date=1 February 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200201101821/https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/asia_pacific/chinas-communist-party-has-one-more-reason-to-celebrate--a-year-longer-in-power-than-the-soviet-union/2019/09/28/993bb832-dfc0-11e9-be7f-4cc85017c36f_story.html |url-status=live}} Xi held the New Gutian Conference in 2014, gathering China's top military officers, re-emphasizing the principle of "[[The Party commands the gun|the party has absolute control over the army]]" first established by Mao at the 1929 [[Gutian Congress]].{{cite news|url=http://china.dwnews.com/news/2014-11-04/59616798.html|last1=Meng|first1=Chuan|date=4 November 2014|work=[[Duowei News]]|access-date=8 September 2019|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150121184703/http://china.dwnews.com/news/2014-11-04/59616798.html|archive-date=21 January 2015|language=zh-hans|script-title=zh:习近平军中"亮剑" 新古田会议一箭多雕|trans-title=Xi Jinping And Central Army's New "Bright Sword" Conference In Gutian Killed Many Birds With Only A Single Stone}} {{Quote box | quote = In the USSR, where the military was depoliticized, separated from the party and nationalized, the party was disarmed. When the Soviet Union came to crisis point, a big party was gone just like that. Proportionally, the Soviet Communist Party had more members than we do, but nobody was man enough to stand up and resist. | source = — Xi Jinping during a speech{{Cite news |last=Grammaticas |first=Damian |date=14 March 2013 |title=President Xi Jinping: A man with a dream |language=en-GB |work=[[BBC News]] |publisher= |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-21790384 |access-date=31 January 2020 |archive-date=20 May 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200520161800/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-21790384 |url-status=live}} | align = right | width = 25em }} Xi announced a reduction of 300,000 troops from the PLA in 2015, bringing its size to 2 million troops. Xi described this as a gesture of peace, while analysts such as Rory Medcalf have said that the cut was done to reduce costs as well as part of PLA's modernization.{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2015/09/03/world/asia/beijing-turns-into-ghost-town-as-it-gears-up-for-military-parade.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150902221802/http://www.nytimes.com/2015/09/03/world/asia/beijing-turns-into-ghost-town-as-it-gears-up-for-military-parade.html |archive-date=2 September 2015 |url-access=limited |url-status=live|title=China Announces Cuts of 300,000 Troops at Military Parade Showing Its Might|last1=Wong|first1=Edward|date=2 September 2015|work=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=10 January 2020|last2=Perlez|first2=Jane|language=en-US|issn=0362-4331|last3=Buckley|first3=Chris}} In November 2015, the Central Leading Group for Military Reform annunced extensive reforms in the PLA called [[Deepening National Defense and Military Reform]].{{Cite news |last=Chan |first=Minnie |date=25 November 2015 |title=China hits the launch button for massive PLA shake-up to create a modern, nimble force |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy-defence/article/1883071/china-hits-launch-button-massive-pla-shake |access-date=5 April 2022 |work=[[South China Morning Post]]}} In 2016, Xi reduced the number of [[Theater command|theater commands of the PLA]] from seven to five.{{Cite news|url=https://thediplomat.com/2016/02/its-official-chinas-military-has-5-new-theater-commands/|title=It's Official: China's Military Has 5 New Theater Commands|last=Tiezzi|first=Shannon|date=2 February 2016|work=[[The Diplomat (magazine)|The Diplomat]]|access-date=10 January 2020|archive-date=6 August 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200806062854/https://thediplomat.com/2016/02/its-official-chinas-military-has-5-new-theater-commands/|url-status=live}} He has also abolished the four autonomous general departments of the PLA, replacing them with 15 agencies directly reporting to the CMC. Two new branches of the PLA were created under his reforms, the [[People's Liberation Army Strategic Support Force|Strategic Support Force]]{{Cite news|url=https://thediplomat.com/2017/02/chinas-strategic-support-force-a-force-for-innovation/|title=China's Strategic Support Force: A Force for Innovation?|last=Kania|first=Elsa|date=18 February 2017|work=[[The Diplomat (magazine)|The Diplomat]]|access-date=31 January 2020|archive-date=11 November 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201111233353/https://thediplomat.com/2017/02/chinas-strategic-support-force-a-force-for-innovation/|url-status=live}} and the [[People's Liberation Army Joint Logistics Support Force|Joint Logistics Support Force]].{{Cite news |last=Blanchard |first=Ben |date=14 September 2016 |title=China sets up new logistics force as part of military reforms |work=[[Reuters]] |publisher= |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-defence-idUSKCN11K05F |access-date=31 January 2020 |archive-date=24 December 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201224052700/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-defence-idUSKCN11K05F |url-status=live}} PLA Second Artillery Corps was also upgraded to the [[People's Liberation Army Rocket Force|PLA Rocket Force]].{{cite journal |last=Fisher |first=Richard D. Jr. |date=6 January 2016 |title=China establishes new Rocket Force, Strategic Support Force |journal=Jane's Defence Weekly |location=Surrey, England |publisher=Jane's Information Group |volume=53 |issue=9 |issn=0265-3818 |quote=This report also quotes Chinese expert Song Zhongping saying that the Rocket Force could incorporate 'PLA sea-based missile unit[s] and air-based missile unit[s]'.}} In March 2016, the CMC announced the [[Cessation of paid services by the Chinese military|abolishment of paid services]] by the PLA and the [[People's Armed Police|People’s Armed Police]] (PAP), meaning activities like military-run hospitals and hotels open to the public, by 2019.{{Cite news |date=28 March 2016 |title=China military to end paid-for services within three years |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/world/china-military-to-end-paid-for-services-within-three-years-idUSKCN0WU03V/ |access-date=27 October 2025 |work=[[Reuters]]}} On 21 April 2016, Xi was named [[Supreme Military Command of the People's Republic of China|commander-in-chief]] of the country's new Joint Operations Command Center of the PLA.{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2016/apr/21/xi-jinping-named-commander-in-chief-chinese-state-media|title=Xi Jinping named as 'commander in chief' by Chinese state media|date=21 April 2016|work=[[The Guardian]]|access-date=25 April 2016|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160425012825/http://www.theguardian.com/world/2016/apr/21/xi-jinping-named-commander-in-chief-chinese-state-media|archive-date=25 April 2016}}{{cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/asia/chinese-president-xi-jinping-commander-in-chief-military-armed-forces-a6997396.html|title=Chinese President Xi Jinping named as military's 'commander-in-chief'|last1=Kayleigh|first1=Lewis|date=23 April 2016|work=[[The Independent]]|access-date=25 April 2016|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160427091000/http://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/asia/chinese-president-xi-jinping-commander-in-chief-military-armed-forces-a6997396.html|archive-date=27 April 2016}} Some analysts interpreted this move as an attempt to display strength and strong leadership and as being more "political than military."{{cite news|url=http://www.chinatopix.com/articles/84000/20160422/president-xi-jinping-is-new-commander-in-chief-of-the-military.htm|title=President Xi Jinping is New Commander-in-Chief of the Military|last1=Sison|first1=Desiree|date=22 April 2016|work=China Topix|access-date=25 April 2016|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160423120421/http://www.chinatopix.com/articles/84000/20160422/president-xi-jinping-is-new-commander-in-chief-of-the-military.htm|archive-date=23 April 2016}} According to Ni Lexiong, a military affairs expert, Xi "not only controls the military but also does it in an absolute manner, and that in wartime, he is ready to command personally."{{cite news|url=http://www.columbiatribune.com/news/perspectives/china-s-xi-moves-to-take-more-direct-command-over/article_b5e96afb-97e6-533e-b33e-db5beab72bf3.html|title=China's Xi moves to take more direct command over military|date=24 April 2016|work=[[Columbia Daily Tribune]]|access-date=25 April 2016|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160425105101/http://www.columbiatribune.com/news/perspectives/china-s-xi-moves-to-take-more-direct-command-over/article_b5e96afb-97e6-533e-b33e-db5beab72bf3.html|archive-date=25 April 2016}} According to a [[University of California, San Diego]] expert on Chinese military, Xi "has been able to take political control of the military to an extent that exceeds what Mao and Deng have done."{{cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/10/11/world/asia/xi-jinping-military-china-purge.html|title=Xi Jinping Presses Military Overhaul, and Two Generals Disappear|last1=Buckley|first1=Chris|date=11 October 2017|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=26 October 2017|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171025083913/https://www.nytimes.com/2017/10/11/world/asia/xi-jinping-military-china-purge.html|archive-date=25 October 2017|last2=Myers|first2=Steven Lee}} In 2018, the PAP was placed under the sole control of the CMC; the PAP was previously under the joint command of the CMC and the State Council through the Ministry of Public Security.{{cite book |last=Wuthnow |first=Joel |url=https://inss.ndu.edu/Portals/82/China%20SP%2014%20Final%20for%20Web.pdf |title=China's Other Army: The People's Armed Police in an Era of Reform |date=16 April 2019 |publisher=Institute for National Strategic Studies |location=Washington |access-date=3 October 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200704193636/https://inss.ndu.edu/Portals/82/China%20SP%2014%20Final%20for%20Web.pdf |archive-date=4 July 2020 |url-status=live}}{{rp|15}} The PLA went through further extensive reforms in 2024; the Strategic Support Force was dissolved and the [[People's Liberation Army Aerospace Force|Aerospace Force]], the [[People's Liberation Army Cyberspace Force|Cyberspace Force]] and the [[People's Liberation Army Information Support Force|Information Support Force]] was established as arms of the PLA, joining the Joint Logistics Support Force.{{Cite news |date=2024-04-19 |title=Xi Orders China's Biggest Military Reorganization Since 2015 |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2024-04-19/xi-orders-china-s-biggest-military-reorganization-since-2015 |access-date=2024-04-19 |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |language=en}} Under Xi, [[Military budget of China|China's official military budget]] has more than doubled,{{Cite news |last1=Han |first1=Chen |last2=Allen-Ebrahimian |first2=Bethany |date=2022-10-15 |title=What China looks like after a decade of Xi Jinping's rule |url=https://www.axios.com/2022/10/15/china-party-congress-2022-xi-jinping-third-term |access-date=2023-04-19 |website=[[Axios (website)|Axios]] |language=en |archive-date=19 April 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230419151133/https://www.axios.com/2022/10/15/china-party-congress-2022-xi-jinping-third-term |url-status=live}} reaching a record $246 billion in 2025.{{Cite news |last1=Chen |first1=Laurie |last2=Torode |first2=Greg |date=5 March 2025 |title=China maintains defence spending increase at 7.2% amid roiling geopolitical tensions |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/china-maintains-defence-spending-increase-72-2025-03-05/ |access-date=31 March 2025 |work=[[Reuters]]}} Though predating Xi, his administration has taken a more assertive stance towards maritime affairs, and has boosted CCP control over the maritime security forces.{{Cite web |last=Miura |first=Kacie |title=The Domestic Sources of China's Maritime Assertiveness Under Xi Jinping |url=https://www.wilsoncenter.org/sites/default/files/media/uploads/documents/Miura_The%20Domestic%20Sources%20of%20China%27s%20Maritime%20Assertiveness%20Under%20Xi%20Jinping.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230519151838/https://www.wilsoncenter.org/sites/default/files/media/uploads/documents/Miura_The%20Domestic%20Sources%20of%20China%27s%20Maritime%20Assertiveness%20Under%20Xi%20Jinping.pdf |archive-date=19 May 2023 |access-date=19 May 2023 |website=[[Woodrow Wilson International Center for Scholars]]}} The [[People's Liberation Army Navy|PLA Navy]] has grown rapidly under Xi, with China adding more warships, submarines, support ships and major amphibious vessels during certain years than the United Kingdom or the United States did.{{Cite news |last1=Lague |first1=David |last2=Kang Lim |first2=Benjamin |date=30 April 2019 |title=China's vast fleet is tipping the balance in the Pacific |work=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/investigates/special-report/china-army-navy/ |access-date=19 April 2023 |archive-date=16 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220516230034/https://www.reuters.com/investigates/special-report/china-army-navy/ |url-status=live}} In 2017, China established the navy's first [[People's Liberation Army Support Base in Djibouti|overseas base in Djibouti]].{{Cite journal |last1=Sun |first1=Degang |last2=Zoubir |first2=Yahia H. |date=2021-07-04 |title=Securing China's 'Latent Power': The Dragon's Anchorage in Djibouti |journal=[[Journal of Contemporary China]] |language=en |volume=30 |issue=130 |pages=677–692 |doi=10.1080/10670564.2020.1852734 |issn=1067-0564 |s2cid=229393446}} Xi has also undertaken an expansion of China's nuclear arsenal, with him calling China to "establish a strong system of strategic deterrence." The [[Federation of American Scientists]] (FAS) has estimated China's total amount of nuclear arsenals to be 600 in 2025, with the [[United States Department of Defense|US Department of Defense]] estimating that China's arsenal could reach 1,000 by 2030.{{Cite journal |last1=Kristensen |first1=Hans M. |author-link1=Hans M. Kristensen |last2=Korda |first2=Matt |last3=Johns |first3=Eliana |last4=Knight |first4=Mackenzie |date=2025-03-04 |title=Chinese nuclear weapons, 2025 |journal=[[Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists]] |language=en |volume=81 |issue=2 |pages=135–160 |bibcode=2025BuAtS..81b.135K |doi=10.1080/00963402.2025.2467011 |issn=0096-3402 |doi-access=free}} ===Foreign policy=== {{See also|Foreign policy of Xi Jinping|Xi Jinping Thought on Diplomacy|List of international trips made by Xi Jinping}} [[File:World leaders attending the 2025 China Victory Day Parade (2).jpg|thumb|220x220px|Xi Jinping together with North Korean leader [[Kim Jong Un]], Russian President [[Vladimir Putin]] and Pakistani Prime Minister [[Shehbaz Sharif]] during the [[2025 China Victory Day Parade]]]] [[File:2017 G20 Hamburg summit leaders group photo.jpg|thumb|World leaders assemble for "family photo" at [[2017 G20 Hamburg summit|G20 summit]] in Hamburg.]] [[File:President Donald Trump greets Chinese President Xi Jinping before a bilateral meeting at the Gimhae International Airport terminal (54890669668).jpg|thumb|Xi Jinping with US President [[Donald Trump]] at the [[APEC South Korea 2025|APEC summit]] in Busan, October 2025]] Xi has taken a harder line on security issues as well as foreign affairs, projecting a more [[Chinese nationalism|nationalistic]] and assertive China on the world stage.{{Cite news |title=Xi Jinping has nurtured an ugly form of Chinese nationalism |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |url=https://www.economist.com/china/2022/07/13/xi-jinping-has-nurtured-an-ugly-form-of-chinese-nationalism |access-date=11 August 2022 |issn=0013-0613 |archive-date=11 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220811010009/https://www.economist.com/china/2022/07/13/xi-jinping-has-nurtured-an-ugly-form-of-chinese-nationalism |url-status=live}} His political program calls for a China more united and confident of its own value system and political structure.{{cite news|url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/article/1586307/xi-jinping-rules-out-western-style-political-reform-china?page=all|title=Xi Jinping rules out Western-style political reform for China|last1=Meng|first1=Angela|date=6 September 2014|work=[[South China Morning Post]]|access-date=7 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180818085415/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/article/1586307/xi-jinping-rules-out-western-style-political-reform-china?page=all|archive-date=18 August 2018|url-status=live}} Foreign analysts and observers have frequently said that Xi's main foreign policy objective is to restore China's position on the global stage as a [[great power]].{{Cite news |last=Buckley |first=Chris |date=26 February 2018 |title=Xi Jinping Thought Explained: A New Ideology for a New Era |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/02/26/world/asia/xi-jinping-thought-explained-a-new-ideology-for-a-new-era.html |access-date=21 August 2022 |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=31 October 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221031175407/https://www.nytimes.com/2018/02/26/world/asia/xi-jinping-thought-explained-a-new-ideology-for-a-new-era.html |url-status=live}}{{Cite journal |last1=Barrass |first1=Gordon |last2=Inkster |first2=Nigel |date=2 January 2018 |title=Xi Jinping: The Strategist Behind the Dream |journal=Survival |volume=60 |issue=1 |pages=41–68 |doi=10.1080/00396338.2018.1427363 |s2cid=158856300 |issn=0039-6338}} Xi advocates "baseline thinking" in China's foreign policy: setting explicit red lines that other countries must not cross.{{Cite book |last=Zhao |first=Suisheng |title=The Dragon Roars Back: Transformational Leaders and Dynamics of Chinese Foreign Policy |date=2023 |publisher=[[Stanford University Press]] |isbn=978-1-5036-3088-8 |location=Stanford, California |page=86 |doi=10.1515/9781503634152 |oclc=1331741429 |author-link=Suisheng Zhao}} In the Chinese perspective, these tough stances on baseline issues reduce strategic uncertainty, preventing other nations from misjudging China's positions or underestimating China's resolve in asserting what it perceives to be in its national interest. Xi stated during the 20th CCP National Congress that he wanted to ensure China "leads the world in terms of [[Comprehensive National Power|composite national strength]] and international influence" by 2049.{{Cite news |date=26 October 2022 |title=Xi's Vow of World Dominance by 2049 Sends Chill Through Markets |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2022-10-26/xi-s-vow-of-world-dominance-by-2049-sends-chill-through-markets |access-date=14 March 2023 |archive-date=18 November 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221118215004/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2022-10-26/xi-s-vow-of-world-dominance-by-2049-sends-chill-through-markets |url-status=live}} Xi's foreign policy thoughts are collectively known as [[Xi Jinping Thought on Diplomacy]].{{Cite news |last=Bishop |first=Bill |date=8 March 2019 |title=Xi's thought on diplomacy is "epoch-making" |url=https://www.axios.com/2019/03/08/xis-diplomacy-epoch-making |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220811202130/https://www.axios.com/2019/03/08/xis-diplomacy-epoch-making |archive-date=11 August 2022 |access-date=11 August 2022 |website=[[Axios (website)|Axios]] |language=en}} Xi has promoted [[major-country diplomacy]], stating that China is already a "big power" and breaking away from previous Chinese leaders who had a more precautious diplomacy.{{Cite journal |last=Hu |first=Weixing |date=2 January 2019 |title=Xi Jinping's 'Major Country Diplomacy': The Role of Leadership in Foreign Policy Transformation |journal=Journal of Contemporary China |volume=28 |issue=115 |pages=1–14 |doi=10.1080/10670564.2018.1497904 |s2cid=158345991 |issn=1067-0564}} Diplomats under Xi have adopted a hawkish foreign policy posture called "[[wolf warrior diplomacy]]" starting from the late 2010s;{{Cite web |last=Auto |first=Hermes |date=5 April 2021 |title=China's 'wolf warrior' diplomats back to howl at Xinjiang critics |url=https://www.straitstimes.com/asia/east-asia/chinas-wolf-warrior-diplomats-back-to-howl-at-xinjiang-critics |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220811202130/https://www.straitstimes.com/asia/east-asia/chinas-wolf-warrior-diplomats-back-to-howl-at-xinjiang-critics |archive-date=11 August 2022 |access-date=11 August 2022 |website=[[The Straits Times]] |language=en}} this form of diplomacy peaked in the early 2020s and declined afterwards.{{Cite news |last=Jost |first=Tyler |date=2024-06-27 |title=Have China's Wolf Warriors Gone Extinct? |url=https://www.foreignaffairs.com/china/have-chinas-wolf-warriors-gone-extinct |access-date=2025-08-01 |work=[[Foreign Affairs]] |language=en-US |issn=0015-7120}} October 2020, he said that "[[the East is rising and the West is declining]]", saying that the power of the Western world was in decline and their COVID-19 response was an example of this, and that China was entering a period of opportunity because of this.{{Cite news |last=Buckley |first=Chris |date=3 March 2021 |title='The East Is Rising': Xi Maps Out China's Post-Covid Ascent |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2021/03/03/world/asia/xi-china-congress.html |access-date=12 August 2022 |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=12 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220812104749/https://www.nytimes.com/2021/03/03/world/asia/xi-china-congress.html |url-status=live}} Xi has frequently alluded to [[Community of Common Destiny|community with a shared future for mankind]], which Chinese diplomats have said doesn't imply an intention to change the international order,{{cite journal |last=Zhang |first=Denghua |date=May 2018 |title=The Concept of 'Community of Common Destiny' in China's Diplomacy: Meaning, Motives and Implications |journal=Asia & the Pacific Policy Studies |volume=5 |issue=2 |pages=196–207 |doi=10.1002/app5.231 |doi-access=free|hdl=1885/255057 |hdl-access=free | issn=2050-2680 }} but which foreign observers say China wants a new order that puts it more at the centre.{{Cite journal |last=Tobin |first=Liza |date=2018 |others=The University Of Texas At Austin, The University Of Texas At Austin |title=Xi's Vision for Transforming Global Governance: A Strategic Challenge for Washington and Its Allies (November 2018) |url=https://tnsr.org/2018/11/xis-vision-for-transforming-global-governance-a-strategic-challenge-for-washington-and-its-allies/ |journal=Texas National Security Review |language=en |doi=10.26153/TSW/863 |doi-access=free |access-date=13 August 2022 |archive-date=13 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813212508/https://tnsr.org/2018/11/xis-vision-for-transforming-global-governance-a-strategic-challenge-for-washington-and-its-allies/ |url-status=live}} Under Xi, China has, along with Russia, also focused on increasing relations with the [[Global North and Global South|Global South]] in order to blunt the effect of [[International sanctions during the Russian invasion of Ukraine|international sanctions]].{{Cite news |last=Seib |first=Gerald F. |date=15 July 2022 |title=Putin and Xi's Bet on the Global South |language=en-US |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/putin-and-xis-bet-on-the-global-south-11657897521 |access-date=21 August 2022 |issn=0099-9660 |archive-date=21 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220821143216/https://www.wsj.com/articles/putin-and-xis-bet-on-the-global-south-11657897521 |url-status=live}} During the Xi administration, China seeks to shape international norms and rules in emerging policy areas where China has an advantage as an early participant.{{Sfn|Tsang|Cheung|2024|p=188}} Xi describes such areas as "new frontiers", and they include policy areas such as space, deep sea, polar regions, the Internet, nuclear safety, anticorruption, and climate change.{{Sfn|Tsang|Cheung|2024|p=188}} Xi has put an emphasis on increasing China's "international discourse power" ({{lang-zh|s=国际话语权|labels=no}}) to create a more favorable global opinion of China in the world.{{Cite web |last=Jones |first=Hugo |date=24 November 2021 |title=China's Quest for Greater 'Discourse Power' |url=https://thediplomat.com/2021/11/chinas-quest-for-greater-discourse-power/ |access-date=29 August 2022 |website=[[The Diplomat (magazine)|The Diplomat]] |language=en-US |archive-date=29 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220829000458/https://thediplomat.com/2021/11/chinas-quest-for-greater-discourse-power/ |url-status=live}} In this pursuit, Xi has emphasised the need to "[[Telling China's stories well|tell China's stories well]]", meaning expanding China's external propaganda ({{lang-zh|s=外宣|labels=no}}) and communications.{{Cite web |date=16 April 2021 |title=Telling China's Story Well |url=https://chinamediaproject.org/the_ccp_dictionary/telling-chinas-story-well/ |access-date=29 August 2022 |website=China Media Project |language=en-US |archive-date=28 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220828224140/https://chinamediaproject.org/the_ccp_dictionary/telling-chinas-story-well/ |url-status=live}} Xi has expanded the focus and scope of the [[United front (China)|united front]], which aims to consolidate support for CCP in non-CCP elements both inside and outside China, and has accordingly expanded the United Front Work Department.{{Cite news |last1=Kynge |first1=James |author-link=James Kynge |last2=Hornby |first2=Lucy |last3=Anderlini |first3=Jamil |date=26 October 2017 |title=Inside China's secret 'magic weapon' for worldwide influence |work=[[Financial Times]] |url=https://www.ft.com/content/fb2b3934-b004-11e7-beba-5521c713abf4 |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221211221240/https://www.ft.com/content/fb2b3934-b004-11e7-beba-5521c713abf4 |archive-date=11 December 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |access-date=21 August 2022}} Xi has unveiled the [[Global Development Initiative]] (GDI),{{Cite news |date=9 June 2022 |title=China's Global Development Initiative is not as innocent as it sounds |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |url=https://www.economist.com/china/2022/06/09/chinas-global-development-initiative-is-not-as-innocent-as-it-sounds |access-date=7 March 2023 |archive-date=6 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230306172739/https://www.economist.com/china/2022/06/09/chinas-global-development-initiative-is-not-as-innocent-as-it-sounds |url-status=live}} the [[Global Security Initiative]] (GSI),{{Cite news |last1=Yao |first1=Kevin |last2=Tian |first2=Yew Lun |date=22 April 2022 |title=China's Xi proposes 'global security initiative', without giving details |language=en |work=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/chinas-xi-says-unilateral-sanctions-will-not-work-2022-04-21/ |access-date=13 August 2022 |archive-date=5 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220805003810/https://www.reuters.com/world/china/chinas-xi-says-unilateral-sanctions-will-not-work-2022-04-21/ |url-status=live}} the [[Global Civilization Initiative|Global Civilisation Initiative]] (GCI), and the [[Global Governance Initiative]] (GGI){{Cite news |last1=Leahy |first1=Joe |last2=Hille |first2=Kathrin |date=1 September 2025 |title=Xi Jinping outlines China's ambition to reshape world order in showpiece summit |url=https://www.ft.com/content/ccf6e56a-0e54-4e0a-9b00-b574455bffff |access-date=2 September 2025 |work=[[Financial Times]]}} in 2021, 2022, 2023 and 2025 respectively, aiming to increase China's influence in the international order.{{Cite news |last=Cai |first=Jane |date=12 June 2023 |title=How China's Xi Jinping promotes mix of Marxism and traditional culture to further Communist Party and 'Chinese dream' |work=[[South China Morning Post]] |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3223805/how-chinas-xi-jinping-promotes-mix-marxism-and-traditional-culture-further-communist-party-and |access-date=12 June 2023 |archive-date=12 June 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230612111635/http://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3223805/how-chinas-xi-jinping-promotes-mix-marxism-and-traditional-culture-further-communist-party-and |url-status=live}} ==== Africa ==== During Xi's administration, China has maintained cordial relationships with each Africa government except [[Eswatini]], which recognizes Taiwan but not the PRC.{{Cite book |last1=Shinn |first1=David H. |author-link=David H. Shinn |title=China's Relations with Africa: a New Era of Strategic Engagement |last2=Eisenman |first2=Joshua |date=2023 |publisher=[[Columbia University Press]] |isbn=978-0-231-21001-0 |location=New York}} Under Xi, China has cut back lending to Africa after fears that African countries couldn't repay their debts to China.{{Cite news |last1=Hille |first1=Kathrin |last2=Pilling |first2=David |date=11 January 2022 |title=China applies brakes to Africa lending |url=https://www.ft.com/content/64b4bcd5-032e-4be5-aa3b-e902f5b1345e |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221211/https://www.ft.com/content/64b4bcd5-032e-4be5-aa3b-e902f5b1345e |archive-date=11 December 2022 |access-date=21 August 2022 |work=[[Financial Times]]}} Xi has also promised that China would write off debts of some African countries.{{Cite web |last=He |first=Laura |date=19 June 2020 |title=China is promising to write off some loans to Africa. It may just be a drop in the ocean |url=https://www.cnn.com/2020/06/19/economy/china-xi-jinping-africa-intl-hnk/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200707050058/https://www.cnn.com/2020/06/19/economy/china-xi-jinping-africa-intl-hnk/index.html |archive-date=7 July 2020 |access-date=21 August 2022 |website=[[CNN]]}} In 2025, China announced that it would remove nearly all import tarrifs on African countries.{{Cite news |last=Miriri |first=Duncan |date=12 June 2025 |title=China says it will remove all tariffs on African exports to boost trade |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/africa/china-says-it-will-remove-all-tariffs-african-exports-boost-trade-2025-06-12/ |access-date=26 October 2025 |work=[[Reuters]]}} ==== Asia ==== [[File:PM Narendra Modi meeting President of China Xi Jinping at the 16th BRICS Summit (2024) 02.jpg|thumb|right|Xi Jinping meeting Indian Prime Minister [[Narendra Modi]] at the [[16th BRICS Summit]] in [[Kazan]], Russia, October 2024]] Under Xi, China initially took a more critical stance on North Korea due to [[North Korea and weapons of mass destruction|its nuclear tests]].{{cite news |last1=Li |first1=Cheng |date=26 September 2014 |title=A New Type of Major Power Relationship? |url=http://www.brookings.edu/research/interviews/2014/09/26-new-type-power-relationship-li |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160708161643/http://www.brookings.edu/research/interviews/2014/09/26-new-type-power-relationship-li |archive-date=8 July 2016 |access-date=7 September 2019 |publisher=[[Brookings Institution]]}} However, starting in 2018, the relations started to improve due to [[Kim–Xi meetings|meetings between Xi and North Korean leader Kim Jong Un]].{{Cite news |last1=Shi |first1=Jiangtao |last2=Chan |first2=Minnie |last3=Zheng |first3=Sarah |date=27 March 2018 |title=Kim's visit evidence China, North Korea remain allies, analysts say |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy-defence/article/2139178/rumours-china-and-north-korea-were-no-longer-allies-put |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190725192608/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy-defence/article/2139178/rumours-china-and-north-korea-were-no-longer-allies-put |archive-date=25 July 2019 |access-date=26 August 2019 |newspaper=[[South China Morning Post]] |language=en}} Xi has initially improved relationships with South Korea, and the two countries signed a [[China–South Korea Free Trade Agreement|free-trade agreement]] in December 2015.{{Cite news |last=Kim |first=Bo-eun |date=26 July 2022 |title=China, South Korea renew service sector talks, opening up a 'win-win for both economies' |url=https://www.scmp.com/economy/global-economy/article/3186792/china-south-korea-renew-service-sector-talks-opening-win-win |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230314194644/https://www.scmp.com/economy/global-economy/article/3186792/china-south-korea-renew-service-sector-talks-opening-win-win |archive-date=14 March 2023 |access-date=14 March 2023 |work=[[South China Morning Post]]}} Starting in 2017, China's relationship with South Korea soured over the [[Terminal High Altitude Area Defense|Terminal High Altitude Area Defence]] (THAAD), a missile defense system, purchase of the latter, but improved after South Korea halted purchase of the THAAD.{{Cite web |last=Park |first=Chan-kyong |date=26 January 2021 |title=Xi charms Moon as China and US compete for an ally in South Korea |url=https://www.scmp.com/week-asia/politics/article/3119363/xi-charms-moon-china-and-us-compete-ally-south-korea |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210321171514/https://www.scmp.com/week-asia/politics/article/3119363/xi-charms-moon-china-and-us-compete-ally-south-korea |archive-date=21 March 2021 |access-date=14 August 2022 |website=[[South China Morning Post]] |language=en}} China–Japan relations have initially soured under Xi's administration; the most thorny issue between the two countries remains the dispute over the [[Senkaku Islands]], which China calls Diaoyu.{{cite news |last1=Osawa |first1=Jun |date=17 December 2013 |title=China's ADIZ over the East China Sea: A "Great Wall in the Sky"? |url=http://www.brookings.edu/research/opinions/2013/12/17-china-air-defense-identification-zone-osawa |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160714181744/http://www.brookings.edu/research/opinions/2013/12/17-china-air-defense-identification-zone-osawa |archive-date=14 July 2016 |access-date=7 September 2019 |publisher=[[Brookings Institution]]}} However, the relations later started to improve, with Xi being invited to visit in 2020,{{Cite web |last=Albert |first=Eleanor |date=16 March 2019 |title=China and Japan's Rapprochement Continues – For Now |url=https://thediplomat.com/2019/05/china-and-japans-rapprochement-continues-for-now/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220705150016/https://thediplomat.com/2019/05/china-and-japans-rapprochement-continues-for-now/ |archive-date=5 July 2022 |access-date=12 August 2022 |website=The Diplomat |language=en-US}} though the trip was later canceled due to the COVID-19 pandemic.{{Cite news |last=Kelly |first=Tim |date=28 February 2021 |title=China's Xi will not make a state visit to Japan this year -Sankei |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/uk-japan-china-xi-idUKKCN2AS0SU |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220812112359/https://www.reuters.com/article/uk-japan-china-xi-idUKKCN2AS0SU |archive-date=12 August 2022 |access-date=12 August 2022 |work=[[Reuters]] |language=en}} Since Xi came to power, China has been rapidly building and militarizing islands in the South China Sea, a decision ''[[Study Times]]'' of the [[Central Party School]] said was personally taken by Xi.{{cite web |date=May 2009 |title=Journal of Current Chinese Affairs |url=http://www.giga-hamburg.de/dl/download.php?d=%2Fcontent%2Fias%2Farchiv%2Fcds%2Fcds_0905.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120217211144/http://www.giga-hamburg.de/dl/download.php?d=%2Fcontent%2Fias%2Farchiv%2Fcds%2Fcds_0905.pdf |archive-date=17 February 2012 |access-date=20 October 2010 |website=giga-hamburg.de}} Relations between China and India had ups and downs under Xi, later deteriorating due to various factors. The two countries had a [[2013 Depsang standoff|standoff in Depsang]] in 2013,{{Cite news |date=6 May 2013 |title=India and China withdraw troops from Himalayan face off|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-india-china-idUSBRE9440B220130506 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924181040/http://www.reuters.com/article/2013/05/06/us-india-china-idUSBRE9440B220130506 |archive-date=24 September 2015 |access-date=29 August 2022 |work=[[Reuters]] |language=en|author-first1=Fayaz|author-last1=Bukhari|author-first2=Satarupa|author-last2=Bhattacharjya}} and [[2017 China–India border standoff|again had a standoff]] over a Chinese construction of a road in [[Doklam]], a territory both claimed by [[Bhutan]], India's ally, and China, in 2017.{{Cite news |date=3 July 2017 |title=China says India violates 1890 agreement in border stand-off |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-india-idUSKBN19O109 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170815072409/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-india-idUSKBN19O109 |archive-date=15 August 2017 |access-date=29 August 2022 |work=[[Reuters]] |language=en}} The most serious crisis in the relationship came when the two countries had a [[2020–2021 China–India skirmishes|deadly clash in 2020]] at the [[Line of Actual Control]], leaving some soldiers dead.{{cite news |last=Janjua |first=Haroon |date=10 May 2020 |title=Chinese and Indian troops injured in border brawl |url=https://www.thetimes.com/world/asia/article/chinese-and-indian-troops-injured-in-border-brawl-3gsfwp37r |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200512042352/https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/chinese-and-indian-troops-injured-in-border-brawl-3gsfwp37r |archive-date=12 May 2020 |access-date=12 May 2020 |newspaper=[[The Times]] |issn=0140-0460}}{{cite news |date=11 May 2020 |title=Indian and Chinese soldiers injured in cross-border fistfight, says Delhi |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2020/may/11/indian-and-chinese-soldiers-injured-in-cross-border-fistfight-says-delhi |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200512035152/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2020/may/11/indian-and-chinese-soldiers-injured-in-cross-border-fistfight-says-delhi |archive-date=12 May 2020 |access-date=12 May 2020 |work=[[The Guardian]] |issn=0261-3077 |agency=Agence France-Presse}} While China has historically been wary of getting closer to the [[Middle East]] countries, Xi has changed this approach, getting closer to both Iran and Saudi Arabia.{{Cite web |last=Vohra |first=Anchal |date=1 February 2022 |title=Xi Jinping Has Transformed China's Middle East Policy |url=https://foreignpolicy.com/2022/02/01/xi-jinping-has-transformed-chinas-middle-east-policy/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220908141215/https://foreignpolicy.com/2022/02/01/xi-jinping-has-transformed-chinas-middle-east-policy/ |archive-date=8 September 2022 |access-date=8 September 2022 |website=[[Foreign Policy]] |language=en-US}} ==== Europe ==== [[File:Emmanuel Macron, Xi Jinping and Ursula von der Leyen in Beijing - 2023.jpg|thumb|Xi Jinping with French President [[Emmanuel Macron]] and European Commission President [[Ursula von der Leyen]] in Beijing, China, April 2023]] China's efforts under Xi has been for the European Union (EU) to stay in a neutral position in their contest with the U.S.{{Cite news |last1=Buckley |first1=Chris |last2=Bradsher |first2=Keith |date=15 April 2022 |title=Faced With a Changed Europe, China Sticks to an Old Script |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/04/15/world/asia/china-europe-russia-ukraine.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813163209/https://www.nytimes.com/2022/04/15/world/asia/china-europe-russia-ukraine.html |archive-date=13 August 2022 |access-date=13 August 2022 |work=[[The New York Times]] |language=en-US |issn=0362-4331}} China and the EU announced the [[Comprehensive Agreement on Investment]] (CAI) in 2020, although the deal was later frozen due to mutual sanctions over Xinjiang.{{Cite news |last=Miller |first=Michael E. |title=China says Macron and Merkel support reviving E.U.-China investment pact. Not so fast. |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/2021/07/06/china-europe-investment-uyghurs/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210926205033/https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/2021/07/06/china-europe-investment-uyghurs/ |archive-date=26 September 2021 |access-date=13 August 2022 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |language=en-US |issn=0190-8286}} Xi has cultivated [[China–Russia relations|stronger relations with Russia]], particularly in the wake of the [[2014 pro-Russian unrest in Ukraine|Ukraine crisis]] of 2014.{{cite news |last1=Baker |first1=Peter |date=8 November 2014 |title=As Russia Draws Closer to China, U.S. Faces a New Challenge |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/11/09/world/vladimir-putin-xi-jinping-form-closer-ties.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190828224309/https://www.nytimes.com/2014/11/09/world/vladimir-putin-xi-jinping-form-closer-ties.html |archive-date=28 August 2019 |access-date=7 September 2019 |work=[[The New York Times]]}} During the [[Russian invasion of Ukraine]], Xi expressed opposition to [[International sanctions during the Russo-Ukrainian War|sanctions against Russia]]{{cite news |last=Ng |first=Teddy |date=2 April 2022 |title=Chinese President Xi Jinping warns it could take decades to repair economic damage caused by Ukraine crisis |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy/article/3172860/chinese-president-xi-jinping-warns-it-could-take-decades |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220515081918/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy/article/3172860/chinese-president-xi-jinping-warns-it-could-take-decades |archive-date=15 May 2022 |access-date=12 August 2022 |work=[[South China Morning Post]]}} and asserted China's [[China and the Russian invasion of Ukraine|support for Russia]] on issues of sovereignty and security, but also said China is committed to respecting "the territorial integrity of all countries",{{Cite web |date=21 April 2022 |title=China's Xi Says International Disputes Should be Resolved Via Dialogue, Not Sanctions |url=https://www.voanews.com/a/china-s-xi-says-international-disputes-should-be-resolved-via-dialogue-not-sanctions/6538799.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220724021113/https://www.voanews.com/a/china-s-xi-says-international-disputes-should-be-resolved-via-dialogue-not-sanctions/6538799.html |archive-date=24 July 2022 |access-date=12 August 2022 |website=[[Voice of America]] |language=en}} while China has cast itself as a neutral party.{{Cite news |last1=Buckley |first1=Chris |last2=Myers |first2=Steven Lee |date=7 March 2022 |title='No Wavering': After Turning to Putin, Xi Faces Hard Wartime Choices for China |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/03/07/world/asia/putin-ukraine-china-xi.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220310022327/https://www.nytimes.com/2022/03/07/world/asia/putin-ukraine-china-xi.html |archive-date=10 March 2022 |access-date=5 April 2022 |work=[[The New York Times]] |language=en-US |issn=0362-4331}} ==== United States ==== Xi has called China–United States relations in the contemporary world a "new type of great-power relations", a phrase the [[Presidency of Barack Obama|Obama administration]] had been reluctant to embrace.{{cite news |last1=Hiroyuki |first1=Akita |date=22 July 2014 |title=A new kind of 'great power relationship'? No thanks, Obama subtly tells China |url=http://asia.nikkei.com/Politics-Economy/International-Relations/A-new-kind-of-great-power-relationship-No-thanks-Obama-subtly-tells-China |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141111181321/http://asia.nikkei.com/Politics-Economy/International-Relations/A-new-kind-of-great-power-relationship-No-thanks-Obama-subtly-tells-China |archive-date=2014-11-11 |access-date=11 November 2014 |newspaper=Nikkei Asian Review}} Xi has indirectly spoken out critically on the U.S. "strategic pivot" to Asia.{{cite news |last=Blanchard |first=Ben |date=3 July 2014 |title=With one eye on Washington, China plots its own Asia 'pivot' |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-diplomacy-idUSKBN0F82GX20140703 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151006010842/http://www.reuters.com/article/2014/07/03/us-china-diplomacy-idUSKBN0F82GX20140703 |archive-date=6 October 2015 |access-date=7 September 2019 |newspaper=Reuters}} Relations with the U.S. soured after Donald Trump became president in 2017.{{Cite news |last=Brown |first=Adrian |date=12 May 2019 |title=China-US trade war: Sino-American ties being torn down brick by brick |url=https://www.aljazeera.com/blogs/asia/2019/05/china-trade-war-sino-american-ties-torn-brick-brick-190512132227395.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190904140940/https://www.aljazeera.com/blogs/asia/2019/05/china-trade-war-sino-american-ties-torn-brick-brick-190512132227395.html |archive-date=4 September 2019 |access-date=19 October 2019 |publisher=[[Al Jazeera English|Al Jazeera]]}} Since 2018, U.S. and China have been engaged in an escalating [[China–United States trade war|trade war]].{{cite news |last=Swanson |first=Ana |date=5 July 2018 |title=Trump's Trade War With China Is Officially Underway |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/07/05/business/china-us-trade-war-trump-tariffs.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180712201844/https://www.nytimes.com/2018/07/05/business/china-us-trade-war-trump-tariffs.html |archive-date=12 July 2018 |access-date=26 May 2019 |work=[[The New York Times]]}} In 2020, the relations further deteriorated due to the COVID-19 pandemic.{{Cite news |date=26 March 2020 |title=Relations between China and America are infected with coronavirus |url=https://www.economist.com/united-states/2020/03/26/relations-between-china-and-america-are-infected-with-coronavirus |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200330025047/https://www.economist.com/united-states/2020/03/26/relations-between-china-and-america-are-infected-with-coronavirus |archive-date=30 March 2020 |access-date=14 August 2022 |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |issn=0013-0613}} ===National security=== Xi has devoted a large amount of work towards national security, calling for a [[Holistic national security|holistic national security architecture]] that encompasses "all aspects of the work of the party and the country."{{Cite news |date=30 December 2020 |title=Xi Sees Threats to China's Security Everywhere Heading Into 2021 |language=en |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2020-12-30/xi-sees-threats-to-china-s-security-everywhere-heading-into-2021 |access-date=21 August 2022 |archive-date=6 July 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210706110907/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2020-12-30/xi-sees-threats-to-china-s-security-everywhere-heading-into-2021 |url-status=live }} He introduced the holistic security concept in 2014, which he defined as taking "the security of the people as compass, political security as its roots, economic security as its pillar, military security and cultural security as its protections, and that relies on the promotion of international security."{{Cite book |last1=Shinn |first1=David H. |title=China's Relations with Africa: a New Era of Strategic Engagement |last2=Eisenman |first2=Joshua |date=2023 |publisher=[[Columbia University Press]] |isbn=978-0-231-21001-0 |location=New York |author-link=David H. Shinn}}{{Rp|page=3}} A new National Security Commission was formed in 2014 with Xi at its helm, which commentators have said would help Xi consolidate over national security affairs.{{Cite news |last=Wuthnow |first=Joel |date=30 June 2016 |title=China's Much-Heralded NSC Has Disappeared |url=https://foreignpolicy.com/2016/06/30/chinas-much-heralded-national-security-council-has-disappeared-nsc-xi-jinping/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190825180323/https://foreignpolicy.com/2016/06/30/chinas-much-heralded-national-security-council-has-disappeared-nsc-xi-jinping/ |archive-date=25 August 2019 |access-date=25 August 2019 |newspaper=[[Foreign Policy (magazine)|Foreign Policy]] |language=en-US}}{{Cite journal |last=Lampton |first=David M. |date=3 September 2015 |title=Xi Jinping and the National Security Commission: policy coordination and political power |journal=Journal of Contemporary China |volume=24 |issue=95 |pages=759–777 |doi=10.1080/10670564.2015.1013366 |issn=1067-0564 |s2cid=154685098 |doi-access=free}} Since its creation by Xi, the National Security Commission has established local security committees, focusing on dissent.{{Cite news |last1=Buckley |first1=Chris |last2=Myers |first2=Steven Lee |date=6 August 2022 |title=In Turbulent Times, Xi Builds a Security Fortress for China, and Himself |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/08/06/world/asia/xi-jinping-china-security.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220807000022/https://www.nytimes.com/2022/08/06/world/asia/xi-jinping-china-security.html/ |archive-date=7 August 2022 |access-date=21 August 2022 |work=[[The New York Times]] |language=en-US |issn=0362-4331}} Xi has championed the [[Fengqiao experience]], calling on officials to contain and resolve conflicts at the grassroots level without needing to involve higher legal bodies.{{Cite news |last=Zhang |first=Phoebe |date=11 November 2023 |title=Mao praised it, Xi Jinping is pushing it: what renewed focus on 'Fengqiao experience' says about China 'in the new era' |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3241179/mao-praised-it-xi-jinping-pushing-it-what-renewed-focus-fengqiao-experience-says-about-china-new-era |access-date=7 March 2025 |work=[[South China Morning Post]]}} In the name of national security, Xi's government has passed numerous laws including a [[Counterespionage Law of the People's Republic of China|counterespionage law]] in 2014,{{Cite news |date=1 November 2014 |title=China passes counter-espionage law |language=en |work=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-lawmaking-spy-idUSKBN0IL2N520141101 |access-date=21 August 2022 |archive-date=3 July 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200703084848/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-lawmaking-spy-idUSKBN0IL2N520141101 |url-status=live }} [[National Security Law of the People's Republic of China|national security]]{{Cite news |last=Wong |first=Chun Han |date=1 July 2015 |title=China Adopts Sweeping National-Security Law |language=en-US |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |url=http://www.wsj.com/articles/china-adopts-sweeping-national-security-law-1435757589 |access-date=21 August 2022 |issn=0099-9660 |archive-date=13 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813073827/https://www.wsj.com/articles/china-adopts-sweeping-national-security-law-1435757589 |url-status=live }} and a [[Counterterrorism Law of the People's Republic of China|counterterrorism law]] in 2015,{{Cite news |last=Blanchard |first=Ben |date=28 December 2015 |title=China passes controversial counter-terrorism law |language=en |work=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-security-idUSKBN0UA07220151228 |access-date=21 August 2022 |archive-date=24 April 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200424234111/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-security-idUSKBN0UA07220151228 |url-status=live }} a [[Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China|cybersecurity law]]{{Cite news |last=Wagner |first=Jack |date=1 June 2017 |title=China's Cybersecurity Law: What You Need to Know |work=[[The Diplomat (magazine)|The Diplomat]] |url=https://thediplomat.com/2017/06/chinas-cybersecurity-law-what-you-need-to-know/ |access-date=21 August 2022 |archive-date=12 December 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181212184441/https://thediplomat.com/2017/06/chinas-cybersecurity-law-what-you-need-to-know/ |url-status=live }} and a law restricting foreign [[Non-governmental organization|NGOs]] in 2016,{{Cite news |last=Wong |first=Edward |date=28 April 2015 |title=Clampdown in China Restricts 7,000 Foreign Organizations |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2016/04/29/world/asia/china-foreign-ngo-law.html |access-date=21 August 2022 |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=4 June 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200604091747/https://www.nytimes.com/2016/04/29/world/asia/china-foreign-ngo-law.html |url-status=live }} a [[National Intelligence Law of the People's Republic of China|national intelligence law]] in 2017,{{Cite news |date=27 June 2017 |title=China passes tough new intelligence law |language=en |work=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-security-lawmaking-idUSKBN19I1FW |access-date=21 August 2022 |archive-date=8 July 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170708131558/http://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-security-lawmaking-idUSKBN19I1FW |url-status=live }} and a [[Data Security Law of the People's Republic of China|data security law]] in 2021.{{Cite news |last=Haldane |first=Matt |date=1 September 2021 |title=What China's new data laws are and their impact on Big Tech |work=[[South China Morning Post]] |url=https://www.scmp.com/tech/policy/article/3147040/what-chinas-new-data-laws-are-and-their-impact-big-tech |access-date=21 August 2022 |archive-date=21 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220821205207/https://www.scmp.com/tech/policy/article/3147040/what-chinas-new-data-laws-are-and-their-impact-big-tech |url-status=live }} Under Xi, [[Mass surveillance in China|China's mass surveillance network]] has dramatically grown, with comprehensive profiles being built for each citizen.{{Cite news |last1=Mozur |first1=Paul |last2=Xiao |first2=Muyi |last3=Liu |first3=John |date=26 June 2022 |title='An Invisible Cage': How China Is Policing the Future |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/06/25/technology/china-surveillance-police.html |access-date=21 August 2022 |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=25 July 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220725021212/https://www.nytimes.com/2022/06/25/technology/china-surveillance-police.html |url-status=live }} [[Grid-style social management in China|Grid-style social management]], which involves subdividing China's counties into smaller zones, and assigning each zone to a person that reports all activity to the local government on a regular basis, has risen in prominence under Xi.{{Cite news |date=6 October 2025 |title=Chinese officials boast a god’s-eye view of towns from above |url=https://www.economist.com/china/2025/10/06/chinese-officials-boast-a-gods-eye-view-of-towns-from-above |access-date=2025-10-27 |work=[[The Economist]] |issn=0013-0613}} Public security spending has increased significantly under Xi, reaching $210 billion in 2020, more than double from the decade prior; this was accompanied by a significant decrease in crime.{{Cite news |date=29 August 2022 |title=China spends more on controlling its 1.4bn people than on defense |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/static/vdata/infographics/china-spends-more-on-controlling-its-1-dot-4bn-people-than-on-defense/ |access-date=27 October 2025 |work=[[Nikkei Asia]]}} ===Hong Kong=== {{See also|Conservatism in Hong Kong#National security law and autocratisation}} [[File:2019-10-01 Demonstration Hong Kong 11.jpg|thumb|[[2019–20 Hong Kong protests|Hong Kong protesters]] throw eggs at Xi Jinping's portrait on [[National Day of the People's Republic of China|National Day]].]]During his leadership, Xi has supported and pursued a greater political and economic integration of [[Hong Kong]] to mainland China, including through projects such as the [[Hong Kong–Zhuhai–Macau Bridge]].{{Cite news |last=Bland |first=Ben |date=2 September 2018 |title=Greater Bay Area: Xi Jinping's other grand plan |work=[[Financial Times]] |url=https://www.ft.com/content/fe5976d8-ab81-11e8-94bd-cba20d67390c |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221211221214/https://www.ft.com/content/fe5976d8-ab81-11e8-94bd-cba20d67390c |archive-date=11 December 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |access-date=11 January 2020}} He has pushed for the [[Guangdong–Hong Kong–Macao Greater Bay Area|Greater Bay Area]] project, which aims to integrate Hong Kong, Macau, and nine other cities in Guangdong.{{Cite web |date=2024-03-03 |title=Young Hong Kongers Who Defied Xi Are Now Partying in China |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2024-03-03/young-hong-kongers-who-defied-xi-are-now-partying-in-china |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20240318022916/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2024-03-03/young-hong-kongers-who-defied-xi-are-now-partying-in-china |archive-date=2024-03-18 |access-date=27 October 2025 |website=[[Bloomberg News]]}} Xi's integration efforts have led to deeper economic links between mainland China and Hong Kong, as well as decreased freedoms and the weakening of Hong Kong's distinct identity from mainland China.{{Cite news |last=Yu |first=Verna |date=5 November 2019 |title=China signals desire to bring Hong Kong under tighter control |work=[[The Guardian]] |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2019/nov/05/chinas-xi-jinping-expresses-high-degree-of-trust-in-hong-kongs-carrie-lam |access-date=11 January 2020 |archive-date=12 November 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201112000411/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2019/nov/05/chinas-xi-jinping-expresses-high-degree-of-trust-in-hong-kongs-carrie-lam |url-status=live }} Many of the views held by the central government and eventually implemented in Hong Kong were outlined in a white paper published by the State Council in 2014 named ''[[The Practice of the 'One Country, Two Systems' Policy in the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region]]'', which outlined that the China's central government has "comprehensive jurisdiction" over Hong Kong.{{Cite news |last1=Buckley |first1=Chris |last2=Wang |first2=Vivian |last3=Ramzy |first3=Austin |date=28 June 2021 |title=Crossing the Red Line: Behind China's Takeover of Hong Kong |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2021/06/28/world/asia/china-hong-kong-security-law.html |access-date=13 August 2022 |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=13 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813084412/https://www.nytimes.com/2021/06/28/world/asia/china-hong-kong-security-law.html |url-status=live }} Under Xi, the Chinese government also declared the [[Sino-British Joint Declaration]] to be legally void. In August 2014, the Standing Committee of the National People's Congress (NPCSC) made a [[2014 NPCSC Decision on Hong Kong|decision]] allowing universal suffrage for the [[2017 Hong Kong Chief Executive election|2017 election]] of the [[Chief Executive of Hong Kong|chief executive of Hong Kong]], also requiring the candidates to "love the country, and love Hong Kong," as well as other measures that ensured the Chinese leadership would be the ultimate decision-maker on the selection, leading to [[2014 Hong Kong protests|protests]],{{Cite news |last1=Buckley |first1=Chris |last2=Forsythe |first2=Michael |date=2014-08-31 |title=China Restricts Voting Reforms for Hong Kong |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/09/01/world/asia/hong-kong-elections.html |access-date=2023-03-18 |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=27 February 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150227110910/http://www.nytimes.com/2014/09/01/world/asia/hong-kong-elections.html?_r=0 |url-status=live }} and the eventual rejection of the reform bill in the Legislative Council due to a walk-out by the [[Pro-Beijing camp (Hong Kong)|pro-Beijing camp]] to delay to vote.{{Cite news |last=Chan |first=Wilfred |date=19 June 2015 |title=Hong Kong legislators reject China-backed reform bill |work=[[CNN]] |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2015/06/18/asia/hong-kong-reform-vote/index.html |access-date=19 March 2023 |archive-date=19 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230319001757/https://edition.cnn.com/2015/06/18/asia/hong-kong-reform-vote/index.html |url-status=live }} In the 2017 chief executive election, [[Carrie Lam]] was victorious, reportedly with the endorsement of the CCP Politburo.{{Cite news |last=Cheng |first=Kris |date=7 February 2017 |title=Carrie Lam is the only leadership contender Beijing supports, state leader Zhang Dejiang reportedly says |work=[[Hong Kong Free Press]] |url=https://hongkongfp.com/2017/02/07/carrie-lam-is-the-only-leadership-contender-beijing-supports-state-leader-zhang-dejiang-reportedly-says/ |access-date=19 March 2023 |archive-date=11 July 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240711081540/https://hongkongfp.com/2017/02/07/carrie-lam-is-the-only-leadership-contender-beijing-supports-state-leader-zhang-dejiang-reportedly-says/ |url-status=live }} Xi supported the [[Government of Hong Kong|Hong Kong Government]] and Carrie Lam against the protesters in the [[2019–2020 Hong Kong protests]], which broke out after a [[2019 Hong Kong extradition bill|proposed bill]] that would allow extraditions to mainland China.{{Cite news |last1=Sin |first1=Noah |last2=Kwok |first2=Donny |date=16 December 2019 |title=China's Xi vows support for Hong Kong leader during 'most difficult' time |publisher=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-hongkong-protests/chinas-xi-vows-support-for-hong-kong-leader-during-most-difficult-time-idUSKBN1YK025 |access-date=11 January 2020 |archive-date=12 November 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201112040524/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-hongkong-protests/chinas-xi-vows-support-for-hong-kong-leader-during-most-difficult-time-idUSKBN1YK025 |url-status=live }} He has defended the [[Hong Kong Police Force|Hong Kong police's]] use of force, saying that "We sternly support the Hong Kong police to take forceful actions in enforcing the law, and the Hong Kong judiciary to punish in accordance with the law those who have committed violent crimes."{{Cite news |last=Zhou |first=Laura |date=14 November 2019 |title=Xi Jinping again backs Hong Kong police use of force in stopping unrest |work=[[South China Morning Post]] |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3037811/xi-jinping-again-backs-hong-kong-police-use-force-stopping |access-date=11 January 2020 |archive-date=22 November 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201122130444/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3037811/xi-jinping-again-backs-hong-kong-police-use-force-stopping |url-status=live }} While visiting [[Macau]] on 20 December 2019 as part of the 20th anniversary of its return to China, Xi warned of foreign forces interfering in Hong Kong and Macau,{{Cite news |date=20 December 2019 |title=China's Xi warns of 'foreign forces' at Macao anniversary |publisher=[[Deutsche Welle]] |url=https://www.dw.com/en/chinas-xi-warns-of-foreign-forces-at-macao-anniversary/a-51747054 |access-date=11 January 2020 |archive-date=11 January 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200111192031/https://www.dw.com/en/chinas-xi-warns-of-foreign-forces-at-macao-anniversary/a-51747054 |url-status=live }} while also hinting that Macau could be a model for Hong Kong to follow.{{Cite news |last1=Siu |first1=Phila |last2=Cheung |first2=Gary |date=19 December 2019 |title=Xi Jinping seen as indirectly lecturing Hong Kong as he tells Macau residents to make 'positive voices' heard and resolve problems with rationality |work=[[South China Morning Post]] |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/hong-kong/politics/article/3042846/macau-handover-anniversary-xi-jinping-tells-residents-make |access-date=11 January 2020 |archive-date=11 January 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200111192029/https://www.scmp.com/news/hong-kong/politics/article/3042846/macau-handover-anniversary-xi-jinping-tells-residents-make |url-status=live }}[[File:庆祝香港回归祖国25周年大会暨香港特别行政区第六届政府就职典礼 20220701-4.png|thumb|Xi swearing in John Lee as chief executive during the 25th anniversary of Hong Kong's return to China]] In 2020, the NPCSC passed a [[2020 Hong Kong national security law|national security law]] in Hong Kong that dramatically expanded government clampdown over the opposition in the city; amongst the measures were the dramatic restriction on political opposition and the creation of a [[Office for Safeguarding National Security of the CPG in the HKSAR|central government office]] outside Hong Kong jurisdiction to oversee the enforcement of the law. This was seen as the culmination of a long-term project under Xi to further closely integrate Hong Kong with the mainland. Xi visited Hong Kong as Chinese leader in 2017 and 2022, in the 20th and 25th anniversary of the handover of Hong Kong respectively.{{Cite news |last1=Yip |first1=Martin |last2=Fraser |first2=Simon |date=30 June 2022 |title=China's President Xi arrives in Hong Kong for handover anniversary |language=en-GB |work=[[BBC News]] |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-61991980 |access-date=13 August 2022 |archive-date=13 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813114744/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-61991980 |url-status=live }} In [[2022 Xi Jinping visit to Hong Kong|his 2022 visit]], he swore in [[John Lee Ka-chiu|John Lee]] as chief executive, a former police officer that was backed by the Chinese government to expand control over the city.{{Cite news |last=Ioanes |first=Ellen |date=2 July 2022 |title=Xi Jinping asserts his power on Hong Kong's handover anniversary |work=[[Vox (website)|Vox]] |url=https://www.vox.com/2022/7/2/23192550/xi-hong-kong-john-lee-china |access-date=13 August 2022 |archive-date=13 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813114742/https://www.vox.com/2022/7/2/23192550/xi-hong-kong-john-lee-china |url-status=live }}{{Cite news |last1=Chan |first1=Ho-him |last2=Riordan |first2=Primrose |date=8 May 2022 |title=Beijing-backed hardliner John Lee chosen as Hong Kong's next leader |work=[[Financial Times]] |url=https://www.ft.com/content/a6466afa-0981-4a82-9787-8c630651d44f |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221211221231/https://www.ft.com/content/a6466afa-0981-4a82-9787-8c630651d44f |archive-date=11 December 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |access-date=13 August 2022}} === Taiwan === {{Further|Cross-Strait relations|First Ma–Xi meeting}} [[File:2015 Ma–Xi Meeting 08.jpg|thumb|Xi Jinping met with then-Taiwanese president [[Ma Ying-jeou]] in November 2015 in their capacity as the leader of mainland China and Taiwan respectively.]] In November 2015, Xi met with Taiwanese president [[Ma Ying-jeou]], which marked the first time the political leaders of both sides of the [[Taiwan Strait]] have met since the end of the [[Chinese Civil War]] in mainland China in 1950.{{cite news |last1=Perlez |first1=Jane |last2=Ramzy |first2=Austin |date=4 November 2015 |title=China, Taiwan and a Meeting After 66 Years |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2015/11/04/world/asia/leaders-of-china-and-taiwan-to-meet-for-first-time-since-1949.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903072421/https://www.nytimes.com/2015/11/04/world/asia/leaders-of-china-and-taiwan-to-meet-for-first-time-since-1949.html |archive-date=3 September 2017 |access-date=1 November 2017 |work=[[The New York Times]]}} Xi said that China and Taiwan are "[[Zhonghua minzu|one family]]" that cannot be pulled apart.{{Cite news |date=7 November 2015 |title=One-minute handshake marks historic meeting between Xi Jinping and Ma Ying-jeou |url=https://www.straitstimes.com/singapore/one-minute-handshake-marks-historic-meeting-between-xi-jinping-and-ma-ying-jeou |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190905182113/https://www.straitstimes.com/singapore/one-minute-handshake-marks-historic-meeting-between-xi-jinping-and-ma-ying-jeou |archive-date=5 September 2019 |access-date=5 September 2019 |work=[[The Straits Times]]}} However, the relations started deteriorating after [[Tsai Ing-wen]] of the [[Democratic Progressive Party]] (DPP) won the [[2016 Taiwanese presidential election|presidential elections in 2016]].{{Cite web |last=Huang |first=Kristin |date=15 June 2021 |title=Timeline: Taiwan's relations with mainland China under Tsai Ing-wen |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy/article/3137231/timeline-cross-strait-relations-under-taiwans-president-tsai |access-date=13 August 2022 |website=[[South China Morning Post]] |language=en |archive-date=23 September 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240923015416/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy/article/3137231/timeline-cross-strait-relations-under-taiwans-president-tsai |url-status=live }} In the 19th Party Congress held in 2017, Xi reaffirmed six of the nine principles that had been affirmed continuously since the 16th Party Congress in 2002, with the notable exception of "Placing hopes on the [[People of Taiwan|Taiwan people]] as a force to help bring about [[Chinese unification|unification]]".{{Cite news |last=Bush |first=Richard C. |date=19 October 2017 |title=What Xi Jinping said about Taiwan at the 19th Party Congress |url=https://www.brookings.edu/blog/order-from-chaos/2017/10/19/what-xi-jinping-said-about-taiwan-at-the-19th-party-congress/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180708013012/https://www.brookings.edu/blog/order-from-chaos/2017/10/19/what-xi-jinping-said-about-taiwan-at-the-19th-party-congress/ |archive-date=8 July 2018 |access-date=8 July 2018 |publisher=[[Brookings Institution]]}} According to the [[Brookings Institution]], Xi used stronger language on potential [[Taiwan independence movement|Taiwan independence]] than his predecessors towards previous DPP governments in Taiwan. Xi said that "we will never allow any person, any organisation, or any political party to split any part of the Chinese territory from China at any time at any form." In January 2019, Xi Jinping called on Taiwan to reject its formal independence from China, saying: "We make no promise to renounce the use of force and reserve the option of taking all necessary means." Those options, he said, could be used against "external interference". Xi also said that they "are willing to create broad space for peaceful reunification, but will leave no room for any form of separatist activities."{{cite news |last=Kuo |first=Lily |date=2 January 2019 |title='All necessary means': Xi Jinping reserves right to use force against Taiwan |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2019/jan/02/all-necessary-means-xi-jinping-reserves-right-to-use-force-against-taiwan |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190822114526/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2019/jan/02/all-necessary-means-xi-jinping-reserves-right-to-use-force-against-taiwan |archive-date=22 August 2019 |access-date=7 September 2019 |work=[[The Guardian]]}}{{cite news |last=Griffiths |first=James |date=2 January 2019 |title=Xi warns Taiwan independence is 'a dead end' |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2019/01/02/asia/xi-jinping-taiwan-tsai-intl/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191003215248/https://edition.cnn.com/2019/01/02/asia/xi-jinping-taiwan-tsai-intl/ |archive-date=3 October 2019 |access-date=7 September 2019 |work=[[CNN]] |publisher=}} President Tsai responded to the speech by saying Taiwan would not accept a [[one country, two systems]] arrangement with the mainland, while stressing the need for all cross-strait negotiations to be on a government-to-government basis.{{cite news |last=Lee |first=Yimou |date=2 January 2019 |title=Taiwan president defiant after China calls for reunification |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-taiwan-president/taiwan-president-defiant-after-china-calls-for-reunification-idUSKCN1OW0FN |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190929044514/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-taiwan-president/taiwan-president-defiant-after-china-calls-for-reunification-idUSKCN1OW0FN |archive-date=29 September 2019 |access-date=7 September 2019 |publisher=[[Reuters]]}} ===Human rights=== {{main|Human rights in China|Antireligious campaigns of the Chinese Communist Party}} According to the [[Human Rights Watch]], Xi has "started a broad and sustained offensive on human rights" since he became leader in 2012.{{Cite book|url=https://www.hrw.org/world-report/2018/country-chapters/china-and-tibet|title=World Report 2018: Rights Trends in China|chapter=China: Events of 2017 |date=9 January 2018|publisher=[[Human Rights Watch]]|language=en|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190828203028/https://www.hrw.org/world-report/2018/country-chapters/china-and-tibet|archive-date=28 August 2019|access-date=7 September 2019}} The HRW also said that repression in China is "at its worst level since the Tiananmen Square massacre."{{Cite
