news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/asia/china-repression-freedom-police-censorship-tiananmen-square-human-rights-watch-a8732501.html|title=Repression in China at worst level since Tiananmen Square, HRW warns|last=Withnall|first=Adam|date=17 January 2019|newspaper=[[The Independent]]|language=en|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190524133120/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/asia/china-repression-freedom-police-censorship-tiananmen-square-human-rights-watch-a8732501.html|archive-date=24 May 2019|access-date=7 September 2019}} Since taking power, Xi has cracked down on grassroots activism, with hundreds being detained.{{Cite news|url=https://www.ft.com/content/b4de464a-7242-11e9-bf5c-6eeb837566c5|archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221211221247/https://www.ft.com/content/b4de464a-7242-11e9-bf5c-6eeb837566c5|archive-date=11 December 2022|url-access=subscription|url-status=live|title=China widens crackdown against grassroot activists|date=9 May 2019|work=[[Financial Times]]|access-date=11 January 2020}} He presided over the [[709 crackdown]] on 9 July 2015, which saw more than 200 lawyers, legal assistants and human rights activists being detained.{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/blogs-china-blog-39974953|title=Wang Quanzhang: The lawyer who simply vanished|last=Sudworth|first=John|date=22 May 2017|publisher=[[BBC News]]|access-date=11 January 2020|archive-date=7 November 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211107070034/https://www.bbc.com/news/blogs-china-blog-39974953|url-status=live}} His term has seen the arrest and imprisonment of activists such as [[Xu Zhiyong]], as well as numerous others who identified with the [[New Citizens' Movement]]. Prominent legal activist [[Pu Zhiqiang]] of the [[Weiquan movement]] was also arrested and detained.{{cite news|url=https://www.bangkokpost.com/world/419842/chinese-dream-turns-sour-for-activists-under-xi-jinping|title=Chinese dream turns sour for activists under Xi Jinping|date=10 July 2014|newspaper=[[Bangkok Post]]|access-date=17 July 2014|archive-date=13 August 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200813235059/https://www.bangkokpost.com/world/419842/chinese-dream-turns-sour-for-activists-under-xi-jinping|url-status=live}} In 2017, the local government of the [[Jiangxi|Jiangxi province]] told [[Christianity in China|Christians]] to replace their pictures of [[Jesus]] with Xi Jinping as part of a general campaign on unofficial churches in the country.{{Cite news|url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/policies-politics/article/2119699/praise-xi-jinping-not-jesus-escape-poverty-christian|title=Replace pictures of Jesus with Xi to escape poverty, Chinese villagers urged|last=Gan|first=Nectar|date=14 November 2017|newspaper=[[South China Morning Post]]|language=en|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190717174448/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/policies-politics/article/2119699/praise-xi-jinping-not-jesus-escape-poverty-christian|archive-date=17 July 2019|access-date=26 July 2019}}{{Cite news|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/worldviews/wp/2017/11/14/jesus-wont-save-you-president-xi-jinping-will-chinese-christians-told/|title=Jesus won't save you – President Xi Jinping will, Chinese Christians told|last=Denyer|first=Simon|date=14 November 2017|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190531100111/https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/worldviews/wp/2017/11/14/jesus-wont-save-you-president-xi-jinping-will-chinese-christians-told/|archive-date=31 May 2019|access-date=7 September 2019}}{{Cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2018/sep/28/we-are-scared-but-we-have-jesus-china-and-its-war-on-christianity|title='We are scared, but we have Jesus': China and its war on Christianity|last=Haas|first=Benjamin|date=28 September 2018|work=[[The Guardian]]|access-date=7 September 2019|language=en-GB|issn=0261-3077|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190823155226/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2018/sep/28/we-are-scared-but-we-have-jesus-china-and-its-war-on-christianity|archive-date=23 August 2019|url-status=live}} According to local social media, officials "transformed them from believing in religion to believing in the party." According to activists, "Xi is waging the most severe systematic [[Christianity in China#Restrictions and international interest|suppression of Christianity]] in the country since religious freedom was written into the Chinese constitution in 1982," and according to pastors and a group that monitors religion in China, has involved "destroying crosses, burning bibles, shutting churches and ordering followers to sign papers renouncing their faith."{{cite news|url=https://www.apnews.com/c09b2ee4b71540c8a7fd6178820c5970|title=Group: Officials destroying crosses, burning bibles in China|last=Bodeen|first=Christopher|date=10 September 2018|publisher=[[Associated Press]]|access-date=7 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191008144601/https://www.apnews.com/c09b2ee4b71540c8a7fd6178820c5970|archive-date=8 October 2019}} Under Xi, the CCP has embraced assimilationist policies towards ethnic minorities, scaling back [[Affirmative action in China|affirmative action]] in the country by 2019,{{Cite news |last=Lau |first=Mimi |date=5 December 2019 |title=From Xinjiang to Ningxia, China's ethnic groups face end to affirmative action in education, taxes, policing |work=[[South China Morning Post]] |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3040577/chinas-ethnic-groups-face-end-affirmative-action-education |access-date=14 March 2023 |archive-date=23 September 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230923224656/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3040577/chinas-ethnic-groups-face-end-affirmative-action-education |url-status=live }} and scrapping a wording in October 2021 that guaranteed the rights of minority children to be educated in their native language, replacing it with one that emphasized teaching the national language.{{Cite news |last=Zhai |first=Keith |date=8 October 2021 |title=China's Communist Party Formally Embraces Assimilationist Approach to Ethnic Minorities |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/chinas-communist-party-formally-embraces-assimilationist-approach-to-ethnic-minorities-11633702544 |access-date=14 March 2023}} In 2020, [[Chen Xiaojiang]] was appointed as head of the [[National Ethnic Affairs Commission]], the first Han Chinese head of the body since 1954.{{cite news |author1=Linda, Lew |date=19 December 2020 |title=China puts Han official in charge of ethnic minority affairs as Beijing steps up push for integration |work=[[South China Morning Post]] |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3114638/china-puts-han-official-charge-ethnic-minority-affairs-beijing |access-date=9 October 2021 |archive-date=21 December 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201221205105/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3114638/china-puts-han-official-charge-ethnic-minority-affairs-beijing |url-status=live }} On 24 June 2022, [[Pan Yue (politician)|Pan Yue]], another Han Chinese, became the head of the commission, with him reportedly holding assimilationist policies toward ethnic minorities.{{Cite news |last=Aaron |first=Glasserman |date=2 March 2023 |title=China's Head of Ethnic Affairs Is Keen to End Minority Culture |work=[[Foreign Policy]] |url=https://foreignpolicy.com/2023/03/02/china-minorities-uyghur-racism/ |access-date=12 March 2023 |archive-date=12 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230312191637/https://foreignpolicy.com/2023/03/02/china-minorities-uyghur-racism/ |url-status=live }} Xi outlined his official views relations between the majority Han Chinese and ethnic minorities by saying "[n]either [[Han chauvinism]] nor [[local ethnic chauvinism]] is conducive to the development of a community for the Chinese nation."{{Cite news |date=28 August 2021 |title=Xi Focus: Xi stresses high-quality development of Party's work on ethnic affairs |work=[[Xinhua News Agency]] |url=http://www.news.cn/english/2021-08/28/c_1310154568.htm |access-date=19 March 2023 |archive-date=11 July 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240711081527/http://www.news.cn/english/2021-08/28/c_1310154568.htm |url-status=live }} ==== Xinjiang ==== {{main|Persecution of Uyghurs in China}} [[File:Xi Jinping Entrance Hall at the XUAR Museum.jpg|thumb|Xi Jinping's picture at the entrance hall of the [[Xinjiang Museum]] in [[Ürümqi]], August 2018]] Following several terrorist attacks in [[Xinjiang]] in 2013 and 2014, the CCP leaders held a secret meeting to find a solution to the attacks,{{Cite magazine |last=Khatchadourian |first=Raffi |date=5 April 2021 |title=Surviving the Crackdown in Xinjiang |magazine=[[The New Yorker]] |url=https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2021/04/12/surviving-the-crackdown-in-xinjiang |access-date=19 March 2023 |archive-date=10 April 2021 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20210410193233/https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2021/04/12/surviving-the-crackdown-in-xinjiang |url-status=live }} leading to Xi to launch the [[Strike Hard Campaign Against Violent Terrorism]] in 2014, which involved mass detention, and surveillance of ethnic [[Uyghurs]] there.{{Cite news|url=https://www.ft.com/content/48508182-d426-11e9-8367-807ebd53ab77|archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221211221202/https://www.ft.com/content/48508182-d426-11e9-8367-807ebd53ab77|archive-date=11 December 2022|url-access=subscription|url-status=live|title=Fear and oppression in Xinjiang: China's war on Uighur culture|last=Shepherd|first=Christian|date=12 September 2019|work=[[Financial Times]]|access-date=19 January 2020}} The campaign included the detainment of 1.8 million people in [[Xinjiang internment camps|internment camps]], mostly Uyghurs but also including other ethnic and religious minorities, by 2020, and a birth suppression campaign that led to a large drop in the Uyghur birth rate by 2019.{{cite news |date=28 June 2020 |title=China cuts Uighur births with IUDs, abortion, sterilization |publisher=[[Associated Press]] |url=https://apnews.com/article/269b3de1af34e17c1941a514f78d764c |url-status=live |access-date=18 December 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201216200613/https://apnews.com/article/269b3de1af34e17c1941a514f78d764c |archive-date=16 December 2020}} Human rights groups and former inmates have described the camps as "concentration camps", where Uyghurs and other minorities have been forcibly assimilated into China's majority [[Han Chinese|ethnic Han]] society.{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2019/jul/11/more-than-20-ambassadors-condemn-chinas-treatment-of-uighurs-in-xinjiang|title=More than 20 ambassadors condemn China's treatment of Uighurs in Xinjiang|date=11 July 2019|newspaper=[[The Guardian]]|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190711031342/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2019/jul/11/more-than-20-ambassadors-condemn-chinas-treatment-of-uighurs-in-xinjiang|archive-date=11 July 2019|access-date=11 July 2019}} This program has been called a genocide by western observers, while [[UN Human Rights Office report on Xinjiang|a report]] by the [[Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights|UN Human Rights Office]] said they may amount to crimes against humanity.{{cite news |date=10 December 2021 |title=China's Xi responsible for Uyghur 'genocide', unofficial tribunal says |work=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/chinas-xi-responsible-uyghur-genocide-unofficial-tribunal-says-2021-12-10/ |access-date=24 October 2022 |archive-date=24 October 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221024104722/https://www.reuters.com/world/china/chinas-xi-responsible-uyghur-genocide-unofficial-tribunal-says-2021-12-10/ |url-status=live }}{{cite news |date=1 September 2022 |title=U.N. says China may have committed crimes against humanity in Xinjiang |work=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/outgoing-un-human-rights-chief-says-serious-human-rights-violations-committed-2022-08-31/ |access-date=24 October 2022 |archive-date=26 November 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221126235118/https://www.reuters.com/world/china/outgoing-un-human-rights-chief-says-serious-human-rights-violations-committed-2022-08-31/ |url-status=live }} [[Xinjiang papers|Internal Chinese government documents]] leaked to the press in November 2019 showed that Xi personally ordered a security crackdown in Xinjiang, saying that the party must show "absolutely no mercy" and that officials use all the "weapons of the [[people's democratic dictatorship]]" to suppress those "infected with the virus of extremism."{{cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2019/11/16/world/asia/china-xinjiang-documents.html|title='Absolutely No Mercy': Leaked Files Expose How China Organized Mass Detentions of Muslims|last1=Ramzy|first1=Austin|last2=Buckley|first2=Chris|date=16 November 2019|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=6 December 2019|archive-date=8 January 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200108214926/https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2019/11/16/world/asia/china-xinjiang-documents.html|url-status=live}}{{cite news|url=https://www.hongkongfp.com/2020/01/19/huaweis-human-rights-record-needs-scrutiny-britain-signs-5g-contracts/|title=Huawei's human rights record needs scrutiny before Britain signs 5G contracts|last=Tugendhat|first=Tom|date=19 January 2020|newspaper=[[Hong Kong Free Press]]|access-date=19 January 2020|archive-date=11 July 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240711081528/https://hongkongfp.com/2020/01/19/huaweis-human-rights-record-needs-scrutiny-britain-signs-5g-contracts/|url-status=live}} The papers also showed that Xi repeatedly discussed about [[Islamic extremism]] in his speeches, likening it to a "virus" or a "drug" that could be only addressed by "a period of painful, interventionary treatment." However, he also warned against the discrimination against Uyghurs and rejected proposals to eradicate [[Islam in China]], calling that kind of viewpoint "biased, even wrong." Xi's exact role in the building of internment camps has not been publicly reported, though he's widely believed to be behind them and his words have been the source for major justifications in the crackdown in Xinjiang.{{Cite news |last=Chin |first=Josh |date=30 November 2021 |title=Leaked Documents Detail Xi Jinping's Extensive Role in Xinjiang Crackdown |language=en-US |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/leaked-documents-detail-xi-jinpings-extensive-role-in-xinjiang-crackdown-11638284709 |access-date=13 August 2022 |issn=0099-9660 |archive-date=12 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220812142807/https://www.wsj.com/articles/leaked-documents-detail-xi-jinpings-extensive-role-in-xinjiang-crackdown-11638284709 |url-status=live }} During a four-day visit to Xinjiang in July 2022, Xi urged local officials to always listen to the people's voices{{Citation |title=Momen Langka! Presiden China Xi Jinping Kunjungi Kampung Muslim Uighur |date=19 July 2022 |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Se2h1pyAjKw |publisher=[[Kompas TV]] |language=en |quote=Selama 4 hari, Xi Jinping mengunjungi sejumlah situs di Xinjiang termasuk perkebunan kapas, zona perdagangan dan museum. Penduduk Uighur pun menyambut Presiden Xi Jinping. Dalam kunjungannya, Xi mendesak agar pejabat Xinjiang selalu mendengarkan suara rakyat demi memenangkan hati dan membuat rakyat bersatu. |via=Youtube |access-date=7 March 2024 |archive-date=7 March 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240307204442/https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Se2h1pyAjKw |url-status=live }} and to do more in preservation of ethnic minority culture.{{Citation |title=China's President Xi visits far western Xinjiang region for first time in 8 years |date=15 July 2022 |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=s0Q2iq_HVFs&t=42s |publisher=[[SCMP]] |language=en |via=Youtube |access-date=7 March 2024 |archive-date=7 March 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240307204440/https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=s0Q2iq_HVFs&t=42s |url-status=live }} He also inspected the [[Xinjiang Production and Construction Corps]] and praised its "great progress" in reform and development.{{Cite web |date=2022-07-15 |title=Xi Jinping visits Xinjiang for first time since crackdown |url=https://www.dw.com/en/china-xi-jinping-visits-xinjiang-for-first-time-since-uyghur-crackdown/a-62481343 |website=[[Deutsche Welle]] |language=en}} During another visit to Xinjiang in August 2023, Xi said in a speech that the region was "no longer a remote area" and should open up more for [[Tourism in Xinjiang|tourism]] to attract domestic and foreign visitors.{{Cite web |author1=Ong Han Sean |date=20 November 2023 |title=China's Xinjiang: A marvel of wild beauty and a land full of culture and charm |url=https://www.thestar.com.my/lifestyle/travel/2023/11/20/xinjiang-is-a-land-that039s-full-of-culture-colour-and-charm |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231120143603/https://www.thestar.com.my/lifestyle/travel/2023/11/20/xinjiang-is-a-land-that039s-full-of-culture-colour-and-charm |archive-date=20 November 2023 |website=[[The Star (Malaysia)|The Star]] |language=en |quote=Our visit came on the heels of Chinese President Xi Jinping's visit to Ürümqi, where he reportedly stressed on the positive promotion of the region to show an open and confident Xinjiang. Xi also called for Xinjiang to be opened more widely for tourism to encourage visits from domestic and foreign tourists.}}{{Cite web |date=2023-09-11 |title=China: How is Beijing whitewashing its Xinjiang policy? |url=https://www.dw.com/en/china-how-is-beijing-whitewashing-its-xinjiang-policy/a-66779242 |access-date=2024-03-07 |website=[[Deutsche Welle]] |language=en |quote=But amid growing global attention on Xinjiang, China has been eager to portray the region as a "success story" by welcoming more tourists. In a speech that he made while visiting the region last month, Xi said Xinjiang was "no longer a remote area" and should open up more to domestic and foreign tourism. |archive-date=7 March 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240307204440/https://www.dw.com/en/china-how-is-beijing-whitewashing-its-xinjiang-policy/a-66779242 |url-status=live }} ===COVID-19 pandemic=== {{See also|Chinese government response to COVID-19|COVID-19 pandemic in mainland China}}On 20 January 2020, Xi commented for the first time on the emerging [[COVID-19 pandemic]] in Wuhan, and ordered "efforts to curb the spread" of the virus.{{Cite web |last=Griffiths |first=James |date=17 February 2020 |title=Did Xi Jinping know about the coronavirus outbreak earlier than first suggested? |url=https://www.cnn.com/2020/02/17/asia/china-coronavirus-xi-jinping-intl-hnk/index.html |access-date=13 August 2022 |website=[[CNN]] |archive-date=23 June 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230623151800/https://www.cnn.com/2020/02/17/asia/china-coronavirus-xi-jinping-intl-hnk/index.html |url-status=live }} He gave premier Li Keqiang some responsibility over the COVID-19 response, in what has been suggested by ''The Wall Street Journal'' was an attempt to potentially insulate himself from criticism if the response failed.{{Cite news |last=Page |first=Jeremy |date=27 January 2020 |title=China's Xi Gives His No. 2 a Rare Chance to Shine in Coronavirus Fight, With Risks for Both |language=en-US |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/chinas-xi-gives-his-no-2-a-rare-chance-to-shine-in-coronavirus-fight-with-risks-for-both-11580138767 |access-date=13 August 2022 |issn=0099-9660 |archive-date=30 January 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200130154514/https://www.wsj.com/articles/chinas-xi-gives-his-no-2-a-rare-chance-to-shine-in-coronavirus-fight-with-risks-for-both-11580138767 |url-status=live }} The government initially responded to the pandemic with a lockdown and censorship, with the initial response causing widespread backlash within China.{{Cite web |last=Griffiths |first=James |title=Wuhan is the latest crisis to face China's Xi, and it's exposing major flaws in his model of control |url=https://www.cnn.com/2020/01/23/asia/wuhan-china-coronavirus-sars-response-intl-hnk/index.html |access-date=29 August 2022 |website=[[CNN]] |date=24 January 2020 |archive-date=8 February 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200208194710/https://www.cnn.com/2020/01/23/asia/wuhan-china-coronavirus-sars-response-intl-hnk/index.html |url-status=live }} He met with [[Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus]], the director-general of the World Health Organization (WHO), on 28 January.{{Cite web |last=Steger |first=Isabella |date=10 February 2020 |title=Xi Jinping emerges to meet the people for the first time in China's coronavirus outbreak |url=https://qz.com/1799914/xi-jinping-appears-in-public-after-long-absence-during-coronavirus/ |access-date=13 August 2022 |website=[[Quartz (publication)|Quartz]] |language=en |archive-date=1 September 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220901093801/https://qz.com/1799914/xi-jinping-appears-in-public-after-long-absence-during-coronavirus/ |url-status=live }} ''[[Der Spiegel]]'' reported that in January 2020 Xi pressured Tedros Adhanom to hold off on issuing a [[Public health emergency of international concern|global warning]] about the outbreak of COVID-19 and hold back information on human-to-human transmission of the virus, allegations denied by the WHO.{{cite news |date=10 May 2020 |title=WHO dementiert Telefongespräch mit Chinas Präsident |language=de |website=[[Der Spiegel]] |url=https://www.spiegel.de/politik/ausland/corona-ausbruch-who-dementiert-telefongespraech-mit-chinas-praesident-a-37678f1c-6960-453c-a317-8ef9feed8b7c |access-date=14 May 2020 |archive-date=14 May 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200514025329/https://www.spiegel.de/politik/ausland/corona-ausbruch-who-dementiert-telefongespraech-mit-chinas-praesident-a-37678f1c-6960-453c-a317-8ef9feed8b7c |url-status=live }} On 5 February, Xi met with Cambodian prime minister [[Hun Sen]] in Beijing, the first foreign leader allowed into China since the outbreak. After the COVID-19 outbreak got under control in Wuhan, Xi visited the city on 10 March.{{Cite news |last1=Yew |first1=Lun Tian |last2=Se |first2=Young Lee |date=10 March 2022 |title=Xi visits Wuhan, signaling tide turning in China's coronavirus battle |language=en |work=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-health-coronavirus-china-idUSKBN20X01F |access-date=13 August 2022 |archive-date=13 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813191157/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-health-coronavirus-china-idUSKBN20X01F |url-status=live }} [[File:Sergio Mattarella and Xi Jinping 2019.jpg|left|thumb|225x225px|Italian president [[Sergio Mattarella]] with Xi in March 2019]] After getting the outbreak in Wuhan under control, Xi has favoured what has officially been termed "dynamic [[Zero-COVID|zero-COVID policy]]"{{Cite web |last=Cao |first=Desheng |title=Xi: Dynamic zero-COVID policy works |url=https://www.chinadaily.com.cn/a/202206/30/WS62bcd80ea310fd2b29e6962b.html |access-date=13 August 2022 |website=[[China Daily]]}} that aims to control and suppress the virus as much as possible within the country's borders. This has involved local lockdowns and mass-testing.{{Cite news |last=Wong |first=Chun Han |date=25 July 2022 |title=China's Zero-Covid Policy Drags on Vaccination Drive |language=en-US |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/chinas-zero-covid-policy-drags-on-vaccination-drive-11658745899 |access-date=13 August 2022 |issn=0099-9660 |archive-date=11 July 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240711082025/https://www.wsj.com/articles/chinas-zero-covid-policy-drags-on-vaccination-drive-11658745899 |url-status=live }} While initially credited for China's suppression of the COVID-19 outbreak, the policy was later criticized by foreign and some domestic observers for being out of touch with the rest of the world and taking a heavy toll on the economy. This approach has especially come under criticism during a 2022 lockdown on Shanghai, which forced millions to their homes and damaged the city's economy,{{Cite web |last=Davidson |first=Helen |date=10 June 2022 |title=Xi Jinping says 'persistence is victory' as Covid restrictions return to Shanghai and Beijing |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/jun/10/xi-jinping-says-persistence-is-victory-as-covid-restrictions-return-to-shanghai-and-beijing |access-date=29 August 2022 |website=[[The Guardian]] |language=en |archive-date=11 July 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240711082026/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/jun/10/xi-jinping-says-persistence-is-victory-as-covid-restrictions-return-to-shanghai-and-beijing |url-status=live }} denting the image of [[Li Qiang]], close Xi ally and Party secretary of the city.{{Cite news |last1=Hadano |first1=Tsukasa |last2=Doi |first2=Noriyuki |date=29 June 2022 |title=Xi ally Li Qiang keeps Shanghai party chief job, but star fades |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Politics/Xi-ally-Li-Qiang-keeps-Shanghai-party-chief-job-but-star-fades |access-date=29 August 2022 |work=[[Nikkei Asia]] |language=en-GB |archive-date=1 September 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220901143524/https://asia.nikkei.com/Politics/Xi-ally-Li-Qiang-keeps-Shanghai-party-chief-job-but-star-fades |url-status=live }} Conversely, Xi has said that the policy was designed to protect people's life safety.{{Cite news |last1=Cai |first1=Jane |last2=Tang |first2=Frank |date=29 June 2022 |title=China to press on with 'zero Covid', despite economic risks: Xi |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3183550/china-press-zero-covid-despite-economic-risks-president-xi |access-date=13 August 2022 |work=[[South China Morning Post]] |language=en |archive-date=13 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813082557/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3183550/china-press-zero-covid-despite-economic-risks-president-xi |url-status=live }} On 23 July 2022, the [[National Health Commission]] reported that Xi and other top leaders have taken the local COVID-19 vaccines.{{Cite news |date=23 July 2022 |title=Covid in China: Xi Jinping and other leaders given domestic vaccine |language=en-GB |work=[[BBC News]] |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-62279377 |access-date=13 August 2022 |archive-date=13 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813191157/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-62279377 |url-status=live }} At the 20th CCP Congress, Xi confirmed the continuation of the zero-COVID policy,{{cite news |last=McDonell |first=Stephen |date=16 October 2022 |title=Xi Jinping speech: Zero-Covid and zero solutions |work=[[BBC News]] |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-63274391 |access-date=11 November 2022 |archive-date=11 November 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221111235314/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-63274391 |url-status=live }} stating he would "unswervingly" carry out "dynamic zero-COVID" and promising to "resolutely win the battle",{{Cite news |date=17 December 2022 |title=Xi Jinping tied himself to zero-Covid. Now he keeps silent as it falls apart |work=[[CNN]] |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2022/12/16/china/china-zero-covid-uturn-xi-jinping-intl-hnk-mic/index.html |access-date=16 December 2022 |archive-date=16 December 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221216085906/https://edition.cnn.com/2022/12/16/china/china-zero-covid-uturn-xi-jinping-intl-hnk-mic/index.html |url-status=live }} though China started a limited easing of the policies in the following weeks.{{Cite news |last=Ramzy |first=Austin |date=11 November 2022 |title=China Eases Zero-Covid Rules as Economic Toll and Frustrations Mount |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/china-eases-some-covid-19-rules-even-as-cases-pass-10-000-11668150369 |access-date=11 November 2022 |archive-date=11 November 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221111073602/https://www.wsj.com/articles/china-eases-some-covid-19-rules-even-as-cases-pass-10-000-11668150369 |url-status=live }} In November 2022, [[2022 COVID-19 protests in China|protests]] broke out against China's COVID-19 policies, with a [[2022 Ürümqi fire|fire in a high-rise apartment building in Ürümqi]] being the trigger.{{Cite news |last1=Che |first1=Chang |last2=Buckley |first2=Chris |last3=Chien |first3=Amy Chang |last4=Dong |first4=Joy |date=2022-12-05 |title=China Stems Wave of Protest, but Ripples of Resistance Remain |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/05/world/asia/china-covid-protests.html |access-date=2022-12-05 |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=5 December 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221205145355/https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/05/world/asia/china-covid-protests.html |url-status=live }} The protests were held in multiple major cities, with some of the protesters demanding the end of Xi's and the CCP's rule. The protests were mostly suppressed by December, though the government further eased COVID-19 restrictions in the time since.{{Cite news |last1=Olcott |first1=Eleanor |last2=Mitchell |first2=Tom |date=4 December 2022 |title=Chinese cities ease Covid restrictions following nationwide protests |work=[[Financial Times]] |url=https://www.ft.com/content/bfbc7afb-3994-4e4c-b649-ca791675b296 |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221211221218/https://www.ft.com/content/bfbc7afb-3994-4e4c-b649-ca791675b296 |archive-date=11 December 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |access-date=5 December 2022}} On 7 December 2022, China announced large-scale changes to its COVID-19 policy, including allowing quarantine at home for mild infections, reducing of [[Polymerase chain reaction|PCR testing]], and decreasing the power of local officials to implement lockdowns.{{Cite news |last1=Che |first1=Chang |last2=Chien |first2=Amy Chang |last3=Stevenson |first3=Alexandra |date=2022-12-07 |title=What Has Changed About China's 'Zero Covid' Policy |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/07/world/asia/china-zero-covid-changes.html |access-date=2022-12-08 |issn=0362-4331}} ===Environmental policy=== {{See also|Xi Jinping Thought on Ecological Civilization|Environmental policy in China}} Xi identifies environmental protection as one of China's five major priorities for national progress.{{Cite book |last=Li |first=David Daokui |author-link=David Daokui Li |title=China's World View: Demystifying China to Prevent Global Conflict |date=2024 |publisher=[[W. W. Norton & Company]] |isbn=978-0-393-29239-8 |location=New York, NY}}{{Rp|page=164}} Xi has popularized a metaphor of "two mountains" to emphasize the importance of environmental protection.{{Rp|page=164}} The concept is that a mountain made of gold or silver is valuable, but [[Clear waters and green mountains|green mountains with clear waters are more valuable]].{{Rp|page=164}} The slogan's meaning is that economic development priorities must also provide for economic protection.{{Rp|page=164}} In September 2020, Xi announced that China will "strengthen its 2030 climate target (NDC), peak emissions before 2030 and aim to achieve [[carbon neutrality]] before 2060." If accomplished, this would lower the expected rise in global temperature by 0.2–0.3 °C – "the biggest single reduction ever estimated by the Climate Action Tracker."{{cite web |date=23 September 2020 |title=China going carbon neutral before 2060 would lower warming projections by around 0.2 to 0.3 degrees C |url=https://climateactiontracker.org/press/china-carbon-neutral-before-2060-would-lower-warming-projections-by-around-2-to-3-tenths-of-a-degree/ |access-date=27 September 2020 |website=[[Climate Action Tracker]]}} Xi mentioned the link between the COVID-19 pandemic and nature destruction as one of the reasons for the decision, saying that "Humankind can no longer afford to ignore the repeated warnings of nature."{{cite news |date=23 September 2020 |title=China, the world's top global emitter, aims to go carbon-neutral by 2060 |work=[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]] |publisher= |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2020-09-23/china-world-top-global-emitter-aims-carbon-neutral-by-2060/12691974 |access-date=29 September 2020}} On 27 September, Chinese scientists presented a detailed plan how to achieve the target.{{cite news |date=29 September 2020 |title=China's top climate scientists unveil road map to 2060 goal |newspaper=[[The Japan Times]] |agency=[[Bloomberg News]] |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2020/09/29/asia-pacific/science-health-asia-pacific/china-climate-change-road-map-2060/ |access-date=29 September 2020}} In September 2021, Xi announced that China will not build "coal-fired power projects abroad", which was said to be potentially "pivotal" in reducing emissions. The Belt and Road Initiative did not include financing such projects already in the first half of 2021.{{cite news |last1=Brant |first1=Robin |date=22 September 2021 |title=China pledges to stop building new coal energy plants abroad |work=[[BBC News]] |publisher= |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-58647481 |access-date=29 September 2021}} Xi Jinping did not attend [[2021 United Nations Climate Change Conference|COP26]] personally. However, a Chinese delegation led by climate change envoy [[Xie Zhenhua (politician)|Xie Zhenhua]] did attend.{{Cite news |last=Faulconbridge |first=Guy |date=15 October 2021 |title=China's Xi will not attend COP26 in person, UK PM Johnson told |work=[[Reuters]] |publisher= |url=https://www.reuters.com/business/environment/chinas-xi-snub-cop26-uk-pm-johnson-told-times-2021-10-15/ |access-date=20 August 2022}}{{Cite news |last=Harvey |first=Fiona |author-link=Fiona Harvey |date=10 November 2021 |title=China's top Cop26 delegate says it is taking 'real action' on climate targets |url=https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2021/nov/10/chinas-top-cop26-delegate-says-it-is-taking-real-action-on-climate-targets |access-date=11 November 2022 |work=[[The Guardian]]}} During the conference, the United States and China agreed on a framework to reduce [[GHG emission]] by co-operating on different measures.{{cite news |last1=Volcovici |first1=Valeria |last2=James |first2=William |last3=Spring |first3=Jake |date=11 November 2021 |title=U.S. and China unveil deal to ramp up cooperation on climate change |work=[[Reuters]] |publisher= |url=https://www.reuters.com/business/cop/china-us-make-joint-statement-cop26-climate-summit-2021-11-10 |access-date=11 November 2021}} ===Governance style=== Known as a very secretive leader, little is known publicly about how Xi makes political decisions, or how he came to power.{{Cite news |date=28 September 2022 |title=An investigation into what has shaped Xi Jinping's thinking |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |url=https://www.economist.com/briefing/2022/09/28/an-investigation-into-what-has-shaped-xi-jinpings-thinking |access-date=30 September 2022 |issn=0013-0613}}{{Cite news |last=Myers |first=Steven Lee |date=5 March 2018 |title=Behind Public Persona, the Real Xi Jinping Is a Guarded Secret |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/03/05/world/asia/xi-jinping-china-leader.html |access-date=13 August 2022 |issn=0362-4331}} Xi's speeches generally get released months or years after they are made. Xi has also never given a press conference since becoming paramount leader, except in rare joint press conferences with foreign leaders.{{Cite web |last=McGregor |first=Richard |date=21 August 2022 |title=Xi Jinping's Radical Secrecy |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/international/archive/2022/08/china-xi-jinping-biography-opacity/671195/ |access-date=21 August 2022 |website=[[The Atlantic]] |language=en}} ''The Wall Street Journal'' reported that Xi prefers micromanaging in governance, in contrast to previous leaders such as Hu Jintao who left details of major policies to lower-ranking officials. Reportedly, ministerial officials try to get Xi's attention in various ways, with some creating slide shows and audio reports. ''The Wall Street Journal'' also reported that Xi created a performance-review system in 2018 to give evaluations on officials on various measures, including loyalty. According to ''The Economist'', Xi's orders have generally been vague, leaving lower level officials to interpret his words.{{Cite news |title=Even in secret, China's leaders speak in code |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |url=https://www.economist.com/china/2022/01/01/even-in-secret-chinas-leaders-speak-in-code |access-date=13 August 2022 |issn=0013-0613}} Chinese state media Xinhua News Agency said that Xi "personally reviews every draft of major policy documents" and "all reports submitted to him, no matter how late in the evening, were returned with instructions the following morning."{{Cite news |last=Blachard |first=Ben |date=17 November 2017 |title=Glowing profile cracks door open on private life of China's Xi |language=en |publisher=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-politics-xi-idUSKBN1DH0GA |access-date=9 December 2019}} With regard to behavior of Communist Party members, Xi emphasizes the "Two Musts" (members must not be arrogant or rash and must keep their hard-working spirit) and the "Six Nos" (members must say no to formalism, bureaucracy, gift-giving, luxurious birthday celebrations, hedonism, and extravagance).{{Sfn|Marquis|Qiao|2022|p=52}} Xi called for officials to practice [[Self-criticism (Marxism–Leninism)|self-criticism]] which, according to observers, is in order to appear less corrupt and more popular among the people.{{cite news |last1=Forde |first1=Brendan |date=9 September 2013 |title=China's 'Mass Line' Campaign |work=[[The Diplomat (magazine)|The Diplomat]] |url=https://thediplomat.com/2013/09/chinas-mass-line-campaign-2/ |url-status=live |access-date=23 June 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180624035650/https://thediplomat.com/2013/09/chinas-mass-line-campaign-2/ |archive-date=24 June 2018}}{{cite news |last1=Levin |first1=Dan |date=20 December 2013 |title=China Revives Mao-Era Self-Criticism, but This Kind Bruises Few Egos |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2013/12/21/world/asia/china-revives-mao-era-self-criticism-but-this-kind-bruises-few-egos.html |url-status=live |access-date=23 June 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180624010400/https://www.nytimes.com/2013/12/21/world/asia/china-revives-mao-era-self-criticism-but-this-kind-bruises-few-egos.html |archive-date=24 June 2018}}{{cite news |last1=Tiezzi |first1=Shannon |date=27 December 2013 |title=The Mass Line Campaign in the 21st Century |work=[[The Diplomat (magazine)|The Diplomat]] |url=https://thediplomat.com/2013/12/the-mass-line-campaign-in-the-21st-century/ |url-status=live |access-date=23 June 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180624010506/https://thediplomat.com/2013/12/the-mass-line-campaign-in-the-21st-century/ |archive-date=24 June 2018}} According to Japanese diplomat [[Hideo Tarumi]], who served as the Japanese ambassador to China, Xi has engaged in heavy process of centralization in order to maintain the legitimacy of the rule of Chinese Communist Party.{{cite web|url=https://www.rfa.org/english/news/china/ambassador-memoir-02152024173555.html|title= China's Xi appeared 'humble' but now rules supreme, ambassador says|first=Chi Chun|last=Lee|newspaper=Radio Free Asia|date=2024-02-15|access-date=2024-04-22}} ==Political positions== {{further|Ideology of the Chinese Communist Party}} {{Quote box | quote = A party and its authority rests on winning the hearts and minds of the people. What the public opposes and hates, we must address and resolve.{{pb}} [M]aterial and cultural needs grown; demands for democracy, rule of law, fairness and justice, security, and a better environment are also increasing each day. | source = — Xi Jinping during a speech in 2017{{cite web | last=Ide | first=Bill | title=Xi Lays Out New Vision for Communist China | website=Voice of America | date=18 Oct 2017 | url=https://www.voanews.com/a/xi-jinping-opens-national-congress/4075475.html | access-date=30 Jul 2024}} | align = right | width = 25em }} ===Chinese Dream=== {{main|Chinese Dream}} [[File:Lujiazui 2016.jpg|thumb|According to ''[[Qiushi]]'', the Chinese Dream is about Chinese prosperity, collective effort, socialism, and national glory.]] Xi and CCP ideologues coined the phrase "Chinese Dream" to describe his overarching plans for China as its leader. Xi first used the phrase during a high-profile visit to the [[National Museum of China]] on 29 November 2012, where he and his [[Politburo Standing Committee of the Chinese Communist Party|Politburo Standing Committee]] colleagues were attending a "national revival" exhibition. Since then, the phrase has become the signature political slogan of the Xi era.{{Cite news|url=https://www.economist.com/leaders/2013/05/04/xi-jinping-and-the-chinese-dream|title=Xi Jinping and the Chinese dream|date=4 May 2013|newspaper=[[The Economist]]|access-date=12 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160510092423/http://www.economist.com/news/leaders/21577070-vision-chinas-new-president-should-serve-his-people-not-nationalist-state-xi-jinping|archive-date=10 May 2016|issn=0013-0613}}{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/worldnews/asia/china/9935609/Xi-Jinping-calls-for-a-Chinese-Dream.html|title=Xi Jinping calls for a Chinese dream|last1=Moore|first1=Malcolm|date=17 March 2013|work=[[The Daily Telegraph]]|access-date=20 March 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130320072154/http://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/worldnews/asia/china/9935609/Xi-Jinping-calls-for-a-Chinese-Dream.html|archive-date=20 March 2013|url-status=live}} The origin of the term "Chinese Dream" is unclear. While the phrase has been used before by journalists and scholars,{{cite news|url=https://www.theatlantic.com/china/archive/2013/05/todays-china-notes-dreams-obstacles/275544/|title=Today's China Notes: Dreams, Obstacles|last=Fallows|first=James|date=3 May 2013|work=[[The Atlantic]]|access-date=7 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180712023142/https://www.theatlantic.com/china/archive/2013/05/todays-china-notes-dreams-obstacles/275544/|archive-date=12 July 2018|url-status=live}} some publications have posited the term likely drew its inspiration from the concept of the [[American Dream]].{{cite news|url=https://www.economist.com/blogs/analects/2013/05/chinese-dream-0|title=The role of Thomas Friedman|last=M.|first=J.|date=6 May 2013|newspaper=[[The Economist]]|access-date=12 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171028145059/https://www.economist.com/blogs/analects/2013/05/chinese-dream-0|archive-date=28 October 2017|url-status=live}} ''The Economist'' noted the abstract and seemingly accessible nature of the concept with no specific overarching policy stipulations may be a deliberate departure from the jargon-heavy ideologies of his predecessors.{{Cite news|url=https://www.economist.com/news/briefing/21577063-chinas-new-leader-has-been-quick-consolidate-his-power-what-does-he-now-want-his|title=Chasing the Chinese dream |date=4 May 2013|newspaper=[[The Economist]]|access-date=12 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161028080732/http://www.economist.com/news/briefing/21577063-chinas-new-leader-has-been-quick-consolidate-his-power-what-does-he-now-want-his|archive-date=28 October 2016|issn=0013-0613}} Xi has linked the "Chinese Dream" with the phrase "[[great rejuvenation of the Chinese nation]]."{{Cite web |last=Tao |first=Xie |date=14 March 2014 |title=Opinion: Is the Chinese dream fantasy or reality? |url=https://www.cnn.com/2014/03/14/world/asia/chinese-dream-anniversary-xi-jinping-president/index.html |access-date=14 August 2022 |website=[[CNN]]}} ===Culture=== {{See also|Xi Jinping Thought on Culture|Religious Sinicization under the general secretaryship of Xi Jinping}} Xi has intensified efforts for a revival of traditional Chinese culture.{{Cite news |date=17 August 2017 |title=The Communist Party is redefining what it means to be Chinese |url=https://www.economist.com/china/2017/08/17/the-communist-party-is-redefining-what-it-means-to-be-chinese |access-date=23 January 2020 |newspaper=[[The Economist]]}} He has called traditional culture the "soul" of the nation and the "foundation" of the CCP's culture.{{Cite news |last=Zi |first=Yang |date=6 July 2016 |title=Xi Jinping and China's Traditionalist Restoration |url=https://jamestown.org/program/xi-jinping-chinas-traditionalist-restoration/ |access-date=23 January 2020 |work=[[The Jamestown Foundation]] |publisher=}} He has praised the "splendid Chinese civilization", calling CCP's rule part of "the uninterrupted development of a civilization for several thousand years" which is "rarely seen among nations in the world".{{Sfn|Tsang|Cheung|2024|p=87}} Xi has also called for integrating the basic tenets of Marxism with China's traditional culture. He has established the "[[Four Confidences|four matters of confidence]]", which has later been added to the CCP constitution, calling for CCP members, government officials and the Chinese people to be "confident in our chosen path, confident in our guiding theories, confident in our political system, and confident in our culture." He has unveiled Global Civilisation initiative in 2023, calling for "respecting the diversity of civilisations, advocating the common values of humanity, valuing the inheritance and innovation of civilisations, and strengthening international people-to-people exchanges and cooperation". In recent years, top political leaders of the CCP such as Xi have overseen the rehabilitation of ancient Chinese philosophical figures like [[Han Fei]] into the mainstream of Chinese thought alongside [[Confucianism]]. At a meeting with other officials in 2013, he quoted [[Confucius]], saying "he who rules by virtue is like the Pole Star, it maintains its place, and the multitude of stars pay homage." While visiting [[Shandong]], the birthplace of Confucius, in November, he told scholars that the Western world was "suffering a crisis of confidence" and that the CCP has been "the loyal inheritor and promoter of China's outstanding [[Traditional Chinese culture|traditional culture]]."{{cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/10/12/world/leader-taps-into-chinese-classics-in-seeking-to-cement-power.html|title=Leader Taps into Chinese Classics in Seeking to Cement Power|last=Buckley|first=Chris|date=12 October 2014|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190912091736/https://www.nytimes.com/2014/10/12/world/leader-taps-into-chinese-classics-in-seeking-to-cement-power.html|archive-date=12 September 2019|access-date=7 September 2019}} According to several analysts, Xi's leadership has been characterised by a resurgence of the ancient political philosophy [[Legalism (Chinese philosophy)|Legalism]].{{cite journal |last1=Schneider |first1=David K. |title=China's New Legalism |journal=The National Interest |date=2016 |issue=143 |pages=19–25 |doi= |jstor=26557304}}{{cite web |last1=Crane |first1=Sam |title=Why Xi Jinping's China is Legalist, Not Confucian |url=https://chinachannel.org/2018/06/29/legalism/ |website=China Channel |date=29 June 2018 |access-date=24 November 2020 |archive-date=7 November 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201107225951/https://chinachannel.org/2018/06/29/legalism/ }} Han Fei gained new prominence with favourable citations; one sentence of Han Fei's that Xi quoted appeared thousands of times in official Chinese media at the local, provincial, and national levels.{{cite news|url=https://thediplomat.com/2015/01/is-chinas-machiavelli-now-its-most-important-political-philosopher/|title=Is 'China's Machiavelli' Now Its Most Important Political Philosopher?|last=Mitchell|first=Ryan Mi|date=16 January 2015|newspaper=[[The Diplomat (magazine)|The Diplomat]]|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190912091739/https://thediplomat.com/2015/01/is-chinas-machiavelli-now-its-most-important-political-philosopher/|archive-date=12 September 2019|access-date=7 September 2019}} Xi has additionally supported the [[Neo-Confucianism|Neo-Confucian]] philosopher [[Wang Yangming]], telling local leaders to promote him.{{Cite news |last=Johnson |first=Ian |date=18 October 2017 |title=Forget Marx and Mao. Chinese City Honors Once-Banned Confucian. |language=en-US |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/10/18/world/asia/china-guiyang-wang-yangming-confucian.html |access-date=13 August 2022 |issn=0362-4331}} Xi has seemingly adopted a [[socially conservative]] view since he began his third leadership in 2022, calling for "a new marriage and childbirth culture, strengthen guidance of young people's views on marriage, parenthood and family".{{cite magazine |date=31 October 2023 |title=Facing Population Decline, China's Xi Promotes 'Childbirth Culture' for Women |url=https://time.com/6330091/china-population-xi-childbirth-culture/ |magazine=[[Time (magazine)|Time]] |access-date=18 June 2025 |archive-date=23 May 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250523120800/https://time.com/6330091/china-population-xi-childbirth-culture/ |url-status=dead }} [[Hanfu]], the traditional dress of Han Chinese, has seen a revival under him, associated with the revival of traditional culture.{{Cite news |last=Buckley |first=Chris |date=30 November 2018 |title=China's 'Hanfu' movement is making it hip to be old-fashioned. Here's why. |url=https://www.afr.com/life-and-luxury/fashion-and-style/why-its-hip-to-be-oldfashioned-in-china-20181129-h18hql |access-date=23 January 2020 |work=[[The Australian Financial Review]]}} Xi supports a socialist artistic revival, including the promotion of patriotic art and red classics.{{Rp|page=253}} Since the [[18th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party|18th Party Congress]], Xi has emphasized utilizing red resources, telling red stories, and inheriting red genes.{{Cite book |last=Li |first=Ying |title=Red Ink: A History of Printing and Politics in China |publisher=Royal Collins Press |year=2024 |isbn=9781487812737}}{{Rp|page=xii}} On 15 October 2014, Xi Jinping emulated the [[Yan'an Forum]] with his 'Speech at the Forum on Literature and Art.'{{Cite book |last=Sorace |first=Christian |title=Afterlives of Chinese Communism: Political Concepts from Mao to Xi |date=2019 |publisher=[[Australian National University Press]] |isbn=978-1-76046-249-9 |location=Acton, Australia |chapter=Aesthetics}}{{Rp|page=15}} Consistent with Mao's view in the Yan'an Talks, Xi believes works of art should be judged by political criteria.{{Rp|page=16}} In 2021, Xi quoted the Yan'an Talks during the opening ceremony of the 11th National Congress of the [[China Federation of Literary and Art Circles]] and the 10th National Congress of the [[China Writers Association|Chinese Writers Association]].{{Sfn|Marquis|Qiao|2022|p=174}} According to Xi, art should be judged by political criteria.{{Rp|page=16}} This view rejects the concept of [[Art for art's sake|art-for-art's-sake]] and contends that art should serve the goal of national rejuvenation.{{Rp|page=16}} Xi criticizes market-driven art which he deems sensationalist, particularly works which "exaggerate society's dark side" for profit.{{Rp|page=16}} He ordered the arts industry to "tell China's stories and spread Chinese voices to strengthen the country's international communication capacity."{{cite web |author1=Joyce Cheng |author2=Bang Xiao |date=2022-07-31 |title=Globally acclaimed author Yan Geling considering giving up writing in native Chinese over censorship |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2022-07-31/author-yan-geling-criticises-beijing-censorship/101276516 |work=[[Australian Broadcasting Corporation|ABC]]}} Xi states that Chinese writers should follow the Party's leadership, serve the cause of socialism, and "let people see the good, feel hope, [and] have dreams".{{Cite book |last=Tu |first=Hang |title=Sentimental Republic: Chinese Intellectuals and the Maoist Past |publisher=[[Harvard University Asia Center]] |year=2025 |isbn=9780674297579}}{{Rp|page=252}} Xi is a proponent of the "[[Religious Sinicization under the general secretaryship of Xi Jinping|Sinicization of Chinese religion]]".{{Cite book |last=Mariani |first=Paul Philip |title=China's Church Divided: Bishop Louis Jin and the Post-Mao Catholic Revival |date=2025 |publisher=[[Harvard University Press]] |isbn=978-0-674-29765-4 |location=Cambridge, Massachusetts |pages=288}} At the [[19th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party|19th Party Congress]], Xi stated, "We will fully implement the Party's basic policy on religious affairs, uphold the principle that religions in China must be Chinese in orientation and provide active guidance to religions so that they can adapt themselves to a socialist society." ===Ideology=== {{See also|Eight-point Regulation}} Xi has said that "only socialism can save China."{{Cite news |last=Tian |first=Yew Lun |date=9 September 2021 |title=Analysis: Unleashing reforms, Xi returns to China's socialist roots |language=en |work=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/unleashing-reforms-xi-returns-chinas-socialist-roots-2021-09-09/ |access-date=17 September 2022}} Xi has also declared [[socialism with Chinese characteristics]] to be the "only correct path to realize national rejuvenation."{{Cite web |date=9 October 2021 |title=Socialism with Chinese characteristics only correct path toward national rejuvenation: Xi |url=http://ex.chinadaily.com.cn/exchange/partners/82/rss/channel/www/columns/np4922/stories/WS61610570a310cdd39bc6dcc1.html |access-date=17 September 2022 |website=[[China Daily]]}} According to [[BBC News]], while the CCP was perceived to have abandoned its communist ideology since it initiated [[Reform and opening up|economic reforms]] in the 1970s, Xi is believed by some observers to be more believing in the "idea of a communist project",{{Cite news |last=McDonell |first=Stephen |date=22 September 2021 |title=Changing China: Xi Jinping's effort to return to socialism |language=en-GB |work=[[BBC News]] |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/business-58579831 |access-date=17 September 2022}} being described as a [[Marxist–Leninist]] by former Australian prime minister Kevin Rudd.{{Cite news |last=Rudd |first=Kevin |author-link=Kevin Rudd |date=10 October 2022 |title=The World According to Xi Jinping |language=en-US |work=[[Foreign Affairs]] |url=https://www.foreignaffairs.com/china/world-according-xi-jinping-china-ideologue-kevin-rudd |access-date=12 October 2022}} Xi's emphasis on prioritizing ideology has included re-asserting the Party's goal of eventually [[Socialist mode of production|realizing communism]] and reprimanding those who dismiss communism as impractical or irrelevant.{{sfn|Pieke|Hofman|2022|p=48}} Xi described the communist ideal as the "calcium" in a Party member's spine, without which the Party member would suffer the "osteoporosis" of political decay and be unable to stand upright.{{sfn|Pieke|Hofman|2022|p=48}} {{Quote box | quote = China's success proves that socialism is not dead. It is thriving. Just imagine this: had socialism failed in China, had our communist party collapsed like the party in the Soviet Union, then global socialism would lapse into a long dark age. And communism, like Karl Marx once said, would be a haunting spectre lingering in limbo. | source = — Xi Jinping during a speech in 2018 | align = right | width = 25em }} Xi has supported greater CCP control over the PRC, saying "government, the military, society and schools, north, south, east and west – [[The Party leads everything|the party leads them all]]."{{Cite web |last=Gan |first=Nectar |date=25 October 2017 |title=The Communist Party's tighter grip on China in 16 characters |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/policies-politics/article/2116836/xi-jinping-thought-communist-partys-tighter-grip-china |access-date=14 August 2022 |website=[[South China Morning Post]] |language=en}} During the [[100th Anniversary of the Chinese Communist Party|100th anniversary of the CCP]] in 2021, he said that "without the Communist Party of China, there would be no new China and no national rejuvenation," and that "the leadership of the Party is the defining feature of socialism with Chinese characteristics and constitutes the greatest strength of this system."{{Cite web |date=1 July 2021 |title=Full text of Xi Jinping's speech on the CCP's 100th anniversary |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Politics/Full-text-of-Xi-Jinping-s-speech-on-the-CCP-s-100th-anniversary |access-date=14 August 2022 |website=[[Nikkei Asia]] |language=en-GB}} Xi has said that "our party is so large, and our country, so huge-it is such that if the Party Central lacks the sole authority to make decisions, nothing can be achieved", comparing the Party Central to the CCP's "[[cerebrum]]" and the "[[central nervous system]]", and said it "should have the sole authority in making decisions".{{Sfn|Tsang|Cheung|2024|p=70}} Xi has said that China, despite many setbacks, has achieved great progress under the CCP, saying that "socialism with Chinese characteristics has become the standard-bearer of 21st-century socialist development."{{Cite web |last=Zheng |first=William |date=16 September 2022 |title=Xi article gives insight into China's direction ahead of party congress |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3192677/xi-article-gives-insight-chinas-direction-ahead-party-congress |access-date=17 September 2022 |website=[[South China Morning Post]] |language=en}} However, he has also warned that it will take a long time for China under the CCP to complete its rejuvenation, and during this timeframe, party members must be vigilant to not let CCP rule collapse. Xi has spoken out against "[[historical nihilism]]", meaning historical viewpoints that challenge the official line of the CCP.{{Sfn|Wong|2023|p=181}} Xi said that one of the reasons for the [[Dissolution of the Soviet Union|collapse of the Soviet Union]] has been historical nihilism.{{Cite news |title=China is struggling to keep control over its version of the past |url=https://www.economist.com/china/2016/10/29/china-is-struggling-to-keep-control-over-its-version-of-the-past |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180714135236/https://www.economist.com/china/2016/10/29/china-is-struggling-to-keep-control-over-its-version-of-the-past |archive-date=14 July 2018 |access-date=13 August 2022 |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |issn=0013-0613}} Subscribing to the view that socialism will eventually triumph over capitalism, Xi has said "Marx and Engels's [[Dialectical materialism|analysis of the basic contradictions]] of [[Capitalist mode of production (Marxist theory)|capitalist society]] is not outdated, nor is the [[historical materialism|historical materialist view]] that capitalism is bound to die out and socialism bound to win."{{Cite web |last=Hancock |first=Tom |date=6 June 2022 |title=Marxism Makes a Comeback in China's Crackdown on 'Disorderly Capital' |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2022-06-06/marxism-makes-a-comeback-in-china-s-crackdown-on-disorderly-capital |access-date=17 September 2022 |website=[[Bloomberg News]]}} Xi has overseen the increase of "Socialist Political Economy With Chinese Characteristics" as a major study topic for academics in China, aiming to decrease the influence of Western-influenced economics. Though he has called a stop to what he considers to be "disorderly expansion of capital", he has also said that "it is necessary to stimulate the vitality of capital of all types, including nonpublic capital, and give full play to its positive role." Xi has ruled out a [[multi-party system]] for China, saying that "constitutional monarchy, imperial restoration, parliamentarism, a multi-party system and a presidential system, we considered them, tried them, but none worked."{{Cite news |date=2 April 2014 |title=Xi says multi-party system didn't work for China |language=en |work=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-politics-xi-idUSBREA3107S20140402 |access-date=13 August 2022}} However, Xi considers China to be a [[Democracy in China|democracy]], saying that "China's [[socialist democracy]] is the most comprehensive, genuine and effective democracy."{{Cite web |title=Democracy |url=https://decodingchina.eu/democracy/ |access-date=13 August 2022 |website=Decoding China |date=4 February 2021 |language=en-US}} China's definition of democracy is different from [[liberal democracies]] and is rooted in Marxism–Leninism, and is based on the phrases people's democratic dictatorship and [[democratic centralism]]. Xi has additionally coined the term whole-process people's democracy which he said was about having "the people as masters".{{Cite web |last=Chen |first=Stella |date=23 November 2021 |title=Whole-Process Democracy |url=https://chinamediaproject.org/the_ccp_dictionary/whole-process-democracy/ |access-date=13 August 2022 |website=China Media Project |language=en-US}} Foreign analysts and observers have widely disputed that China is a democracy, saying that it is a one-party authoritarian state and Xi an authoritarian leader.{{refn|name=dictatorandauthoritarian|{{Cite journal|last=Brady|first=Anne-Marie|date=2015|title=China's Foreign Propaganda Machine|url=https://muse.jhu.edu/content/crossref/journals/journal_of_democracy/v026/26.4.brady.html|journal=Journal of Democracy|language=en|volume=26|issue=4|pages=51–59|doi=10.1353/jod.2015.0056|s2cid=146531927|issn=1086-3214|url-access=subscription}}{{Cite journal|last=Amako|first=Satoshi|date=2 January 2018|title=China's authoritarian path to development: is democratization possible?, by Liang Tang, Abingdon, Routledge, 2017, 263pp., {{text|ISBN}}: 978-1-138-01647-7|journal=Journal of Contemporary East Asia Studies|volume=7|issue=1|pages=81–83|doi=10.1080/24761028.2018.1483700|issn=2476-1028|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|last=Tung|first=Hans H.|title=Economic Growth and Endogenous Authoritarian Institutions in Post-Reform China|publisher=Palgrave Macmillan|year=2019|isbn=978-3-030-04828-0|location=|pages=}}{{Cite journal|last1=Howell|first1=Jude|last2=Pringle|first2=Tim|date=2019|title=Shades of Authoritarianism and State–Labour Relations in China|journal=British Journal of Industrial Relations|language=en|volume=57|issue=2|pages=223–246|doi=10.1111/bjir.12436|s2cid=158485609|issn=1467-8543|url=https://eprints.soas.ac.uk/29847/1/Howell%20and%20Pringle_Shades%20of%20Authoritarianism_British_Journal_of_Industrial_Relations.pdf}}{{Cite journal|last=Düben|first=Björn Alexander|date=3 March 2020|title=Xi Jinping and the End of Chinese Exceptionalism|journal=Problems of Post-Communism|volume=67|issue=2|pages=111–128|doi=10.1080/10758216.2018.1535274|s2cid=158657283|issn=1075-8216}}{{Cite book|last=Tung|first=Hans H.|title=Economic Growth and Endogenous Authoritarian Institutions in Post-Reform China|publisher=Palgrave Macmillan|year=2019|isbn=978-3-030-04828-0}}}} Xi has sometimes referred to as "[[Traditionalist conservatism|traditionalist]]" or "[[Conservatism in China#Neoauthoritarianism|neo-authoritarian]]".{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=8t6VLxjTZ2IC&dq=%22Neo+Authoritarian%22+%22Xi%22&pg=RA2-PR32 |title=The Penguin History of Modern China: The Fall and Rise of a Great Power, 1850 – 2009 |date=29 May 2008 |publisher=Penguin Books Limited |editor=Jonathan Fenby |page=xxxii |isbn=978-0-14-191761-0 |quote=Wang Huning, had fashioned the neo-authoritarian creed Xi was busy putting into practice.}}{{Cite news |last=Zi |first=Yang |date=6 July 2016 |title=Xi Jinping and China's Traditionalist Restoration |url=https://jamestown.org/program/xi-jinping-chinas-traditionalist-restoration/ |access-date=22 June 2024 |work=The Jamestown Foundation}}{{Cite news |last=Cai |first=Jane |date=12 June 2023 |title=How China's Xi Jinping promotes mix of Marxism and traditional culture to further Communist Party and 'Chinese dream' |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3223805/how-chinas-xi-jinping-promotes-mix-marxism-and-traditional-culture-further-communist-party-and |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230612111635/http://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3223805/how-chinas-xi-jinping-promotes-mix-marxism-and-traditional-culture-further-communist-party-and |archive-date=12 June 2023 |access-date=22 June 2024 |work=South China Morning Post}} Xi has additionally rejected [[Westernization|Westernisation]] as the only way to modernize, instead promoting what he says is "[[Chinese-style modernization|Chinese-style modernisation]]".{{Cite news |date=8 February 2023 |title=Xi Rejects Westernization in Show of Faith in Self Reliance |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-02-08/xi-calls-for-china-s-modernization-path-to-surpass-capitalism |access-date=10 February 2023}} He has identified five concepts as part of Chinese-style modernisation, including modernisation of a huge population, common prosperity, material and cultural-ethical advancement, harmony between humanity and nature, and peaceful development.{{Cite news |last=Xi |first=Jinping |date=16 October 2022 |title=Hold High the Great Banner of Socialism with Chinese Characteristics and Strive in Unity to Build a Modern Socialist Country in All Respects |work=[[Qiushi]] |url=http://en.qstheory.cn/2023-01/06/c_845251.htm |access-date=10 February 2023}} ====Xi Jinping Thought==== {{main|Xi Jinping Thought}} [[File:A political slogan on the wall in Longhua District, Shenzhen, Guangdong, China, picture1.jpg|thumb|A billboard promoting [[Xi Jinping Thought]] in [[Shenzhen]]]] In September 2017, the CCP Central Committee decided that Xi's political philosophies, generally referred to as "Xi Jinping Thought on Socialism with Chinese Characteristics for a New Era," would become part of the Party Constitution.{{Cite news|url=http://news.xinhuanet.com/politics/2017-09/18/c_1121683516.htm|script-title=zh:中共中央政治局召开会议 研究拟提请党的十八届七中全会讨论的文件-新华网|last1=Liu|first1=Xiaodong|last2=Chen|first2=Yu|date=18 September 2017|publisher=[[Xinhua News Agency]]|language=zh-hans|trans-title=The Political Bureau of the CPC Central Committee Convened to Study the Documents to be Submitted to the Seventh Plenary Session of the 18th CPC Central Committee|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171011094706/http://news.xinhuanet.com/politics/2017-09/18/c_1121683516.htm|archive-date=11 October 2017|access-date=4 October 2017}}{{Cite news|url=http://chinadigitaltimes.net/2017/09/party-constitution-amendment-signal-xis-power/|title=CCP Constitution Amendment May Signal Xi's Power – China Digital Times (CDT)|last=Rudolph|first=Josh|date=19 September 2017|work=[[China Digital Times]] (CDT)|access-date=4 October 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171003234252/http://chinadigitaltimes.net/2017/09/party-constitution-amendment-signal-xis-power/|archive-date=3 October 2017|url-status=live}} Xi first made mention of the "Thought on Socialism with Chinese Characteristics for a New Era" in his opening day speech delivered to the 19th Party Congress in October 2017. His Politburo Standing Committee colleagues, in their own reviews of Xi's keynote address at the Congress, prepended the name "Xi Jinping" in front of "Thought".{{cite news|url=http://news.xinhuanet.com/english/2017-10/19/c_136689808.htm|title=CPC creates Xi Jinping Thought on Socialism with Chinese Characteristics for a New Era|last1=Zhang|first1=Ling|date=18 October 2017|work=[[Xinhua News Agency]]|access-date=19 October 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171018195301/http://news.xinhuanet.com/english/2017-10/19/c_136689808.htm|archive-date=18 October 2017}} On 24 October 2017, at its closing session, the 19th Party Congress approved the incorporation of Xi Jinping Thought into the Constitution of the CCP, while in March 2018, the National People's Congress changed the state constitution to include Xi Jinping Thought.{{Cite web |last=Ken |first=Suzuki |date=27 November 2018 |title=China's New "Xi Jinping Constitution": The Road to Totalitarianism |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a05803/ |access-date=11 August 2022 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} [[File:201901 Xi Jinping's books at Shanghai Library.jpg|left|thumb|''[[The Governance of China]]'' in different languages presented at [[Shanghai Library]]]] Xi himself has described the Thought as part of the broad framework created around socialism with Chinese characteristics, a term coined by Deng Xiaoping that places China in the [[primary stage of socialism]]. In official party documentation and pronouncements by Xi's colleagues, the Thought is said to be a continuation of [[Marxism–Leninism]], [[Mao Zedong Thought]], [[Deng Xiaoping Theory]], the [[Three Represents]], and the [[Scientific Outlook on Development]], as part of a series of guiding ideologies that embody "Marxism adopted to Chinese conditions" and contemporary considerations. It has additionally been described as the "21st century Marxism" by two professors in the Central Party School of the CCP. [[Wang Huning]], a top political adviser and a close ally of Xi, has been described as pivotal to developing Xi Jinping Thought. The concepts and context behind Xi Jinping Thought are elaborated in Xi's ''[[The Governance of China]]'' book series, published by the [[Foreign Languages Press]] for an international audience. Volume one was published in September 2014, followed by volume two in November 2017.{{Cite news|url=http://news.xinhuanet.com/english/2017-11/07/c_136733705.htm|title=Second volume of Xi's book on governance published|last=Yang|first=Yi|date=7 November 2017|publisher=[[Xinhua News Agency]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171206143920/http://news.xinhuanet.com/english/2017-11/07/c_136733705.htm|archive-date=6 December 2017|access-date=6 December 2017}} ''[[Xuexi Qiangguo]]'', an app for teaching Xi Jinping Thought had become the most popular smartphone app in China in 2019, as the CCP launched a new campaign that calls on its cadres to immerse themselves in the political doctrine every day.{{Cite news|url=https://www.scmp.com/tech/apps-social/article/2186037/chinas-most-popular-app-propaganda-tool-teaching-xi-jinping-thought|title=China's most popular app is a propaganda tool teaching Xi Jinping Thought|last=Huang|first=Zheping|date=14 February 2019|work=[[South China Morning Post]]|access-date=7 September 2019|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190920105809/https://www.scmp.com/tech/apps-social/article/2186037/chinas-most-popular-app-propaganda-tool-teaching-xi-jinping-thought|archive-date=20 September 2019}} ==Personal life== ===Family=== [[File:Peng Liyuan, Xi Jinping and Barack Obama in the Lincoln Bedroom.jpg|thumb|Xi, [[Peng Liyuan]] and U.S. president [[Barack Obama]] in the Lincoln Bedroom]] Xi's first marriage was to [[Ke Lingling]], the daughter of [[Ke Hua]], China's ambassador to the United Kingdom in the early 1980s. They divorced within a few years.{{cite news|url=https://edition.cnn.com/2012/11/07/world/asia/china-xi-jinping-profile/index.html|title=Xi Jinping: From 'sent-down youth' to China's top|last=Yuan|first=Elizabeth|date=8 November 2012|publisher=[[CNN]]|access-date=8 November 2012|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121108195546/http://edition.cnn.com/2012/11/07/world/asia/china-xi-jinping-profile/index.html|archive-date=8 November 2012}} The two were said to fight "almost every day," and after the divorce, Ke moved to England. In 1987, Xi married the prominent Chinese folk singer Peng Liyuan.{{Cite news|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2007-oct-23-fg-chileaders23-story.html|title=China's 'fifth generation' of leaders reflects nation's shifts|last=Magnier|first=Mark|date=23 October 2007|work=[[Los Angeles Times]]|access-date=21 December 2009|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823133224/http://articles.latimes.com/2007/oct/23/world/fg-chileaders23|archive-date=23 August 2011}} Xi and Peng were introduced by friends as many Chinese couples were in the 1980s. Xi was reputedly academic during their courtship, inquiring about singing techniques.{{Cite news|url=https://blogs.wsj.com/chinarealtime/2012/02/13/peng-liyuan-meet-chinas-folk-song-singing-first-lady-in-waiting/|title=Meet China's Folk Star First Lady-in-Waiting|last=Page|first=Jeremy|date=12 February 2012|work=[[The Wall Street Journal]]|access-date=7 November 2012|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120511045002/http://blogs.wsj.com/chinarealtime/2012/02/13/peng-liyuan-meet-chinas-folk-song-singing-first-lady-in-waiting/|archive-date=11 May 2012}} Peng Liyuan, a [[Celebrity|household name]] in China, was better known to the public than Xi until his political elevation. The couple frequently lived apart due largely to their separate professional lives. Peng has played a much more visible role as China's "first lady" compared to her predecessors; for example, Peng hosted U.S. First Lady [[Michelle Obama]] on her high-profile visit to China in March 2014.{{cite news|url=https://time.com/33164/michelle-obama-china-beijing-peng-liyuan/|title=Michelle Obama Tours Beijing With China's First Lady|last1=Beech|first1=Hannah|date=21 March 2014|newspaper=[[Time (magazine)|Time]]|access-date=17 July 2014|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140626122108/http://time.com/33164/michelle-obama-china-beijing-peng-liyuan/|archive-date=26 June 2014}} Xi and Peng have a daughter named [[Xi Mingze]], who graduated from [[Harvard University]] in the spring of 2015. While at Harvard, she used a pseudonym and studied Psychology and English.{{Cite news|url=https://www.newyorker.com/news/news-desk/what-did-chinas-first-daughter-find-in-america|title=What Did China's First Daughter Find in America?|last=Osnos|first=Evan|date=6 April 2015|magazine=[[The New Yorker]]|access-date=29 October 2018|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181030035420/https://www.newyorker.com/news/news-desk/what-did-chinas-first-daughter-find-in-america|archive-date=30 October 2018}} Xi's family has a home in [[Jade Spring Hill]], a garden and residential area in north-western Beijing run by the CMC.{{cite news|url=https://www.rfa.org/english/news/china/hundreds-02202015111702.html|title=Beijing Police Detain Hundreds For Trying to Visit Chinese Leaders Over New Year|last=Qiao|first=Long|date=20 February 2015|publisher=[[Radio Free Asia]]|access-date=1 March 2018|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180301164338/https://www.rfa.org/english/news/china/hundreds-02202015111702.html|archive-date=1 March 2018}} In June 2012, [[Bloomberg News]] reported that members of Xi's extended family have substantial business interests, although there was no evidence he had intervened to assist them.{{cite news|url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/2012-06-29/xi-jinping-millionaire-relations-reveal-fortunes-of-elite.html|title=Xi Jinping Millionaire Relations Reveal Fortunes of Elite|last1=Bennett|first1=Amanda|date=29 June 2012|publisher=[[Bloomberg News]]|access-date=11 September 2019|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120704222305/http://www.bloomberg.com/news/2012-06-29/xi-jinping-millionaire-relations-reveal-fortunes-of-elite.html|archive-date=4 July 2012|last2=Hirschberg|first2=Peter}} The Bloomberg News website was blocked in [[mainland China]] in response to the article.{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2012/jun/29/china-bloomberg-xi-jinping|title=China blocks Bloomberg for exposing financial affairs of Xi Jinping's family|last=Branigan|first=Tania|date=29 June 2012|newspaper=[[The Guardian]]|access-date=11 September 2012|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131120041457/http://www.theguardian.com/world/2012/jun/29/china-bloomberg-xi-jinping|archive-date=20 November 2013}} Since Xi embarked on an anti-corruption campaign, ''The New York Times'' reported members of his family were selling their corporate and real estate investments beginning in 2012.{{cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/06/18/world/asia/chinas-president-xi-jinping-investments.html|title=As China's Leader Fights Graft, His Relatives Shed Assets|last1=Forsythe|first1=Michael|date=17 June 2012|work=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=16 July 2014|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140716105611/http://www.nytimes.com/2014/06/18/world/asia/chinas-president-xi-jinping-investments.html|archive-date=16 July 2014|url-access=registration}} Relatives of highly placed Chinese officials, including seven current and former senior leaders of the Politburo of the CCP, have been named in the [[Panama Papers]], including [[Deng Jiagui]],{{cite news|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/worldviews/wp/2016/04/04/the-panama-papers-are-super-awkward-for-beijing/|title=The Panama Papers are super awkward for Beijing|last1=Rauhala|first1=Emily|date=4 April 2016|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|access-date=7 September 2019|url-status=live|url-access=limited|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160421044242/https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/worldviews/wp/2016/04/04/the-panama-papers-are-super-awkward-for-beijing/|archive-date=21 April 2016}} Xi's brother-in-law. Deng had two [[Shell corporation|shell companies]] in the [[British Virgin Islands]] while Xi was a member of the Politburo Standing Committee, which became dormant by the time Xi became general secretary of the CCP in November 2012.{{cite news|url=http://www.marketplace.org/2016/04/04/world/xi-jinpings-family-linked-panama-papers|title=Xi Jinping's family linked to Panama Papers|last=Schmitz|first=Rob|date=4 April 2016|newspaper=[[Marketplace (radio program)|Marketplace]]|access-date=6 April 2016|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160407224017/http://www.marketplace.org/2016/04/04/world/xi-jinpings-family-linked-panama-papers|archive-date=7 April 2016}} ===Personality=== Peng described Xi as hardworking and down-to-earth: "When he comes home, I've never felt as if there's some leader in the house. In my eyes, he's just my husband."{{cite news|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/shared/spl/hi/in_depth/china_politics/leadership/html/6.stm|title=China's Leaders|publisher=[[BBC News]]|access-date=26 August 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130808233513/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/shared/spl/hi/in_depth/china_politics/leadership/html/6.stm|archive-date=8 August 2013|url-status=live}} In 1992, ''[[The Washington Post]]'' journalist Lena H.
