Sun had an interview with Xi, then CCP secretary of Fuzhou; Sun described Xi as considerably more at ease and confident than many officials his age, and said that he talked without consulting notes.{{Cite news |last=Sun |first=Lena H. |date=8 June 1992 |title=Post for a 'princeling' |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/politics/1992/06/08/post-for-a-princeling/3acd443c-37e3-45ff-9103-3a9f8b0a64ea/ |access-date=12 June 2023}} He was described in a 2011 ''The Washington Post'' article by those who know him as "pragmatic, serious, cautious, hard-working, down to earth and low-key." He was described as a good hand at problem solving and "seemingly uninterested in the trappings of high office."{{cite news|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/asia-pacific/xi-jinping-likely-chinas-next-leader-called-pragmatic-low-key/2011/08/15/gIQA5W83GJ_story.html|title=Xi Jinping, likely China's next leader, called pragmatic, low-key|last=Richburg|first=Keith B.|date=15 August 2011|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|access-date=7 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191029173940/https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/asia-pacific/xi-jinping-likely-chinas-next-leader-called-pragmatic-low-key/2011/08/15/gIQA5W83GJ_story.html|archive-date=29 October 2019|url-status=live}} The Chinese state media has also cast him as a fatherly figure and a man of the people, determined to stand up for Chinese interests. ===Football=== Xi is an avid football fan.{{cite web|url=https://www.chinadaily.com.cn/a/202310/10/WS65250929a31090682a5e7bf1.html|title=Xi shows off his football skill|work=[[China Daily]]|date=2023-10-10}} While working in Hebei, it was reported that Xi often asked his friend [[Nie Weiping]], a professional [[Go (game)|Go]] player, for football tickets.{{cite web|url=https://www.chinadaily.com.cn/china/2014-10/11/content_18723212_6.htm|title=What saddens President Xi Jinping?|work=[[China Daily]]|date=2014-10-11}} During a trip to [[Republic of Ireland|Ireland]] in 2012 as China's vice-president, Xi visited [[Croke Park]], the home of the [[Gaelic Athletic Association]]. In 2011, Xi outlined a vision to turn China from a footballing minnow to a football superpower. He outlined a three-stage plan for the national team: to qualify for another [[FIFA World Cup|World Cup]], to host a World Cup and to win a World Cup.{{cite web |author1=Andrew McNicol |author2=Andrew Raine |date=2023-04-07 |title=Xi Jinping wanted China to be a global football power. What went wrong? |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2023/04/07/china/xi-jinping-china-football-dream-fail-intl-hnk-dst/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20240712102342/https://edition.cnn.com/2023/04/07/china/xi-jinping-china-football-dream-fail-intl-hnk-dst/index.html |archive-date=2024-07-12 |work=[[CNN]]}} In 2015, Xi approved China's 50-point plan for the sport, which included including football in the national school curriculum and setting up 50,000 football schools in the country by 2025.{{cite web|author=Tariq Panja|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/03/29/sports/soccer/china-soccer.html|title=China's Soccer Experiment Was a Flop. Now It May Be Over.|work=[[The New York Times]]|date=2023-03-29}} However, according to [[CNN]], "poor financial decisions and alleged high-level corruption coupled with a [[COVID-19 pandemic in mainland China|three-year pandemic]] have left the sport in tatters". In 2023, Xi said he is "not so sure" of the abilities of the national team.{{cite web |date=2023-11-19 |title=Xi says 'not so sure' of Chinese football team's abilities |url=https://www.channelnewsasia.com/asia/china-xi-not-so-sure-football-team-abilities-world-cup-3931906 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231214013447/https://www.channelnewsasia.com/asia/china-xi-not-so-sure-football-team-abilities-world-cup-3931906 |archive-date=2023-12-14 |work=[[CNA (TV network)|Channel NewsAsia]]}} === Interests === Unlike previous Chinese leaders, Chinese state media has given a more encompassing view of Xi's private life, although still strictly controlled. According to Xinhua News Agency, Xi would swim one kilometer and walk every day as long as there was time, and is interested in foreign writers, especially Russian. Some of his favorite foreign authors include [[Leo Tolstoy]], [[Mikhail Sholokhov]], [[Victor Hugo]], [[Honoré de Balzac]], [[Johann Wolfgang von Goethe]] and [[Jack London]].{{Cite web |title=A look at what's on Chinese President Xi Jinping's shelves |url=https://www.usfq.edu.ec/sites/default/files/inline-files/a-look-at-whats-on-chinese-president-xi-jinpings-shelves.pdf |website=[[Universidad San Francisco de Quito]]}} Xi reportedly invoked ''[[What Is to Be Done? (novel)|What Is to Be Done?]]'' by [[Nikolay Chernyshevsky]] as a guide during the [[16th BRICS summit]].{{Cite news |last1=Garnaut |first1=John |last2=Chetwin |first2=Sam |date=2024-12-15 |title=This Unreadable Russian Novel Drives Xi's Struggle Against America |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2024/12/15/opinion/china-brics-xi-jinping-trump.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241215061246/https://www.nytimes.com/2024/12/15/opinion/china-brics-xi-jinping-trump.html |archive-date=15 December 2024 |access-date=2024-12-15 |work=[[The New York Times]] |language=en |url-status=live }} Xi is reported to also like films and TV shows such as ''[[Saving Private Ryan]]'',{{Cite news |last=Buckley |first=Chris |date=8 February 2012 |title=China leader-in-waiting carries heavy political baggage to U.S. |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-usa-xi-idUSTRE8170FZ20120208 |access-date=29 August 2022 |work=[[Reuters]] |language=en |quote=At a 2007 dinner with the U.S. ambassador to Beijing, Xi mentioned his affection for Hollywood films, including World War II stories such as "Saving Private Ryan," according to U.S. diplomatic cables made public by WikiLeaks.}} ''[[Sleepless in Seattle]]'', ''[[The Godfather]]'',{{Cite news |date=2022-10-19 |title=Xi Jinping: the making of a dictator |url=https://www.economist.com/1843/2022/10/19/xi-jinping-the-making-of-a-dictator |archive-url=https://archive.today/20221020045843/https://www.economist.com/1843/2022/10/19/xi-jinping-the-making-of-a-dictator |archive-date=2022-10-20 |access-date=2024-05-03 |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |issn=0013-0613 |quote=He likes football, claims to swim 1,000 metres a day and is a fan of "Sleepless in Seattle", "The Godfather" and "Saving Private Ryan".}} and ''[[Game of Thrones]]'',{{cite news |date=28 April 2019 |title=Xi Jinping shows his love of Game of Thrones but warns reality must not mirror fantasy |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3007991/xi-jinping-shows-his-love-game-thrones-warns-reality-must-not |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190505041030/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/politics/article/3007991/xi-jinping-shows-his-love-game-thrones-warns-reality-must-not |archive-date=5 May 2019 |access-date=5 May 2019 |work=[[South China Morning Post]]}} and he has praised Chinese independent film-maker [[Jia Zhangke]].{{cite news |last=Branigan |first=Tania |date=13 February 2012 |title=The Guardian profile: Xi Jinping |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2012/feb/13/xi-jinping-profile-china |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131106055944/http://www.theguardian.com/world/2012/feb/13/xi-jinping-profile-china |archive-date=6 November 2013 |access-date=14 February 2012 |newspaper=[[The Guardian]] |location=UK}} ==Public image== It is hard to gauge the opinion of the Chinese public on Xi, as no independent surveys exist in China and social media is heavily censored.{{Cite news |last=Lin |first=Liza |date=13 October 2022 |title=Do Chinese People Like Xi Jinping? You Won't Find an Easy Answer Online |language=en-US |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/what-do-chinese-people-think-of-xi-jinping-its-very-hard-to-tell-11665664627 |access-date=17 October 2022 |issn=0099-9660}} However, he is believed to be widely popular in the country.{{Cite news |last=Phillips |first=Tom |date=14 October 2017 |title=Chairman Xi crushes dissent but poor believe he's making China great |language=en-GB |work=[[The Guardian]] |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2017/oct/14/xi-jinping-crushes-dissent-but-making-china-great-again |url-status=live |access-date=26 August 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190826122356/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2017/oct/14/xi-jinping-crushes-dissent-but-making-china-great-again |archive-date=26 August 2019 |issn=0029-7712}}{{Cite news |last1=Zhou |first1=Christina |last2=Mantesso |first2=Sean |date=6 March 2019 |title='No room for mercy in this system': Xi Jinping's rise from cave dweller to post-modern chairman |language=en-AU |work=[[ABC News (Australia)]] |publisher= |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2019-03-06/the-astonishing-rise-of-chinese-president-xi-jinping/10794486 |url-status=live |access-date=26 August 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190826051002/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2019-03-06/the-astonishing-rise-of-chinese-president-xi-jinping/10794486 |archive-date=26 August 2019}} According to a 2014 poll co-sponsored by the [[John F. Kennedy School of Government|Harvard Kennedy School's]] [[Ash Center for Democratic Governance and Innovation]], Xi ranked 9 out of 10 in domestic approval ratings.{{Cite news|url=https://thediplomat.com/2014/12/the-worlds-most-popular-leader-chinas-president-xi/|title=The World's Most Popular Leader: China's President Xi|last=Tiezzi|first=Shannon|date=20 December 2014|newspaper=[[The Diplomat (magazine)|The Diplomat]]|language=en-US|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191027062238/https://thediplomat.com/2014/12/the-worlds-most-popular-leader-chinas-president-xi/|archive-date=27 October 2019|access-date=27 October 2019}} A [[YouGov]] poll released in July 2019 found that about 22% of people in mainland China list Xi as the person they admire the most, a plurality, although this figure was less than 5% for residents of Hong Kong.{{cite web|url=https://today.yougov.com/topics/international/articles-reports/2019/07/18/2019-worlds-most-admired|title=Michelle Obama is the world's most admired woman|last=Smith|first=Matthew|date=18 July 2019|website=[[YouGov]]|language=en-us|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190729151150/https://today.yougov.com/topics/international/articles-reports/2019/07/18/2019-worlds-most-admired|archive-date=29 July 2019|access-date=17 August 2019}} In the spring of 2019, the Pew Research Center made a survey on confidence on Xi Jinping among six-country medians based on Australia, India, Indonesia, Japan, Philippines and South Korea, which indicated that a median 29% have confidence in Xi Jinping to do the right thing regarding world affairs, meanwhile a median of 45% have no confidence; these numbers are slightly higher than those of North Korean leader Kim Jong Un (23% confidence, 53% no confidence).{{cite news |last1=Silver |first1=Laura |last2=Devlin |first2=Kat |last3=Huang |first3=Christine |date=5 December 2019 |title=China's Economic Growth Mostly Welcomed in Emerging Markets, but Neighbors Wary of Its Influence |publisher=[[Pew Research Center]] |url=https://www.pewresearch.org/global/2019/12/05/chinas-economic-growth-mostly-welcomed-in-emerging-markets-but-neighbors-wary-of-its-influence/ |access-date=4 August 2020}} A poll by [[Politico]] and [[Morning Consult]] in 2021 found that 5% of Americans have a favorable opinion of Xi, 38% unfavorable, 17% no opinion and 40%, a plurality, never hearing of him.{{Cite web |date=19–22 March 2021 |title=National Tracking Poll #2103129 |url=https://www.politico.com/f/?id=00000178-6298-d5c9-ab7c-e79a8aa50000 |access-date=29 August 2022 |website=Morning Consult and Politico}} In 2017, ''The Economist'' named him the most powerful person in the world.{{Cite news|url=https://www.economist.com/leaders/2017/10/14/xi-jinping-has-more-clout-than-donald-trump-the-world-should-be-wary|title=Xi Jinping has more clout than Donald Trump. The world should be wary|date=14 October 2017|newspaper=[[The Economist]]|access-date=11 September 2019|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190918133802/https://www.economist.com/leaders/2017/10/14/xi-jinping-has-more-clout-than-donald-trump-the-world-should-be-wary|archive-date=18 September 2019|issn=0013-0613|url-access=subscription}} In 2018, ''[[Forbes]]'' ranked him as [[Forbes list of the World's Most Powerful People|the most powerful and influential person in the world]], replacing [[President of Russia|Russian President]] Vladimir Putin, who had been ranked so for five consecutive years.{{cite news|url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/davidewalt/2018/05/08/the-worlds-most-powerful-people-2018/#7e7884216c47|title=The World's Most Powerful People 2018|last1=Ewalt|first1=David M.|date=31 May 2018|work=[[Forbes]]|access-date=7 September 2019|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180509222602/https://www.forbes.com/sites/davidewalt/2018/05/08/the-worlds-most-powerful-people-2018/#7e7884216c47|archive-date=9 May 2018|author-link=David McWalter}} Since 2013, [[Reporters Without Borders]], an international non-profit and non-governmental organization with the stated aim of safeguarding the right to freedom of information, included Xi among the list of [[press freedom predator]]s.{{cite web |author= |date=17 November 2016 |title=Xi Jinping |url=https://rsf.org/en/predator/xi-jinping |access-date=1 November 2021 |website=[[Reporters Without Borders]] |publisher=}} == Honours == {{Main|List of awards and honours received by Xi Jinping}} Xi has received state honours from several countries, including the [[Order of the Golden Eagle]] from Kazakhstan and the [[Orders, decorations, and medals of Tajikistan|Order of the Crown]] from Tajikistan.{{cite web |last=Виктор Николаев |date=14 September 2022 |title="I value this order very much": how the award ceremony for Xi Jinping was held |script-title=ru:"Я очень дорожу этим орденом": как прошла церемония награждения Си Цзиньпина |url=https://www.nur.kz/politics/universe/1987795-ya-ochen-dorozhu-etim-ordenom-kak-proshla-tseremoniya-nagrazhdeniya-si-tszinpina/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221029101603/https://www.nur.kz/politics/universe/1987795-ya-ochen-dorozhu-etim-ordenom-kak-proshla-tseremoniya-nagrazhdeniya-si-tszinpina/ |archive-date=29 October 2022 |website={{interlanguage link|Nur.kz|ru}}}}{{Cite web |date=17 June 2019 |title=China's President Xi receives Zarrintoj Order from President Emomali Rahmon |url=https://www.asiaplustj.info/en/news/tajikistan/politics/20190617/chinas-president-xi-receives-zarrintoj-order-from-president-emomali-rahmon |website=[[Asia-Plus]] |language=en}} ==Selected {{Short description|Prime Minister of Ukraine (2005, 2007–2010)}} {{pp|small=yes}} {{Use dmy dates|date=January 2023}} {{Very long|words=16,000|date=August 2024}} {{Infobox officeholder | name = Yulia Tymoshenko | native_name = {{nobold|Юлія Тимошенко}} | image = EPP Political Assembly, 13 & 14 January 2025 (54265908219) (cropped).jpg | caption = Tymoshenko in 2025 | office = [[Prime Minister of Ukraine]] | president = {{ubli|[[Viktor Yushchenko]] | [[Viktor Yanukovych]]}} | deputy = [[Oleksandr Turchynov]] | term_start = 18 December 2007 | term_end = 4 March 2010 | predecessor = Viktor Yanukovych | successor = Oleksandr Turchynov (acting) | president1 = Viktor Yushchenko | deputy1 = [[Anatoliy Kinakh]] | term_start1 = 24 January 2005 | term_end1 = 8 September 2005
Acting: 24 January 2005 – 4 February 2005 | predecessor1 = [[Mykola Azarov]] (acting) | successor1 = [[Yuriy Yekhanurov]] | office2 = Deputy Prime Minister
for [[Ministry of Energy and Coal Mining (Ukraine)|Energy and Coal Mining]] | president2 = [[Leonid Kuchma]] | primeminister2 = Viktor Yushchenko | term_start2 = 30 December 1999 | term_end2 = 19 January 2001 | successor2 = | office3 = [[People's Deputy of Ukraine]] | term_start3 = 27 November 2014 | term4 = 23 November 2007 – 19 December 2007 | term5 = 25 May 2006 – 14 June 2007 | term6 = 14 May 2002 – 4 February 2005 | term7 = 16 January 1997 – 2 March 2000 | birth_name = Yulia Volodymyrivna Hrihyan | birth_date = {{birth date and age|1960|11|27|df=y}} | birth_place = [[Dnipropetrovsk]], [[Ukrainian SSR]], Soviet Union | death_date = | death_place = | party = [[All-Ukrainian Union "Fatherland"|Batkivshchyna]] (since 1999) | otherparty = {{ubli|[[Hromada (political party)|Hromada]] (1997–1999)|[[Yulia Tymoshenko Bloc]] (2001–2012)|[[Dictatorship Resistance Committee]] (2011–2014)}} | spouse = {{marriage|[[Oleksandr Tymoshenko]]|1979}} | children = [[Eugenia Tymoshenko]] | alma_mater = {{ubli|[[National Mining University of Ukraine]]|[[Oles Honchar Dnipropetrovsk National University|Dnipropetrovsk National University]]|[[Kyiv National Economic University]]}} | website = {{URL|https://web.archive.org/web/20190805013623/https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/|tymoshenko.ua/en (archived)}} | native_name_lang = uk }} {{Infobox officeholder | deputy1 = [[File:Logo of the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine.svg|100px]]
[[People's Deputy of Ukraine]] }} '''Yulia Volodymyrivna Tymoshenko'''{{family name footnote|Volodymyrivna|Hrihyan|lang=Eastern Slavic}}{{efn|{{langx|uk|Юлія Володимирівна Тимошенко}}, {{IPA|uk|ˈjul⁽ʲ⁾ijɐ woloˈdɪmɪr⁽ʲ⁾iu̯nɐ tɪmoˈʃɛnko|IPA}} }} ({{née}} '''Hrihyan'''{{efn|{{langx|uk|Грігян}}[https://books.google.com/books?id=gCJpAAAAMAAJ An orange revolution: a personal journey through Ukrainian history] by [[Askold Krushelnycky]], [[Harvill Secker]], 2006, {{ISBN|978-0-436-20623-8}}, p. 169.}} born 27 November 1960) is a Ukrainian politician, who served as [[Prime Minister of Ukraine]] in 2005, and again from 2007 until 2010; the first woman in [[Ukraine]] to hold that position.{{cite news |date=6 Mar 2020 |script-title=uk:Як змінювався гендерний склад Кабміну за час незалежності України |language=Ukrainian |trans-title=How the gender composition of the Cabinet changed during Ukraine's independence |work=Слово і Діло |url=https://www.slovoidilo.ua/2020/03/06/infografika/polityka/yak-zminyuvavsya-hendernyj-sklad-kabminu-chas-nezalezhnosti-ukrayiny |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210418095734/https://www.slovoidilo.ua/2020/03/06/infografika/polityka/yak-zminyuvavsya-hendernyj-sklad-kabminu-chas-nezalezhnosti-ukrayiny |archive-date=18 April 2021}} She has been a member of the [[Verkhovna Rada]] as [[People's Deputy of Ukraine]] several times between 1997{{in lang|uk}} [https://itd.rada.gov.ua/mps/info/page/1792 Тимошенко Юлія Володимирівна] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210127201409/https://itd.rada.gov.ua/mps/info/page/1792|date=27 January 2021}}, [[Rada.gov.ua]] (29 August 2019) and 2007, and presently as of 2014, and was [[First Deputy Prime Minister of Ukraine]] for the fuel and energy complex from 1999 to 2001. She is a Candidate of Economic Sciences.Тимошенко Ю. В. «Государственное регулирование налоговой системы: Диссертация на соискание учёной степени кандидата экономических наук». 1999. Национальная библиотека Украины им. В. И. Вернадского Tymoshenko is the leader of the [[Batkivshchyna]] ({{langx|uk|Батьківщина}}) political party. She supports Ukraine's integration into the [[European Union]] and strongly opposes the membership of Ukraine in the Russia-led [[Eurasian Customs Union]]. She supports [[Ukraine–NATO relations|NATO membership for Ukraine]].{{cite web|url=https://www.brookings.edu/blog/order-from-chaos/2019/06/06/natos-ukraine-challenge/|title=NATO's Ukraine challenge Ukrainians want membership, but obstacles abound|website=www.brookings.edu|date=6 June 2019|access-date=19 April 2021|archive-date=28 March 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230328022930/https://www.brookings.edu/blog/order-from-chaos/2019/06/06/natos-ukraine-challenge/|url-status=live}} She co-led the [[Orange Revolution]] and was the first woman twice appointed and endorsed by parliamentary majority to become prime minister, serving from 24 January to 8 September 2005, and again from 18 December 2007 to 4 March 2010. She placed third in ''[[Forbes]]'' magazine's [[Forbes list of The World's 100 Most Powerful Women#2005 (top 10 selection)|list of the world's most powerful women]] in 2005.{{cite web |url= https://www.forbes.com/2005/07/27/powerful-women-world-cz_05powom_land_print.html |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20060111162918/https://www.forbes.com/2005/07/27/powerful-women-world-cz_05powom_land_print.html |url-status=dead|archive-date=11 January 2006|title=100 most powerful women in the world|website=Forbes}} Tymoshenko finished second in the [[2010 Ukrainian presidential election]] runoff, losing by 3.5 percentage points to the winner, [[Viktor Yanukovych]]. From 2011 to 2014, she was detained due to a criminal case that was seen by many as politically motivated persecution by President Viktor Yanukovych, but after the [[Revolution of Dignity]] she was rehabilitated by the [[Supreme Court of Ukraine]] and the [[European Court of Human Rights]]. In the concluding days of the Revolution of Dignity, she was released after [[Criminal cases against Yulia Tymoshenko since 2010|three years in jail]]. She again finished second in the [[2014 Ukrainian presidential election]], this time to [[Petro Poroshenko]]. After being a heavy favorite in the polls for several years,{{cite web |url= https://scripties.uba.uva.nl/scriptie/662187 |title=The woman in a white dress, Master's thesis |date=2018}} she came third in the first round of the [[2019 Ukrainian presidential election]], receiving 13.40% of the vote, thus failing to qualify for the second round.{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-europe-47767440 |title=Ukraine election: Comedian leads presidential contest |date=1 April 2019 |publisher=BBC |archive-date=31 March 2019 |access-date=20 April 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190331173621/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-europe-47767440 |url-status=live }} Re-elected to Ukraine's parliament in 2019, she led her party in opposition.{{cite web|url=https://www.pravda.com.ua/rus/news/2019/11/13/7231865/|title=Фракция Тимошенко перешла в оппозицию|publisher=pravda.com.ua|date=13 November 2019|access-date=19 April 2021|archive-date=13 November 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191113181228/https://www.pravda.com.ua/rus/news/2019/11/13/7231865/|url-status=live}} ==Early life and career== Tymoshenko was born Yulia Hrihyan{{cite web |url=https://www.armeniandiaspora.com/showthread.php?15778-Azeri-reporter-pesters-Yulia-Timoshenko-about-being-Armenian |title=Azeri reporter pesters Yulia Timoshenko about being Armenian |publisher=Armeniandiaspora.com |date=27 December 2004 |access-date=11 October 2011 |archive-date=29 March 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190329112814/https://www.armeniandiaspora.com/showthread.php?15778-Azeri-reporter-pesters-Yulia-Timoshenko-about-being-Armenian |url-status=live }}{{cite web |last=Ivanova |first=Galina |url=https://russianelection2008.blogspot.com/2007/11/yuliya-tymoshenko.html |title=Yuliya Tymoshenko |publisher=Russianelection2008.blogspot.com |date=12 November 2007 |access-date=11 October 2011 |archive-date=26 August 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110826115037/http://russianelection2008.blogspot.com/2007/11/yuliya-tymoshenko.html |url-status=live }} on 27 November 1960, in [[Dnipro]]petrovsk, [[Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic|Ukrainian SSR]], [[Soviet Union]].[https://books.google.com/books?id=NI1G_9j1AhcC&pg=PA604 Eastern Europe, Russia and Central Asia 2004 (Regional Surveys of the World)] by [[Europa Publications]], [[Routledge]], 2003, {{ISBN|978-1-85743-187-2}}, p. 604. Her mother, Lyudmila Telehina (''née'' Nelepova), was born on 11 August 1937, also in Dnipropetrovsk.{{cite web|url=https://makuha.info/makuha/book2.html |title=ЮЛІЯ ТИМОШЕНКО: І. РОДОВІД Ю. ТИМОШЕНКО (Yuliya Tymoshenko: [Part] I. Ancestry of Yu. Tymoshenko) |last=Chobit' |first=Dmytro |publisher=Makuha.info |language=uk|access-date=27 January 2014 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100111105106/https://makuha.info/makuha/book2.html |archive-date=11 January 2010}} Yulia's father, Volodymyr Hrihyan, who according to his [[Soviet Union passport]] was [[Latvians|Latvian]], was born on 3 December 1937, also in Dnipropetrovsk. He abandoned his wife and young daughter when Yulia was between one and three years old; Yulia used her mother's surname.{{in lang|ru}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20110321020204/https://files.korrespondent.net/persons/timoshenko Тимошенко Юлия], [[Korrespondent]] Yulia's paternal grandfather, Abram Kapitelman ({{langx|uk|Абрам Кельманович Капітельман}}), was born in 1914. After graduating from [[Dnipropetrovsk State University]] in 1940, Kapitelman was sent to work in [[Western Ukraine]], where he worked "one academic quarter" as the director of a public Jewish school in the city [[Sniatyn]]. Kapitelman was mobilized into the army in the autumn of 1940 and subsequently was killed while taking part in the [[Eastern Front (World War II)|Great Patriotic War (1941–1945)]] on 8 November 1944, with the rank of "lieutenant" in [[Signal corps]]. ===Education=== In 1977, Tymoshenko graduated from high school No. 75 in Dnipropetrovsk.{{cite web |url=https://gazeta.ua/articles/people-newspaper/_u-uliyi-timoshenko-odnoklasniki-spisuvali-kontrolni-z-matematiki/342490 |title=''Anna Usik.'' Classmates wrote off work in mathematics at Julia Tymoshenko. Newspaper "Newspaper in Ukrainian" № 1039, 09.06.2010. Category "People". Website "Gazeta.ua" |date=8 June 2010 |publisher=Gazeta.ua |access-date=11 October 2011 |archive-date=23 July 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180723212429/https://gazeta.ua/articles/people-newspaper/_u-uliyi-timoshenko-odnoklasniki-spisuvali-kontrolni-z-matematiki/342490 |url-status=live }} In 1978, Tymoshenko was enrolled in the Automatization and Telemechanics Department of the [[National Mining University of Ukraine|Dnipropetrovsk Mining Institute]].{{cite web |url=https://www.kmu.gov.ua/control/ru/publish/article?art_id=242352550&cat_id=206672379 |title=Government portal: Julia Tymoshenko got acquainted with the achievements of "the National Mining University in Dnipropetrovsk" |publisher=Kmu.gov.ua |date=18 September 2009 |access-date=11 October 2011 |archive-date=15 February 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170215023307/http://www.kmu.gov.ua/control/ru/publish/article?art_id=242352550&cat_id=206672379 |url-status=live }} In 1979, she transferred to the Economics Department of the Dnipropetrovsk State University, majoring in cybernetic engineering and graduating in 1984 with first degree honors as an engineer-economist.{{cite news|url=https://lenta.ru/lib/14159943/full.htm|script-title=ru: Тимошенко, Юлия: Бывший премьер-министр Украины|trans-title=Yulia Tymoshenko: Former Ukrainian Prime Minister|language=ru|work=LENTA.ru|date=19 October 2011|access-date=7 November 2007|archive-date=3 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303202129/http://lenta.ru/lib/14159943/full.htm|url-status=live}} In 1999, she defended her PhD dissertation, titled ''State Regulation of the tax system'', at the [[Kyiv National Economic University]]{{cite web|url=https://www.irbis-nbuv.gov.ua/cgi-bin/irbis_nbuv/cgiirbis_64.exe?Z21ID=&I21DBN=ARD&P21DBN=ARD&S21STN=1&S21REF=10&S21FMT=fullwebr&C21COM=S&S21CNR=20&S21P01=0&S21P02=0&S21P03=A=&S21COLORTERMS=1&S21STR=%D0%A2%D0%B8%D0%BC%D0%BE%D1%88%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE%20%D0%AE.%D0%92.$ |title=Dissertation of Y. Tymoshenko "State Regulation of the tax system" |publisher=Nbuv.gov.ua |access-date=28 December 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140802001656/https://www.irbis-nbuv.gov.ua/cgi-bin/irbis_nbuv/cgiirbis_64.exe?Z21ID= |archive-date=2 August 2014}} and received a Ph.D. in economics. ===Commercial career=== Tymoshenko has worked as a practicing economist and academic. Prior to her political career, she became a successful but controversial businesswoman in the [[Natural gas|gas]] industry, becoming by some estimates one of the richest people in the country. Before becoming Ukraine's first female prime minister in 2005, Tymoshenko co-led the [[Orange Revolution]].[[#BBCprofile|BBC News profile]] She was placed third in [[Forbes list of The World's 100 Most Powerful Women#2005 (top 10 selection)|''Forbes'' magazine's List of The World's 100 Most Powerful Women 2005]]. After graduating from the Dnipropetrovsk State University in 1984, Tymoshenko worked as an engineer-economist in the "Dnipro Machine-Building Plant" (which produced missiles) in Dnipropetrovsk until 1988.{{cite web|url=https://eurasianhome.org/xml/t/databases.xml?lang=en&nic=databases&country=188&letter=all&person=478|title=Eurasian Home Databases – Personalities – Tymoshenko Yuliya Volodymyrivna|work=eurasianhome.org|date=2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120303072454/https://eurasianhome.org/xml/t/databases.xml?lang=en&nic=databases&country=188&letter=all&person=478|archive-date=3 March 2012}} In 1988, as part of the ''[[perestroika]]'' initiatives, Yulia and Oleksandr Tymoshenko borrowed 5,000 [[Soviet rouble|roubles]] and opened a video-rental cooperative, perhaps with the help of Oleksander's father, Gennadi Tymoshenko, who presided over a regional film-distribution network in the provincial council.{{cite web|url= https://2000.net.ua/2000/svoboda-slova/realii/71218|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20111003121331/https://2000.net.ua/2000/svoboda-slova/realii/71218|archive-date= 3 October 2011 |title= Alexander Fidel. "Unsinkable Tymoshenko". Weekly "2000", № 3 (542), 21–27 January 2011 |publisher= 2000.net.ua |date=28 January 2010 |access-date= 11 October 2011}} From 1989 to 1991, Yulia and [[Oleksandr Tymoshenko]] founded and led a commercial [[video rental shop|video-rental]] company "Terminal" in Dnipropetrovsk,[https://www.rferl.org/content/Tymoshenko_Profile/1291005.html Ukraine's Gold-Plaited Comeback Kid], [[Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty]] (23 September 2008) In 1991, Tymoshenko established (jointly with her husband Oleksandr, Gennadi Tymoshenko, and Olexandr Gravets) "The Ukrainian Petrol Corporation", a company that supplied the agriculture industry of Dnipropetrovsk with fuel from 1991 to 1995. Tymoshenko worked as a general director. In 1995, this company was reorganized into United Energy Systems of Ukraine.{{cite web |url=https://jamestown.org/program/will-yulia-tymoshenko-be-ukraines-first-woman-prime-minister/ |title=Will Yulia Tymoshenko be Ukraine's first woman prime minister? |first=Oleg |last=Varfolomeyev |work=[[Prism (Jamestown)|Prism]], Volume 4, Issue 3 |publisher=[[Jamestown Foundation]] |date=6 February 1998 |access-date=4 February 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061125034223/https://www.jamestown.org/publications_details.php?volume_id=5&issue_id=249&article_id=2820 |archive-date=25 November 2006 |url-status=dead }} Tymoshenko served as the president of [[United Energy Systems of Ukraine]], a privately owned middleman company that became the main importer of Russian natural gas to Ukraine, from 1995 to 1 January 1997.[https://books.google.com/books?id=udlwTxw8FkYC&dq=%22United+Energy+Systems%22+Ukraine&pg=PA26 Staff Country Report Ukraine], [[International Monetary Fund]] (October 1997)[https://books.google.com/books?id=tRnMeZcgry8C&dq=%22United+Energy+Systems%22+Ukraine&pg=RA1-PA61 Ukraine: State and Nation Building] by [[Taras Kuzio]], [[Routledge]], 1998, {{ISBN|0415171954}}. During that time she was nicknamed [https://www.reuters.com/article/us-ukraine-election-tymoshenko-idUSKCN1QL0LT Ukraine's Tymoshenko: 'gas princess', prisoner, and next president?] [[reuters]] accessed 26 February 2022 the "gas princess".[https://books.google.com/books?id=C8C3xuqd6aMC&q=Energy+Systems&pg=PA340 How Ukraine Became a Market Economy and Democracy] by [[Anders Åslund]], [[Peterson Institute for International Economics]], 2009, {{ISBN|0-88132-427-2}}.[https://www.upi.com/Business_News/Energy-Industry/2012/03/29/More-gas-charges-against-Tymoshenko/UPI-51531333021974/ More gas charges against Tymoshenko] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171010211504/https://www.upi.com/Business_News/Energy-Industry/2012/03/29/More-gas-charges-against-Tymoshenko/UPI-51531333021974/ |date=10 October 2017 }}, [[United Press International]] (29 March 2012) She was also accused of "having given [[Pavlo Lazarenko]] kickbacks in exchange for her company's stranglehold on the country's gas supplies", although Judge [[Martin Jenkins]] of the [[United States District Court for the Northern District of California]], on 7 May 2004, dismissed the allegations of money laundering and conspiracy regarding UESU, Somoli Ent. et al. (companies affiliated with Yulia Tymoshenko) in connection with Lazarenko's activities.{{cite web |url= https://www.ukrweekly.com/old/archive/2004/210406.shtml |title= U.S. judge throws out 23 of 53 counts against Lazarenko (05/23/04) |publisher= Ukrweekly.com |date= 23 May 2004 |access-date= 28 December 2013 |archive-date= 29 October 2013 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20131029195757/http://www.ukrweekly.com/old/archive/2004/210406.shtml |url-status= dead}}{{Cite news|title=Many Charges Dropped Against Ukraine's Ex-Premier|url=https://www.rferl.org/a/1052721.html|access-date=1 October 2021|newspaper=Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty|date=8 April 2008|language=en|last1=Kupchinsky|first1=Roman|archive-date=1 October 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211001153940/https://www.rferl.org/a/1052721.html|url-status=live}} During this period, Tymoshenko was involved in business relations (either co-operative or hostile) with many important figures of Ukraine.[https://predprinimatel.co.ua/en/entrepreneurship/success-stories/victor-pinchuk-stanovlenie-privatizaciya-pohishenie Victor Pinchuk: formation, privatization, kidnapping] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131004214103/https://predprinimatel.co.ua/en/entrepreneurship/success-stories/victor-pinchuk-stanovlenie-privatizaciya-pohishenie |date=4 October 2013}}, Journal of business Entrepreneur (25 June 2011)[https://ukrainianweek.com/Economics/29575 Ukrainian Oligarchs] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170918172403/http://ukrainianweek.com/Economics/29575 |date=18 September 2017 }}, [[The Ukrainian Week]] (29 August 2011)[https://predprinimatel.co.ua/en/business/interview/kolomoyskiy-pinchuk Igor Kolomoisky: "I said Pinchuk, "Life it's a supermarket, take whatever you like, but the ticket office front"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160316125049/https://predprinimatel.co.ua/en/business/interview/kolomoyskiy-pinchuk |date=16 March 2016}}, Journal of business Entrepreneur (23 December 2010) Tymoshenko also had to deal with the management of the Russian corporation, [[Gazprom]].[https://books.google.com/books?id=uHHWDreGPZsC&pg=PA173 Annual Survey of Eastern Europe and the Former Soviet Union 1997: The Challenge of Integration], by Institute for East-West Studies, [[M. E. Sharpe]], 1998, {{ISBN|978-0-7656-0359-3}}, p. 173. Tymoshenko claims that, under her management, UESU successfully solved significant economic problems: from 1995 to 1997, Ukraine's multi-billion debt for Russian natural gas was paid; Ukraine resumed international cooperation in machine building, the pipe industry and construction; and Ukraine's export of goods to Russia doubled.{{cite web |url= https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SQd2KsIZ03Y |title= "Юлия" – документальный фильм о Юлии Тимошенко |publisher= YouTube |date= 25 January 2013 |access-date= 28 December 2013 |archive-date= 6 May 2016 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20160506223549/https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SQd2KsIZ03Y |url-status= live }} In the period of 1995 to 1997, Tymoshenko was considered one of the richest business people in Ukraine.According to Matthew Brzezinski (author of [https://www.amazon.co.uk/dp/0684869772 "Casino Moscow: A Tale of Greed and Adventure on Capitalism's Wildest Frontier"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210311211132/https://www.amazon.co.uk/dp/0684869772 |date=11 March 2021 }}), she "gained control over nearly 20% of Ukraine's gross national product, an enviable position that probably no other private company in the world could boast." Quoted by James Meek, [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2004/nov/26/ukraine.gender "The millionaire revolutionary] ," [[The Guardian]] (26 November 2004) When Tymoshenko made her initial foray into national politics, her company became an instrument of political pressure on her and on her family. UESU top management faced prosecution.{{cite web |url= https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SQd2KsIZ03Y |title= Yulia ( documentary by Coppola Production) |publisher= Youtube.com |date= 25 January 2013 |access-date= 28 December 2013 |archive-date= 6 May 2016 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20160506223549/https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SQd2KsIZ03Y |url-status= live }} Since 1998, Tymoshenko has been a prominent politician in Ukraine. She was not included in the list of "100 richest Ukrainians" in 2006.{{cite web |url=https://www.unian.net/society/38676-100-samyih-bogatyih-lyudey-ukrainyi.html |title="100 richest people in Ukraine" by the experts of the magazine "Focus" in 2007 |publisher=Unian.net |date=23 March 2007 |access-date=28 December 2013 |archive-date=16 April 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190416170840/https://www.unian.net/society/38676-100-samyih-bogatyih-lyudey-ukrainyi.html |url-status=live }}{{cite web |author= Фото: Корреспондент |url= https://korrespondent.net/business/1227140-zolotaya-sotnya-polnyj-spisok-samyh-bogatyh-lyudej-ukrainy |title= The Golden Hundred: a complete list of the richest people of Ukraine |publisher= Korrespondent.net |date= 7 September 2010 |access-date= 28 December 2013 |archive-date= 26 June 2018 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20180626111719/https://korrespondent.net/business/1227140-zolotaya-sotnya-polnyj-spisok-samyh-bogatyh-lyudej-ukrainy |url-status= live }} ==Political career== ===Early career=== Tymoshenko entered politics in 1996, when she was elected to the Verkhovna Rada (the Ukrainian parliament) in constituency No. 229, [[Bobrynets]], [[Kirovohrad Oblast]], winning a record 92.3% of the vote. In Parliament, Tymoshenko joined the ''Constitutional Centre'' faction.{{Cite news |trans-title=Prime Minister Yulia Tymoshenko. Unofficial Biography |script-title=uk:Прем'єр-міністр Юлія Тимошенко. Неофіційна біографія |url=https://www.pravda.com.ua/articles/2005/02/4/3006785/ |access-date=2023-07-08 |website=[[Ukrainska Pravda]] |language=uk |archive-date=2 August 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190802233256/https://www.pravda.com.ua/articles/2005/02/4/3006785/ |url-status=live }} In February 1997 this [[centrists]] faction was 56 lawmakers strong{{cite book|first=Taras|last=Kuzio|title=Contemporary Ukraine: Dynamics of Post-Soviet Transformation|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tedMMgz3RV8C&pg=PA26|access-date=30 January 2016|year=1998|publisher=M.E. Sharpe|isbn=978-0-7656-3150-3|page=26}} and, according to [[Ukrainska Pravda]], at first it supported the policies of [[Ukrainian President]] [[Leonid Kuchma]]. In late 1997, Tymoshenko called for impeachment and the [[1999 Ukrainian presidential election|next Ukrainian Presidential elections]] to be held not in 1999, but in the fall of 1998. In late November 1997, the [[General Prosecutor of Ukraine]] asked the Verkhovna Rada to lift Tymoshenko's parliamentary immunity, but the deputies voted against it.[https://books.google.com/books?id=uHHWDreGPZsC&pg=PA174 Annual Survey of Eastern Europe and the Former Soviet Union 1997: The Challenge of Integration] by [[Peter Rutland]], [[M. E. Sharpe]], 1998, {{ISBN|978-0-7656-0359-3}}, p. 174. Tymoshenko was [[1998 Ukrainian parliamentary election|re-elected in 1998]], winning a constituency in the Kirovohrad Oblast, and was also number six on the party list of [[Hromada (political party)|Hromada]].{{cite web|url=https://www.cvk.gov.ua/pls/vd2002/webproc0e?kodvib=11|title=CECU1998 People's Deputies elected in single-mandate constituencies|work=[[Central Election Commission of Ukraine]]|access-date=22 January 2016|archive-date=21 March 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120321154222/http://www.cvk.gov.ua/pls/vd2002/webproc0e?kodvib=11|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=https://www.cvk.gov.ua/pls/vd2002/webproc0e?kodvib=11&rejim=0|title=People's deputies who have left: Elections 29 November 1998|work=[[Central Election Commission of Ukraine]]|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110607110136/https://www.cvk.gov.ua/pls/vd2002/WEBPROC66e?kodvib=1|archive-date=7 June 2011}} She became an influential person in the parliament,[https://books.google.com/books?id=uHHWDreGPZsC&pg=PA172 Annual Survey of Eastern Europe and the Former Soviet Union 1997: The Challenge of Integration], by Institute for East-West Studies, [[M.E. Sharpe]], {{ISBN|978-0-7656-0359-3}}, p. 172. and was appointed the Chair of the Budget Committee of the Verkhovna Rada.[https://www.brama.com/ukraine-embassy/pre0721.html Ukrainian Parliament Formed Its Permanent Committees] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220311050349/http://www.brama.com/ukraine-embassy/pre0721.html |date=11 March 2022}}, Embassy of Ukraine to the United States of America (21 July 1998){{cite book|first1=Anders|last1=Åslund|author-link1=Anders Åslund|first2=Michael|last2=McFaul|author-link2=Michael McFaul|title=Revolution in Orange: The Origins of Ukraine's Democratic Breakthrough|url=https://archive.org/details/revolutioninoran00ande|url-access=registration|year=2006|publisher=Carnegie Endowment for International Peace|isbn=978-0-87003-221-9|page=[https://archive.org/details/revolutioninoran00ande/page/12 12]}}{{cite book|first=Sarah|last=Whitmore|title=State Building in Ukraine: The Ukrainian parliament, 1990-2003|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jlja1Brj8HkC&pg=PA106|date=20 May 2004|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-0-203-39812-8|page=106}} After Hromada's party leader Pavlo Lazarenko fled to the United States in February 1999 to avoid investigations for embezzlement, various faction members left Hromada to join other parliamentary factions,{{cite book|first=Taras|last=Kuzio|title=Democratic Revolution in Ukraine: From Kuchmagate to Orange Revolution|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Rr3aAAAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PT29|date=13 September 2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-317-99647-7|chapter=4}} among them Tymoshenko, who set up the [[All-Ukrainian Union "Fatherland"]] faction in March 1999 in protest against the methods of Lazarenko.{{in lang|uk}} [https://www.rbc.ua/ukr/vyboru2012/party/p2/ All-Ukrainian Union "Fatherland"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220220144401/https://www.rbc.ua/ukr/vyboru2012/party/p2 |date=20 February 2022 }}, [[RBC Ukraine]] "Fatherland" was officially registered as a political party in September 1999, and began to attract the voters who had voted for [[Yevhen Marchuk]] in the October 1999 presidential election. In 2000, "Fatherland" went in opposition to President Kuchma. ====Deputy Prime Minister for fuel and energy==== [[File:Julija tymoschenko 2002.jpg|thumb|Yulia Tymoshenko in 2002]] From late December 1999 to January 2001, Tymoshenko was the Deputy Prime Minister for the fuel and energy sector in the [[Cabinet of Ministers of Ukraine|cabinet]] of [[Viktor Yushchenko]].{{cite book|first1=Andrej N.|last1=Lushnycky|first2=Mykola|last2=Riabchuk|author-link2=Mykola Riabchuk|title=Ukraine on Its Meandering Path Between East and West|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=cQqr7f9QkngC&pg=PA87|access-date=7 November 2015|year=2009|publisher=Peter Lang|isbn=978-3-03911-607-2|page=87}} She officially left parliament on 2 March 2000. Under her guidance, Ukraine's revenue collections from the electricity industry grew by several thousand percent. She scrapped the practice of barter in the electricity market, requiring industrial customers to pay for their electricity in cash.[https://books.google.com/books?id=uU-uTX6IYCQC&pg=PT77 Energy Dependency, Politics and Corruption in the Former Soviet Union: Russia's Power, Oligarchs' Profits and Ukraine's Missing Energy Policy, 1995–2006] by Margarita M. Balmaceda, [[Routledge]], 2007, {{ISBN|978-0-415-43779-0}}, p. 59. She also terminated exemptions for many organizations[https://www.taraskuzio.net/media18_files/37.pdf Tymoshenko 1 & 2] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110724092956/https://www.taraskuzio.net/media18_files/37.pdf |date=24 July 2011}}, ''Kyiv Post'' (6 July 2006) which excluded them from having their power disconnected. Her reforms meant that the government had sufficient funds to pay civil servants and increase salaries.[https://books.google.com/books?id=F1fhvklyl-IC Women's Social Activism in the New Ukraine: Development and the Politics of Differentiation] by Sarah D. Phillips, [[Indiana University Press]], 2008, {{ISBN|978-0-253-21992-3}}, p. 44. In 2000, Tymoshenko's government provided an additional 18 billion hryvna for social payments. Half of this amount was collected due to withdrawal of funds from shadow schemes, the ban on barter payments and the introduction of competition rules to the energy market.{{cite web |first=Mary |last=Mycio |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2001-feb-09-mn-23176-story.html |title=Ukraine's Deputy Premier May Be Out, but She's Not Down |publisher=Articles.latimes.com |date=9 February 2001 |access-date=28 December 2013 |archive-date=4 March 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304102807/http://articles.latimes.com/2001/feb/09/news/mn-23176 |url-status=live }} On 18 August 2000, her husband Oleksandr Tymoshenko, CEO of United Energy Systems of Ukraine (UESU), was detained and arrested. Tymoshenko herself stated that her husband's arrest was the result of political pressure on her.{{cite web|url=https://www.kyivpost.com/content/ukraine/tymoshenko-says-husband39s-arrest-is-the-result-3-6056.html |title=Tymoshenko says husband's arrest is the result of political pressure on her |publisher=Kyivpost.com |date=21 November 2000 |access-date=28 December 2013}} On 19 January 2001, Kuchma ordered Tymoshenko to be dismissed. Then, Yushchenko silently accepted her dismissal, despite her achievements in the energy sector. Ukrainian media called it "the first betrayal of Viktor Yushchenko".{{cite web |url=https://www.pravda.com.ua/news/2001/01/23/2981721/ |title="Yushchenko betrayed Tymoshenko" (source in Ukrainian language) |publisher=Pravda.com.ua |date=23 January 2001 |access-date=28 December 2013 |archive-date=25 June 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180625075254/https://www.pravda.com.ua/news/2001/01/23/2981721/ |url-status=live }} Soon after her dismissal, Tymoshenko took leadership of the [[National Salvation Committee (Ukraine)|National Salvation Committee]] and became active in the ''[[Ukraine without Kuchma]]'' protests.[https://books.google.com/books?id=gP_-8rXzQs8C&pg=PA4295 Europa World Year Book 2], [[Routledge]], 2004, {{ISBN|978-1-85743-255-8}}, p. 4295. The movement embraced a number of opposition parties, such as [[Yulia Tymoshenko Bloc]], [[Batkivshchyna|"Fatherland"]], [[Ukrainian Republican Party]], [[Ukrainian Conservative Republican Party]], [[Ukrainian Platform "Sobor"|"Sobor"]], [[Ukrainian Social-Democratic Party]], Ukrainian Christian-Democratic Party and Patriotic Party.{{cite web|url=https://www.pravda.com.ua/news/2001/02/9/2981835/?attempt=1|title=February 9 – historical date?|publisher=Pravda.com.ua|date=9 February 2001|access-date=9 February 2001|archive-date=22 June 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150622200656/http://www.pravda.com.ua/news/2001/02/9/2981835/?attempt=1|url-status=live}} ===Campaigns against Kuchma and 2002 election=== On 9 February 2001, Tymoshenko founded the Yulia Tymoshenko Bloc (the National Salvation Committee merged into it), a political bloc that received 7.2% of the vote in the [[2002 Ukrainian parliamentary election|2002 parliamentary election]]. She has been head of the [[All-Ukrainian Union Fatherland|Batkivshchina]] (Fatherland) political party since the party was organised in 1999.{{Cite web|url=https://batkivshchyna.te.ua/?page=history|title=Тернопільська обласна організація Батьківщина|website=batkivshchyna.te.ua|access-date=24 July 2019|archive-date=15 May 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190515210319/https://batkivshchyna.te.ua/?page=history|url-status=live}} On 13 February 2001, Tymoshenko was arrested and charged with forging customs documents and smuggling gas in 1997 (while president of UESU).[https://books.google.com/books?id=B3bwDpegCCYC&pg=PA549 The countries of the former Soviet Union at the turn of the twenty-first century: the Baltic and European states in transition] by Ian Jeffries, {{ISBN|978-0-415-25230-0}} (2004), p. 536. Her political supporters organized numerous protest rallies near the [[Lukyanivska Prison]] where she was held in custody.{{in lang|uk}} [https://www.pravda.com.ua/news/2001/02/19/2981921/ Пристрасті за українською Жанною д'Арк] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180723182509/https://www.pravda.com.ua/news/2001/02/19/2981921/ |date=23 July 2018 }}, [[Ukrainska Pravda]] (19 February 2001) In a letter to the editor of the British newspaper ''Financial Times'', Tymoshenko claimed that Kuchma was consciously building a totalitarian system in the country:{{in lang|uk}} [https://ua.korrespondent.net/ukraine/231866-timoshenko-britancyam-kuchma-budue-totalitarizm Тимошенко британцям: Кучма будує тоталітаризм '' Tymoshenko British: Kuchma builds totalitarianism ''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220301144444/https://ua.korrespondent.net/ukraine/231866-timoshenko-britancyam-kuchma-budue-totalitarizm |date=1 March 2022 }}, ua.korrespondent.net (14 March 2001) {{Blockquote |text= I believe that Mr Kuchma's regime may go so far as to eliminate me physically, not just politically, but I have made my choice and will continue to fight him by democratic methods. President Kuchma says I have committed a crime. My only "crime" has been to fight the corruption, shadow economy and totalitarianism that have been created by this president of Ukraine. Yulia Tymoshenko Prisoner of Conscience and Former Deputy Prime Minister, Ukraine.{{Cite web |last= Tymoshenko |first= Yulia |date= 14 March 2001 |title= Письмо Тимошенко в Financial Times |trans-title= Tymoshenko's Letter to the Financial Times |website= Maidan.org.ua |url= https://maidan.org.ua//arch/arch2001/984593720.html |access-date= 11 March 2022 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20210418160155/https://maidan.org.ua//arch/arch2001/984593720.html |archive-date= 18 April 2021 |url-status= live}}}} In March 2001, Pechersk District Court (Kyiv) found the charges groundless and cancelled the arrest sanction. According to Tymoshenko, the charges were fabricated by Kuchma's regime at the behest of oligarchs threatened by her efforts to eradicate corruption and institute market-based reforms. On 9 April 2003, the Kyiv Court of Appeal issued a ruling that invalidated and cancelled proceedings on the criminal cases against Yulia and Oleksandr Tymoshenko. Despite Tymoshenko being cleared of the charges, Moscow maintained an arrest warrant for her should she enter Russia. In 2005, all charges were declared groundless and lifted.[https://www.reuters.com/article/us-russia-ukraine-tymoshenko-analysis-idUSTRE4940GP20081005 ANALYSIS-Russia finds unlikely ally in Ukraine's Tymoshenko] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170705160224/http://www.reuters.com/article/us-russia-ukraine-tymoshenko-analysis-idUSTRE4940GP20081005 |date=5 July 2017 }}, [[Reuters]] (5 October 2008)[https://www.euronews.com/2005/09/26/arrest-warrant-lifted-on-tymoshenko/ Arrest warrant lifted on Tymoshenko] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180925120659/https://www.euronews.com/2005/09/26/arrest-warrant-lifted-on-tymoshenko |date=25 September 2018}}, [[Euronews]] (26 September 2005) The criminal case was closed in Ukraine in January 2005 due to lack of evidence, and in Russia in December 2005 by reason of lapse of time.{{cite news|url=https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/108074/|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110711150920/https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/108074/|archive-date=11 July 2011|title=Tymoshenko faces yet another investigation|work=Kyiv Post|date=5 July 2011}}
{{cite news|url=https://www.unian.info/politics/554288-new-criminal-case-is-filed-against-tymoshenko.html|title=New criminal case is filed against Tymoshenko|work=UNIAN|date=13 October 2011|access-date=6 November 2015|archive-date=16 April 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190416204850/https://www.unian.info/politics/554288-new-criminal-case-is-filed-against-tymoshenko.html|url-status=live}}
{{cite news|first=Helen|last=Fawkes|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/4283844.stm|work=[[BBC News]]|title=Ukraine ex-PM warrant cancelled|date=26 September 2005|access-date=26 May 2010|archive-date=9 November 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109064005/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/4283844.stm|url-status=live}} On 18 November 2005, the Supreme Court of Ukraine issued a ruling which invalidated all criminal cases against Tymoshenko and her family.{{cite web|first=Gregory|last=Johnson|url=https://www.resourcesforlife.com/docs/item1387|title=Yulia Tymoshenko of Ukraine|work=ResourcesForLife.com|date=21 November 2008|access-date=7 November 2015|archive-date=26 May 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160526234059/http://www.resourcesforlife.com/docs/item1387|url-status=dead}} [[Criminal cases against Yulia Tymoshenko since 2010#Non-delivery by United Energy Systems of Ukraine (in 1996) of goods to Russia|Despite this, the case was reopened in Ukraine]] since May 2010, after Yanukovych came to power. {{Quote box|width=40%|align=right|quote= '''Tymoshenko on renewed cases against her:''' "This is a direct instruction from Yanukovych. This is purely political repression – this is obvious"|source= Yulia Tymoshenko's press briefing on 12 May 2010{{cite news|url= https://ria.ru/20100512/233416152.html|title=Tymoshenko received a decision to initiate a criminal case|publisher= Ria.ru|date=12 May 2010|access-date=12 May 2010}}}} Tymoshenko's husband, Oleksandr, spent two years (2002–2004) in hiding in order to avoid incarceration on charges the couple said were unfounded and politically motivated by the former Kuchma administration.[https://books.google.com/books?id=dt2TXexiKTgC&pg=PA212 A political and economic dictionary of Eastern Europe] by Alan John Day, Roger East and Richard Thomas, [[Routledge]], 2002, {{ISBN|978-1-85743-063-9}}, p. 212.[https://books.google.com/books?id=3s2IWaoo-WwC&pg=PA536 The Countries of the Former Soviet Union at the Turn of the Twenty-First Century: The Baltic and European States in Transition] by [[Ian Jeffries (author)|Ian Jeffries]], [[Routledge]], 2004, {{ISBN|978-0-415-25230-0}}, p. 536.[https://books.google.com/books?id=mw8WAQAAIAAJ Profiles of people in power: the world's government leaders] by Roger East, Carina O'Reilly and Richard Thomas, [[Routledge]], 2006, {{ISBN|978-1-85743-346-3}}, p. 600. On 30 December 2010, the US State Department informed the Ukrainian government of its concern, and indicated that "the prosecution of Tymoshenko should not be selective or politically motivated.{{cite news|url= https://www.inopressa.ru/article/31Dec2010/ft/ukraina.html|title= США предупреждают Украину в связи с арестами оппозиции|publisher= Inopressa|date= 31 December 2010|archive-date= 31 March 2019|access-date= 12 January 2022|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20190331042701/https://www.inopressa.ru/article/31Dec2010/ft/ukraina.html|url-status= live}} Once the charges were dropped, Tymoshenko reassumed her place among the leaders of the grassroots campaign against Kuchma for his alleged role in the murder of the journalist [[Georgiy Gongadze]]. In this campaign, Tymoshenko first became known as a passionate, revolutionist leader, an example of this being a TV broadcast of her smashing prison windows during one of the rallies. At the time, Tymoshenko wanted to organise a national referendum to [[impeach]] Kuchma.Ian Jeffries (2004) [https://books.google.com/books?id=B3bwDpegCCYC&pg=PA549 The countries of the former Soviet Union at the turn of the twenty-first century: the Baltic and European states in transition], {{ISBN|978-0-415-25230-0}}, p. 542. {{Quote box|width=50%|align=right|quote=Our government was doing almost an underground work under the rigorous pressure of president Kuchma and criminal-oligarchic groups. All anti-shadow and anti-corruption initiatives of the Cabinet of Ministers were being blocked, while the Government was being an object of blackmailing and different provocations. People were arrested only because their relatives were working for the Cabinet of Ministers and were carrying out real reforms that were murderous for the corrupted system of power. |source=Yulia Tymoshenko [[Nezavisimaya Gazeta]] interview (25 October 2001)[https://web.archive.org/web/20070527041203/https://www.tymoshenko.com.ua/eng/exclusive/interview/18/ "It is possible to establish order in this country in two or three years"], Julia Tymoshenko official website (25 October 2001)}} On 11 August 2001, civilian and military prosecutors in Russia opened a new criminal case against Tymoshenko accusing her of bribery.[https://books.google.com/books?id=B3bwDpegCCYC&pg=PA549 The countries of the former Soviet Union at the turn of the twenty-first century: the Baltic and European states in transition] by Ian Jeffries, {{ISBN|978-0-415-25230-0}} (2004), p. 546. On 27 December 2005, Russian prosecutors dropped these charges. Russian prosecutors had suspended an arrest warrant when she was appointed prime minister, but reinstated it after she was fired in September 2005. The prosecutors suspended it again when she came to Moscow for questioning{{cite news|agency=[[Reuters]]|title=Russia Drops Charges Against Ukrainian|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2005/12/27/international/europe/27ukraine.html|access-date=29 July 2012|date=27 December 2005|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220622231654/https://www.nytimes.com/2005/12/27/international/europe/27ukraine.html?_r=1|archive-date=22 June 2022|newspaper=The New York Times|url-status=dead}} on 25 September 2005.[https://www.ua-reporter.com/eng/13610 Russian prosecutors cancel arrest warrant for Tymoshenko] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160305210215/http://ua-reporter.com/eng/13610 |date=5 March 2016 }}, UA-Reporter (27 September 2005) Tymoshenko didn't travel to Russia during her first seven months as prime minister (the [[first Tymoshenko Government]]). In January 2002, Tymoshenko was involved in a car accident that she survived with minor injuries.{{cite news|title=Ukraine opposition leader injured|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/world/europe/1788924.stm|work=BBC News|date=29 January 2002|access-date=5 October 2007|archive-date=7 January 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090107012246/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/1788924.stm|url-status=live}} ===Role in the Orange Revolution=== [[File:Timoshenko-bez-kosy.JPG|thumb|Yulia Tymoshenko, 2007]] In late 2001, both Tymoshenko and Yushchenko attempted to create a broad opposition bloc against Kuchma, in order to win the [[2004 Ukrainian presidential election|Ukrainian presidential election of 2004]]. In late 2002, Tymoshenko, [[Oleksandr Moroz]] ([[Socialist Party of Ukraine]]), [[Petro Symonenko]] ([[Communist Party of Ukraine]]) and Yushchenko ([[Our Ukraine (political party)|Our Ukraine]]) issued a joint statement concerning "the beginning of a state revolution in Ukraine". In the 2004 Ukrainian presidential election, the communist party stepped out of the alliance, but the other parties remained allied and Symonenko was against a single candidate from the alliance[https://books.google.com/books?id=Wp7VKL4p7kQC&pg=PA117 Understanding Ukrainian Politics: Power, Politics, and Institutional Design] by [[Paul D'Anieri]], [[M. E. Sharpe]], 2006, {{ISBN|978-0-7656-1811-5}}, p. 117. (until July 2006).[https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/5169068.stm Ukraine coalition born in chaos] , BBC News (11 July 2006) In March 2004, Tymoshenko announced that leaders of "Our Ukraine", BYuT and Socialist Party of Ukraine were working on a coalition agreement concerning joint participation in the presidential campaign. Tymoshenko decided not to run for president and give way to Yushchenko. On 2 July 2004, Our Ukraine and the Yulia Tymoshenko Bloc established the ''Force of the people'', a coalition which aimed to stop "the destructive process that has, as a result of the incumbent authorities, become a characteristic for Ukraine." The pact included a promise by Yushchenko to nominate Tymoshenko as prime minister if Yushchenko should win the October 2004 presidential election. Tymoshenko was actively campaigning for Yushchenko, touring and taking part in rallies all over Ukraine. After Yushchenko had dropped out of the campaign due to his mysterious poisoning, Tymoshenko continued campaigning on his behalf.{{cite web|url=https://en.for-ua.com/news/2009/12/11/162738.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120708110747/https://en.for-ua.com/news/2009/12/11/162738.html|archive-date=8 July 2012 |title=Tymoshenko does not regret supporting Yushchenko in 2004 |publisher=En.for-ua.com |date=11 December 2009 |access-date=28 December 2013}} After the initial vote on 31 October, two candidates – Yanukovych and Yushchenko – proceeded to a runoff. As Tymoshenko earlier envisaged, Yushchenko received endorsement from former competitors who didn't make it to the runoff, such as Oleksandr Moroz (Socialist Party), Anatoliy Kinakh (Party of Industrials and Entrepreneurs), former Kyiv city mayor Oleksanrd Omelchenko and others. On 6 November 2004, Tymoshenko asked people to spread the orange symbols (orange was the color of Yushchenko's campaign). "Grab a piece of the cheapest orange cloth, make ribbons and put them everywhere" she said. "Don't wait until the campaign managers give those to you". When allegations of fraud began to spread, the "orange team" decided to conduct a parallel vote tabulation during 21 November 2004 runoff and announce the results immediately to people on Independence Square ([[Maidan Nezalezhnosti]]) in Kyiv. Tymoshenko called Kyiv residents to gather on the square and asked people from other cities and towns to come and stand for their choice. "Bring warm clothes, lard and bread, garlic and onions and come to Kyiv" she said. On 22 November 2004, massive protests broke out in cities across Ukraine: The largest, in Kyiv's Maidan Nezalezhnosti, attracted an estimated 500,000 participants.{{cite news|last=Khokhlova |first=Veronica |title=New Kids on the Bloc |url=https://select.nytimes.com/gst/abstract.html?res=F30A1FFE395A0C758EDDA80994DC404482 |newspaper=The New York Times |date=26 November 2004 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121106070544/https://select.nytimes.com/gst/abstract.html?res=F30A1FFE395A0C758EDDA80994DC404482 |archive-date=6 November 2012 |access-date=29 July 2012 |url-status=dead}} These protests became known as the Orange Revolution. On 23 November 2004, Tymoshenko led the participants of the protest to the President's Administration. On Bankova Street, special riot police prevented the procession from going any further, so people lifted Tymoshenko up and she walked on the police's shields to the Administration building. On 3 December 2004, the [[Supreme Court of Ukraine]] invalidated the results of the runoff and scheduled the re-run for 26 December 2004. After the cancellation of Yanukovych's official victory and the second round of the election, Yushchenko was elected president with 51.99% of votes (Yanukovych received 44.2% support).[https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/4035789.stm Profile: Viktor Yushchenko] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220311081638/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/4035789.stm |date=11 March 2022 }}, BBC News (9 October 2008) During the protests, Tymoshenko's speeches on the ''Maidan'' kept the momentum of the street protests going.[https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/4070663.stm Ukraine's 'goddess of revolution'] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220301144501/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/4070663.stm |date=1 March 2022 }}, BBC News (5 December 2004) Her popularity grew significantly to the point where the media began to refer to her as the Ukrainian or Slavic "Joan of Arc"{{cite news|title=The queen of Ukraine's image machine|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/world/europe/7025980.stm|newspaper=[[BBC]]|date=4 October 2007|first=Kathryn|last=Westcott|quote="Orange princess", "Gas Princess", "Goddess of the Revolution", the "Princess Leia of Ukrainian politics", "a Slavic Joan of Arc" – her nicknames over the years have acquired mythological proportions.|archive-date=11 March 2022|access-date=1 March 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220311021816/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/7025980.stm|url-status=live}} as well as "Queen of the Orange revolution"{{cite news|title=Yulia Tymoshenko|url=https://www.themoscowtimes.com/mt_profile/yulia_tymoshenko/433778.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140221141250/https://www.themoscowtimes.com/mt_profile/yulia_tymoshenko/433778.html|archive-date=21 February 2014|newspaper=[[The Moscow Times]] via the internet archive}} in addition to her pre existing sobriquet from the 1990s decade as the "Gas Princess". Additional nicknames included "Goddess of the Revolution" and the "Princess Leia of Ukrainian politics". ===First term as prime minister (February – September 2005)=== {{main|First Tymoshenko Government}} [[File:Tymoshenko Appointment Feb04 2005.jpg|thumb|Yulia Tymoshenko in [[Verkhovna Rada|Parliament]], 4 February 2005]] On 24 January 2005, Tymoshenko was appointed acting prime minister of Ukraine under Yushchenko's presidency. On 4 February, Tymoshenko's premiership appointment was ratified by the parliament with an overwhelming majority of 373 votes (226 were required for approval).{{Cite Ukrainian law|type=[[President of Ukraine|Presidential decree]]|number=144/2005|law=144/2005|name= On the recognition of J. Tymoshenko as the Prime Minister of Ukraine|date=4 February 2004}} She is the first woman appointed [[Prime Minister of Ukraine]].{{cite news |first=Mark |last=MacKinnon |url=https://www.theglobeandmail.com/news/world/peace-deal-that-frees-yulia-tymoshenko-a-harsh-blow-to-ukraines-president/article17052169/ |title=Peace deal that frees Yulia Tymoshenko a harsh blow to Ukraine's President |publisher=The Globe and Mail |date=21 February 2014 |access-date=7 November 2015 |archive-date=4 March 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304214611/http://www.theglobeandmail.com/news/world/peace-deal-that-frees-yulia-tymoshenko-a-harsh-blow-to-ukraines-president/article17052169/ |url-status=live }}{{#tag:ref|In July 2025 [[Yulia Svyrydenko]] would become the second woman to be appointed [[Prime Minister of Ukraine]].{{cite web |author=Svitlana Dorosh|title=Yulia Svirydenko - Prime Minister. What is known about her and why she is linked to Andriy Yermak|url=https://www.bbc.com/ukrainian/articles/cq6mpm7vg90o|website=[[BBC News Ukrainian]]|date=17 July 2025|access-date=30 August 2025|language=Ukrainian}}|group=nb}} The Tymoshenko cabinet did not have any other members of party Yulia Tymoshenko Bloc besides Tymoshenko herself and [[Turchynov|Oleksandr Turchynov]], who was appointed the chief of [[Security Service of Ukraine]].{{cite web |url=https://www.themoscowtimes.com/news/article/tymoshenko-appointed-names-reformist-cabinet/225353.html |title=Tymoshenko Appointed, Names Reformist Cabinet |date=7 February 2005 |publisher=The Moscow Times |access-date=28 December 2013 |archive-date=4 October 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131004225424/http://www.themoscowtimes.com/news/article/tymoshenko-appointed-names-reformist-cabinet/225353.html |url-status=live }}[https://www.europarl.europa.eu/meetdocs/2004_2009/documents/fd/ua_20050223_04_/ua_20050223_04_en.pdf President Yushchenko Appoints the New Prime Minister and Members of Her Cabinet] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924052547/http://www.europarl.europa.eu/meetdocs/2004_2009/documents/fd/ua_20050223_04_/ua_20050223_04_en.pdf |date=24 September 2015 }}, [[European Parliament]] (4 February 2005) The ministers who were working with her took her side in the later confrontation with Viktor Yushchenko.{{cite news|url=https://www.pravda.com.ua/rus/news/2005/09/28/4392424/|title=CEC: Turchynov thinks Yushchenko removed Tymoshenko to use the administrative resource|work=Ukrainian Pravda|date=28 September 2005|access-date=28 September 2005|archive-date=14 December 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181214122926/https://www.pravda.com.ua/rus/news/2005/09/28/4392424/|url-status=live}} On 28 July 2005, ''[[Forbes]]'' named Tymoshenko the third most powerful woman in the world, behind only [[Condoleezza Rice]] and [[Wu Yi (politician)|Wu Yi]].{{cite news|first1=Elizabeth|last1=MacDonald|first2=Chana R.|last2=Schoenberger|title=The 100 Most Powerful Women|url=https://www.forbes.com/2005/07/27/powerful-women-world-cz_05powom_land.html|work=[[Forbes]]|date=28 July 2005|access-date=5 October 2007|archive-date=8 March 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190308214022/https://www.forbes.com/2005/07/27/powerful-women-world-cz_05powom_land.html|url-status=live}} However, in the magazine's list published on 1 September 2006, Tymoshenko's name was not among the top 100.{{cite news|title=The 100 Most Powerful Women|url=https://www.forbes.com/lists/2006/11/06women_The-100-Most-Powerful-Women_Rank.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061005001324/https://www.forbes.com/lists/2006/11/06women_The-100-Most-Powerful-Women_Rank.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=5 October 2006|work=Forbes|date=31 August 2006}} In September 2005, Tymoshenko received the "Person of the Year of Central and Eastern Europe" award according to the 15th International Economic Forum in Krinitsa Gurska.{{cite web|url=https://www.pravda.com.ua/news/2005/09/8/3013135/|title=TYMOSHENKO BECAME A MAN OF THE YEAR|publisher=Pravda.com.ua|access-date=8 September 2005|archive-date=30 October 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141030040838/http://www.pravda.com.ua/news/2005/09/8/3013135/|url-status=live}} Several months into her government, internal conflicts within the post‐Revolution coalition began to damage Tymoshenko's administration.[https://web.archive.org/web/20051118115613/https://www.kyivpost.com/opinion/editorial/23161/ Zinchenko's bombshell], ''Kyiv Post'' (7 September 2005)[https://web.archive.org/web/20120418221254/https://www.kyivpost.com/news/nation/detail/23653/ Oleksandr Zinchenko talks], ''Kyiv Post'' (21 December 2005)[https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/4226814.stm Orange Revolution in turmoil], BBC News (8 September 2005) On 24 August 2005, Viktor Yushchenko gave an Independence Day speech during which he called Tymoshenko's government "the best".{{cite news|url=https://www.pravda.com.ua/articles/2005/08/24/3012700//|title=CEC: The statement by Yushchenko on the Maidan on the occasion of Independence Day|work=Ukrainian Pravda|date=24 August 2005|access-date=24 August 2005|archive-date=28 December 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131228133702/http://www.pravda.com.ua/articles/2005/08/24/3012700/|url-status=live}} Yet on 8 September, after the resignation of several senior officials, including the Head of the [[National Security and Defense Council of Ukraine|Security and Defense Council]] [[Petro Poroshenko]][https://books.google.com/books?id=fvmnFExEBrUC&pg=PT206 Design for a New Europe] by John Gillingham, [[Cambridge University Press]], 2006, {{ISBN|978-0-521-68664-8}}, p. 207. and Deputy Prime Minister [[Mykola Tomenko]],[https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/4225566.stm Ukraine leader sacks government] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220311050450/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/4225566.stm |date=11 March 2022 }}, BBC News (8 September 2005) Tymoshenko's government was dismissed by Yushchenko[https://books.google.com/books?id=LzChTG9xYJcC&pg=PA184 State-Building: A Comparative Study of Ukraine, Lithuania, Belarus, and Russia] by State-Building: A Comparative Study of Ukraine, Lithuania, Belarus, and Russia, [[Central European University Press]], 2007, {{ISBN|978-963-7326-90-5}}, p. 184. during a live television address to the nation.[https://www.rferl.org/content/article/1066936.html Ukraine: A Post-Orange Revolution Political Timeline] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303214405/http://www.rferl.org/content/article/1066936.html |date=3 March 2016 }}, [[Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty]] (21 March 2006 ) Yushchenko went on to criticize her work as head of the Cabinet, suggesting it had led to an economic slowdown and political conflicts within the ruling coalition. He said that Tymoshenko was serving interests of some businesses, and the government decision to re-privatize the Nikopol Ferroalloy Plant (previously owned by Kuchma's son in law Viktor Pinchuk) "was the last drop" that made him dismiss the government.{{cite web|url=https://www.chicagotribune.com/2005/09/27/in-ukraine-old-whiff-of-scandal-in-new-regime/ |title=In Ukraine, old whiff of scandal in new regime |publisher=Articles.chicagotribune.com |date=27 September 2005 |access-date=28 December 2013}} On 13 September 2005, Yushchenko accused Tymoshenko of betrayal of "Orange Revolution" ideas. In his interview for the Associated Press, he said that during the time of her presidency at UESU, Tymoshenko accumulated an 8 million Hryvna debt, and that she had used her authority as prime minister to write off that debt. Tymoshenko has repeatedly stated that the mentioned amount was not a debt, but fines imposed by the Tax Inspection from 1997 to 1998, and that all the cases regarding UESU had been closed before she became prime minister.{{cite web|url=https://usatoday30.usatoday.com/news/world/2005-09-13-ukraine_x.htm|title=Ukraine president lashes out at ousted PM |publisher=Usatoday30.usatoday.com|date=13 September 2005 |access-date=28 December 2013}} Tymoshenko blamed Yushchenko's closest circle for scheming against her and undermining the activities of her Cabinet. She also criticised Yushchenko, telling the BBC that he had "practically ruined our unity, our future, the future of the country", without rooting out corruption as he pledged to do and that the president's action was absolutely illogical.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/4231414.stm |title=Ukrainian ex-PM slams dismissal|work=BBC News|date=9 September 2005|access-date=28 December 2013}} At the time, Tymoshenko saw a rapid growth of approval ratings, while Yushchenko's approval ratings went down.{{cite web|url=https://edition.cnn.com/2005/WORLD/europe/09/08/ukraine.yushchenko/|title=Yushchenko fires government – Sep 8, 2005 |publisher=CNN |date=8 September 2005 |access-date=28 December 2013}} This tendency was later proved by the results of parliamentary elections in 2006, when for the first time ever BYuT outran "Our Ukraine" party, winning 129 seats vs. 81, respectively. During the previous parliamentary elections of 2002, BYuT had only 22 members of parliament, while "Our Ukraine" had 112.{{cite news|url=https://www.ukrainereforms.info/?p=408|title=CEC: Parliamentary elections in March 2002|work=Ukraine Reforms|date=15 May 2013|access-date=31 March 2002|archive-date=5 November 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141105001546/http://www.ukrainereforms.info/?p=408|url-status=live}} The work of Tymoshenko as prime minister in 2005 was complicated due to internal conflicts in the "orange" team. According to Tymoshenko, Yushchenko and Poroshenko were trying to turn the [[National Security and Defense Council]] into the "second Cabinet of Ministers".{{cite web|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/content/article/2005/09/09/AR2005090901916.html|title=Ukraine's Ex-Premier Splits With Her Partner|work=Washingtonpost.com|date=10 September 2005|access-date=28 December 2013|archive-date=12 August 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180812023012/http://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/content/article/2005/09/09/AR2005090901916.html|url-status=live}} Tymoshenko was succeeded as prime minister by [[Yuriy Yehanurov]]. ===Opposition and 2006 parliamentary election=== [[File:Flickr - europeanpeoplesparty - EPP Summit 19 March 2009 (91) (cropped).jpg|thumb|Yulia Tymoshenko with Viktor Yushchenko in 2009]] Soon after Tymoshenko's discharge in September 2005, the General Prosecutor Office of the Russian Federation dismissed all charges against her. On 18 November 2005, the Supreme Court of Ukraine issued a ruling which invalidated all criminal cases against Tymoshenko and her family.{{cite news|url=https://censor.net.ua/photo_news/186197/verhovnyyi_sud_postavil_tochku_v_dele_timoshenko_esche_v_2005_godu_dokument|title=CEC: The Supreme court put the point in the case of Tymoshenko in 2005. DOCUMENT|work=Censor Net|date=27 October 2011|access-date=27 October 2011|archive-date=2 April 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190402231041/https://censor.net.ua/photo_news/186197/verhovnyyi_sud_postavil_tochku_v_dele_timoshenko_esche_v_2005_godu_dokument|url-status=live}} After her dismissal, Tymoshenko started to tour the country in a bid to win the [[2006 Ukrainian parliamentary election]] as the leader of her Bloc.{{#tag:ref|On 7 December 2005, Bloc of Yulia Tymoshenko held a convention in Kyiv. At this convention Yulia Tymoshenko presented a new ideology of the Bloc ("solidarism") and announced the first top 10 of the party list for the upcoming parliamentary election of 2006. Unlike the other parties that ran for election, BYuT did not disclose the rest of the names on the list. On 26 March 2006, BYuT won 22.27% of votes (5,648,345 votes) and came first in 14 regions of Ukraine.|group=nb}} Tymoshenko soon announced that she wanted to return to the post of prime minister.[https://books.google.com/books?id=LHn9kJgVWF4C&pg=PA75 Europe's Influence on Ukrainian State Building – A Struggle for Identity between Europe and Russia (Master Thesis)] by Alexandra Weber, [[University of Hamburg]], 2006, {{ISBN|978-3-638-61729-1}}, p. 75. She managed to form a strong team that started a political fight on two fronts – with Yanukovych's and Yushchenko's camps.{{cite news|url=https://www.pravda.com.ua/articles/2007/12/21/3342047/?attempt=1|title=CEC: The orbit of the government of Yulia Tymoshenko|work=Ukrainian Pravda|date=21 December 2007|access-date=21 December 2007|archive-date=9 January 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160109180125/http://www.pravda.com.ua/articles/2007/12/21/3342047/?attempt=1|url-status=live}} With the Bloc coming second in the election, and winning 129 seats, many speculated that she might form a coalition with Yushchenko's "Our Ukraine" party and the Socialist Party of Ukraine (SPU) to prevent the [[Party of Regions]] from gaining power.
