Tymoshenko again reiterated her stance in regard to becoming prime minister.[https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/4848958.stm Ukraine leaders jostle for power], BBC News (27 March 2006) However, negotiations with "Our Ukraine" and SPU faced many difficulties as the various blocs fought over posts and engaged in counter-negotiations with other groups.[https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/country_profiles/1107869.stm Timeline: Ukraine] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308071459/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/country_profiles/1107869.stm |date=8 March 2022 }}, BBC News (last updated 26 October 2009) On 21 June 2006, the Ukrainian media reported that the parties had finally reached a coalition agreement, which appeared to have ended nearly three months of political uncertainty.[https://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/world/europe/5101380.stm Ukraine allies 'agree coalition'] , BBC News (21 June 2006)[https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/5105192.stm Ukraine coalition deal confirmed] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220301144455/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/5105192.stm |date=1 March 2022 }}, BBC News (22 June 2006) Tymoshenko's nomination and confirmation as the new prime minister was expected to be straightforward. However, the political intrigue that took place broke the plan. BYuT partners "Our Ukraine" and Socialist Party of Ukraine (SPU) could not come to agreement regarding distribution of powers, thus creation of the Coalition of Democratic Forces was put on hold. Yushchenko and oligarchs from his narrow circle were trying to impede Tymoshenko from returning to the office of prime minister. Her nomination was preconditioned on the election of her long-time rival Poroshenko from "Our Ukraine" to the position of speaker of the parliament. Moroz, the chairman of the Socialist Party of Ukraine, also expressed his interest in becoming speaker. Tymoshenko stated that she would vote for any speaker from the coalition. Within a few days of the signing of the coalition agreement, it became clear that the coalition members mistrusted each other,{{Cite web |title="Чинник Мороза" |url=https://day.kyiv.ua/article/panorama-dnya/chynnyk-moroza |access-date=2023-07-08 |website=day.kyiv.ua |language=uk |archive-date=1 December 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241201013432/https://day.kyiv.ua/article/panorama-dnya/chynnyk-moroza |url-status=live }} since they considered it a deviation from parliamentary procedures to hold a simultaneous vote on Poroshenko as the speaker and Tymoshenko as prime minister.[https://www.europeanforum.net/news/166/oleksandr_moroz_announced_a_new_coalition_for_ukraine Oleksandr Moroz announced a new coalition for Ukraine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110725235037/https://www.europeanforum.net/news/166/oleksandr_moroz_announced_a_new_coalition_for_ukraine|date=25 July 2011}}, [[European Forum for Democracy and Solidarity]] (11 July 2006)https://www.jamestown.org/single/?no_cache=1&tx_ttnews[tt_news]=31839 {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303211451/http://www.jamestown.org/single/?no_cache=1&tx_ttnews%5Btt_news%5D%3D31839 |date=3 March 2016 }}, [[The Jamestown Foundation]] (4 July 2006) The Party of Regions announced an ultimatum to the coalition demanding that parliamentary procedures be observed, asking that membership in parliamentary committees be allocated in proportion to seats held by each fraction, and demanding chairmanship in certain Parliamentary committees as well as Governorships in the [[Subdivisions of Ukraine|administrative subdivisions]] won by the Party of Regions.[https://www.kyivpost.com/news/business/bus_general/detail/24728/ Ukrainian coalition, opposition to hold round table aimed to solve political crisis], ''Kyiv Post'' (3 July 2006) {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110605051803/https://www.kyivpost.com/news/business/bus_general/detail/24728/ |date=5 June 2011}} The Party of Regions complained that the coalition agreement deprived the Party of Regions and the communists of any representation in the executive and leadership in parliamentary committees, while in the local regional councils won by the Party of Regions the coalition parties were locked out of all committees as well.[https://www.rferl.org/content/article/1069496.html Opposition Blocks Ukrainian Parliament Session], [[Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty]] (27 June 2006 ) Members from the Party of Regions blocked the parliament from 29 June{{cite news|title=Sit-in disrupts Ukraine assembly|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/5127414.stm|work=BBC News|date=29 June 2006|access-date=26 August 2006|archive-date=7 July 2006|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060707194416/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/5127414.stm|url-status=live}} through 6 July.[https://www.ecoi.net/file_upload/1329_1200307242_ukraine-bulletin-100806.pdf ELECTION OF NEW PRIME MINISTER AND GOVERNMENT] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304024610/http://www.ecoi.net/file_upload/1329_1200307242_ukraine-bulletin-100806.pdf|date=4 March 2016}}, [[European Country of Origin Information Network]] (August 2006) After lengthy negotiations, SPU suddenly pulled out of the Coalition and joined the alliance with the Party of Regions and the Communist Party of Ukraine. Moroz assured that the team of Yushchenko was conducting secret negotiations with the Party of Regions. According to that deal, Yanukovych was supposed to become the speaker, while Yuriy Yekhanurov kept the prime minister portfolio. These negotiations were conducted by Yekhanurov himself upon Yushchenko's request. Later, Yekhanurov admitted this fact in his interview with the "Ukrainska Pravda" website.{{cite news|url=https://www.unian.ua/politics/7829-yanukovich-bude-ne-premerom-a-spikerom.html|title=CEC: Yanukovych would not be Prime Minister, and speaker|work=information Agency Unian|date=15 June 2006|access-date=15 June 2006|archive-date=14 December 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181214125405/https://www.unian.ua/politics/7829-yanukovich-bude-ne-premerom-a-spikerom.html|url-status=live}} Following the surprise nomination of Moroz as the Rada speaker and his subsequent election late on 6 July with the support of the Party of Regions, the "Orange coalition" collapsed. (Poroshenko had withdrawn his candidacy and had urged Moroz to do the same on 7 July).[https://www.kyivpost.com/news/business/bus_general/detail/24772/ Lawmakers loyal to Yushchenko call for new elections in Ukraine after pro-Russian parties announce coalition], ''Kyiv Post'' (11 July 2006) {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110605051823/https://www.kyivpost.com/news/business/bus_general/detail/24772/ |date=5 June 2011}}[https://www.kyivpost.com/news/world/detail/24763/ Ukraine's Orange coalition falls apart after Socialists break ranks], ''Kyiv Post'' (7 July 2006) {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110605051843/https://www.kyivpost.com/news/world/detail/24763/ |date=5 June 2011}} After the creation of a large coalition of majority composed of the Party of Regions, the Socialist Party of Ukraine and the Communist Party of Ukraine, Yanukovych became prime minister, and the other two parties were left in the wilderness.[https://www.kyivpost.com/news/nation/detail/24790/ Ukraine's political fate shifts amid Rada chaos], ''Kyiv Post'' (13 July 2006) {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110605051901/https://www.kyivpost.com/news/nation/detail/24790/ |date=5 June 2011}}[https://www.rferl.org/content/article/1069700.html Ukraine's Leftists Announce New Coalition] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303170320/http://www.rferl.org/content/article/1069700.html |date=3 March 2016 }}, [[Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty]] (7 July 2006 ) On 3 August 2006, Tymoshenko refused to sign the "Universal of National Unity" declaration initiated by Yushchenko.{{in lang|uk}} [https://www.radiosvoboda.org/a/949233.html У Секретаріаті Президента України підписали Універсал національної єдності ''The Universal of National Unity was signed in the Secretariat of the President of Ukraine''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211031205536/https://www.radiosvoboda.org/a/949233.html|date=31 October 2021}}, [[Radiosvoboda.org]] (03.09.2006) The document, signed by Yushchenko, Yanukovych and leaders of Socialist and Communist parties, sealed Yanukovych's appointment as prime minister. Tymoshenko called it "the act of betrayal". In September 2006, Tymoshenko announced that her political force would be in opposition to the new government.[https://archive.tymoshenko.com.ua/eng/exclusive/speeches-appeals/3352/ "We will be either in the opposition or we will fight in a new election"]{{dead link|date=November 2016 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes}}, Yulia Tymoshenko official website (7 July 2006) Our Ukraine stalled until 4 October 2006, when it too joined the opposition.{{cite web|url=https://www.rferl.org/content/article/1071859.html|title=Ukraine: Two Viktors, But No Clear Winner|access-date=6 October 2006|publisher=[[Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty]]|archive-date=11 December 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081211154303/http://www.rferl.org/content/Article/1071859.html|url-status=live}} On 12 January 2007, a BYuT vote in the parliament overrode the president's veto of the "On the Cabinet of Ministers" law that was advantageous for the president. (In exchange, BYuT voted for the "On Imperative Mandate" and "On Opposition" laws). This vote was one of many steps undertaken by BYuT to ruin a fragile alliance between president Yushchenko and prime minister Yanukovych.{{#tag:ref|See more at [[2007 Ukrainian political crisis]].|group=nb}}{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/6512941.stm|title=Ukrainian rivals rally in capital|date=1 April 2007|work=BBC News|archive-date=10 March 2022|access-date=1 March 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220310174240/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/6512941.stm|url-status=live}} In March 2007, Tymoshenko traveled to the United States, where she held high-level meetings with Vice President [[Dick Cheney]], Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice and [[Stephen Hadley]], the National Security Advisor under President George W. Bush.{{#tag:ref| The key purpose of her visit was to explain to the US administration "the biggest problem of Ukrainian politics": Yanukovych's actions toward unconstitutional enlargement of the "ruling coalition" that could potentially remove Viktor Yushchenko from power. The only possible solution envisaged by Tymoshenko was dismissal of the Verkhovna Rada (according to the Constitution) and an early parliamentary election. In addition to meeting with top officials of the Bush administration, Tymoshenko gave speeches at the Kennedy Center for Strategic and International Studies and the National Press Club. She also received an award for her contribution to the development of democracy in Ukraine from the influential NGO Conservative Political Action Conference. |group=nb}} On 31 March 2007, Tymoshenko initiated a "100 thousand people Maidan" aimed to urge the president to call an early parliamentary election.{{#tag:ref|On 31 March 2007, Tymoshenko and her political party initiated and conducted the "100 thousand people Maidan". Other Orange Revolution leaders such as Viacheslav Kyrylenko and Yuriy Lutsenko attended the meeting. The participants urged Yushchenko (who was not there) to dismiss the Verkhovna Rada and call early election.{{in lang|uk}} [https://gazeta.ua/articles/politics-newspaper/_na-8-bereznya-timoshenko-chekae-vid-yuschenka-rishennya-pro-rozpusk-parlamentu/152680 На 8 Березня Тимошенко чекає від Ющенка рішення про розпуск парламенту ''On 8 March Tymoshenko expects Yushchenko to dissolve parliament''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029203110/http://gazeta.ua/articles/politics-newspaper/_na-8-bereznya-timoshenko-chekae-vid-yuschenka-rishennya-pro-rozpusk-parlamentu/152680 |date=29 October 2013 }}, [[Gazeta.ua]] (06.03.2007) During this trip she received an award for contribution into democracy development from [[Conservative Political Action Conference]].[https://www.ukrweekly.com/archive/pdf3/2007/The_Ukrainian_Weekly_2007-10.pdf Tymoshenko tells U.S. that Ukraine is in "deep constitutional crisis"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308173440/https://www.ukrweekly.com/archive/pdf3/2007/The_Ukrainian_Weekly_2007-10.pdf |date=8 March 2022}}, [[The Ukrainian Weekly]] (11 March 2007)|group=nb}} On 4 April 2007, Yushchenko issued an edict "On early termination of duties of the Verkhovna Rada" as a reaction to violation of the Constitution by the Party of Regions, which had started dragging individual deputies into the "ruling coalition" (this being illegal, as coalitions should be formed by factions and not by individual deputies). In doing so, the Party of Regions was trying to achieve a constitutional majority of 300 votes which would enable Yanukovych to override the president's veto and control the legislative process. Party of Regions didn't obey this edict. In order to dismiss the Verkhovna Rada, Tymoshenko and her supporters in the parliament (168 deputies from BYuT and "Our Ukraine" factions) quit their parliamentary factions on 2 June 2007. That step invalidated the convocation of the Verkhovna Rada and cleared the path to an early election. An early parliamentary election was held on 30 September 2007. ===2007 parliamentary election=== [[File:Vladimir Putin and Yulia Tymoshenko-1.jpg|thumb|Yulia Tymoshenko and [[Vladimir Putin]] meeting on 19 March 2005. In November 2009, Putin praised Tymoshenko's political choices and stated that he found it comfortable to work with her.]] Following balloting in the [[2007 Ukrainian parliamentary election|2007 parliamentary elections]] held on 30 September 2007, Orange Revolution parties had won majority of 229 votes of BYUT fraction (30,71% of the votes (156 seats) and the Our Ukraine/People's Self-defence faction.{{cite web|url=https://www.cvk.gov.ua/info/protokol_cvk_2007.pdf|script-title=uk:Позачергові вибори народних депутатів України – 30 вересня 2007 року – Протокол Центральної Виборчої Комісії: Про результати виборів народних депутатів України|trans-title=Extraordinary election of national deputies of Ukraine – 30 September 2007 – Protocol of the Central Election Commission: On the results of elections of people deputies of Ukraine|language=uk|work=cvk.gov.ua|date=30 September 2007|access-date=11 November 2015|archive-date=23 September 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150923212105/http://www.cvk.gov.ua/info/protokol_cvk_2007.pdf|url-status=live}} On 3 October 2007, an almost final tally gave the alliance of Tymoshenko and Yushchenko a slim lead over the rival party of Prime Minister Yanukovych, thanks in part to a vigorous BYuT campaign in the industrial east, a Party of Regions stronghold.{{Citation | first = Karl | last = Rahder | title = Tymoshenko on Top | newspaper = ISN Security Watch | date = 8 October 2007 | url = https://www.isn.ethz.ch/Digital-Library/Articles/Detail/?lng=en&id=53812 | access-date = 1 March 2022 | archive-date = 3 March 2016 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303173750/http://www.isn.ethz.ch/Digital-Library/Articles/Detail/?lng=en&id=53812 | url-status = live }} Although Yanukovych, whose party won the single biggest share of the vote, also claimed victory,{{cite news|title=Orange bloc edges to poll victory|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/7025382.stm|work=BBC News|date=3 October 2007|access-date=26 August 2006|archive-date=7 January 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090107025816/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/7025382.stm|url-status=live}} one of his coalition allies, the Socialist Party of Ukraine, failed to gain enough votes to retain seats in Parliament.{{cite web|url=https://www.newsru.ua/arch/ukraine/01oct2007/vuborru.html|title=The Yulia Tymoshenko Bloc and Our Ukraine – People's Self-Defense get the majority. Socialist Party of Ukraine does not pass|publisher=Newsru.ua|date=1 October 2007|access-date=2 October 2007|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151118114339/https://www.newsru.ua/arch/ukraine/01oct2007/vuborru.html|archive-date=18 November 2015}} On 15 October 2007, the Our Ukraine–People's Self-Defense Bloc and the Yulia Tymoshenko Bloc agreed to form a majority coalition in the new parliament of the 6th [[convocation]].{{cite news|title=Ukrainian Parliament Continues Shift Towards Yushchenko|url=https://korrespondent.net/ukraine/politics/212097-svershilos-byut-i-nu-ns-parafirovali-soglashenie-o-sozdanii-koalicii|work=[[Korrespondent]]|date=15 October 2007|access-date=15 October 2007|language=ru|archive-date=17 April 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190417002413/https://korrespondent.net/ukraine/politics/212097-svershilos-byut-i-nu-ns-parafirovali-soglashenie-o-sozdanii-koalicii|url-status=live}} On 29 November, a coalition was signed between the Yulia Tymoshenko Bloc and Our Ukraine–People's Self-Defense Bloc, which was associated with President Yushchenko. Both parties are affiliated with the Orange Revolution. On 11 December 2007, the Coalition failed in its attempt to appoint Tymoshenko prime minister, falling one vote short (225 members of parliament supported her nomination). On 12 December 2007, the media reported on the possible attempted assassination of Tymoshenko. BYuT and Tymoshenko herself said it was an intimidation. On 18 December, Tymoshenko was once again elected as prime minister (supported by 226 deputies, the minimal number needed for passage), heading the [[second Tymoshenko Government]].{{cite news|title= Parliament named Tymoshenko as the Prime Minister of Ukraine|url= https://korrespondent.net/ukraine/politics/319536-parlament-naznachil-timoshenko-premer-ministrom-ukrainy|work= [[Korrespondent]]|date= 18 December 2007|access-date= 18 December 2007|language= ru|archive-date= 17 April 2019|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20190417002435/https://korrespondent.net/ukraine/politics/319536-parlament-naznachil-timoshenko-premer-ministrom-ukrainy|url-status= live}} ===Second term as prime minister 2007–2010, and 2008 political crisis=== {{main|Second Tymoshenko Government}} On 11 July 2008, the Party of Regions tried to vote no-confidence to Tymoshenko's government in the parliament, but could not collect enough votes.[https://www.express.co.uk/news/world/52072/Premier-survives-no-confidence-vote Premier survives no confidence vote] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303192212/http://www.express.co.uk/news/world/52072/Premier-survives-no-confidence-vote |date=3 March 2016 }}, ''[[Daily Express]]'' (11 July 2008) The coalition of Tymoshenko's Bloc (BYuT) and Yushchenko's Our Ukraine–People's Self-Defense Bloc (OU-PSD) was put at risk due to deliberate misinterpretation of Tymoshenko's opinion on the ongoing [[2008 South Ossetia War]] between [[Georgia (country)|Georgia]] and Russia. Yushchenko's office accused her of taking a softer position in order to gain support from Russia in the upcoming 2010 election. Andriy Kyslynskyi, the president's deputy chief of staff, went as far as to accuse her of 'high treason'.{{cite news|url=https://www.nysun.com/foreign/ukraine-pm-accused-of-high-treason/84135/|title=Ukraine PM Accused of 'High Treason'|newspaper=The New York Sun|date=19 August 2008|access-date=19 February 2011|archive-date=23 July 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180723182414/https://www.nysun.com/foreign/ukraine-pm-accused-of-high-treason/84135/|url-status=dead}} According to Ukrainska Pravda and [[Newswire.ca]] Tymoshenko expressed her solidarity with Georgia on 13 and 14 August, and later preferred to stay neutral on the issue as according to Constitution President Yuschenko headed foreign policy issues.{{cite news|url=https://www.pravda.com.ua/news/2008/08/14/3522613/|title=Tymoshenko keeps silent on Georgia. Nemyria takes the blow|publisher=Ukrainska Pravda|date=14 August 2008|access-date=14 August 2008|archive-date=4 November 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181104211044/https://www.pravda.com.ua/news/2008/08/14/3522613/|url-status=live}}{{cite news|url=https://www.pravda.com.ua/news/2008/08/14/3524289/|title=Tymoshenko's spokesperson has refuted her Georgian support|publisher=Ukrainska Pravda|date=14 August 2008|access-date=14 August 2008|archive-date=4 November 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181104170044/https://www.pravda.com.ua/news/2008/08/14/3524289/|url-status=live}} {{Quote box|width=40%|align=right|quote= '''Tymoshenko on Russo–Georgian war:''' "We stand in solidarity with the democratically-elected leadership of Georgia. Georgia's sovereignty and territorial integrity must be respected|source= Yulia Tymoshenko's press briefing on 13 August 2008{{cite web|url=https://www.newswire.ca/fr/story/383011/tymoshenko-ukraine-stands-in-solidarity-with-georgia |title=Tymoshenko: Ukraine Stands in Solidarity with Georgia |publisher=Newswire.ca |date=13 August 2008 |access-date=28 December 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029201938/https://www.newswire.ca/fr/story/383011/tymoshenko-ukraine-stands-in-solidarity-with-georgia |archive-date=29 October 2013}}}} According to [[BYuT]], Viktor Baloha (Chief of Staff of the Presidential Secretariat) criticized the premier at every turn, doing the dirty job for the President and accusing her of everything from not being religious enough to damaging the economy and plotting to kill him, and the accusation of 'betrayal' over Georgia was simply one of the latest and most pernicious attacks directed at the premier.{{cite news|url=https://www.intv-inter.net/downloads/pdf/newsletter/BYuT_Inform_Newsletter_Issue_84.pdf |title=Newsletter for the international community providing views and analysis from the Bloc of Yulia Tymoshenko (BYuT) |publisher=[[Bloc Yulia Tymoshenko]] |date=8 September 2008 |access-date=8 September 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081029054137/https://www.intv-inter.net/downloads/pdf/newsletter/BYuT_Inform_Newsletter_Issue_84.pdf |archive-date=29 October 2008}}{{cite news|url=https://www.unian.info/politics/133612-baloha-tymoshenko-not-orthodox-she-practices-another-faith.html|title=Baloha: Tymoshenko not Orthodox, she practices another faith|publisher=[[UNIAN]]|date=29 July 2008|access-date=29 July 2008|archive-date=24 July 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180724032133/https://www.unian.info/politics/133612-baloha-tymoshenko-not-orthodox-she-practices-another-faith.html|url-status=live}}{{cite news|url=https://www.unian.info/politics/130584-baloha-tymoshenko-promised-putin-not-to-support-ukraines-joining-nato-map.html|title=Baloha: Tymoshenko promised Putin not to support Ukraine's joining NATO MAP|publisher=[[UNIAN]]|date=16 July 2008|access-date=16 July 2008|archive-date=23 July 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180723212359/https://www.unian.info/politics/130584-baloha-tymoshenko-promised-putin-not-to-support-ukraines-joining-nato-map.html|url-status=live}}{{cite news|url=https://www.unian.info/politics/109143-baloha-accuses-tymoshenko-of-deliberate-destroying-coalition.html|title=Baloha accuses Tymoshenko of deliberate destroying coalition UNIAN|date=10 April 2008|access-date=10 April 2008|archive-date=24 July 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180724032125/https://www.unian.info/politics/109143-baloha-accuses-tymoshenko-of-deliberate-destroying-coalition.html|url-status=live}}{{cite news|url=https://www.unian.info/society/110138-baloha-calls-on-to-sack-ministers-from-governments-economic-bloc.html|title=Baloha calls on to sack Ministers from government's economic bloc|publisher=[[UNIAN]]|date=15 April 2008|access-date=15 April 2008|archive-date=23 July 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180723212411/https://www.unian.info/society/110138-baloha-calls-on-to-sack-ministers-from-governments-economic-bloc.html|url-status=live}} [[File:Flickr - europeanpeoplesparty - EPP Summit Lisbone 18 October 2007 (5).jpg|thumb|Tymoshenko with President of the European Commission [[José Manuel Barroso]] in the [[European People's Party|EPP]] Summit in Lisbon, 18 October 2007]] [[File:GeorgeBush-Juliia Tymoshenko (2008)-Ukraine.JPG|thumb|U.S. President [[George W. Bush]] and Tymoshenko, Kyiv, 1 April 2008]] [[File:Julia Tymoshenko and Lech Kaczyński(2008).jpg|thumb|Tymoshenko and President of Poland [[Lech Kaczyński]], 14 February 2008]] After Tymoshenko's BYuT voted alongside the Communist Party of Ukraine and the Party of Regions to pass legislation that would facilitate the procedure of impeachment for future presidents{{cite news|url=https://www.unian.info/politics/141722-tymoshenko-calms-down-baloha-saying-his-chief-must-not-be-concerned.html|title=Tymoshenko 'calms down' Baloha saying his chief must not be concerned|publisher=[[Ukrainian Independent Information Agency]]|date=1 September 2008|access-date=10 September 2008|archive-date=23 July 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180723212408/https://www.unian.info/politics/141722-tymoshenko-calms-down-baloha-saying-his-chief-must-not-be-concerned.html|url-status=live}} and limit the president's power while increasing the prime minister's powers, President Yushchenko's [[OU-PSD]] bloc pulled out of the coalition and Yushchenko promised to veto the legislation{{cite magazine|url=https://content.time.com/time/world/article/0,8599,1838848,00.html|title=Why Ukraine's Pro-Western Coalition Split|magazine=Time|date=4 September 2008|access-date=10 September 2008|archive-date=8 December 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131208030413/http://content.time.com/time/world/article/0,8599,1838848,00.html|url-status=live}}{{cite news|url=https://www.bloomberg.com/apps/news?pid=20601085&sid=avVAAysbcd8I&refer=europe|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090626150255/https://www.bloomberg.com/apps/news?pid=20601085&sid=avVAAysbcd8I&refer=europe|url-status=dead|archive-date=26 June 2009|title=Yushchenko May Dissolve Ukraine Parliament, Call Vote|publisher=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=3 September 2008|access-date=10 September 2008}} and threatened to hold an election if a new coalition was not formed soon. This resulted in the [[2008 Ukrainian political crisis]], which culminated in Yushchenko calling an [[Next Ukrainian parliamentary election|early parliamentary election]] on 8 October 2008.{{cite web |title=Ukraine gets third election in three years |publisher=[[Radio Netherlands]] |date=8 October 2008 |url=https://www.radionetherlands.nl/news/international/5999526/Ukraine-gets-third-election-in-three-years |access-date=8 October 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090308130952/https://www.radionetherlands.nl/news/international/5999526/Ukraine-gets-third-election-in-three-years |archive-date=8 March 2009 }}{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/7660058.stm|title=Snap election called in Ukraine|work=BBC News|date=8 October 2008|access-date=8 October 2008|archive-date=11 October 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081011022502/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/7660058.stm|url-status=live}} [[File:Yulia Tymoshenko November 2009-2.jpeg|thumb|Tymoshenko in November 2009]] Tymoshenko was fiercely opposed to the [[snap election]], stating that if Yushchenko and Yanukovych did enact a snap election, they will bear responsibility for the effects of the [[2008 financial crisis]].{{cite web |title=Tymoshenko to propose creating "megacoalition" during NSDC meeting |publisher=Personal web site of Yulia Tymoshenko |date=20 October 2008 |url=https://www.tymoshenko.com.ua/eng/news/first/6440/ |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081231020848/https://www.tymoshenko.com.ua/eng/news/first/6440/|archive-date=31 December 2008 }} Initially, the election was to be held on 7 December 2008,[https://www.reuters.com/article/us-ukraine-coalition-idUSTRE4979YT20081008 Ukraine calls early election] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160306111137/http://www.reuters.com/article/us-ukraine-coalition-idUSTRE4979YT20081008 |date=6 March 2016 }}, Reuters, 8 October 2008.{{cite news|title=Ukraine president calls election for December|newspaper=The Irish Times|date=9 October 2008|url=https://www.irishtimes.com/newspaper/breaking/2008/1009/breaking12.htm|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090717073801/http://www.irishtimes.com/newspaper/breaking/2008/1009/breaking12.htm|url-status=dead|archive-date=17 July 2009|access-date=9 October 2008}} but was later postponed to an unknown date.{{cite news|url=https://www.unian.net/eng/news/news-280065.html|title=Ukraine election date is uncertain, says president|publisher=[[UNIAN]]|date=22 October 2008}}{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/7680916.stm|title=Finance crises delay Ukraine vote|work=BBC News|date=20 October 2008|archive-date=1 April 2022|access-date=1 March 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220401035518/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/7680916.stm|url-status=live}}{{cite news|url=https://www.unian.net/eng/news/news-284007.html|title=Ukraine's president says no election this year|publisher=[[UNIAN]]|date=12 November 2008|archive-date=22 February 2012|access-date=1 March 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120222085608/http://www.unian.net/eng/news/news-284007.html|url-status=live}} Tymoshenko had no intention of resigning{{cite news|title=Tymoshenko Not Intending To Resign |agency=[[Ukrainian News Agency]] |date=10 October 2008 |url=https://www.ukranews.com/eng/article/154884.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090122112700/https://www.ukranews.com/eng/article/154884.html |archive-date=22 January 2009}} until a new coalition was formed.{{cite web|title=Tymoshenko won't resign until a new coalition is formed|publisher=[[UNIAN]]|date=10 October 2008|url=https://www.unian.net/eng/news/news-277806.html|access-date=1 March 2022|archive-date=13 August 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110813162007/http://www.unian.net/eng/news/news-277806.html|url-status=live}} In early December 2008, there were negotiations between BYuT and the Party of Regions to form a coalition,[https://www.ukranews.com/eng/article/166617.html Experts Admit Party Of Regions-Tymoshenko Bloc Coalition If Pliusch Nominated For Speaker's Position], Ukrainian News Agency (3 December 2008) {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090108152605/https://www.ukranews.com/eng/article/166617.html |date=8 January 2009}} but after [[Volodymyr Lytvyn]] was elected [[Chairman of the Verkhovna Rada]] ([[Verkhovna Rada|parliament of Ukraine]]) on 9 December 2008, he announced the creation of a coalition between his [[Lytvyn Bloc]], BYuT and OU-PSD.[https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/7774328.stm Ukraine coalition set to reform] , BBC News (9 December 2008) After negotiations,[https://www.unian.net/eng/news/news-289455.html New parliamentary majority receives name] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120222085620/http://www.unian.net/eng/news/news-289455.html |date=22 February 2012 }}, [[UNIAN]] (11 December 2008)[https://www.ukranews.com/eng/article/168815.html Lavrynovych Speaking About Majority Between BYuT, OU PSD, Lytvyn Bloc And Communist Party Faction At Rada] {{webarchive|url=https://archive.today/20121208222050/http://www.ukranews.com/eng/article/168815.html |date=8 December 2012}}, Ukrainian News Agency (13 December 2008) "Lytvyn announced about creating a coalition between BYuT, the "Our Ukraine" – People's Self-Defense Bloc faction and the Lytvyn Bloc. However, the coalition agreement has not been signed so far." the three parties officially signed the coalition agreement on 16 December.{{cite news|url=https://www.ukranews.com/eng/article/169237.html|title=Tymoshenko Bloc, OU-PSD, And Lytvyn Bloc Sign Rada Coalition Agreement|work=Ukrainian News Agency|date=16 December 2008|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090122235938/https://www.ukranews.com/eng/article/169237.html|archive-date=22 January 2009}} It was not known whether this coalition would stop the snap election,[https://www.unian.net/eng/news/news-289482.html President calls on VR to focus on overcoming economic crisis], [[UNIAN]] (11 December 2008)[https://www.unian.net/eng/news/news-290123.html Yushchenko categorically opposed to "coalition of three" – Hrytsenko] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120222085648/http://www.unian.net/eng/news/news-290123.html |date=22 February 2012 }}, [[UNIAN]] (15 December 2008)[https://www.interfax.com.ua/eng/main/2979/ Presidential Secretariat urges parliament to include early election funds in 2009 budget] {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081230212126/https://www.interfax.com.ua/eng/main/2979/ |date=30 December 2008}}, [[Interfax|Interfax-Ukraine]] (15 December 2008) although Speaker Volodymyr Lytvyn predicted the Verkhovna Rada would work until 2012.{{cite news|url=https://www.ukranews.com/eng/article/168813.html|title=Lytvyn Predicts Rada's Work Until 2012|work=Ukrainian News Agency|date=13 December 2008|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090123000019/https://www.ukranews.com/eng/article/168813.html|archive-date=23 January 2009|quote='I can reassure everyone that snap elections will not be held... If the Rada is working adequately and the public sees its efficiency, the Parliament will work next four-year', he said.}} On 5 February 2009, Tymoshenko's opponents in the parliament were trying to dismiss her government again, but again the vote failed.{{cite web |title=Ukraine Parliament fails to pass non-confidence bill against cabinet |url=https://en.ce.cn/World/Europe/200902/06/t20090206_18128793.shtml |access-date=28 December 2013 |website=[[Economic Daily]] |publisher= |archive-date=16 December 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131216002300/http://en.ce.cn/World/Europe/200902/06/t20090206_18128793.shtml |url-status=dead}}{{Better source needed|reason=The current source is insufficiently reliable ([[WP:NOTRS]]).|date=December 2022}} The following day, president Yushchenko strongly criticized Tymoshenko and the economic policies of her government. Tymoshenko accused him of spreading "a mix of untruths, panic and hysteria."{{cite web |last=Cullison |first=Alan |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/SB123387574604054371 |title=Rivalry Deepens Ukraine Economic Woe |publisher=Online.wsj.com |date=6 February 2009 |access-date=28 December 2013 |archive-date=4 October 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131004231946/http://online.wsj.com/article/SB123387574604054371.html |url-status=live }} On 18 December 2008, for the first time Tymoshenko accused the National Bank of Ukraine in the conscious manipulation of the hryvnia, and President Yushchenko of colluding with the leadership of the NBU, which led to depreciation of the national currency to the level of ₴8 per US dollar.{{cite web|url=https://www.pravda.com.ua/articles/2008/12/18/3642740/|title=The scandalous Tymoshenko briefing: President covers and earns on currency speculation|publisher=Pravda.com.ua|date=18 December 2008|access-date=18 December 2008|archive-date=14 December 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181214133543/https://www.pravda.com.ua/articles/2008/12/18/3642740/|url-status=live}} A large part of Tymoshenko's second term as prime minister coincided in time with the [[2008 financial crisis]], which required her government to respond to numerous challenges that could have led the country's economic collapse.{{#tag:ref|Tymoshenko herself outlines the biggest achievements of her government{{cite web|url=https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/page/achievements|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110813013132/https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/page/achievements|archive-date=13 August 2011 |title=Achievements |publisher=Tymoshenko.ua |date=24 December 2013 |access-date=28 December 2013}} introducing anti crisis program for the key industries (mining and metallurgical sector, agrarian sector, chemical production, construction and development), strengthening Ukraine's energy independence through direct gas deals with Russian Federation, restoring Ukraine's fame as the world's bread basket due to unprecedentedly rich crops of grain (53.3 million tons in 2008, and 46 million tons in 2009), preventing drop in the agricultural production, revitalization of high-tech industries such as space engineering and aircraft construction, resuming of series construction of Antonov aircraft, getting final [[UEFA]]'s confirmation for conduct [[UEFA Euro 2012|Euro-2012 tournament in Ukraine]], introducing independent high school graduate testing, obtaining WTO membership, and commencing negotiations on [[EU association]].|group=nb}} Tymoshenko's government launched an anti-corruption campaign and identified it as one of its priorities.{{#tag:ref|Tymoshenko introduced an apparatus of the Cabinet of Ministers' Commissioner for anti corruption issues and made sure that a number of anti corruption laws and government regulations (acts) were adopted. Tymoshenko herself was personally in charge of fight with "gas" and "land" mafia, which, in the first case, ended up with removal of corruption intermediary from gas trade between Russia and Ukraine; in the second case it ended up with introduction of free of charge registration and issuing land ownership documents for citizens. According to Tymoshenko, the efficient anti corruption campaign [167] and significant financial discipline armed the government with additional resources for dealing with burning social problems during the crisis period. Due to that the following goals were achieved: pensions, stipends and salaries to the state-run organizations' employees were paid on time; gas and electricity prices for households were not raised; people received their deposits from the bankrupt banks; additional payments for teachers, healthcare workers and librarians were provided; financial aid to families with a newborn child was increased in several times; those who qualify, received more than 5500 state subsidized apartments; about 6 million of clients of former Soviet Oshchadbank received compensations for their lost savings; people could register their land plots free of charge.|group=nb}} ===Gas dispute between Russia and Ukraine (2009)=== [[File:Flickr - europeanpeoplesparty - EPP Congress Bonn (781).jpg|thumb|Yulia Tymoshenko, 9 December 2009]] The conditions leading to the [[2009 Russia–Ukraine gas dispute|2009 gas dispute]]{{cite web|url=https://www.oxfordenergy.org/2009/02/the-russo-ukrainian-gas-dispute-of-january-2009-a-comprehensive-assessment/|title=The russo-ukrainian gas dispute of January 2009: a comprehensive assessment|publisher=Oxfordenergy.org|access-date=1 March 2009|archive-date=9 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110709114516/http://www.oxfordenergy.org/2009/02/the-russo-ukrainian-gas-dispute-of-january-2009-a-comprehensive-assessment/|url-status=live}} were created in 2006, under the Yushchenko government, when Ukraine started buying Russian gas through an intermediary, Swiss-registered RosUkrEnergo. (Fifty percent of RosUkrEnergo shares were owned by the Russian Gazprom, with 45 percent and 5 percent owned by Ukrainian businessmen Dmytro Firtash and Ivan Fursin, respectively). Some sources indicate that notorious criminal boss [[Semion Mogilevich]] (associated with Dmytro Firtash) also owned shares in the company.{{cite web |first=Luke |last=Harding |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2010/dec/01/wikileaks-cables-russian-mafia-gas |title=WikiLeaks cables link Russian mafia boss to EU gas supplies |work=The Guardian |date=December 2010 |access-date=28 December 2013 |archive-date=16 April 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190416115301/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2010/dec/01/wikileaks-cables-russian-mafia-gas |url-status=live }}{{cite web |url=https://www.kyivpost.com/content/ukraine/new-and-conflicting-details-emerge-over-mogilevich-92521.html |title=New and conflicting details emerge over Mogilevich's alleged involvement in nation |publisher=Kyivpost.com |date=10 December 2010 |access-date=28 December 2013 |archive-date=24 January 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160124071826/http://www.kyivpost.com/content/ukraine/new-and-conflicting-details-emerge-over-mogilevich-92521.html |url-status=live }} When Tymoshenko resumed her prime minister duties in 2007, she initiated direct relations between Ukraine and Russia with regard to gas trading. A 2 October 2008 Memorandum signed by Tymoshenko and Putin stipulated liquidation of intermediaries in gas deals between the two countries and outlined detailed conditions for future gas contracts. The gas conflict of 2009 broke out because of two factors, the lack of a gas contract for 2009 and a $2.4 billion debt that Ukraine had yet to pay for gas received in 2008.{{citation needed|date=March 2023}} Tymoshenko stated that it was the RosUkrEnergo company that was responsible for the debt, rather than the state of Ukraine. She called for an end to corruption in the gas trade area and the establishment of direct contracts with the Russian Federation.{{cite web |url=https://www.kmu.gov.ua/control/en/publish/article?art_id=192188475&cat_id=244315174 |title=Yulia Tymoshenko: RosUkrEnergo has lost nothing, but only paid off its debts to Gazprom |publisher=Kmu.gov.ua |date=1 January 2009 |access-date=28 December 2013 |archive-date=3 March 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303204110/http://www.kmu.gov.ua/control/en/publish/article?art_id=192188475&cat_id=244315174 |url-status=live }} RosUkrEnergo, with the aid of its ties to Yushchenko's administration, managed to disrupt the signing of a gas contract scheduled for 31 December 2008. [[Alexey Miller]], head of Gazprom, stated that trader RosUkrEnergo broke down talks between Gazprom and Naftogaz Ukrainy: "Yes indeed, in late December 2008, the prime ministers of Russia and Ukraine came to agreement, and our companies were ready to seal the deal for $235 per 1000 cubic meters of natural gas with the condition that all the export operations from Ukraine will be done bilaterally. RosUkrEnergo then suggested to buy gas at $285 price." On 31 December 2008, Yushchenko gave Oleg Dubyna, head of Naftogaz Ukrainy, a direct order to stop talks, not sign the agreement and recall the delegation from Moscow. The decision made by the president of Ukraine brought on the crisis.{{cite web |url=https://zik.ua/en/news/2010/03/06/219714 |title=Oleh Dubyna Yushchenko and RUE guilty of 20-day gas spat with Russia in 2008 |publisher=Zik.ua |access-date=28 December 2013 |archive-date=15 December 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131215231652/http://zik.ua/en/news/2010/03/06/219714 |url-status=dead}}{{cite web|url=https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/article/9758x75e |title=Prosecution witness confirms links between Yushchenko and RosUkrEnergo (audio) |publisher=Tymoshenko.ua |access-date=28 December 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131215231540/https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/article/9758x75e |archive-date=15 December 2013}} On 14 January 2009, Tymoshenko said, "The negotiations on $235 gas price and $1.7–1.8 transit price, that started on October 2 and successfully have been moving forward since, have been broken up because, unfortunately, Ukrainian politicians were trying to keep RosUkrEnergo in business as a shadow intermediary... The negotiations between the two prime ministers and later between Gazprom and Naftogaz Ukrainy were ruined by those Ukrainian political groups, who have gotten and are planning to get corrupt benefits from RosUkrEnergo." On 17 January 2009, President of Russia Dmitriy Medvedev said, "I think that our Ukrainian partners and us can trade gas without any intermediaries, especially without intermediaries with questionable reputation. The problem is that some participants of negotiations insisted on keeping the intermediary referring to the instructions from the top."{{cite web|last=Bierman |first=Stephen |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/apps/news?pid=newsarchive&sid=agCru3iDPGAA |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131022141903/https://www.bloomberg.com/apps/news?pid=newsarchive&sid=agCru3iDPGAA |url-status=dead |archive-date=22 October 2013 |title=Russia-Ukraine Gas Row May Center on RosUkrEnergo, Analyst Says |publisher=Bloomberg |date=8 January 2009 |access-date=28 December 2013}} On 1 January 2009, at 10 am, Gazprom completely stopped pumping gas to Ukraine.{{cite web|url=https://www.wsws.org/en/articles/2009/01/russ-j03.html?view=print|title=Russia cuts off Ukrainian gas supply – World Socialist Web Site|first=Niall|last=Green|date=3 January 2009|access-date=1 March 2022|archive-date=4 November 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181104130219/http://www.wsws.org/en/articles/2009/01/russ-j03.html?view=print|url-status=live}} On 4 January, the Russian monopolist offered to sell Ukraine gas for $450 per 1000 cubic meter (minus a fee for gas transit through Ukraine), which was defined as a standard price for Eastern European countries. On 8 January 2009, the prime minister of Russia, Vladimir Putin, said that Ukraine would have to pay $470 for 1000 cubic meters of natural gas.{{cite web|url=https://www.unian.ua/society/178348-putin-yakscho-ukrajina-pide-na-470-dolariv-tranzit-mojliviy-za-3-4-dolari.html|title=Putin: if Ukraine goes to $ 470, transit is possible for 3–4 dollars|publisher=Unian.ua|date=8 January 2009|access-date=8 January 2009|archive-date=17 April 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190417002012/https://www.unian.ua/society/178348-putin-yakscho-ukrajina-pide-na-470-dolariv-tranzit-mojliviy-za-3-4-dolari.html|url-status=live}} Between 1 and 18 January, Central and Eastern European countries received significantly less gas. Ukrainian heat-and-power stations were working to utmost capacity. Due to sub-zero temperatures, the entire housing and public utilities sectors were on the verge of collapse. On 14 January, the European Commission and the Czech presidency in the European Union demanded the immediate renewal of gas deliveries in full capacity lest the reputations of Russia and Ukraine as reliable EU partners be seriously damaged. On 18 January 2009, after five-day-long talks, prime ministers Putin and Tymoshenko came to agreement on the renewal of gas delivery to Ukraine and other EU countries. The parties agreed upon the following: A return to direct contract deals between Gazprom and Naftogaz Ukrainy; the removal of non-transparent intermediaries; the introduction of formula-based pricing for Ukraine (which also works for other Eastern European countries); and a switch to a $2.7 transit fee, which is close to the average price in Europe. According to the new gas contract, in 2009 Ukraine paid an average price of $232.98 per 1000 cubic meters,{{cite web |url=https://ukraineanalysis.wordpress.com/2009/02/01/the-new-gas-deal-threats-and-risks-for-ukraine/ |title=THE NEW GAS DEAL: THREATS AND RISKS FOR UKRAINE |publisher=Ukraineanalysis.wordpress.com |date=1 February 2009 |access-date=28 December 2013 |archive-date=16 April 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190416171843/https://ukraineanalysis.wordpress.com/2009/02/01/the-new-gas-deal-threats-and-risks-for-ukraine/ |url-status=live }} while other European consumers were paying above $500 per 1000 cubic meters.{{cite web |url=https://en.ria.ru/russia/20081112/118261776.html |title=Gazprom says natural gas prices for Europe to drop in 2009 |publisher=En.ria.ru |date=12 November 2008 |access-date=28 December 2013 |archive-date=13 November 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131113192041/http://en.ria.ru/russia/20081112/118261776.html |url-status=live }} ===2010 presidential election=== {{Main|2010 Ukrainian presidential election}} Tymoshenko was a candidate in the [[2010 Ukrainian presidential election|Ukrainian presidential elections of 2010]], but lost that election to [[Viktor Yanukovych]] (Tymoshenko received 45.47% of the votes in the second and final round of the election, 3% less than her rival).[https://www.reuters.com/article/idUSTRE60H3OU20100118 Ukrainian PM to face old rival in runoff election] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100207163520/http://www.reuters.com/article/idUSTRE60H3OU20100118 |date=7 February 2010 }}, [[Reuters]] (18 January 2010) In 2009, the relations between Tymoshenko and Yushchenko,[https://www.interfax.com.ua/eng/main/7403/ Tymoshenko accuses Yuschenko of obstructing executive authorities' teamwork] {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120515212242/https://www.interfax.com.ua/eng/main/7403/ |date=15 May 2012}}, [[Interfax-Ukraine]] (6 February 2009)[https://www.interfax.com.ua/eng/main/6784/ Yuschenko demands immediate amendments to 2009 budget to save Ukraine's economy – televised address to nation] {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120515212257/https://www.interfax.com.ua/eng/main/6784/ |date=15 May 2012}}, [[Interfax|Interfax-Ukraine]] (30 January 2009)[https://zaxid.net/news/showNews.do?timoshenko_rozpovila_na_chomu_bazuvavsya_yiyi_konflikt_z_yushhenkom&objectId=1125830/ Tymoshenko told based on what a conflict with Yushchenko] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200512153423/https://zaxid.net/news/showNews.do?timoshenko_rozpovila_na_chomu_bazuvavsya_yiyi_konflikt_z_yushhenkom&objectId=1125830/ |date=12 May 2020 }}, zaxid.net (29 March 2011)[https://www.unian.net/eng/news/news-299984.html Agreement with Russia threatens Ukraine's security – President] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090213194604/http://www.unian.net/eng/news/news-299984.html |date=13 February 2009 }}, [[UNIAN]] (10 February 2009) the [[Secretariat of the President of Ukraine]][https://www.interfax.com.ua/eng/main/7322/ Presidential secretariat considers PM's report "theatrical performance"] {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120515212314/https://www.interfax.com.ua/eng/main/7322/ |date=15 May 2012}}, [[Interfax-Ukraine]] (5 February 2009) and the oppositional Party of Regions remained hostile.[https://en.interfax.com.ua/news/general/7377.html Premier says Regions Party wanted to destabilize government work] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171010104636/http://en.interfax.com.ua/news/general/7377.html |date=10 October 2017 }}, [[Interfax-Ukraine]] (5 February 2009) This company then was owned by Dmitry Firtash – 45%, by Yushchenko family – 27%, and the Russian Gazprom. In 2007, Tymoshenko argued for a direct contract for gas supplies from Russia to Ukraine. In 2008, the two prime ministers signed a Memorandum on the elimination of gas intermediaries.[https://maidan.org.ua/arch/arch2009/1235116115.html/Tymoshenko and Putin has thwarted a deal-Firtash Yushchenko, Kaczynski]{{dead link|date=November 2016 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes}}, maidan.org.ua (20 February 2009)[https://www.kmu.gov.ua/control/en/publish/article?art_id=192188475&cat_id=244315174/Yulia Tymoshenko: RosUkrEnergo has lost nothing, but only paid off its debts to Gazprom] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171010155550/http://www.kmu.gov.ua/control/en/publish/article?art_id=192188475&cat_id=244315174/Yulia |date=10 October 2017 }}, [[kmu.gov.ua]] (11 February 2009) She emphasized early in February 2009 that the "election struggle for the next presidential elections has virtually begun."[https://www.ukranews.com/eng/article/179917.html Tymoshenko: Political Competition Accounts For Conflict With Yuschenko], [[Ukrainian News Agency]] (11 February 2009) {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120315131337/https://www.ukranews.com/eng/article/179917.html |date=15 March 2012}}[https://www.ukranews.com/eng/article/178868.html Rada Denies Support To No Confidence In Cabinet], [[Ukrainian News Agency]] (5 February 2009) {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120330071954/https://www.ukranews.com/eng/article/178868.html |date=30 March 2012}}{{cite news|url=https://en.interfax.com.ua/news/general/7352.html|title=Ukrainian parliament rejects no-confidence vote in government|work=[[Interfax-Ukraine]]|date=5 February 2009|access-date=29 January 2016|archive-date=16 April 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190416171156/https://en.interfax.com.ua/news/general/7352.html|url-status=live}} "This is a competition during economic crisis; [some people] prefer to collect political benefits from these problems instead of solving them together", Tymoshenko said in an interview with German newspaper Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung in February 2009.[https://economics.unian.net/other/189462-timoshenko-yuschenko-moy-politicheskiy-konkurent.html/ Tymoshenko: Yushchenko – my political opponent] {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160818214143/https://economics.unian.net/other/189462-timoshenko-yuschenko-moy-politicheskiy-konkurent.html/ |date=18 August 2016}}, economics.unian.net (10 February 2009)[https://www.pravda.com.ua/news/2009/02/10/3722659/?attempt=1?attempt=2/ Tymoshenko told the Europeans about his relationship with Yushchenko] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181214123123/https://www.pravda.com.ua/news/2009/02/10/3722659/?attempt=1%3Fattempt%3D2%2F |date=14 December 2018 }}, [[Pravda.com.ua]] (10 February 2009) Later, in an interview with the French paper Le Monde, the prime minister said that "the president treats her as a rival striving for president's office."[https://www.lemonde.fr/international/article/2009/03/03/ioulia-timochenko-il-faut-des-elections-anticipees-en-ukraine_1162457_3210.html/Yulia Tymoshenko: "There must be elections in Ukraine"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200512153410/https://www.lemonde.fr/international/article/2009/03/03/ioulia-timochenko-il-faut-des-elections-anticipees-en-ukraine_1162457_3210.html/Yulia |date=12 May 2020 }}, [[Lemonde.fr]] (3 March 2009) She also added that the previously mentioned political instability fuels economic crisis. Tymoshenko then called for an early presidential election. Having long being considered a possible candidate for President of Ukraine in the 2010 election, {{cite news|first=Gregory|last=Feifer|title=Ukraine's Tymoshenko Likely Prime Minister|url=https://www.npr.org/templates/story/story.php?storyId=14887224&ft=1&f=3|publisher=[[NPR]]|date=2 October 2007|access-date=5 October 2007}} {{cite news|first1=Roman|last1=Olearchyk|first2=Stefan|last2=Wagstyl|title=A tough and populist maverick|url=https://us.ft.com/ftgateway/superpage.ft?news_id=fto100120071546266144|work=Financial Times|date=2 October 2007|access-date=5 October 2007|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071012003409/https://us.ft.com/ftgateway/superpage.ft?news_id=fto100120071546266144|archive-date=12 October 2007}} Tymoshenko announced that she would indeed compete in the upcoming presidential election in a statement broadcast live on national TV on 7 June 2009.[https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/8088258.stm Ukraine PM to stand for president] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220301174311/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/8088258.stm |date=1 March 2022 }}, BBC News (7 June 2009)[https://www.kommersant.com/p883325/r_1/PACE_Ukrainian_presidential_election/ Yulia Tymoshenko Won't Be President] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081120154839/https://www.kommersant.com/p883325/r_1/PACE_Ukrainian_presidential_election/ |date=20 November 2008}}, [[Kommersant]] (17 April 2008) Tymoshenko also stated that if she lost the presidential election she would not challenge the results.[https://en.interfax.com.ua/news/general/20852.html Tymoshenko not to challenge election results if defeated] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303201650/http://en.interfax.com.ua/news/general/20852.html |date=3 March 2016 }}, [[Interfax-Ukraine]] (24 September 2009)[https://www.unian.info/politics/269057-tymoshenko-promised-not-to-dispute-results-of-election-of-president.html Tymoshenko promised not to dispute results of election of President] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304002538/http://www.unian.info/politics/269057-tymoshenko-promised-not-to-dispute-results-of-election-of-president.html |date=4 March 2016 }}, [[UNIAN]] (24 September 2009) On 12 September 2009, a tour in support of Tymoshenko's candidacy, called "With Ukraine in Heart", began on Kyiv's Maidan Nezalezhnosti. Popular Ukrainian singers and bands took part in the tour.Artists included [[Ruslana]], [[Oleksandr Ponomariov|Oleksandr Ponomaryov]], [[Ani Lorak]], [[Potap]] and [[Nastia Kamenskikh]], [[Tina Karol]], [[Natalia Mogilevska]], [[Iryna Bilyk]], [[TIK (band)|TIK]], [[TNMK]], "[[Druha Rika]]", [[Mad Heads XL]]. See the concert here {{cite web |title=VideoInfo.com.ua |url=https://www.videoinfo.com.ua/ |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090922215248/https://www.videoinfo.com.ua/ |archive-date=22 September 2009 |access-date=24 September 2009}}{{cite web|url=https://photo.unian.net/eng/themes/14524|title=Events by themes: Allukrainian round "With Ukraine in a heart!"|work=unian.net|access-date=7 November 2015|archive-date=21 November 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151121202715/http://photo.unian.net/eng/themes/14524|url-status=live}}[https://www.unian.info/politics/264689-mogilevska-went-to-tymoshenko.html Mogilevska went to Tymoshenko] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304053756/http://www.unian.info/politics/264689-mogilevska-went-to-tymoshenko.html |date=4 March 2016 }}, [[UNIAN]] (11 September 2008) On 24 October 2009, the delegates of all-Ukrainian union "Batkivshchyna" formally and unanimously endorsed Tymoshenko as their candidate for the next Presidential election.{{cite web|url=https://kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/51264/ |title=Tymoshenko enters presidential race |date=25 October 2009 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101128082228/https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/51264/ |archive-date=28 November 2010}}{{cite web|url=https://www.rawstory.com/news/afp/Ukraine_PM_enters_tight_presidentia_10242009.html|archive-url=http://webarchive.loc.gov/all/20190905190618/https://www.rawstory.com/news/afp/Ukraine_PM_enters_tight_presidentia_10242009.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=5 September 2019|title=Ukraine PM enters tight presidential race|date=24 October 2009|access-date=1 March 2022}} The 200 thousand congress took place on Kyiv's Independence Square. On 31 October 2009, the Central Election Commission registered Tymoshenko as a candidate for presidential election in 2010.{{cite web|url=https://www04.cvk.gov.ua/pls/acts/getdf719.html?id=24475&ptext=|title=The RESOLUTION from October 31, 2009 № 228 On registration of the candidate on a post of the President of Ukraine Tymoshenko Yulia Vladimirovna|publisher=Cvk.gov.ua|date=31 October 2009|access-date=31 October 2009}}{{dead link|date=November 2017 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes}} The Tymoshenko candidacy was also endorsed by prominent Ukrainian politicians such as [[Borys Tarasyuk]], [[Yuriy Lutsenko]], former President [[Leonid Kravchuk]],{{in lang|uk}} [https://tabloid.pravda.com.ua/photos/4ae4e8869323c/ Тимошенко розповіла, як розвантажувала шини, а Скрипка за неї співав] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190621112047/https://tabloid.pravda.com.ua/photos/4ae4e8869323c/ |date=21 June 2019 }}, [[Табло ID]] (26 October 2009) the [[Christian Democratic Union (Ukraine)|Christian Democratic Union]],[https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/53839/ Christian-Democratic Union party to support Tymoshenko at presidential election], ''Kyiv Post'' (28 November 2009) {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091202121219/https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/53839/ |date=2 December 2009}} the [[European Party of Ukraine]][https://en.interfax.com.ua/news/general/26968.html European Party gives its support to Tymoshenko in presidential race] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171010104609/http://en.interfax.com.ua/news/general/26968.html |date=10 October 2017 }}, [[Interfax-Ukraine]] (4 December 2009) and others.{{cite news|url=https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/54883/|title=Interior Minister takes short leave, urges voters to support Tymoshenko|work=Kyiv Post|date=11 December 2009|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110201035035/https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/54883/|archive-date=1 February 2011}} Putin stated that he was cooperating with Tymoshenko as Prime Minister of Ukraine, but that he was not supporting her in the election.[https://www.reuters.com/article/russia-ukraine-idUSGEE5B217420091203 Russia's Putin says not backing Ukraine's Tymoshenko], [[Reuters]] (3 December 2009)[https://blogs.pravda.com.ua/authors/kuzyo/4b65a60d57cd9/ In Ukrainian election, Russia supports Yanukovych] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171010155712/http://blogs.pravda.com.ua/authors/kuzyo/4b65a60d57cd9/ |date=10 October 2017 }}, [[Pravda.com.ua]] (31 January 2010)[https://ua.ukrrudprom.ua/digest/Prorosyskiy_kandidat.html/a Pro-Russian candidate]{{dead link|date=November 2016 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes}}, ua.ukrrudprom.ua (1 September 2009)[https://www.volyn.com.ua/?rub=4&article=0&arch=978/ Who Is Really Pro-Russian Candidate for President of Ukraine], volyn.com.ua (22 August 2009) {{Quote box|width=50%|quote=As soon as [[Viktor Yushchenko|Yushchenko]] and [[Viktor Yanukovych|Yanukovych]] appear on the tribune, expect failure. And how can we forget [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA second round#Matches|the match between Ukraine and Greece, when our team lost the trip to South Africa]]. Why? Because two "lucky" politicians came to the deciding match and transferred their lucky aura to the entire [[Ukraine national football team|Ukrainian team]]. |source=Yulia Tymoshenko's personal blog (7 December 2009)[https://blog.tymoshenko.ua/en/article/4x5c879u About football and luck] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100112185202/https://blog.tymoshenko.ua/en/article/4x5c879u |date=12 January 2010}}, Yulia Tymoshenko official personal blog (7 December 2009)}} Tymoshenko's campaign was expected to have cost $100 to $150 million. Tymoshenko expected early parliamentary elections after the 2010 presidential election if Yanukovych won the vote, but she was against this.[https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/53628/ Tymoshenko: Early parliamentary elections may follow presidential ballot in Ukraine], ''Kyiv Post'' (26 November 2009) {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110605122257/https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/53628/ |date=5 June 2011}} On 1 December 2009, Tymoshenko urged "national democratic forces" to unite around the candidate who garnered the largest number of votes after the first round of the presidential elections. "If we are not able to strengthen our efforts and unite the whole national-patriotic and democratic camp of Ukraine... we will be much weaker than those who want revenge."[https://en.interfax.com.ua/news/general/26599.html Tymoshenko urges national democratic forces to unite around candidate in second round] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303181608/http://en.interfax.com.ua/news/general/26599.html |date=3 March 2016 }}, [[Interfax-Ukraine]] (1 December 2009) On 5 December 2009, she declared she would go into opposition if she lost the presidential election. She also complained of flaws in the election legislation, and expressed her certainty that attempts were being made by her opponents to carry out vote rigging.[https://tsn.ua/ukrayina/severinsen-pr-znovu-stvorila-umovi-dlya-falsifikatsiyi-viboriv.html/ Severinsen: Party of Regions has again created conditions for falsification of elections] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100207071728/http://tsn.ua/ukrayina/severinsen-pr-znovu-stvorila-umovi-dlya-falsifikatsiyi-viboriv.html |date=7 February 2010}}, tsn.ua (4 February 2010) [[File:Ukraine Presidential Jan 2010 Vote (Tymoshenko).png|thumb|Yulia Tymoshenko (first round) – percentage of total national vote (25%)]] [[File:Ukraine Presidential Feb 2010 Vote (Tymoshenko).png|thumb|Yulia Tymoshenko (second round) – percentage of total national vote (45%)]] In the first round of the presidential election on 17 January 2010, Tymoshenko took second place with 25% of the vote, and Yanukovych took first place with 35%. The two proceeded to a [[two-round system|runoff]].{{cite web|url=https://www.pravda.com.ua/articles/2010/01/18/4630133/|title=Official Election Results 2010. The First Round|publisher=Pravda.com.ua|date=18 January 2010|access-date=18 January 2010|archive-date=3 April 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190403184840/https://www.pravda.com.ua/articles/2010/01/18/4630133/|url-status=live}} On 3 February 2010, two days before the run-off, the deputies from Party of Regions, Communist Party of Ukraine, "Our Ukraine – People's Self-Defense" bloc and independent MPs amended the Law on Election of President, which changed the mode of composition and functioning of election commissions. BYuT warned that these amendments would create opportunities for the massive rigging of elections. Tymoshenko called on president Yushchenko to veto the law. Hanne Severinsen, former rapporteur of PACE Monitoring Committee on Ukraine, also called on the president to veto the law. Severinsen's statement read: "Unfortunately, the Party of Regions, as in 2004, is trying to create conditions for vote fraud."{{cite web|url=https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/article/67xmnoz4 |title=Hanne Severinsen: Party of Regions trying to repeat 2004 |publisher=Tymoshenko.ua |access-date=28 December 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131111224530/https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/article/67xmnoz4 |archive-date=11 November 2013}} Despite these requests, Yushchenko signed the amended Law. This action generated vast international criticism from the Council of Europe and from members of the US congress' Commission on Security and Cooperation in Europe.{{cite web |url=https://www.ukrinform.net/rubric-ukrnews/916527-pace_changes_to_presidential_election_law_should_not_be_applied_on_election_day_179275.html |title=PACE statement |publisher=Ukrinform.ua |access-date=28 December 2013 |archive-date=9 January 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160109180126/http://www.ukrinform.net/rubric-ukrnews/916527-pace_changes_to_presidential_election_law_should_not_be_applied_on_election_day_179275.html |url-status=dead}}{{cite web |url=https://www.csce.gov/index.cfm?FuseAction=ContentRecords.ViewDetail&ContentRecord_id=852&ContentType=P&ContentRecordType=P&UserGroup_id=50®ion_id=50&year=0&month=0&Subaction=PressReleases&IsTextOnly=True&CFID=16178548&CFTOKEN=40297160 |title=Statement of the Commission on Security and Cooperation in Europe |publisher=Csce.gov |date=19 January 2010 |access-date=28 December 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131111223842/https://www.csce.gov/index.cfm?FuseAction=ContentRecords.ViewDetail&ContentRecord_id=852&ContentType=P&ContentRecordType=P&UserGroup_id=50®ion_id=50&year=0&month=0&Subaction=PressReleases&IsTextOnly=True&CFID=16178548&CFTOKEN=40297160 |archive-date=11 November 2013}} The Committee of Voters of Ukraine stated that the amendments to the Law on Election of President "contained the biggest threats for democratic mode of the run-off."{{cite web |url=https://www.pravda.com.ua/news/2010/02/5/4718329/ |title=Новий закон – найбільша загроза виборам, Українська правда |publisher=Pravda.com.ua |date=5 February 2010 |access-date=28 December 2013 |archive-date=27 June 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180627034402/https://www.pravda.com.ua/news/2010/02/5/4718329/ |url-status=live }} Tymoshenko did not receive endorsement from other candidates who had not survived the first round of voting.{{cite web|first=Serhiy|last=Kudelia|url=https://www.utoronto.ca/jacyk/ElectionWatch/Blog/Entries/2010/1/29_Tymoshenkos_Looming_Defeat__How_Did_She_Make_It_Inevitable-_Serhiy_Kudelia.html|title=Tymoshenko's Looming Defeat: How Did She Make It Inevitable?|date=29 January 2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131111222814/https://www.utoronto.ca/jacyk/ElectionWatch/Blog/Entries/2010/1/29_Tymoshenkos_Looming_Defeat__How_Did_She_Make_It_Inevitable-_Serhiy_Kudelia.html|archive-date=11 November 2013}} In the run-off held on 7 February 2010,Because no candidate in the first round ballot had 50% or more votes, the two highest polling candidates faced off in a second round of the elections (Source: [https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/57219/ Presidential election gets under way in Ukraine], ''Kyiv Post'' (17 January 2010)) {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120603125154/https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/57219/ |date=3 June 2012}} Yanukovych was elected President of Ukraine. According to the Central Election Commission, he received 48.95% of the votes; Tymoshenko received 45.47% of the votes.{{in lang|uk}}[https://www.cvk.gov.ua/pls/vp2010/WP0011 Regular elections of the President of Ukraine 17 January 2010], [[Central Election Commission of Ukraine]] {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150519022003/https://www.cvk.gov.ua/pls/vp2010/WP0011 |date=19 May 2015}} Tymoshenko won 17 of 27 constituencies in the western, central and north regions of Ukraine and in Kyiv.{{cite web|url=https://www.pravda.com.ua/rus/articles/2010/02/7/4730368/|title=The Results of the 2010 Election. The Second Round|publisher=Pravda.com.ua|date=7 February 2010|access-date=7 February 2010|archive-date=4 November 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181104165948/https://www.pravda.com.ua/rus/articles/2010/02/7/4730368/|url-status=live}} Yulia Tymoshenko Bloc members immediately claimed that there was systematic and large-scale vote rigging in the run-off election.[https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/59198/ Turchynov: vote rigging in favor of Yanukovych was systematic, large-scale], ''Kyiv Post'' (10 February 2010) {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120609121309/https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/59198/ |date=9 June 2012}}[https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/article/6za4s343 Nataliya Korolevska: victory will be ours], Yulia Tymoshenko official website (8 February 2010) {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140815142406/https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/article/6za4s343 |date=15 August 2014}}[https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/article/37u69796 Andriy Shevchenko: whole gamut of fraud in Donbas], Yulia Tymoshenko official website (8 February 2010) {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140815142413/https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/article/37u69796 |date=15 August 2014}} However, Tymoshenko herself did not issue a statement about the election[https://www.rferl.org/content/Ukraines_Tymoshenko_Slams_Rival_No_Comment_On_Election_Result/1954993.html Ukraine's Tymoshenko Slams Rival, No Comment On Election Result] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303214304/http://www.rferl.org/content/Ukraines_Tymoshenko_Slams_Rival_No_Comment_On_Election_Result/1954993.html |date=3 March 2016 }}, [[Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty]] (11 February 2010)[https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/8508276.stm Ukraine election: Yanukovych urges Tymoshenko to quit] , BBC News (10 February 2010) until a live televised broadcast on 13 February 2010, in which she said that she would challenge the election result in court. Tymoshenko alleged widespread fraud{{cite news | title = Tymoshenko calls Ukraine vote rigged | url = https://georgiandaily.com/index.php?option=com_content&task=view&id=17058&Itemid=65 | agency = Associated Press | access-date = 1 March 2022 | archive-date = 28 March 2022 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20220328184306/http://georgiandaily.com/index.php?option=com_content&task=view&id=17058&Itemid=65 | url-status = dead}} (14 February 2010) (according to Tymoshenko, a million votes were invalid) and said Yanukovych was not legitimately elected. "Whatever happens in future, he will never become the legitimately elected President of Ukraine." Tymoshenko did not call people into the streets to protest, and stated that she "won't tolerate civil confrontation."{{cite web|url=https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/article/986a3822|title=Yulia Tymoshenko's appeal to the people|work=Official website of Yulia Tymoshenko|date=13 February 2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100217183100/https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/article/986a3822|archive-date=17 February 2010}}{{cite news|url=https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/59461/|title=Update: Tymoshenko says Ukraine vote was rigged|work=Kyiv Post|date=14 February 2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110129124307/https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/59461/|archive-date=29 January 2011}}{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/8514517.stm|title=Ukraine: Tymoshenko vows to contest election result|work=BBC News|date=15 February 2010|access-date=29 January 2016|archive-date=30 January 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160130181125/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/8514517.stm|url-status=live}} On 10 February 2010, Yanukovych called on Tymoshenko to abandon her protests and resign as prime minister. Yanukovych stated he wanted to form a new coalition, and may try to call snap parliamentary elections. On 12 February, Yanukovych stated he would not rule out talks with Tymoshenko if she would publicly apologize to him for accusations she made during her election campaign.{{cite news|url=https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/59383/|title=Yanukovych: Talks with Tymoshenko possible only if she apologizes|publisher=Kyiv Post|date=12 February 2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110201054907/https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/59383/|archive-date=1 February 2011}} Tymoshenko's government did not want to resign voluntarily.{{cite news|url=https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/59272/|title=Update: Ukraine turmoil as defiant Tymoshenko clings on as Prime Minister|publisher=Kyiv Post|agency=Reuters|date=11 February 2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100213164432/https://www.kyivpost.com/news/politics/detail/59272/|archive-date=13 February 2010}} On 17 February 2010, the Higher Administrative Court of Ukraine suspended the results of the election on Tymoshenko's appeal.[https://www.nytimes.com/2010/02/18/world/europe/18ukraine.html High Court in Ukraine Weighs Appeal on Election] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141110122544/http://www.nytimes.com/2010/02/18/world/europe/18ukraine.html |date=10 November 2014 }}, ''[[The New York Times]]'' (17 February 2010) The court suspended the [[Central Election Commission of Ukraine]] ruling that announced that Yanukovych won the election.[https://web.archive.org/web/20100221065939/http://www.google.com/hostednews/ap/article/ALeqM5hYHMIGxCNY8KpokmVs53ufhC8qIQD9DTT0H00 Ukrainian election results suspended on appeal], [[Associated Press]] (17 February 2010)[https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/8519922.stm Ukrainian election result suspended after PM's appeal], BBC News (17 February 2010) Tymoshenko withdrew her appeal on 20 February 2010, after the Higher Administrative Court in Kyiv rejected her petition to scrutinize documents from election districts in [[Crimea]] and to question election and law-enforcement officials.{{cite news |last=Levy |first=Clifford J. |title=Ukraine Leader Drops Vote Challenge |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2010/02/21/world/europe/21ukraine.html?_r=1 |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=21 February 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220121065059/https://www.nytimes.com/2010/02/21/world/europe/21ukraine.html?_r=2 |archive-date=21 January 2022 |page=A8 |url-status=live |access-date=8 October 2013}} According to Tymoshenko, "It became clear that the court is not out to establish the truth, and, unfortunately, the court is as biased as the Central Election Commission, which includes a political majority from Yanukovych."[https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/article/uv4w2uwz Yulia Tymoshenko: sooner or later an honest court will assess the fraudulent 2010 elections], Official website of Yulia Tymoshenko (20 February 2010) {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141110114613/https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/article/uv4w2uwz |date=10 November 2014}} Tymoshenko also stated, "At the very least there was rigging of votes using the main methods of falsification, and I think that for history this lawsuit with all the documentation will remain in the Higher Administrative Court of Ukraine, and sooner or later, an honest prosecutor's office and an honest court will assess that Yanukovych wasn't elected President of Ukraine, and that the will of the people had been rigged."[https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/article/vu8az6s6 Yulia Tymoshenko will not challenge election results in Supreme Court], Official website of Yulia Tymoshenko (20 February 2010) {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151018204137/https://www.tymoshenko.ua/en/article/vu8az6s6 |date=18 October 2015}} On 22 February 2010, Tymoshenko announced in a televised speech that she believed the presidential election to have been rigged and did not recognize its results. "As well as millions of Ukrainians, I state: Yanukovych is not our president", she said. She called on the democratic parliamentary factions to not seek "political employment" at the Party of Regions (meaning to avoid negotiations with the Party of Regions regarding the new coalition) and to "quit arguing and create a united team that would not let an anti-Ukrainian dictatorship usurp the power". ===In opposition after 2010 presidential election=== {{Quote box|width=40%|align=right|quote= The falsifications decided the elections, not you. Like millions of [[Ukrainians]], I assert that Yanukovych is not our president.|source=[[Prime minister|PM]] Tymoshenko televised speech (22 February 2010)}} During a [[Television in Ukraine|nationally televised]] address on 22 February, Tymoshenko said of President-elect Yanukovych and "Yanukovych's team" (she referred to them in the speech as "The [[oligarchy]]"): "They need cheap labour, poor and disenfranchised people who can be forced to work at their factories for peanuts, they also need Ukraine's riches, which they have been stealing for the last 18 years." During the speech she also accused outgoing President Viktor Yushchenko of "opening the door to massive and flagrant election rigging" days before 7 February runoff of the January 2010 presidential election by amending the election law.
